1 INTRODUCTION
1.1 Complaint by Costa Rica
1.1. On 11 January 2021, Costa Rica requested consultations with Panama pursuant to Articles 1
and 4 of the Understanding on Rules and Procedures Governing the Settlement of Disputes (DSU),
Article 19 of the Agreement on Agriculture, Article 11 of the Agreement on the Application of Sanitary
and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement), and Article XXII of the General Agreement on Tariffs
and Trade 1994 (GATT 1994), with respect to the measures and claims set out below.2
1.2. Consultations were held on 8 February 2021 but failed to resolve the dispute.
1.2 Establishment and composition of the Panel
1.3. On 19 August 2021, Costa Rica requested the establishment of a panel pursuant to Article 6 of
the DSU, with the standard terms of reference.3 At its meeting on 27 January 2021, the Dispute
Settlement Body (DSB) established a panel pursuant to the request of Costa Rica in document
WT/DS599/4, in accordance with Article 6 of the DSU.4
1.4. The Panel's terms of reference are the following:
To examine, in the light of the relevant provisions of the covered agreements cited by
the parties to the dispute, the matter referred to the DSB by Costa Rica in document
WT/DS599/4 and to make such findings as will assist the DSB in making the
recommendations or in giving the rulings provided for in those agreements.5
1.5. On 12 January 2022, Costa Rica requested the Director-General to determine the composition
of the Panel, pursuant to Article 8.7 of the DSU. On 24 January 2022, the Director-General
accordingly composed the Panel as follows:
Chairperson: Mr Mario MATUS
Members: Ms Delilah CABB AYALA
Ms María Manuela MOCCERO
1.6. Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, the European Union, Guatemala, Honduras, India, Mexico,
Nicaragua, the Russian Federation, Chinese Taipei, the United Kingdom, and the United States
notified their interest in participating in the Panel proceedings as third parties.
1.3 Panel proceedings
1.3.1 General
1.7. The Panel held an organizational meeting with the parties on 7 April 2022.6 After consulting
with the parties, the Panel adopted its Working Procedures7 and partial timetable on 19 April 2022.
On the same date, in response to Panama's request and having consulted the parties, the Panel also
adopted its Additional Working Procedures Concerning Business Confidential Information.8 The Panel
revised its timetable several times, after consultations with the parties, most recently on 30 January
2024.9

from Brazil, the European Union, and the United States.
1.9. Following a request from Panama to postpone the first substantive meeting with the parties,
originally scheduled for the week of 28 November 2022, the Panel held the meeting on 12 and
13 December 2022.10 A session with the third parties took place on 13 December 2022. Prior to the
first substantive meeting with the parties, on 5 December 2022, the Panel sent the parties a list of
advance questions to be answered at the meeting. Following the meeting, on 18 January 2023, the
Panel sent written questions to the parties and the third parties.
1.10. On 23 January 2023, Costa Rica requested that the deadline for the parties' second written
submissions be extended from 6 April 2023 to 21 April 2023. On the same date, Panama requested
that the date for the submission of responses to the Panel's questions following the first substantive
meeting with the parties be postponed from 15 February 2023 to 1 March 2023. Neither party
objected to the other's request, and the Panel agreed to modify the timetable on 27 January 2023
to reflect the changes requested.
1.11. On 15 February 2023, Australia, Brazil, Canada, the European Union, and the United States
submitted their third-party responses to the Panel's questions. On 1 March 2023, the Panel received
responses from Costa Rica and Panama.
1.12. Costa Rica and Panama filed their second written submissions on 21 April 2023. The Panel
held the second substantive meeting with the parties on 19 and 20 September 2023. Prior to the
second substantive meeting with the parties, on 11 September 2023, the Panel sent the parties a
list of advance questions to be answered at the meeting. Following the meeting, on 6 October 2023,
the Panel sent written questions to the parties.

receipt of the parties' responses to the Panel's questions and the parties' comments on the other
party's responses. Costa Rica also suggested that the integrated executive summaries be filed on
15 January 2024 instead of 4 December 2023. Panama stated that it had no objection in this regard.
The Panel accepted the extensions requested by Costa Rica. On 10 November 2023, the Panel
received the responses of Costa Rica and Panama. On 15 December 2023, the Panel received the
comments of Costa Rica and Panama on the other party's responses.
1.14. On 31 January 2024, the Panel issued the draft descriptive part of its report to the parties.
The Panel issued its Interim Report to the parties on 14 August 2024.11 The Panel issued its
Final Report to the parties on 16 October 2024.

1.15. On 13 June 2022, Panama requested the Panel to find that Costa Rica's panel request was
inconsistent with Article 6.2 of the DSU. Panama submitted that Costa Rica's panel request did not
satisfy the requirement to provide "a brief summary of the legal basis of the complaint sufficient to
present the problem clearly".12

2 FACTUAL ASPECTS
2.1 Measures at issue
2.1.1 Introduction
2.1. This dispute concerns certain measures imposed by Panama on imports of (a) strawberries;
(b) dairy and meat products; (c) pineapples; and (d) plantains and bananas from Costa Rica.22
2.2. In its request for the establishment of a panel, Costa Rica challenges the following measures:
a. "Measure restricting or prohibiting the importation of strawberries originating in
Costa Rica" (Measure 1)23, 24;
b. "Measure restricting or prohibiting the importation of: (i) dairy products; (ii) meat products
of bovine animals; (iii) meat products of swine; (iv) meat products (sausages) of bovine
animals, swine and poultry; (v) aquatic animal (fish) food; and (vi) pet (dog) food
containing protein of ruminant origin, all originating in Costa Rica" (Measure 2)25;
c. "Measure restricting or prohibiting the importation of fresh pineapples originating in
Costa Rica" (Measure 3)26; and
d. "Measure restricting or prohibiting the importation of plantains and bananas originating in
Costa Rica" (Measure 4).27
2.3. Each of the measures at issue is described below on the basis of Costa Rica's panel request, as
are their relevant regulatory contexts. Those matters of fact on which the parties disagree shall be
addressed by the Panel in its findings (section 7 of the report) to the extent necessary.
2.1.2 Measure concerning the importation of strawberries (Measure 1)
2.1.2.1 Description
2.4. According to Costa Rica, Measure 1 consists of a "ban on imports of strawberries from
Costa Rica"28, which Panama adopted on 20 February 2020, through Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020.29

2.5. In that communication, the Panamanian Food Safety Authority (AUPSA) informed the State
Phytosanitary Service of Costa Rica (SFE) that it had detected situations of non-compliance, in
February 2020, with respect to the level of chemical residues permitted in strawberries originating
in Costa Rica, in particular of the pesticide oxamyl.30 AUPSA also indicated that, as a result, imports
of strawberries from Costa Rica to the Republic of Panama would be "temporarily disallowed"
("deshabilita[das] temporalmente").31
2.1.2.2 The relevant Panamanian regulatory framework
2.6. Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008, the inactivation ("inhabilitación") of which was notified to
Costa Rica on 20 February 202032, establishes the phytosanitary requirements for imports of "fresh
strawberries (Fragaria ananassa) for human consumption and/or processing, originating in
Costa Rica".33 That Resolution requires, inter alia, that consignments of strawberries be accompanied
by a phytosanitary certificate, issued by the national plant protection organization (NPPO) of the
country of origin, certifying compliance with a series of requirements.34 The Resolution also requires
that the strawberries comply with other applicable national regulations.35
2.7. Among the regulations with which compliance is required in order to import strawberries is
Executive Decree No. 467 of 7 November 2007. This Decree is the legal instrument by which Panama
adopts the "Sanitary regulation establishing maximum residue limits [MRLs]36 of pesticides37 and
other contaminants in fruit and vegetables for domestic consumption and export" (MRL Regulation).
2.8. According to Article 2 of Executive Decree No. 467, the MRLs established in the MRL Regulation
are based on those "set by the Codex Alimentarius Commission or, in their absence, those
established by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European
Community (EC)".38 The MRL Regulation does not expressly establish an MRL for oxamyl in
strawberries.39 However, Panama indicates that, pursuant to Article 2 of Executive Decree No. 467,
it adopted the MRL of the European Union for oxamyl in strawberries, corresponding to the limit of

31 Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020 (Exhibit CRI-7).

for consumption". (Note DNSV-0111-2021-OIAR (Exhibit CRI-13)).
33 Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008 concerning the importation of strawberries (Exhibit PAN-34).

(b) the strawberries come from areas, places or sites subject to inspections by the NPPO of the country of
origin during the current growing period, the picking and packing of the foodstuff; and (c) the consignments
are free from the pests Frankliniella occidentalis and Peridroma saucia.
35 Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008 concerning the importation of strawberries (Exhibit PAN-34),

36 The MRL Regulation defines "maximum residue limit" (MRL) as "the maximum concentration of a
pesticide residue (expressed as mg/kg), legally permitted in the Republic of Panama in food commodities.
MRLs are based on Good Agricultural Practice". (Ministry of Health of Panama, Executive Decree No. 467
adopting the MRL Regulation (2007) (Exhibit CRI-8), Title I, Chapter I, Article 1 of the MRL Regulation). The
Codex defines an MRL as the highest level of a pesticide residue that is legally tolerated in or on food or feed
when pesticides are applied correctly in accordance with Good Agricultural Practice. (Codex, Maximum Residue
Limits (MRLs) (2021) (Exhibit CRI-370)).
37 The MRL Regulation defines "pesticide" as "any substance or mixture of substances intended for
preventing, destroying, attracting, repelling or controlling any pest, including unwanted species of plants or
animals during the production, storage, transport, distribution and processing of food, agricultural commodities
or animal feeds. The term includes insecticides, fungicides, herbicides, acaricides, molluscicides, nematicides,
rodenticides; plant-growth regulators, desiccants, defoliants, fruit thinning agents, agents for preventing the
premature fall of fruit, substances applied to crops either before or after harvest. Fertilizers, plant nutrients
and food additives are excluded from this definition." (Ministry of Health of Panama, Executive Decree No. 467
adopting the MRL Regulation (2007) (Exhibit CRI-8), Chapter I, Article 1 of the MRL Regulation).
38 Ministry of Health of Panama, Executive Decree No. 467 adopting the MRL Regulation (2007)
(Exhibit CRI-8), Title I, Chapter I, Article 2 of the MRL Regulation. Although Article 2 of the MRL Regulation
explicitly refers to the MRLs "set out in the annex to this regulation", the said MRLs are stipulated in Article 17
of the Regulation.

(Exhibit CRI-8), Article 17 of the MRL Regulation, p. 18.

detection, namely 0.01 mg/kg, as neither the Codex Commission nor the EPA had established an

2.1.3 Measure concerning the importation of dairy and meat products (Measure 2)
2.1.3.1 Description
2.9. In its panel request, Costa Rica describes Measure 2 as an "import ban" on animal products
from Costa Rica in force since 30 June 2020.41 Costa Rica states that this "import ban" is maintained
by virtue of:
a. the non-renewal of sanitary approvals of various Costa Rican establishments that produce
and export the products concerned; and
b. Panama's requirement that Costa Rica undergo a new animal health eligibility procedure
as a condition to restore trade in these products, as if it were a country that was exporting
for the first time.42
2.10. Costa Rica lists in its panel request the 18 Costa Rican establishments that it considers to
have been affected by the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals by Panama.43
2.11. Given that Panama disagrees with Costa Rica's characterization of Measure 2, the Panel will
address this matter in section 7 of its report to the extent necessary, together with other disputed
factual matters.
2.1.3.2 The relevant Panamanian regulatory framework
2.12. Pursuant to the Panamanian regulatory framework44, the following cumulative preconditions
must be met in order to import dairy and meat products into Panama.
2.13. The first condition refers to the sanitary eligibility and animal health eligibility of the country
or region from which such products are intended to be exported. The AUPSA Manual on the sanitary
eligibility, phytosanitary recognition and approval of production chains and/or processing plants
(2013) (hereinafter, the AUPSA Manual on sanitary eligibility) establishes the procedures to be
followed by countries or regions in order to obtain sanitary eligibility45 granted by AUPSA, by product

2.18. According to Costa Rica, Measure 4 consists of a "ban on imports of Costa Rican plantains and

Costa Rica.62 AUPSA stated that subsequently it had "proceeded to temporarily inactivate
("inhabilitar temporalmente") [those] requirements".63
2.21. The parties disagree over whether the risk of introduction of the pest Fusarium oxysporum f.

2.2.2.2 The pesticide oxamyl
2.25. Oxamyl is a pesticide that belongs to the group of carbamate compounds71 used as an
insecticide, acaricide or nematicide.72 Oxamyl can be applied as a foliar spray, by soil incorporation,
or by watering onto soil in field, fruit and vegetable crops.73 Oxamyl is taken up by the plants through
the roots or by direct contact74 and controls a broad spectrum of insects, mites, ticks, and
roundworms.75

inspection by and registration with the NPPO of the country of origin; (b) that the consignment be free from
any live insects, as well as other biological, chemical and physical contaminants, including soil; and (c) that the

2.26. In terms of effects on human health, the EPA notes that oxamyl can overstimulate the nervous
system causing nausea, dizziness, confusion, and at very high exposures (e.g. accidents or major
spills), respiratory paralysis and death.76 It is also reported that exposure to oxamyl (by inhalation
or through the skin) can cause rapid, severe poisoning, with headache, dizziness, blurred vision,
tightness in the chest, sweating, nausea and vomiting, among other harmful effects.77
2.27. In 2008, the WHO classified oxamyl as highly hazardous.78 Similarly, the European authorities
have deemed oxamyl very toxic when inhaled or ingested, and the EPA considers it highly toxic via
the oral, dermal, and inhalation routes.79 It is included on the list of extremely hazardous substances
in the United States.80
2.28. As indicated above, Panama applies an MRL for oxamyl in strawberries of 0.01 mg/kg.81 For
its part, Costa Rica indicates that the use of oxamyl is prohibited in its strawberry crops.82
2.2.2.3 The pink hibiscus mealybug pest (Maconellicoccus hirsutus (Green))
2.29. The pink hibiscus mealybug (Maconellicoccus hirsutus (Green)) is a highly polyphagous insect,
so-called because of its colour and demonstrated preference for species of the Hibiscus genus.83
2.30. The pink mealybug is a small, soft-bodied insect. The female, which, unlike the male, is
non-flying, lays its eggs in ovisacs, which it deposits on the host, sometimes in great numbers and
visible as a whitish covering. The female, the nymphal stages and the male are also very visible on
the host, and all stages are reddish to pink in colour, but covered in white mealy wax, with the body
colour showing through.84 The pink mealybug forms dense colonies in cracks and crevices.85 While
feeding, this insect injects saliva, which contains a phytotoxic substance, into the host plant. Host
plants differ in their susceptibility and degree of response to the toxin.86 Symptoms range from
deformed flowers, fruits, leaves and stems to the death of the plant.87
2.31. The number of host plants88 of this polyphagous pest fluctuates, according to some sources,
between 224 genera in more than 78 families89 and more than 300 plant genera in over 85 families.90

Hosts include vegetable, ornamental, fruit, forest, and wild plants.91 Pineapple is considered a host
plant of the pest.92
2.32. The pest is present in most tropical and subtropical areas of the world and in some temperate
regions.93 Costa Rica indicates that the pest has been present in some areas of its territory since
2014, is associated with the host Hibiscus spp., and is under official control.94 Panama states that
the pest has not yet reached its territory.95
2.33. The pink mealybug can be spread through the following means: (a) natural dispersal (by
first-instar nymphs (crawlers), which actively walk about or are picked up by wind or on a passing
animal); (b) vector transmission (by ants, which may pick up and carry the mealybugs to new sites);
(c) accidental introduction (by human transport and, to a lesser extent, produce and flowers); or
(d) intentional introduction (although there is no reason for this to occur and no evidence to suggest
that it ever has).96 The pest is identified by visual inspection.97
2.34. The pest is considered difficult to eradicate after it becomes established.98 The sources on the
record identify the following pest control methods: (a) chemical controls; (b) biological controls;
(c) cultural controls; (d) legal controls; and (e) physical controls.99 Biological controls are the best
long-term solution.100
2.35. The pest is considered to be of economic importance because of the direct damage it causes
to a wide range of hosts101, the problems it creates for exports of and trade in agricultural
products102, its impact on crop production, and control costs.103

of different cultivars of the Musaceae species (bananas and plantains)106, although there are no

2.37. Race 4 (R4) is considered the most dangerous of the three because it also affects the
Cavendish cultivar108, which today accounts for 99% of world banana exports109 and is resistant to
races 1 and 2 (R1 and R2). Foc R4 is subdivided into two races: tropical (TR4) and subtropical
(SR4).110 The race that Panama considers relevant in this dispute is TR4. Neither Panama nor
Costa Rica has reported the presence of this race of Foc in its territory.111 In Latin America, the pest
has been reported only in Colombia (2019), Peru (2021), and Venezuela (2023).112
2.38. The most conspicuous and classic external symptom of the disease caused by all Foc races is
that the leaves turn yellow and gradually collapse. Internally, there is vascular discoloration.113
2.39. Foc infects the plant through the root system and reaches the xylem vessels, where it grows
and multiplies, occluding them and limiting the nutrient and water absorption by the plant.114 The
pathogen can also grow in the surrounding tissues as plants die, and produces chlamydospores that
contaminate the soil, where they can survive for more than 20 years.115 These chlamydospores
affect the roots of other plants, thus spreading the pest.116 Foc can be dispersed by plant material
for planting, water, soil particles, tools, footwear, and machinery.117 Dispersal is fast on susceptible
cultivars.118
2.40. Foc is considered to be the main phytopathological problem of banana plantations in tropical
areas.119 In the Cavendish cultivar, this pest has caused a significant reduction of production and
significant increases in production costs.120 In terms of control, no long-term measures are available
other than the planting of resistant cultivars.121

2.2.3 International standards and guidelines cited in this dispute
2.2.3.1 Introduction
2.41. In this dispute, Costa Rica cites the Codex Guidelines and the International Standards for
Phytosanitary Measures (ISPM) presented below.
2.2.3.2 The Codex Alimentarius Commission and the Codex Alimentarius
2.2.3.2.1 Institutional information and functions
2.42. Annex A(3)(a) to the SPS Agreement identifies the Codex Alimentarius Commission (Codex)
as the relevant international organization that develops standards, guidelines and recommendations
for food safety (including standards relating to food additives, veterinary drug and pesticide residues,
contaminants, methods of analysis and sampling, and codes and guidelines of hygiene practice).
2.43. Currently, the Codex has 189 members, including Panama and Costa Rica.122 The Codex
adopts and, together with its subsidiary bodies, revises the standards and related texts of the
Codex Alimentarius, or "Food Code", to ensure that they reflect the most up-to-date scientific
knowledge.123
2.44. The Codex Alimentarius is a collection of food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice,
and other recommendations, the purpose of which is to protect the health of consumers and ensure
fair practices in the food trade.124 Codex Alimentarius standards can be general or specific. The
general standards, guidelines, and codes of practice are the core texts of the Codex Alimentarius
and apply to all products and product categories. These texts deal with hygienic practice, labelling,
additives, inspection and certification, nutrition, and residues of veterinary drugs and pesticides.
Codex Alimentarius commodity standards refer to a specific product, although increasingly Codex
now develops standards for food groups.125 Codex Alimentarius standards are based on sound
science provided by independent international risk assessment bodies and ad hoc consultations
organized by the Food and Agricultural Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the World
Health Organization (WHO).126

2.46. Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 provide a framework for the development of import and export
inspection and certification systems consistent with the relevant Codex principles.129 They are
intended to assist countries in the application of requirements and the determination of equivalence,
thereby protecting consumers and facilitating trade in foodstuffs.130
2.47. Costa Rica refers specifically to paragraphs 34 and 35 of these Guidelines.131 These
paragraphs are found in section 6, entitled "Inspection and certification system infrastructure", and
more specifically in the part entitled "Decision criteria and action". These criteria and actions pertain
to the establishment and enforcement of import controls, and the response to situations of
non-compliance.
2.2.3.2.2.2 Guidelines for Food Import Control Systems CAC/GL 47-2003
2.48. Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 provide a framework for the development and operation of an
import control system to protect consumers and facilitate fair practices in food trade while ensuring
unjustified technical barriers to trade are not introduced.132 These Guidelines complement the
CAC/GL 26-1997 Guidelines, described above.133
2.49. Costa Rica refers specifically to paragraph 20 of the appendix to Guidelines
CAC/GL 47-2003.134 This paragraph is found in section 6 of the appendix, entitled "Implementing
the import inspection programme based on risk". This paragraph addresses situations where "a
failure of food shipments to meet importing country food safety requirements" occurs and sets out
possible responses to such situations.135
2.2.3.2.2.3 Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between Countries on Rejections
of Imported Food CAC/GL 25-1997
2.50. Guidelines CAC/GL 25-1997 provide the basis for structured information exchange on
rejections of imported food where the reason for the rejection is related to food safety and fair
practices in food trade. These guidelines apply where food has been refused entry to a country due
to a failure to comply with importing country requirements.136

2.2.3.3.2.1 ISPM 2: Framework for pest risk analysis
2.57. ISPM 2 provides a framework that describes the pest risk analysis (PRA) process within the
scope of the IPPC.151 The ISPM provides detailed guidance on the initiation stage of PRA (Stage 1)152,
as well as the subsequent stages, namely, pest risk assessment (Stage 2) and pest risk management
(Stage 3).153 This ISPM refers to other ISPM for further details on PRA Stages 2 and 3, including
ISPM 11 on Pest risk analysis for quarantine pests.154 ISPM 2 also addresses generic PRA issues
relating to information gathering, documentation, risk communication, uncertainty, and
consistency.155

occurs.159

party requested an interim review meeting.

because: (a) it was not submitted in accordance with the Working Procedures of the Panel; and (b)
Panama did not have "any meaningful opportunity" to comment on that exhibit, despite the fact that
due process demands that each party be afforded a meaningful opportunity to comment on evidence
adduced by the other party. Panama notes that this exhibit was submitted by Costa Rica together
with its comments on Panama's response to Panel question No. 114, and relates to an issue that
was not explored by the Panel.171
6.6. Costa Rica requests the Panel to reject Panama's suggestion for two reasons.172 First,
Costa Rica claims that paragraph 5.1 of the Working Procedures of the Panel expressly permits the
submission of evidence during the procedural stage at which comments are made on the responses
provided by the other party.173 Second, Costa Rica considers that Panama is mistaken in its
description of the context that led to the submission of Exhibit CRI-405 because the Panel was not
required to rule, as Panama suggests, on whether the legal instruments contained in
Exhibits CRI-392 and CRI-405 "could work together to monitor MRL compliance in Costa Rica when
the measure at issue was adopted".174 According to Costa Rica, this issue "goes beyond the Panel's
analysis of the sufficiency of the scientific evidence".175
6.7. The Panel disagrees with Panama's assertion that Exhibit CRI-405, referred to in
paragraph 7.173, was not submitted in accordance with the Working Procedures of the Panel, and
that Panama's due process rights were violated. First, the Panel refers to paragraph 5.1 of the
Working Procedures, which provides that parties may submit evidence after the first substantive
meeting if that evidence is necessary "for purposes of rebuttal" or "for responding to questions or
commenting on responses provided by the other party". As Costa Rica indicates, Exhibit CRI-405
was submitted in the context of Costa Rica's comments on Panama's response to Panel question
No. 114. Accordingly, the Panel considers that this exhibit was submitted in accordance with the
provisions of its Working Procedures.
6.8. Second, with respect to the alleged violation of Panama's due process rights, the Panel
disagrees with Panama because: (a) Panama did not object to that exhibit when it was submitted,
nor did it request the Panel to give it an opportunity to respond; and (b) that exhibit relates to an
issue that was addressed in the round of questions following the second substantive meeting,
namely, whether Panama had sufficient information on Costa Rica's good agricultural and production
practices with regard to strawberries and compliance therewith. Therefore, this was not a new issue
that had been raised for the first time in Costa Rica's comments on Panama's responses. As is clear
from paragraph 7.173, the Panel concluded that Panama had sufficient information on the relevant
issue, primarily on the basis of the Manual on good agricultural and production practices for

7 FINDINGS
7.1 The Panel's terms of reference
7.1.1 Preliminary ruling
7.1. As mentioned in section 1.3.2 above, Panama raised two issues relating to the Panel's terms
of reference, which the Panel addressed in a single preliminary ruling, namely:
a. whether Costa Rica's panel request provides a brief summary of the legal basis of the
complaint sufficient to present the problem clearly, in accordance with Article 6.2 of the
DSU; and
b. whether Costa Rica's claims under Annex B(5)(b) and Annex B(6)(b) to the
SPS Agreement are outside the Panel's terms of reference.
7.2. On the first issue, the Panel concluded that Costa Rica's panel request clearly connected the
challenged measures with the provisions of the covered agreements claimed to have been infringed.
The Panel therefore found that the panel request was not inconsistent with the requirement under
Article 6.2 of the DSU to provide "a brief summary of the legal basis of the complaint sufficient to
present the problem clearly".215 Accordingly, the Panel also found that Panama's due process rights
had not been violated as a result of the failure to comply with the above requirement of Article 6.2.216
7.3. On the second issue, with respect to Costa Rica's claims under Annex B(5)(b) and
Annex B(6)(a) to the SPS Agreement, the Panel concluded that Costa Rica had not included such
claims in its panel request. The Panel therefore found that such claims were outside its terms of
reference.217
7.1.2 Whether the procedures challenged by Costa Rica in relation to Measures 1, 3, and
4 are within the Panel's terms of reference
7.4. Costa Rica claims that Panama acted inconsistently with Article 8 and Annex C(1)(a) of the
SPS Agreement by unduly delaying the initiation and completion of procedures in order to check and
ensure the fulfilment of the prohibitions on the importation of strawberries, pineapples, and plantains
and bananas from Costa Rica (Measures 1, 3, and 4, respectively).218
7.5. Costa Rica describes the challenged procedures related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 as follows:
a. The procedure related to Measure 1 consists of "the procedure for Panama to consider
Costa Rica's request to withdraw the prohibition on imports of strawberries imposed on
20 January 2020".219

b. The procedure related to Measure 3 consists of "'Panama's review process of the
phytosanitary requirements for the importation of pineapples from Costa Rica, which
includes a risk assessment process'".220
c. The process related to Measure 4 consists of "the 'review process that includes a risk
assessment, which, in turn, requires the approval of Costa Rican packing plants'".221
7.6. This description of the procedures, coupled with Costa Rica's description of the measures at
issue in its panel request, led the Panel to deem that it was appropriate to examine whether these
procedures had been duly identified in the panel request. In this connection, pursuant to Articles 6.2
and 7 of the DSU, the terms of reference of a panel are limited to the measures identified and to the
claims presented in the complainant's panel request. Accordingly, panels must deal with all issues
which go to the root of their jurisdiction, even on their own motion, in order to satisfy themselves
that they have authority to proceed.222 Thus, the Panel considers it necessary to satisfy itself, in this
particular case, that the procedures related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 challenged by Costa Rica are
within its terms of reference. The Panel will proceed to analyse this issue below.
7.1.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.1.2.1.1 Costa Rica
7.7. Costa Rica submits that the procedures related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 are within the Panel's
terms of reference. In Costa Rica's view, its panel request described the acts and omissions that
comprise the measures at issue, including these procedures.223
7.8. Costa Rica states that, according to WTO jurisprudence, any act or omission attributable to a
Member can be identified as a measure at issue in a panel request. Costa Rica adds that it is
important not to confuse this requirement with the requirement to set out the legal claims, and that
Article 6.2 of the DSU does not require that the acts or omissions comprising the measure at issue
be described in the panel request in a manner that includes the legal claims.224 Furthermore,
Costa Rica observes that Panama has not raised any jurisdictional objection related to the description
of the measures in the panel request.225
7.9. Costa Rica considers that it did not simply describe the measures as import prohibitions in its
panel request. Costa Rica states that its description of the measures includes an extensive account
that reflects its exchanges with Panama aimed at resuming trade. According to Costa Rica, the
events it describes indicate that these exchanges were part of a process to determine whether
Panama would re-authorize imports of strawberries, pineapples, and plantains and bananas from
Costa Rica.226
7.10. With respect to Measure 1, Costa Rica considers that its panel request contains a description
of the communications through which Costa Rica requested Panama to "withdraw the prohibition on
imports" of strawberries from Costa Rica. According to Costa Rica, the procedure related to
Measure 1 is Panama's consideration of that request, as described in the panel request.227
7.11. With respect to Measure 3, Costa Rica considers that its panel request contains a description
of different facts that identify acts and omissions attributable to Panama over a long period of

7.13. Panama considers that the procedures challenged by Costa Rica in its submissions with

7.17. Article 6.2 of the DSU defines the requirements that a panel request must meet. Pursuant to
that provision, in addition to being made in writing and indicating whether consultations were held,
the panel request shall "identify the specific measures at issue and provide a brief summary of the
legal basis of the complaint sufficient to present the problem clearly". It follows that measures not
identified and claims not included or included in a defective manner in Costa Rica's panel request
could not be examined by the Panel.240
7.18. As mentioned above, the Panel considers it necessary to satisfy itself that the procedures
related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 are within its terms of reference. Thus, the relevant requirement for
the Panel's analysis is the requirement to identify the specific measures at issue. Given that the
panel request fulfils a due process objective, this requirement has been interpreted to include
identifying the measures at issue "with sufficient particularity so as to indicate the nature of the
measure and the gist of what is at issue".241
7.19. The Panel concurs with Costa Rica242 that when examining whether a measure has been
identified in a panel request, the measure should not have to be identified in terms of the obligation
that is alleged to have been breached. In addition, it has been deemed that the sufficiency of a panel
request in identifying a measure must be established by reading the request as a whole and on the
basis of the language used therein.243
7.20. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel will next address whether the procedures challenged
by Costa Rica related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 were identified with sufficient particularity in
Costa Rica's panel request, and which are, therefore, within its terms of reference.
7.1.2.2.2 Whether Costa Rica identified the procedures it is challenging in relation to
Measures 1, 3, and 4 in its panel request
7.1.2.2.2.1 Procedure related to Measure 1
7.21. Costa Rica notes that "in relation to the prohibition on imports of strawberries, Costa Rica
challenges Panama's procedure for considering Costa Rica's request to withdraw the prohibition on
imports of strawberries from Costa Rica".244 The Panel will proceed to review the text of Costa Rica's
panel request to determine whether Costa Rica identified with sufficient particularity the procedure
it is challenging in relation to Measure 1.
7.22. Costa Rica describes the measure related to imports of strawberries (Measure 1) in section I.1
of its panel request, entitled "Measure restricting or prohibiting imports of strawberries originating
in Costa Rica". This section comprises six paragraphs. The first paragraph describes the measure as
Panama's decision "to prohibit strawberry imports from Costa Rica", about which Panama informed
Costa Rica in Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020 of 20 February 2020, and explains the reasons why Panama
imposed this prohibition. In the four paragraphs that follow, Costa Rica refers to an exchange of
communications between Costa Rica and Panama in relation to Measure 1.245 These paragraphs
make no mention of a request by Costa Rica for Panama to withdraw the prohibition on imports of
its strawberries, noting only that in the communications of 12 June 2020 and 19 February 2021,
Costa Rica reiterated its readiness to "resolve this situation, with the fundamental aim being to
resume exports of Costa Rican strawberries".246 The last paragraph of this section concludes that
"[d]espite the information and explanations provided by Costa Rica, Panama continues to maintain
the ban on imports of Costa Rican strawberries …, which is baseless and lacks scientific evidence, a
risk analysis or any other valid justification under WTO rules".247

7.23. The Panel considers that Costa Rica's description of Measure 1 in section I.1 of its panel
request and, in particular, paragraphs 2 to 6 thereof, does not make it clear that Costa Rica is
challenging, in addition to a prohibition on imports of strawberries from Costa Rica that it alleges
was imposed without scientific justification, a procedure consisting of the consideration of the
request for the prohibition to be withdrawn. While the narrative in the panel request provides context
for the reason for the prohibition and the subsequent attempts at dialogue and technical
clarifications, at no point is reference made, directly or indirectly, to a procedure such as the one
described by Costa Rica in its communications. In the Panel's view, this lack of particularity made it
impossible for Panama to know, based on the terms of the panel request, that Costa Rica was
challenging not only the prohibition on imports of strawberries from Costa Rica, but also a procedure
related to that prohibition.
7.24. The lack of particularity in section I.1 of the panel request in identifying the procedure
challenged by Costa Rica is not remedied by the manner in which Costa Rica asserted its claim under
Article 8 and Annex C in section II of its panel request. In this section, which includes a brief
summary of the legal basis of the complaint, Costa Rica states that "[e]ach and every one of the
measures described in Section I of this request (collectively referred to as "measures at issue") are
inconsistent with each and every one of the following obligations for Panama under the WTO covered
agreements"248 and claims that these measures are inconsistent with:
a. Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement, read in conjunction with Article 8, because Panama
has incurred undue delays when undertaking and completing the necessary procedures to
check and ensure the fulfilment of each of the measures at issue.
b. Annex C(1)(b) to the SPS Agreement, read in conjunction with Article 8, in relation to the
processing by Panama of the procedures to check and ensure the fulfilment of each of the
measures at issue.
c. Annex C(1)(c) to the SPS Agreement, read in conjunction with Article 8, because Panama
requires more information than is necessary to carry out the control, inspection, and
approval procedures related to each of the measures at issue.249
7.25. In this section, Costa Rica refers to "the necessary procedures to check and ensure the
fulfilment of each of the measures at issue". This reference does not indicate the necessary
procedures to check and ensure the fulfilment of the prohibition on strawberry imports to which
Costa Rica is referring. It is not clear to the Panel how the challenged procedure (namely
consideration of Costa Rica's request to Panama to withdraw this prohibition) could have been
understood by Panama as a procedure related to the verification and fulfilment of that very
prohibition. Thus, when describing the measure challenged under Article 8 and Annex C, the panel
request does not provide any additional information that would indicate that Costa Rica was
challenging the procedure as described in its communications. This conclusion is confirmed by the
emphasis that Costa Rica places on the lack of scientific basis and justification for Measure 1, which
indicates that it was challenging the prohibition on imports of Costa Rican strawberries, and not a
procedure related to that prohibition.
7.26. In conclusion, reading the panel request as a whole, the Panel considers that Costa Rica failed
to identify in that request, as a measure at issue, the procedure it is challenging with respect to
Measure 1, namely Panama's consideration of Costa Rica's request to withdraw the prohibition on
imports of strawberries from Costa Rica. Accordingly, the Panel finds that the procedure challenged
by Costa Rica in relation to Measure 1 is outside its terms of reference.
7.1.2.2.2.2 Procedure related to Measure 3
7.27. Costa Rica describes the procedure related to Measure 3 as the "review process" of the PIR
for pineapple from Costa Rica250, "which includes a risk assessment, for which Panama must

7.28. Costa Rica describes the measure related to imports of pineapples (Measure 3) in section I.3

originating in Costa Rica". This section comprises five paragraphs. The first paragraph describes the

Panama informed Costa Rica through Note AUPSA-AG-032-2019 of 29 January 2019, and explains
the reasons why Panama took this decision. In the three paragraphs that follow, Costa Rica refers

Panama, noting that, in the first communication, Panama requested Costa Rica "to provide further

"without any technical justification".254 The last paragraph of this section concludes that "[t]o date,
Panama has maintained the ban on imports of fresh Costa Rican pineapples".255
7.29. The Panel considers that Costa Rica's description of Measure 3 in section I.3 of its panel
request, and in particular, paragraphs 2 to 5 thereof, does not make it clear that Costa Rica is
challenging, in addition to the prohibition on pineapple imports that it alleges was imposed without
scientific justification, a procedure consisting of the review of the PIR for pineapple. While the
narrative in the panel request provides context for the reason for the prohibition and the subsequent
attempts at dialogue and technical clarifications, it does not mention, directly or indirectly, a
procedure such as that described by Costa Rica in its communications. In the Panel's view, this lack
of particularity does not allow Panama to know, from the terms of the panel request, that Costa Rica
was challenging not only the prohibition on pineapple imports from Costa Rica, but also a procedure
related to that prohibition.
7.30. As noted above256, section II of the panel request, when describing the claims under Article 8
and Annex C, refers to the "the necessary procedures to check and ensure the fulfilment of each of
the measures at issue". This reference does not indicate the necessary procedures to check and
ensure fulfilment of the prohibition on pineapple imports to which Costa Rica is referring. It is not
clear to the Panel how, on the basis of the language used in the panel request, Panama could have
understood the challenged procedure (i.e. the review procedure for the PIR for pineapple from
Costa Rica) to be a procedure related to the verification and enforcement of the import prohibition
on Costa Rican pineapples. Thus, the description of the subject of the claims under Article 8 and
Annex C in the panel request fails to provide any additional information that would indicate that
Costa Rica was challenging the process as described in its communications. This conclusion is
confirmed by the emphasis that Costa Rica places on the lack of scientific basis or justification for
the measure, which indicates that it was challenging the prohibition on imports of Costa Rican
pineapples, and not a procedure related to that prohibition.
7.31. In conclusion, reading the panel request as a whole, the Panel considers that Costa Rica failed
to identify in the panel request, as a measure at issue, the procedure that it is challenging in relation
to Measure 3, namely the review of the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica, including a risk

7.1.2.2.2.3 Procedure related to Measure 4
7.32. Costa Rica describes the procedure related to Measure 4 as the technical review process of
the PIR for plantain and banana from Costa Rica, including, in the case of plantains, approvals for
packing plants.257 Costa Rica adds that this review process includes a risk assessment.258 The Panel
will proceed to review the text of Costa Rica's panel request to determine whether Costa Rica
identified with sufficient particularity the challenged procedure in relation to Measure 4.
7.33. In section I.4 of its panel request, entitled "Measure restricting or prohibiting the importation
of plantains and bananas originating in Costa Rica", Costa Rica describes the measure related to
imports of plantains and bananas (Measure 4). This section comprises six paragraphs. The first
paragraph describes the measure as a prohibition on imports of plantains and bananas from
Costa Rica, about which Panama informed Costa Rica through Notes AUPSA-AG-392-2019 and
AUPSA-AG-424-2019 of 10 and 25 October 2019, and which, in Costa Rica's view, was adopted
without any scientific justification. In that paragraph of its request, Costa Rica adds that, when
notifying the measures, "Panama stated that the importation of these products would remain
suspended until Panama had completed a technical review of the existing phytosanitary
requirements and had approved Costa Rican packing plants".259 Costa Rica also describes the trade
situation prior to the imposition of this measure. In the four paragraphs that follow, Costa Rica refers
to the exchange of communications between Costa Rica and Panama following the adoption of
Measure 4, which included statements by Panama on the reasons for adopting the measure. In these
paragraphs, Costa Rica states that "there had been no change in Costa Rica's phytosanitary status
to justify closing the market" and insists that Panama kept "the market closed to Costa Rican
plantains and bananas without providing any [scientific or technical] justification".260 The sixth and
final paragraph of this section concludes that "Panama has maintained the ban on imports of
Costa Rican plantains and bananas".261
7.34. The Panel considers that Costa Rica's description of Measure 4 in section I.4 of its panel
request, and in particular, paragraphs 1 to 6 thereof, does not make it clear that Costa Rica is
challenging, in addition to the prohibition on imports of plantains and bananas that it alleges was
imposed without scientific justification, a procedure consisting of the review of the PIR for plantain
and banana from Costa Rica and the approval of Costa Rican packing plants. While the narrative of
the panel request provides context for the reason for the prohibition on imports of plantains and
bananas and the subsequent attempts at dialogue and technical clarifications, it does not refer in a
sufficiently precise manner to a procedure other than the prohibition itself as a measure at issue. In
the Panel's view, Costa Rica's references to the review of the PIR and the approval of the packing
plants seek to explain how the challenged prohibition works, while indicating that the suspension of
imports of plantains and bananas would remain in place until that review and approval had been
carried out. In this connection, the Panel considers that Costa Rica was not referring to a procedure
independent from the prohibition. This lack of particularity does not allow Panama to know, from the
terms of the panel request, that Costa Rica was challenging, in addition to the prohibition on imports
of plantains and bananas, a procedure related to that prohibition.
7.35. As noted above262, section II of the panel request, in describing the claims under Article 8 and
Annex C, refers to the "the necessary procedures to check and ensure the fulfilment of each of the
measures at issue". This reference does not indicate the necessary procedures to check and ensure
the fulfilment of the prohibition on plantain and banana imports to which Costa Rica is referring. It
is not clear to the Panel how, on the basis of the language used in the panel request, Panama could
have understood that the challenged procedure (namely the technical review of the PIR for plantain
and banana, including, in the case of plantains, packing plant approvals) to be a procedure related
to verification and fulfilment of the prohibition on imports of plantains and bananas from Costa Rica.

7.38. Having clarified the scope of its terms of reference, the Panel will now address the order in

7.2 Order of analysis
7.39. In its panel request, Costa Rica has put forward claims with respect to the four measures at
issue under the following provisions: Articles 1.1, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 3.1, 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 5.5, 5.6, 5.7,
5.8, 6.1, 7, 8, Annex B(1), and Annex C(1)(a), (b) and (c) of the SPS Agreement, Articles I.1, X:1,
X:3(a), and XI:1 of the GATT 1994, and Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the
Agreement on Agriculture.263
7.40. The Panel will organize its analysis by measure, since this approach will enable it to address
the aspects particular to each measure at issue in a clearer and more structured manner.264 The
Panel also considers it necessary to decide for each of the measures which agreement it will examine
first and the order in which it will examine the respective claims.265 The Panel notes that, as a general
principle, panels have discretion when determining the order in which they will analyse the different
measures and claims.266 The main limitation on this discretion is that imposed by the very "structure
and logic" of the provisions in question.267 In this regard, the Panel could be committing an error of
law should it fail to follow a particular order arising from the relevant provisions, if any.268
7.41. Moreover, the obligation under Article 11 of the DSU, to make an objective assessment of the
matter before it, does not require the Panel to rule on all the claims made by the complainant. The
Panel may examine only those claims it considers necessary to secure a positive solution to the
dispute.269 In this regard, it has been considered that panels may consider the possibility of
exercising judicial economy when deciding in what order to structure their analysis of the
complaining party's claims.270

7.2.1 Order of analysis of the agreements cited by Costa Rica
7.42. Costa Rica has put forward claims under the three covered agreements, namely: the
SPS Agreement, the GATT 1994, and the Agreement on Agriculture. Costa Rica considers that, if the
Panel finds Panama has acted inconsistently with the obligations under the SPS Agreement, it may
deem that it is not necessary to make findings under the GATT 1994 and the
Agreement on Agriculture to resolve the matter.271
7.43. Panama also considers that, in the event that the Panel finds a violation of the
SPS Agreement, it may exercise judicial economy with respect to the claims under the GATT 1994
and the Agreement on Agriculture, just as previous panels have done.272
7.44. The Panel notes that, in previous disputes where claims were made under these agreements,
the decision was taken to begin the analysis with the SPS Agreement as it is the more specific
agreement.273 In particular, account was taken of the fact that the claims under the SPS Agreement
should be examined even if the measure is determined to be consistent with the GATT 1994, while
the reverse is not necessarily true.274
7.45. In this case, the Panel sees no reason that justifies adopting a different approach. As a result,
the Panel will begin its analysis with Costa Rica's claims under the SPS Agreement. Depending on
its findings, the Panel will decide whether it is necessary to make findings under the GATT 1994
and/or the Agreement on Agriculture to secure a positive solution to the dispute.
7.2.2 Order of analysis of the claims under the SPS Agreement
7.46. Costa Rica calls for the Panel to maintain the same order that Costa Rica used in its first
written submission, namely: first, examine the applicability of the SPS Agreement to the measures
at issue; second, Panama's defence under Article 5.7; and then the consistency with Articles 5.1,
5.2, 5.3, and 2.2; 3.1; 5.6 and 2.2; 2.3 and 5.5; Annex C read in conjunction with Article 8; and
Article 7 and Annex B, to the extent relevant to each measure.275
7.47. Panama distinguishes between Measure 2 on the one hand, and the rest of the measures at
issue on the other. In the case of Measure 2, Panama considers that the Panel should begin its
analysis by examining the content of the measure in question and the applicability of the disciplines
of the SPS Agreement to this measure.276 Should the Panel determine that, as Panama claims,
Measure 2 qualifies as an approval procedure under Article 8 and Annex C, Panama considers that
the Panel should examine whether Costa Rica established the inconsistency of Measure 2 with
Annex C(1)(a) and Annex C(1)(c). In Panama's view, the rest of the provisions of the
SPS Agreement do not apply to Measure 2.277 In relation to Measures 1, 3, and 4, Panama considers
that the Panel should begin its analysis with Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement, as it is "a qualified
right that exempts them" from the provisions of Article 2.2 and, consequently, of Articles 2.3, 3.1,
5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.5, and 5.6.278 According to Panama, if Measures 1, 3, and 4 are consistent with

7.48. As Panama offers arguments specific to Measure 2, regarding which disciplines of the
SPS Agreement are applicable to the measure in the light of its nature, the Panel must address this
issue to determine which aspects of the measure Costa Rica has demonstrated and which provisions
of the SPS Agreement are applicable to it. Therefore, the Panel will follow a different order of analysis
for the claims under Measures 1, 3, and 4 on the one hand, and Measure 2 on the other.
7.49. With respect to Measures 1, 3, and 4, the Panel will begin its analysis by examining the
applicability of the SPS Agreement to these measures.280 The Panel will then, as suggested by the
parties281, assess whether Measures 1, 3, and 4 are covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
since this provision has been considered to be a qualified exemption from the obligation under
Article 2.2 to not maintain an SPS measure without sufficient scientific evidence, an obligation
expounded in Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement.282
7.50. If the Panel finds that the measures at issue are provisional measures under Article 5.7, as
Panama claims, it will determine whether this exemption applies only to Articles 2.2 and 5.1, as
Costa Rica proposes, or to other obligations under the SPS Agreement, as Panama proposes. If,
however, the Panel finds such measures are not covered by Article 5.7, as Costa Rica claims, it will
examine whether these measures are inconsistent with Article 5.1 and, consequently, with
Articles 5.2, 5.3283, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement.284
7.51. The Panel will then continue with its analysis of the rest of Costa Rica's claims, if necessary.
These claims concern: (a) whether there is arbitrary, unjustifiable discrimination (Articles 2.3
and 5.5)285; (b) the necessity of the measure and its trade restrictiveness (Articles 2.2 and 5.6); (c)
the harmonization with international standards, guidelines and recommendations (Article 3.1); (d)
the transparency (Article 7 and Annex B(1); and (e) the inconsistency of the measures at issue with
the SPS Agreement (Articles 1.1 and 2.1).
7.52. The Panel notes that the SPS Agreement does not establish an order of analysis among the
provisions mentioned in the preceding paragraph. Moreover, the Panel observes that none of the
considerations over the exercise of judicial economy guide the order of analysis with respect to these
provisions. It should, however, be noted that in previous disputes, claims under Articles 1.1 and 2.1
were examined at the end of the panel's assessment under the SPS Agreement, given that any
findings of inconsistency with one or more provisions of the SPS Agreement constitutes a violation
of both these provisions.286
7.53. As a result, and bearing these observations in mind, the Panel will address Costa Rica's claims
in the following order: (a) Articles 2.3 and 5.5; (b) Articles 2.2 and 5.6; (c) Article 3.1; (d) Article 7
and Annex B; and (e) Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement.

7.3 Measure concerning the importation of strawberries (Measure 1)

SPS Agreement with respect to Measure 1. While Panama does not question the fact that this

7.3.1.1.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.3.1.1.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.60. Article 1 of the SPS Agreement provides in relevant part:
1. This Agreement applies to all sanitary and phytosanitary measures which may,
directly or indirectly, affect international trade. Such measures shall be developed and
applied in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.
2. For the purposes of this Agreement, the definitions provided in Annex A shall apply.
7.61. Therefore, the SPS Agreement applies to any measure that meets two requirements: (a) that
it constitutes a SPS measure in accordance with the definition given in Annex A to the
SPS Agreement; and (b) that it may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade.292 The Panel
will now examine whether Measure 1 meets both requirements.
7.62. With respect to the first requirement for the applicability of the SPS Agreement, the Panel
refers first to the definition of "sanitary or phytosanitary measure" in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.
1. Sanitary or phytosanitary measure - Any measure applied:
(a) to protect animal or plant life or health within the territory of the Member from
risks arising from the entry, establishment or spread of pests, diseases, disease-
carrying organisms or disease-causing organisms;
(b) to protect human or animal life or health within the territory of the Member from
risks arising from additives, contaminants, toxins or disease-causing organisms in
foods, beverages or feedstuffs;
(c) to protect human life or health within the territory of the Member from risks
arising from diseases carried by animals, plants or products thereof, or from the entry,
establishment or spread of pests; or
(d) to prevent or limit other damage within the territory of the Member from the
entry, establishment or spread of pests.
Sanitary or phytosanitary measures include all relevant laws, decrees, regulations,
requirements and procedures including, inter alia, end product criteria; processes and
production methods; testing, inspection, certification and approval procedures;
quarantine treatments including relevant requirements associated with the transport of
animals or plants, or with the materials necessary for their survival during transport;
provisions on relevant statistical methods, sampling procedures and methods of risk
assessment; and packaging and labelling requirements directly related to food safety.
7.63. For the measure at issue to be considered an SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1),
the measure must be applied to achieve at least one of the objectives listed therein.293 The Panel
292 Panel Reports, EC – Hormones (Canada), para. 8.39; and EC – Hormones (US), para. 8.36. See also,
e.g. Panel Reports, Costa Rica – Avocados (Mexico), para. 7.69; Korea – Radionuclides, para. 7.22; Russia –
Pigs (EU), para. 7.191; US – Animals, para. 7.30; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.136; and US – Poultry
(China), para. 7.62.
293 The Panel is of the view that determining the objective of the measure, including the risk it claims to
address, is important not only to establish the applicability of the SPS Agreement to the measure at issue, but
also when examining various provisions of the SPS Agreement. For instance, the objective of a measure (and,
in particular, the relevant risk) affect the determination of whether an SPS measure is maintained without
sufficient scientific evidence, within the meaning of Article 2.2, given that the analysis of this issue requires
examining the relationship between the SPS measure and the scientific evidence relating to the risk that the
measure addresses. Additionally, when examining whether an SPS measure discriminates between Members
where identical or similar conditions prevail, the objective of the SPS measure may inform which conditions
must be identical or similar. (See Panel Report, Japan – Apples, para. 8.102; and Appellate Body Report, Korea
– Radionuclides, para. 5.59).

WT/DS599/R
- 62 -
recalls that, in previous SPS disputes, it was considered that "the relationship of the measure and
one of the objectives listed in Annex A(1) must be manifest in the measure itself or otherwise evident
from the circumstances related to the application of the measure".294 In this regard, it has been
deemed that "the purpose of a measure is to be ascertained on the basis of objective
considerations"295, and that scrutiny of the circumstances must reveal "a clear and objective
relationship between that measure and the specific purposes enumerated in Annex A(1)".296 In this
respect, it has been noted that the purpose may be ascertained not only from the objectives of the
measure as expressed by the responding party, but also from the text and structure of the measure,
its surrounding regulatory context, and the way in which it is designed and applied.297 It should be
noted that panels have considered a wide range of factual elements, both before and after the
adoption of the measure at issue, in order to determine its objective.298 The Panel agrees with this
approach.
7.64. The Panel also observes that neither the definition of "sanitary or phytosanitary measure" in
Annex A(1), nor any other provision in the SPS Agreement, indicates a reference period with respect
to which a panel should discern the purpose of a measure or how such an analysis should be
undertaken.299
7.65. With respect to a reference period for determining the objective of an SPS measure, it should
be noted that Annex B(5)(b) and Annex B(6)(a) to the SPS Agreement, concerning the notification
procedures for sanitary and phytosanitary regulations, require that a "brief indication of the objective
and the rationale of the regulation" be given when notifying a regulation. In accordance with these
provisions, notifications should take place at an early stage (in the case of draft regulations, as
provided for in Annex B(5)(b)) or immediately (in the case of urgent problems of health protection,
as provided for in Annex B(6)(a)). However, in the Panel's view, the content of these provisions on
when to notify the "objective and rationale" of a regulation concern compliance with the transparency
obligations contained therein, but does not define a reference period for determining the objective
of an SPS measure.
7.66. Concerning the list of instruments that can be used to adopt an SPS measure, enumerated in
the second sentence of Annex A(1), the Panel notes that this list is illustrative, as is reflected in the
use of the use of the phrase "including, inter alia". As has been indicated in the past, this list
"illustrate[s], through a set of concrete examples, the different types of measures that, when they
exhibit the appropriate nexus to one of the specified purposes, will constitute SPS measures and,
accordingly, be subject to the disciplines set out in the SPS Agreement".300 Hence, the Panel does
not rule out that measures adopted through instruments different to those mentioned in the second
sentence of Annex A(1) may constitute an SPS measure.

7.67. Lastly, with respect to the second requirement for applicability of the SPS Agreement, namely
that the measure may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade, the Panel notes that a broad
interpretation has been given to the word "affect" in previous disputes.301 The use of the words "may
affect" and "directly or indirectly" in Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement seems to suggest that the
Agreement covers measures that may have a broad range of potential effects on international
trade.302 The Panel shares the assessments of previous panels, which found that an import ban, by
its very nature, affects international trade.303
7.3.1.1.2.2 Whether Measure 1 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1) to the SPS Agreement
7.68. As mentioned in the descriptive part, Measure 1 disallows imports of strawberries from
Costa Rica.304 Taking into account the legal standard outlined above, the task of this Panel is to
determine whether Measure 1 has been applied to achieve at least one of the objectives listed in
Annex A(1)(a)-(d) to the SPS Agreement, which are set out in paragraph 7.62 above. In this
respect, the Panel notes that in Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020, whereby Panama informed Costa Rica
of Measure 1, Panama stated that this measure was adopted due to the "health situation" arising
from the detection of the pesticide oxamyl in shipments of Costa Rican strawberries. In particular,
this note states that "imports of strawberries from Costa Rica … [have] be[en] temporarily
disallowed" until the Costa Rican health authorities indicate the corrective measures taken to remedy
the nonconformity detected by Panama, given that nonconformities could "affect the health of [its]
consumers."305
7.69. Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020 contains information on the nonconformity detected by Panama,
including the exporter involved (Fresas Gego), the product (strawberries for human consumption),
the pesticide (oxamyl), and the legal basis for the finding of nonconformity
(Executive Decree No. 467 adopting the MRL Regulation).306 In this regard, the Panel is of the view
that the note announces a measure the application of which seeks to address Panama's concerns
regarding the risk to human health. This risk arises from the presence of a pesticide at levels
exceeding Panama's MRL in a product destined for human consumption.
7.70. The Panel notes that the word "contaminants" within the meaning of the SPS Agreement
covers "pesticide and veterinary drug residues and extraneous matter".307 Therefore, the pesticide
oxamyl may be considered a contaminant for the purpose of the definition of an SPS measure under
Annex A to the SPS Agreement. The Panel also notes that, in addition to the reference to the risks
to human health set out in Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020, the record contains information on the
harmful effects of this pesticide on human health.308
7.71. Therefore, the Panel considers the objective of Measure 1 to correspond with the objective of
"protect[ing] human or animal life or health within the territory of the Member from risks arising
from additives, contaminants, toxins or disease-causing organisms in foods, beverages or feedstuff",
set out in Annex A(1)(b) to the SPS Agreement.
7.72. As for the type of measure, the Panel recalls that Measure 1 is the decision to "disallow ...
imports of strawberries from Costa Rica".309 Therefore, in the Panel's view, Measure 1 could qualify
as a requirement for the purposes of Annex A(1).310 In any event, as the Panel stated above, a

7.3.1.1.2.3 Whether Measure 1 may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade
7.73. As stated above, the Panel shares the assessments of previous panels, which found that an
import ban, by its very nature, affects international trade.312 The Panel considers that Measure 1, to
the extent that it makes it impossible to import Costa Rican strawberries into Panama as a result of
Panama's decision to "temporarily disallow"313 such imports, directly affects international trade. The
Panel therefore finds that Measure 1 affects international trade.
7.3.1.1.3 Conclusion on the applicability of the SPS Agreement to Measure 1
7.74. Having determined that Measure 1 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1)(b) to the SPS Agreement and that it directly affects international trade within the
meaning of Article 1.1 of the Agreement, the Panel finds that the SPS Agreement does apply to this
measure.
7.75. The Panel will therefore examine Costa Rica's claims under the SPS Agreement with respect
to Measure 1. As indicated in the section relating to the order of analysis, the Panel will first examine
the claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 2.2, and 5.7 of this Agreement.
7.3.1.2 Claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 2.2, and 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.3.1.2.1 Order of analysis
7.76. Costa Rica claims that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1 and 5.2 of the
SPS Agreement because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of
the risks to human life or health. Costa Rica also claims that, as a result, Measure 1 is not based on
scientific principles and is maintained without sufficient scientific evidence, in a manner inconsistent
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement. Costa Rica also submits that Measure 1 is not covered by
Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement and, therefore, if Panama cites this provision to justify the lack of
risk analysis, this justification is not applicable.314
7.77. For its part, Panama asserts that Costa Rica has failed to establish a prima facie case that
Panama does not meet the four requirements under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. Panama
requests the Panel to conclude that Measure 1 is a provisional measure covered by Article 5.7 and
that, consequently, Panama has not acted inconsistently with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2 of the
SPS Agreement.315
7.78. In the light of the arguments of the parties, the Panel considers it necessary to examine the
relationship between Articles 5.1, 5.2, 2.2, and Article 5.7 to determine the order in which it will
analyse the relevant claims.

7.79. Costa Rica notes that the Panel could begin its analysis with Panama's defence under

7.80. The Panel notes that the obligations under Article 5 of the SPS Agreement on the Assessment
of Risk and Determination of the Appropriate Level of Sanitary or Phytosanitary Protection (ALOP)
elaborate on the basic rights and obligations set out in Article 2 of the Agreement.318 Article 2.2 of
the SPS Agreement requires Members to ensure that their SPS measures are based on scientific
principles and are not maintained without sufficient scientific evidence. These obligations are
expounded in Article 5.1319, which establishes the obligation to base SPS measures on a risk
assessment. This assessment should take into account the elements listed in Article 5.2. Moreover,
Article 5.7 provides an exemption to Article 2.2, which extends to Article 5.1.320
7.81. In accordance with the foregoing, in previous disputes it has been considered that the
preferred means of complying with the basic obligations under Article 2 is through the "particular
routes" or "specific obligations" set out in Article 5.321 Accordingly, when complainants put forward
claims under Articles 2 and 5 of the SPS Agreement, the panels began their analysis with Article 5.322
In cases where the complainants put forward claims under Articles 2.2 and 5.1, and the respondent
asserted that the measure was covered by Article 5.7, the panels addressed Article 5.7 first.323
7.82. Given that Costa Rica has brought claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2, and Panama has
invoked Article 5.7, this Panel also considers it appropriate to begin its analysis by examining
whether Measure 1 is a provisional SPS measure covered by Article 5.7. If necessary, the Panel will
then examine the claims under Article 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2.

7.3.1.2.2.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.107. The Panel will now assess whether Measure 1 constitutes a provisional SPS measure covered
by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. This assessment will be preceded by the relevant legal standard
and observations by the Panel with respect to the burden of proof under this provision.
Relevant legal standard
7.108. Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement provides as follows:
In cases where relevant scientific evidence is insufficient, a Member may provisionally
adopt sanitary or phytosanitary measures on the basis of available pertinent
information, including that from the relevant international organizations as well as from

7.109. Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement, therefore, allows Members to adopt and maintain a
provisional measure provided that the Member adopting this measure complies with each and every
one of the following requirements:
a. the Member must adopt the measure in a situation where "relevant scientific evidence is
insufficient";
b. the Member must adopt the measure "on the basis of available pertinent information";
c. the Member adopting the measure must "seek to obtain the additional information
necessary for a more objective assessment of risk"; and
d. the Member adopting the measure must "review the ... measure accordingly within a
reasonable period of time".393
7.110. The Panel notes that the first two requirements refer to the adoption of a provisional SPS
measure, whereas the last two address its maintenance.394 These four requirements under
Article 5.7 are cumulative.395 Therefore, if one of these requirements is not met, the measure at
issue will not be covered by Article 5.7.396
7.111. The first requirement relates to the adoption of the measure in situations where there is
insufficient relevant scientific evidence. This requirement has been interpreted as covering situations
where deficiencies in the body of available scientific evidence do not allow a Member, in quantitative
or qualitative terms, to perform an adequate assessment of risks as required under Article 5.1 and
as defined in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.397 In such circumstances, the Member in question will
be unable to arrive at a sufficiently objective conclusion in relation to risk.398
7.112. Under the second requirement the Member must adopt the provisional measure on the basis
of available pertinent information.399 According to the text of Article 5.7, this information may include
"that from the relevant international organizations as well as from [SPS] measures applied by other
Members".400 Bearing in mind the context of Article 5.7, pertinent information refers to evidentiary
393 Appellate Body Report, Japan – Agricultural Products II, para. 89. See also Appellate Body Reports,
US/Canada – Continued Suspension, para. 676; Japan – Apples, para. 176; and Korea – Radionuclides,
para. 5.107; and Panel Reports, Japan – Apples, para. 8.213; Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.632.
394 Appellate Body Reports, US/Canada – Continued Suspension, para. 676.
395 Appellate Body Report, Japan – Agricultural Products II, para. 89.
396 Appellate Body Report, Japan – Apples, para. 176 (quoting Appellate Body Report,

397 Appellate Body Reports, Japan – Apples, para. 179; US/Canada – Continued Suspension, paras. 674
and 677. The Appellate Body made these observations taking into account the text and context of Article 5.7.
Article 5.7 is part of Article 5, entitled "Assessment of Risk and Determination of the Appropriate Level of
Sanitary or Phytosanitary Protection", is preceded by the requirement to base SPS measures on a risk
assessment, and refers to a "more objective assessment of risk".
398 Appellate Body Reports, US/Canada – Continued Suspension, para. 677.
399 The Panel notes that the expression "on the basis of" is similar to the expressions "as the basis for"
and "based on". The latter two expressions have been interpreted in previous disputes, in the context of
Article 2.4 of the TBT Agreement and Article 3 of the SPS Agreement, to mean that "there must be a very
strong and very close relationship between two things in order to be able to say that one is 'the basis for' the
other". In this regard, something cannot be considered a "basis" for something else if the two are
contradictory. (Appellate Body Reports, EC – Sardines, para. 245; India – Agricultural Products, para. 5.77).
400 The Panel notes that both the English and French versions of the SPS Agreement refer to "Members"
and "Membres", respectively, rather than "partes contratantes" (contracting parties), which is the term used in
the Spanish version of the Agreement. Additionally, in accordance with Explanatory Note 2(a) to the
GATT 1994, the references to a "contracting party" in the provisions of GATT 1994 shall be deemed to read
"Member". The Panel notes that, in this respect, the preamble to the SPS Agreement states that the
SPS Agreement elaborates rules for the application of the relevant provisions of GATT 1994. Therefore, any
reference to "partes contratantes" under Article 5.7 in the Spanish version of the SPS Agreement should be
understood as referring to "Members".
