bases indicating the possible existence of a risk. Therefore, there must be a rational and objective
relationship between information concerning the risk and the provisional measure.401 With regard to
the existence of a rational and objective relationship between the information concerning the risk
and the provisional measure, the Panel considers the interpretation of this sentence in the context
of Article 2.2 to be relevant. In particular, no "rational and objective relationship" has been found to
exist between the measure and the pertinent scientific evidence when the measure was "clearly
disproportionate" to the risk identified on the basis of scientific evidence available.402
7.113. The third requirement mandates that the Member adopting the provisional measure shall
seek to obtain the additional information necessary for a more objective assessment of risk. This
has been interpreted to mean that, once the provisional measure has been adopted, the Member
must make best efforts to remedy the insufficiencies in the relevant scientific evidence with
additional scientific research or by gathering information from relevant international organizations
or other sources.403 Bearing in mind that the objective of seeking to obtain additional information is
to perform "a more objective assessment of risk", this information should be germane to conducting
such an assessment.404 The "more objective assessment of risk" referred to in Article 5.7
corresponds to the risk assessment as required under Article 5.1 and as defined in Annex A(4).405
7.114. The fourth requirement concerns the review of the provisional measure within a reasonable
period of time. The term "razonable" ("reasonable") is defined as "adecuado, conforme a razón"
("adequate, within reason") or "proporcionado o no exagerado" ("proportionate, not
exaggerated").406 What constitutes a "reasonable period of time" should be established according to
the specific circumstances, including the difficulty of obtaining the additional information necessary
for the review and the characteristics of the provisional measure.407 Previous disputes have taken
into account whether the delay in concluding the review of the provisional measure is legitimate and
justifiable, as opposed to unwarranted or excessive, in determining whether the review was
conducted within a "reasonable period of time".408
7.115. The Panel agrees with these assessments of the legal standard applicable under Article 5.7
made in the above-mentioned disputes and will proceed with its assessment on this basis. Before
that, the Panel considers it appropriate to clarify which party bears the burden of proving that the
measure at issue is covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.

with the complainant to raise the inapplicability of Article 5.7, it is the respondent who ultimately
decides if it wishes to invoke the article as a defence, in which case it bears the burden of proof.409

7.121. The Panel agrees with the allocation of the burden of proof in these disputes and considers

7.122. Having clarified the matter of the allocation of the burden of proof, the Panel will continue
its analysis by examining the first requirement under Article 5.7 concerning whether Panama
adopted Measure 1 in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence.
Whether Panama adopted Measure 1 in a situation where relevant scientific evidence was
insufficient
7.123. In accordance with the legal standard set out above421, the Panel will now examine whether
Measure 1 was adopted in a situation where relevant scientific evidence was insufficient, within the
meaning of the first requirement under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. The Panel recalls that
Article 5.7 covers situations where deficiencies in the body of scientific evidence do not allow a
Member, in quantitative or qualitative terms, to perform an adequate assessment of risks, as
required under Article 5.1 and as defined in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.422
7.124. Costa Rica submits that Panama has failed to demonstrate that there was a situation of
insufficient scientific evidence that prevented it from performing a risk assessment that would
provide the basis for applying a measure that ensures compliance with the oxamyl MRL in future
shipments of strawberries by the producer concerned.423 Panama, for its part, asserts that Costa Rica
has failed to demonstrate that the scientific evidence was sufficient for the performance of a risk
assessment of the potential for adverse effects on human health arising from the presence of oxamyl
in strawberries.424
7.125. Given that the parties disagree on what constitutes a situation of insufficient scientific
evidence for the purposes of Article 5.7, the Panel will examine this matter before addressing other
aspects relevant to its analysis.

7.132. The Panel observes that both parties refer to the assertions of the Appellate Body in
US/Canada – Continued Suspension. As Costa Rica asserts, the Appellate Body noted in that dispute
that the Member adopting a provisional SPS measure should be able to identify the insufficiencies in
the relevant scientific evidence.437 Article 5.7 establishes a qualified right to adopt an SPS measure
temporarily438 while the additional information necessary for a more objective risk assessment is
obtained. The Panel considers that a logical consequence of the foregoing is that the Member
adopting the measure should be able to identify the insufficiencies in the scientific evidence that
justify its adoption in order to address those insufficiencies. The Panel notes that, in the case of
Measure 1, Panama has identified what it considers to be an insufficiency of scientific evidence,
namely the lack of up-to-date information on the suitability of Costa Rica's production and control
system for strawberries.439
7.133. With respect to situations where scientific evidence previously considered sufficient becomes
insufficient, the parties agree that new scientific evidence may make it necessary to obtain additional
information, without which it would be impossible to perform a risk assessment. Both parties also
acknowledge that new scientific evidence may come to light that calls "into question" the previous
conclusions of a Member with respect to the relevant risk. However, the parties disagree over
whether, in this case, the detections at issue called "into question" Panama's previous conclusions
relating to the risk to such an extent that it was impossible to conduct a new risk assessment.
7.134. The Panel concurs that there may be situations where new scientific evidence about a certain
aspect of the risk associated with the imported product calls into question the risk assessor's
conclusions, which formed the basis for the SPS measure adopted to manage that risk. In the Panel's
view, this scientific evidence may be a qualified and respected scientific opinion, as Costa Rica
asserts440, or another type of scientific evidence, such as the results of detecting a contaminant in
food, as referred to by Panama in this case.441
7.135. However, the Panel considers that the emergence of new relevant scientific evidence does
not automatically make the body of previously relevant scientific evidence insufficient for the
purposes of Article 5.7, even if the new evidence calls into question previous conclusions about the
risk. The Panel recalls that the threshold for insufficiency of scientific evidence within the meaning
of Article 5.7 is determined by the obligation to base the SPS measure on an assessment of risk, as
required under Article 5.1 and Annex A.442 The Panel therefore considers that its examination should
focus on whether, at the time Measure 1 was adopted, following the detections in question, the body
of relevant scientific evidence, including both new and available evidence, permitted Panama to
conduct a risk assessment.443
7.136. In this respect, the Panel agrees with the Appellate Body's observation in US/Canada –
Continued Suspension that, in some cases, new scientific developments would not permit a Member
to perform a sufficiently objective assessment of risk, whereas in others, it would.444 In this context,

Manual on good agricultural and production practices for strawberry crops, which it submitted during

7.146. For its part, Panama contends that the risk in question cannot be assessed appropriately
without up-to-date information on the suitability of Costa Rica's production and control system for
strawberries.465 Panama claims that the information on specific shipments, the pesticide and the
producer in question is not sufficient for an appropriate assessment of the risk because it says
nothing about the effectiveness of Costa Rica's sanitary control systems.466 When it comes to risks
arising from a specific type of product from specific countries, Panama argues that the authority of
an importing country must necessarily consider the management of the pesticide in question in the
exporting country; in this case, how Costa Rica manages the use of oxamyl on a national level. With
respect to the importation of strawberries from Costa Rica, Panama asserts that the PIR contained
in Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008 took into account Costa Rica's national SPS controls,
including its pesticide control measures, and the fact that these operated together with the MRLs
established for specific pesticides.467
7.147. The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 covers situations where all the necessary information is not
available for an appropriate assessment of the risks as required by Article 5.1 and defined in Annex A
to the SPS Agreement, given that Article 5.7 refers to the need to "obtain the additional information
necessary for a more objective assessment of risk". In this regard, the insufficiency of relevant
scientific evidence referred to in Article 5.7 is determined by the information that would be necessary
for the performance of an appropriate assessment of the risks. The Panel therefore considers it
necessary to identify the information that Panama needed to assess the risk in question in order to
determine whether, on the date Measure 1 was adopted, the available scientific evidence was
relevant and sufficient for the assessment of that risk.
7.148. The Panel recalls that the risk assessment Panama has to perform relates to the potential
for adverse effects on human health arising from the presence of oxamyl in Costa Rican strawberries.
It has been considered that this type of risk assessment, as provided for in the second sentence of
Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement, requires a Member to: (a) identify the additives, contaminants,
toxins or disease-causing organisms in food, beverages or feedstuffs at issue; (b) identify any
possible adverse effect on human or animal health; and (c) evaluate the potential for that adverse
effect to arise from the presence of the identified additives, contaminants, toxins or disease-causing
organisms in food, beverages, or feedstuffs.468
7.149. The record does not contain any risk assessment technique developed by a relevant
international organization that could guide the Panel with respect to the information necessary to
assess the risk in relation to the adverse effects on human health arising from the presence of
pesticide residues in food. However, three main components of risk analysis are described in
Costa Rica's "Procedure for the analysis of risks related to the chemical safety of unprocessed
vegetables", as defined by the Codex: (a) risk assessment; (b) risk management; and (c) risk
communication. In this procedure, it is noted that a risk assessment is a scientifically-based process
consisting of the four following steps: (a) hazard identification; (b) hazard characterization;
(c) exposure assessment; and (d) risk characterization.469
7.150. No further details are contained in the record about what these four steps entail.
Nevertheless, the Panel finds that the Codex food safety guidelines in the record (Guidelines CAC/GL
47-2003 and CAC/GL 26-1997), which address inspection based on risk and risk management
according to the degree of risk, may shed light on the information relevant to the assessment of the

be relevant, as it addresses the information to be borne in mind when determining food safety risks,
as described below.
7.151. The appendix to Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 is entitled "Principles and Guidelines for
Imported Food Inspection Based on Risk", that being food safety risks and the probability and
severity of an adverse health effect, consequential to hazards in food.471 Section 4 of the appendix
provides guidance on relevant information when assessing the level of risk associated with the
imported food. According to that section, relevant information could include:
The scientific determination of the food safety risk to the extent possible.
The adequacy of processing controls in place in the exporting country as evidenced by
its laws, regulations, and other policies; its infrastructure; and its ability to enforce food
safety requirements, as may be verified by audits and on-site visits by the competent
authority of the importing country.
The compliance history of the food generally, irrespective of the source of the food.
The compliance history of the food with respect to the source of the food, including,
where available, the compliance history of:
• the exporting country or region/area within an exporting country;
• the producer and manufacturer;
• the exporter;
• the shipper; and
• the importer.
Reports from officially recognized inspection bodies and/or certification bodies.472
7.152. The Panel also notes that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 indicate that, in the event of non-
compliance with safety requirements, to ensure that any measure is proportional to the degree of
risk, the following criteria should be considered: (a) repeated non-conformity in the same product
or in the same category of products; (b) history of non-conformity of those responsible for handling
the products; and (c) reliability of checks made by the country of origin.473
7.153. The Panel considers that the foregoing guidance from the Codex supports Panama's position
that the product processing regulations and relevant controls in the country of origin are elements
relevant to a food safety risk assessment. Additionally, on the basis of this guidance, the scientific
determination of the food safety risk and the compliance history of the food in question can also be
considered relevant.
7.154. Moreover, the Panel finds support in the text of the SPS Agreement for the interpretation
that commodity production regulations and controls may be necessary to the assessment of the risk
in question. In this respect, the Panel notes that Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement stipulates that, in
the assessment of risks, Member shall take into account "available scientific evidence; relevant
processes and production methods; relevant inspection, sampling and testing methods". In the

Panel's view, these elements may include regulations on the use of a pesticide in the production of

7.155. The Panel will now address the information available as of February 2020, when Measure 1
was adopted, and possible deficiencies in the body of relevant scientific evidence, in the light of the
information Panama needed to assess the risk at issue.
Relevance and sufficiency of the scientific evidence available at the time of the adoption
of Measure 1
7.156. Based on the arguments of the parties and the evidence in the record, the Panel identifies
the following categories of information that were available at the time of the adoption of Measure 1
in February 2020:
a. general information on the use of oxamyl and its effects on human health;
b. information on the existence or absence of MRLs for oxamyl in the United States, the
European Union (previously, the European Communities) and the Codex;
c. information on the control of the use of oxamyl in Costa Rica;
d. information on the detections in question; and
e. information on the compliance history of Fresas Gego and other potential Costa Rican
exporters.
General information on the use of oxamyl and its effects on human health
7.157. The Panel notes that the record contains a number of documents that describe the chemical
characteristics, toxicity and potential for adverse effects on human health of oxamyl and its residues
in food. In particular, these aspects of oxamyl have been assessed by the Joint FAO/WHO Meeting
on Pesticide Residues in 2002, as well as by the EPA in 2000, and by the European authorities in
2006.475 The Panel considers that these documents contain relevant scientific evidence for the
purposes of Article 5.7, given that they precede the adoption of Measure 1 and are related to the
adverse effects on human health associated with the presence of oxamyl residues resulting from the
use of oxamyl in agricultural production.
7.158. Costa Rica submits that, given that it does not challenge Panama's MRL, the risk analysis
that Panama should have performed in this case should not have focused on the adverse effects of
oxamyl residues in strawberries in the abstract.476 In this regard, the Panel considers that,
irrespective of the fact that Costa Rica does not challenge Panama's MRL and that the parties do not
disagree on the sufficiency of the scientific evidence regarding the adverse effects of oxamyl in
general, this scientific evidence forms part of the body of relevant scientific evidence.
7.159. In any case, it should be noted that Panama is not arguing that there is insufficient
information on the toxicity of oxamyl for human health resulting from a certain quantity of residues
of this pesticide in strawberries. The Panel understands that Panama is expressing concerns about
the alleged higher risk of oxamyl residues being present in harmful quantities in strawberries from
Costa Rica as a result of non-compliance with Panama's MRLs. In this regard, Panama's concern and

- 83 -
its allegations of insufficient relevant scientific evidence focus on Costa Rica's control system for
complying with Panama's MRLs.477 Before proceeding with the analysis of the alleged insufficiency
of scientific evidence concerning Costa Rica's control system, the Panel will address the information
on the imposition or absence of MRLs in the United States, the European Union, and the Codex,
referred to by Panama in its arguments with respect to the first requirement of Article 5.7.
Information on the imposition or absence of MRLs in the United States, the European
Union, and the Codex
7.160. Panama asserts that its MRL for oxamyl in strawberries was based on the European Union's
MRL, which corresponded to the lower limit of analytical quantification. Panama adds that "neither
the Codex nor the United States considered that the scientific evidence was sufficient to establish
an MRL for oxamyl in strawberries that was safe for human consumption."478
7.161. The record contains information about the imposition or absence of MRLs for oxamyl in
strawberries in the United States, the European Union, and the Codex.479 The Panel notes that, as
Panama indicates, MRLs "are not a substitute for risk" but an "instrument" through which Members
can address a specific risk.480 MRLs represent a reference value for the highest acceptable level of a
pesticide residue in a product from a crop to which that pesticide can be applied, which is why they
are an import requirement for that product. The Panel understands that MRLs result from risk
assessments and that MRLs already established for a pesticide may inform a Member's decision on
what MRL to impose in relation to that pesticide. In particular, the Codex MRLs, as international
recommendations, may serve as a guide.481
7.162. In this regard, information on the existence or values of MRLs for oxamyl in strawberries
from the relevant international organizations or other Members could be relevant to the risk
management phase, in particular, to the adoption of an MRL for this pesticide. The Panel does not
rule out that an MRL value and the considerations that led to its establishment may be relevant to
a risk assessment, especially when such considerations are reflected in a risk assessment. However,
the record contains no information on why there is no MRL for oxamyl in the United States or the
Codex.482 It also contains no explanation of the MRL for oxamyl in strawberries in the
European Union, although it is understood that 0.01 mg/kg corresponds to the lower limit of
analytical quantification achieved with the detection techniques available at that time.483 Thus, in
this case, the Panel does not consider that the existence of an MRL for oxamyl in strawberries in the
European Union and the absence of such an MRL in the United States and the Codex is, in itself,
proof of a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence for the assessment of the risk in
question.
477 See Panama's response to Panel question No. 122, para. 60. Panama asserts that the risk that the
suspension of imports was intended to manage is the risk of importing strawberries from Costa Rica containing
oxamyl at a level well above the established MRL, from producers responsible for repeated breaches during the
COVID-19 pandemic. Panama does not explain its reference to the COVID-19 pandemic in the context of the
risk associated with oxamyl detections in Costa Rican strawberry shipments in February 2020.

- 84 -
Information on the control of the use of oxamyl in Costa Rica
7.163. With respect to the sufficiency of the scientific evidence, the main point of contention
between the parties is the relevance and availability of information on the control of the use of
oxamyl in Costa Rica.
7.164. Panama asserts that its MRL operated in conjunction with the fact that oxamyl is not
generally approved for use in strawberries by Panama's trading partners. Panama claims that the
insufficiency of scientific evidence stems from the fact that the detection of oxamyl residues in the
two shipments of Costa Rican strawberries caused it to question whether oxamyl was actually being
used in strawberry production in Costa Rica, despite such usage supposedly being prohibited. For
Panama, it was as a consequence of having detected oxamyl in two shipments of strawberries that
the lack of information on the effectiveness of Costa Rican health controls emerged.484
7.165. Costa Rica considers that, when establishing an MRL, the importing country has no reason
to consider the legislation of exporting countries on the use of the pesticide. Costa Rica adds that,
even assuming that Panama had considered Costa Rica's prohibition on the use of oxamyl in
strawberries as scientific evidence for the management of the risk, Panama has not demonstrated
that the detected instances of non-compliance at issue have called this scientific evidence "into
question".485
7.166. As mentioned in paragraph 7.153 above, in the Panel's view, the product processing
regulations and relevant controls in the country of origin are elements relevant to a food safety risk
assessment. The Panel bases this understanding on, inter alia, the appendix to Guidelines
CAC/GL 47-2003 on the relevant information for assessing the level of risk associated with an
imported food. These Guidelines state that the adequacy of processing controls in place in the
exporting country is determined on the "evidence[] [of] its laws, regulations and other policies"; and
that the ability to effectively enforce food safety requirements is established according to what "may
be verified by audits and on-site visits by the competent authority of the importing country".486 The
Panel therefore understands that the relevant risk assessment would take into account (a) the laws,
regulations and other policies of the exporting Member; and (b) the verification at origin carried out
by the authorities of the importing Member.
7.167. The Panel addresses below the information in the record regarding the regulation of the use
of oxamyl in strawberry production in Costa Rica and the relevant pre-export controls of that country,
in order to determine whether the information in question was sufficient for the purposes of
Article 5.7 at the time Measure 1 was adopted.
7.168. Regarding its legislation, Costa Rica refers to the national prohibition on the use of oxamyl
in strawberry crops. Costa Rica submits a "list of formulated pesticides and related substances
authorized in strawberry crops" in Costa Rica, certified in 2022 by the Head of the Pesticide and
Application Equipment Registration Unit of the SFE of Costa Rica.487 The fact that this list does not
include oxamyl confirms that this pesticide was not registered for use in strawberry crops in
Costa Rica at the time the certification was issued in 2022. Although this certification post-dates the
adoption of Measure 1, Panama has confirmed that it "was aware that Costa Rica did not authorize
the use of oxamyl in strawberry production" at that time.488
7.169. Moreover, Costa Rica asserts that Panama had available pertinent information from its
Manual on good agricultural and production practices for strawberry crops, which expressly regulates
the use of pesticides such as oxamyl, and confirms that the Government of Costa Rica maintains a

- 85 -
system of good practices that is mandatory for strawberry producers.489 Costa Rica confirms that
the Manual was published in 2017 on both the SFE website and the website of the Inter-American
Institute for Cooperation on Agriculture (IICA), which collaborated in the development of the
Manual.490 In this regard, Costa Rica notes that, in order to comply with a country's MRLs, producers
must apply the GAP, which aim to reduce the risk of pesticide residues remaining in the relevant
products.491 Costa Rica asserts that the Manual in question is mandatory for all producers, field
personnel, transporters, and technicians involved in strawberry production in Costa Rica.492
Costa Rica adds that, while the SFE monitors and verifies that Costa Rican producers comply with
the provisions of the Manual, ultimate responsibility rests with each individual producer.493
7.170. Panama states that, when Measure 1 was communicated to Costa Rica in February 2020,
Panama did not have information about Costa Rica's implementation of the Manual.494 Panama notes
that the Manual does not refer to the ability of producers to implement it, nor to the existence of a
surveillance system or penalties for non-compliance.495
7.171. The Panel observes that the Manual on good agricultural and production practices for
strawberry crops, published in 2017 by MAG and the SFE of Costa Rica, "brings together a set of
technical recommendations and standards, with a preventive approach, focused on the control of
microbial, chemical and physical hazards at the various stages of strawberry production".496 These
technical recommendations and standards cover the activities that are undertaken, ranging from the
selection of the land to the transportation to the packing plant, as well as aspects related to the
handling, packaging and protection of the cargo.497
7.172. The Manual contains a number of provisions on the use of pesticides which state, inter alia,
that: (a) only pesticides currently registered in the country for use strawberry crops may be used,
in accordance with the recommendations on the label and in the accompanying leaflet concerning
dosage, expiry date, etc., in order to comply with the MRLs; (b) in the case of products for export,
pesticides that are officially prohibited for use in the country of destination must not be applied; (c)
it is recommended to carry out pesticide residue analysis on fruits at least once a year; (d)
documented records of pesticide residue analysis results must be kept; (e) pesticide residue analysis
results must be issued by a competent laboratory; (f) pesticide residues must not exceed the MRLs
established by national legislation or stipulated by the country of destination; and (g) in the case of
harvested fruit that does not comply with pesticide MRLs, the legislation in force will apply. With
regard to producers' ability to apply the GAP on pesticide use, the Manual specifies that producers
may make recommendations on pesticide applications, provided they demonstrate their technical
competence; and that the workers who apply pesticides must be trained in the appropriate
procedures and demonstrate competence in, and knowledge of, the matter.498 The Manual also
states that the SFE will carry out surveillance and verify the implementation of the various provisions
of the Manual that correspond directly to its competences.499

7.173. Given that the Manual was published in 2017 and was therefore available to Panama in
February 2020500, the Panel considers that, when adopting Measure 1, Panama had detailed
information on the good agricultural and production practices that the Costa Rican authorities
recommend for the strawberry production chain. This information covers the management of the
production, packaging, and transportation of strawberries, and includes instructions on the use of
pesticides in strawberry crops. The Manual refers to the ability of producers to apply pesticides in
accordance with GAP and the SFE's surveillance obligation. In addition, with regard to surveillance
and penalties in the event of non-compliance with MRLs, the Panel notes that Costa Rican
Decree No. 27683-MAG-MEIC-S of 1998, applicable to the entire agricultural sector, establishes
penalties and obligations relating to the observation status of producers or companies that infringe
MRLs, including the corrective actions to be taken. The Decree also includes provisions on the
surveillance of non-compliant products.501 The Decree was published in La Gaceta, the Official
Journal of the Government of Costa Rica, in 1999502, meaning that it was also available to Panama
in February 2020.
7.174. The Panel underscores that, in February 2020, Panama had prior knowledge of Costa Rica's
controls, given that Panama had imposed requirements for the importation of Costa Rican
strawberries in 2008 and had imported strawberries from Costa Rica since at least 2015.503 Indeed,
the Panel considers that the above-mentioned requirements – contained in Resolution AUPSA-
DINAN-006-2008504- are indicative of the information on the Costa Rican sanitary control systems
that Panama needed in order to have sufficient confidence in the food safety of Costa Rican
strawberries and, as a result, permit their importation.
7.175. Panama explains that it imposed such requirements following a verification of the pests of
quarantine concern notified by the country of origin of the product, which it characterized as a "risk
analysis ... [which] was not documented as such".505 Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008 includes
among its requirements that strawberries imported from Costa Rica should be covered by a
phytosanitary certificate which attests, among other things, that "[t]he goods come from areas and
places of production subject to inspection by the [NPPO] of the country of origin during the active
growth period, harvesting and packaging of the food".506 Another requirement of the same
Resolution is that the production, harvesting, and packaging of strawberries should take place in line
with the GAP and good manufacturing practices (GMP).507
7.176. In the Panel's view, the foregoing shows that, when it adopted Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-
006-2008, Panama knew that the NPPO of Costa Rica was inspecting areas and places of production,
since it required certification of that fact. In addition, it should be noted that Panama required
Costa Rica to ensure GAP compliance, but did not request it to provide evidence of such compliance
in order to import Costa Rican strawberries. This implies that Panama considered that the
information it had in 2008 regarding Costa Rica's GAP was sufficient to assess the risk in question.
7.177. Panama argues that the regulatory environment in which strawberry producers were
operating in Costa Rica in 2020 is "allegedly different" to that of 2008. In this regard, Panama cites
the 2017 introduction to Costa Rica's Manual on good agricultural and production practices for
strawberry crops. However, Panama asserts that it does not have enough information to assess the
exact difference in the regulatory environment.508 Costa Rica, in turn, maintains that pre-export

controls on strawberries, or any other non-processed plant product, are carried out in the field as it

Therefore, the Panel considers that, in February 2020, Panama had sufficient information on the
regulations concerning controls on strawberry production in Costa Rica, in particular with respect to

7.178. Furthermore, Panama has continued to import other fruits and vegetables from Costa Rica
following the imposition of Measure 1511, and Costa Rica points out that its procedure for monitoring
pesticide residues covers all types of fresh plant products.512 That being the case, it would indicate
that Panama had general information on pesticide residue monitoring in Costa Rica in order to import
other plant products from that country.
7.179. After having concluded that Panama had sufficient information on Costa Rica's relevant
regulations, including the information available on its GAP, the Panel will now address Panama's
argument that the detections in question caused a situation of insufficient scientific evidence by
calling into doubt the effective implementation of controls on the use of oxamyl in the production of
Costa Rican strawberries for export. For this reason, Panama considered it necessary to obtain

7.180. In the light of this argument, the Panel will analyse whether Panama had sufficient scientific
evidence on the implementation of pesticide use controls in Costa Rica.
7.181. The Panel considers that the information referred to by Panama pertains to the exporting

Panel recalls that, according to the Codex Guidelines, verification at origin provides information on

information through verifications at origin. However, in the context of Costa Rican strawberry
imports, Panama has not referred to the necessity of carrying out verifications at origin, which
suggests that, when Measure 1 was adopted, Panama did not consider it necessary to obtain
information from these verifications in order to assess the risk.

7.182. More than a year after Measure 1 was adopted, on 17 March 2021, Panama requested
information from Costa Rica on GAP control programmes, sampling at export production sites,
laboratory results and methods of analysis.515 When Panama notified Measure 1 to Costa Rica on
20 February 2020, after detailing the cases in question where oxamyl had been detected, Panama
stated that "[i]n view of this health situation, imports of strawberries from Costa Rica to the Republic
of Panama will be temporarily disallowed until the health authority of our sister country indicates
the corrective actions taken".516 The Codex Guidelines for the Exchange of Information between
Countries on Rejections of Imported Food, which apply where a consignment is found not to comply
with a country's safety requirements, state the following517:
Where appropriate, the competent authority of the exporting country should have
reasonable access to the evidence found by the importing country, so as to be able to
investigate the cause of the non-compliance and implement and manage any corrective
actions as required.
If requested, the competent authority in the exporting country should provide the
competent authority in the importing country with information on the outcome of the
necessary investigations and corrective actions taken.518
7.183. In this regard, the Panel understands that detection of non-compliance with an MRL may
justify the importing country requesting information from the exporting country about its
investigation into the cause of the non-compliance, and that this information may include information
on sampling, laboratory results, and methods of analysis employed in that investigation. The Panel
considers that Panama's request for information from Costa Rica in its communication of
20 February 2020 referred precisely to this exchange of information between the competent
authorities of the exporting and importing countries. In this respect, the Panel considers that the
fact that the competent authority of the importing Member needs to collect information does not
necessarily imply that the importing Member is in a situation where it is impossible for it to conduct
a risk assessment.519 In addition, the Panel notes that Panama's request for additional information
in the context of the adoption of Measure 1 was limited to the corrective actions taken by Costa Rica
in response to the non-compliance of two shipments of a Costa Rican producer, notified on
20 February 2020.520 Panama's request for information does not refer to a lack of information on
the relevant risk and does not indicate that there was insufficient information to conduct an
assessment of that risk. In the Panel's view, Panama has failed to demonstrate that, in the case of
the detections at issue, while waiting for Costa Rica's responses to its request for information on
corrective actions, it was in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence which prevented it
from conducting the relevant risk assessment.

input for assessing and deciding how to manage the risk. In this regard, while Costa Rica considers
that Panama had before it all the relevant information on the circumstances which, in its view,
implied a risk to the health of its consumers as a result of the instances in question where oxamyl
was detected522, Panama submits that the information regarding those detections was not sufficient
to conduct a risk assessment. For Panama, the "alarming" level of oxamyl detected in the two
shipments of Costa Rican strawberries called into question its conclusions on the relevant risk, raising
as it did "substantial concerns" about adverse effects on the health of its consumers.523 Costa Rica,
in turn, considers that the detected level is irrelevant for the purpose of determining the sufficiency
of the scientific evidence.524
7.185. The Panel observes that, although Panama asserts that the detections called into question
its conclusions on the risk, its arguments confirm that the detection details constitute relevant
information for the assessment of the risk. The Panel refers to Panama's arguments that cases of
non-compliance with MRLs "constitute, by definition, evidence of a risk that is higher than a
Member's ALOP", and that the levels detected, together with other pieces of information, point to a
higher risk of exposure and ingestion for Panamanian consumers.525
7.186. The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement covers situations where the importing
Member could not arrive at a sufficiently objective conclusion in relation to risk.526 Such situations
are different from those where, due to changes in circumstances, the Member needs to revise its
risk assessment. In the latter case, the Member could reach objective conclusions about the risk,
even though these conclusions differ from those arrived at previously. In the Panel's view, in this
case, the detected level does not affect Panama's ability to perform a risk assessment on the basis
of the available pertinent information, which includes the information on oxamyl and its effects on
human health, the information on Costa Rica's controls, and the details of the detection in question.
7.187. Given that the MRL is defined as the highest level of a pesticide residue that is legally
tolerated in food when pesticides are applied correctly in accordance with GAP527, the Panel
understands that non-compliance with an MRL may indicate that GAP have not been followed in the
exporting country with respect to the MRL-infringing shipment. Instances of non-compliance could
therefore inform the health risk assessment. In this regard, border controls could allow data on the
prevalence of non-compliance to be collected, and an infringement would not necessarily create a
situation of insufficiency for the purposes of Article 5.7.

7.190. The Panel also observes that Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008 stipulates that, upon the

Panama reserves the right to carry out further controls as it sees fit, at any point in the food chain.
In the Panel's view, it would be reasonable to consider that Panama's controls would allow it to
detect potential instances of non-compliance with its regulations, including those stemming from

strawberries. By means of these controls, Panama was able to obtain data that allowed it to keep a
compliance history in order to re-assess the risk posed by the importation of Costa Rican
strawberries.
7.191. Panama has presented no evidence of implementation of the above-mentioned controls,

7.198. Panama asserts that, in any case, Fresas Gego was an exporter "of systemic importance",
as its exports in the first two months of 2020 had far exceeded Costa Rica's total exports in 2019.546
7.199. The quantity exported by Fresas Gego in 2019 is significant (25.30% of Costa Rica's total
exports of strawberries to Panama that year547), especially in the light of the fact that Fresas Gego
was registered as an exporter in Costa Rica on 27 November 2019. Between 1 January and
20 February 2020, Fresas Gego exported 94.62% of the total exports of strawberries from Costa Rica
to Panama.548 This suggests that Fresas Gego was a major exporter in the period during which it
exported strawberries to Panama, i.e. between 27 November 2019 and 20 February 2020. However,
the data on Costa Rican strawberry exports to Panama is of limited relevance, given that Measure 1
was adopted in February 2020 and, from that date on, Panama has not permitted imports of
strawberries from any Costa Rican exporter.
7.200. After having concluded that Fresas Gego was not the only exporter of Costa Rican
strawberries in the years preceding the adoption of Measure 1, the Panel concludes that Panama
has not demonstrated that the MRL-infringing shipments represented "all" imports in such a way
that the detections gave rise to an insufficiency of scientific evidence that prevented Panama from
assessing the risk in question at the time Measure 1 was adopted. As mentioned in
paragraph 7.190 above, the Panel considers that Panama had a compliance history of strawberries
from Costa Rica that could serve as an input for its assessment of the risk.
Conclusion on whether Panama adopted Measure 1 in a situation in which the relevant
scientific evidence was insufficient
7.201. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that, at the time Measure 1 was adopted in
February 2020, the body of relevant scientific evidence was sufficient for Panama to perform an
assessment of the risk. In particular, the Panel considers the information on the following to be
relevant and sufficient: (a) oxamyl and its effects on human health; (b) Costa Rican regulations
relating to the use of oxamyl in strawberry production; (c) the non-compliance by Fresas Gego to
which Measure 1 was the response; and (d) the compliance history of Costa Rican strawberries.
Although the detections in question could have justified Panama requesting certain information from
Costa Rica, the Panel considers that Panama has failed to demonstrate that the detections affected
its ability to perform a risk assessment in accordance with Article 5.1 and the second clause of
Annex A(4) of the SPS Agreement.
7.202. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Panama has not demonstrated that it adopted Measure 1
in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence within the meaning of Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement.
7.203. In the Panel's view, this finding would be sufficient to conclude that Measure 1 is not covered
by Article 5.7, given that the requirements of Article 5.7 are cumulative.549 However, the Panel
considers it appropriate to also address the second requirement of Article 5.7, which, like the first,
concerns the adoption of the measure. The Panel will therefore proceed to examine whether Panama
has demonstrated that it adopted Measure 1 on the basis of available pertinent information, within
the meaning of Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.

Whether Panama adopted Measure 1 on the basis of available pertinent information
7.204. In accordance with the legal standard described above550, the Panel will examine below
whether Panama adopted Measure 1 on the basis of available pertinent information within the
meaning of Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. The Panel recalls that, as the parties have indicated551,
in order to satisfy this requirement there must be a "rational and objective relationship" between
the information concerning a certain risk and a Member's provisional SPS measure.552
7.205. Taking into account these considerations, the Panel will now proceed to analyse whether
Panama's decision to "temporarily disallow imports of strawberries from Costa Rica" in response to
the detection of non-compliance in two shipments of strawberries from the Costa Rican producer
Fresas Gego was based on pertinent information available to Panama on 20 February 2020.553 To
this end, the Panel will examine whether there is a rational and objective relationship between
Measure 1 and pertinent available information regarding the risk in question, namely the risk of the
potential for adverse effects on human health arising from the presence of oxamyl in shipments of
strawberries from Costa Rica.554 The Panel will address the information that each party identifies as
pertinent for the purposes of the second requirement of Article 5.7, which is summarized in the table
below.

"basada en" ("based on"), which have been interpreted, in the context of Article 2.4 of the TBT Agreement and
Article 3 of the SPS Agreement, to mean that there must be a very strong and very close relationship between
two things. Therefore, the measure must not contradict the information in question. (See also Appellate Body
Reports, EC – Sardines, para. 245; India – Agricultural Products, para. 5.77). The Panel also recalls that it has

General information on oxamyl and the establishment of MRLs for oxamyl
General information on oxamyl
7.207. As mentioned above, the record contains sources of information on the use and harmful

adoption of Measure 1 confirm that oxamyl is a highly hazardous substance for human health.567 In
the Panel's view, the proven high toxicity of oxamyl could be a relevant consideration in determining
how to manage the risk of its potential presence in imported strawberries.

not Panama's MRL for oxamyl in strawberries, but rather its response to a violation of this MRL.570

respect to the EPA, a search of its database contained in the record shows that the United States
has not set an MRL for oxamyl.572 As mentioned above573, the Panel observes that there is nothing

Codex Guidelines and measures of other Members
7.212. The Panel observes that the parties disagree on the pertinence of the Codex Guidelines and
the measures of other Members cited by Costa Rica. The Panel will address this matter below,
starting with the pertinence of the Guidelines.
Pertinence of the Codex Guidelines and the measures of other Members
7.213. Regarding the pertinence of the Codex Guidelines, Costa Rica notes that Article 5.7 states
that the pertinent information that must be considered when adopting a provisional SPS measure
includes that from the relevant international organizations.577 Costa Rica asserts that, given that
Measure 1 was adopted due to Panama's food safety concerns, the Codex standards are pertinent

that the Codex Guidelines that it identifies are pertinent to Measure 1, as they set out appropriate

nothing in the text of Article 5.7 indicates that "pertinent" information must refer to the "particular
risk" that the Member is trying to mitigate.580 Costa Rica asserts that the fact that an international
organization issues standards or guidelines applicable to general situations does not mean that they
are not relevant to a specific risk. According to Costa Rica, these standards and guidelines are
designed to guide all countries in a general manner, regardless of their particular circumstances,
and it is up to the importing Member to apply them to its specific circumstances when determining
appropriate SPS measures.581
7.214. For its part, Panama submits that the Codex Guidelines to which Costa Rica refers are not
pertinent information within the meaning of Article 5.7 because they do not say anything about the
particular risk that Panama is assessing with respect to Measure 1. Panama asserts that these
Guidelines are generic guidelines on the operation of inspection, certification, and control systems,

and that they do not constitute an "international food safety standard that assesses the risk to
human health arising from the presence of oxamyl residues in strawberries".582 Panama is of the
view that the reference to the word "pertinent" in Article 5.7 requires that the international standard
in question should have at least some relationship to the particular risk being mitigated by the
WTO Member.583
7.215. The third parties commented on the Spanish word "pertinente" (rendered as "relevant" and
"pertinent" in English), which appears in Article 5.7 in relation to "scientific evidence" and
"information", respectively. In Australia's view, "relevant" means any scientific evidence that is
"pertinent" to performing a risk assessment.584 Similarly, Brazil asserts that the information provided
must fit in the context of the risk assessment under way, and that its pertinence will depend on the
specific risk under examination.585 Canada refers to the ordinary meaning of the term "relevant",
namely "bearing upon or relating to the matter at hand; pertinent".586 The European Union states
that the scientific evidence must pertain to the risk assessed.587 The United States is of the view
that, in the context of Article 5.7, "relevance" must be understood in terms of the obligation under
Article 5.1 to perform a risk assessment. The United States adds that the provisional measure is
taken in consideration of "available pertinent information", which is a fortiori information that need
not be of the same character or sufficiency as that necessary for a risk assessment under
Annex A(4).588
7.216. The Panel recalls that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 provide a framework
for the development and operation of import and export inspection and certification systems.589 The
paragraphs of these Guidelines referred to by Costa Rica address the management of situations of
non-compliance, and set out criteria to take into account and specific measures that may be applied
in such situations.590
7.217. The Panel observes that the disagreement between the parties focuses on whether the
information pertinent to the adoption of Measure 1 is limited to information on the particular risk in
the sense of an international food safety standard, as asserted by Panama, or whether it can also
include information relating to the management of this risk, such as that contained in Guidelines
CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003, as stated by Costa Rica.
7.218. The Panel observes that Article 5.7 provides that a Member may provisionally adopt sanitary
or phytosanitary measures "on the basis of available pertinent information".591 The ordinary meaning
of "pertinente" ("pertinent") is "bearing upon or relating to the matter at hand"592, or "perteneciente
o correspondiente a algo" ("pertaining or corresponding to something").593 As Costa Rica asserts594,
the term "pertinent" is not accompanied by a term that limits the pertinent information to the
"particular risk" that the Member is trying to mitigate (namely the adverse effects of a particular
pesticide or the entry, establishment or spread of a particular pest).

7.219. In addition, the Panel shares the view, expressed in previous disputes, that in order for a
measure to be considered adopted on the basis of pertinent information, "there must be a rational
and objective relationship between the information concerning a certain risk and a Member's
provisional SPS measure".595 The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 contemplates temporary situations
that last until information is obtained that permits a more objective assessment of the risk.596 In the
meantime, SPS measures must be based on the pertinent available information. Given this context,
the Panel understands that the pertinent information on which the SPS measure must be based must
provide the Member with useful guidance for performing a preliminary risk assessment and adopting
a temporary regulatory response in this regard.
7.220. The Panel notes that the term "pertinent information" is followed by the phrase "including
that from the relevant international organizations as well as from sanitary or phytosanitary measures
applied by other Members".597 This phrase indicates that international standards from relevant
international organizations and SPS measures applied by other Members could constitute "pertinent
information" for the purposes of Article 5.7. With regard to the "relevant international organizations",
the preamble to the SPS Agreement mentions the Codex among such organizations598, and
Annex(3)(a) of the SPS Agreement establishes that the Codex is the relevant international
organization for food safety. Accordingly, the Panel considers that the Guidelines developed under
the auspices of Codex constitute information "from" that organization, and that they could therefore
constitute "pertinent" information for the purposes of Article 5.7.
7.221. Annex A(3)(a) of the SPS Agreement refers specifically to "standards, guidelines and
recommendations established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission relating to food additives,
veterinary drug and pesticide residues, contaminants, methods of analysis and sampling, and codes
and guidelines of hygienic practice". The Panel considers that this list may include Guidelines
CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003, given that these Guidelines cover inspection systems dealing
with all types of risks arising from food imports, which may include risks related to pesticide residues
in food. In this connection, the Panel observes that the preamble to the SPS Agreement recognizes
the importance of standards that concern both the development as well as the adoption and
enforcement of SPS measures.599
7.222. The Panel also observes that the term "medida" ("measure"), which refers to risk
management given that the measure is a risk control tool600, follows the phrase "información
pertinente" ("pertinent information") in Article 5.7. For the Panel, this indicates that information that
may help the Member decide how to manage a risk may constitute pertinent information within the
meaning of Article 5.7.
7.223. The Panel also considers that the provisions of the SPS Agreement that relate to risk
assessment in the framework of the Agreement provide relevant context for the interpretation of
the phrase "pertinent information" in Article 5.7.601 The Panel notes that Article 5.1 of the

601 The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 refers to the objective of performing a "more objective assessment

WT/DS599/R
- 98 -
SPS Agreement refers to "risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant international
organizations", which must be taken into account in the risk assessment on which an SPS measure
is based. In the Panel's view, this indicates that the information pertinent to the adoption of a
measure is not limited to the information on the particular risk. The Panel also observes that
Articles 5.2 and 5.3, which are linked to Article 5.1, relate to elements and factors that pertain to
risk management, namely relevant inspection methods, quarantine treatment602, and the cost-
effectiveness of alternative approaches to limiting risks.603
7.224. In previous disputes, it has been considered that Article 5 and Annex A of the SPS Agreement
do not support a restrictive interpretation of the concept of risk assessment, which is why in the
context of the SPS Agreement no distinction is made between "risk assessment" and "risk
management".604 Similarly, the definition of the first type of risk assessment in Annex A(4) of the
SPS Agreement includes considerations relating to risk management, as it refers to the
"phytosanitary measures which might be applied, and of the associated potential biological and
economic consequences".605 The Panel therefore understands that the risk assessment referred to
in the SPS Agreement also includes the risk management phase.606
7.225. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that "pertinent information" within the
meaning of Article 5.7 does not refer exclusively to information that addresses the particular risk in
question, but may also refer to information relating to the management of that risk. Thus, in the
Panel's view, Article 5.7 would not exclude international standards of a general nature that address
the management of a particular situation, such as the adoption of measures in situations of non-
conformity, although such standards do not specifically refer to the product or risk (pest or
contaminant) in question that the Member seeks to mitigate.
7.226. After concluding that, in general, Article 5.7 does not necessarily exclude international
standards of a general nature that address the management of a particular situation, the Panel must
also determine whether the specific Guidelines or measures invoked by Costa Rica are "pertinent"
in the specific context of Measure 1. In relation to Measure 1, Costa Rica challenges Panama's
response to the detection of non-compliance with the oxamyl MRL in strawberries by two shipments
of Costa Rican strawberries, rather than the MRL itself. Therefore, in the circumstances of this case,
the pertinent information is not limited to information on the specific risk to human health of oxamyl,
but also includes the information that would allow Panama to react to non-compliance with its MRL.
As mentioned above607, the paragraphs of the Codex Guidelines cited by Costa Rica specifically
address situations of non-compliance with the safety requirements of the importing country. While
these paragraphs are general in nature, and do not link the situation of non-compliance with any
particular product, pest or contaminant, they do relate to the management of the particular risk
situation at issue in this dispute, namely, non-compliance with the MRL of the importing country.
Therefore, the Panel considers that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 provide
pertinent information for the adoption of Measure 1.
7.227. Panama further asserts that if these Guidelines were "pertinent" to the risk in question, this
would automatically impose the particular ALOP reflected in the Codex Guidelines on all Members
with respect to all products, contrary to Article 3.3 of the SPS Agreement.608 The Panel understands
must be based, in the context of Article 5.7, includes information that would be relevant to an assessment of
risk within the meaning of Article 5.1.

Panel considers that the factors listed in this provision may inform its analysis of whether the information
pertinent to risk assessment under the SPS Agreement includes information related to the management of that
risk.

that Panama argues that its provisional measures could not be adopted on the basis of international
standards because those standards did not reflect Panama's higher ALOP. The Panel considers that
even if these standards reflect a different ALOP, this does not necessarily mean that the standards
are not pertinent. The Panel also recalls that Article 3.3 of the SPS Agreement allows Members to
"introduce or maintain [SPS] measures which result in a higher level of sanitary or phytosanitary
protection than would be achieved by measures based on the relevant international standards,
guidelines or recommendations" in certain circumstances.609 The Panel finds nothing in the
SPS Agreement to indicate that this right does not apply to provisional measures. In any case, the
Panel notes that this is not an absolute right 610 and that Panama should substantiate its decision to
deviate from the relevant international standards in the context of both Article 3.3 and Article 5.7.
Under Article 5.7, Panama must provide some basis for selecting the measure in question in order
to demonstrate that the measure is based on the pertinent information available at the time of its
adoption.
7.228. Regarding the pertinence of other Members' SPS measures, Costa Rica notes that Article 5.7
states that the pertinent information to be considered when adopting a provisional SPS measure
includes SPS measures applied by other Members.611 Costa Rica identifies as pertinent the regulatory
responses of the European Union, the United States and Australia in cases of non-compliance with
MRLs.612 According to Costa Rica, it was up to Panama to base Measure 1 on, inter alia, these
measures, as part of its analysis of the appropriate provisional measure.613 Costa Rica submits that,
if such measures are not considered pertinent because they do not address Panama's specific
circumstances, the requirement to consider other Members' measures when adopting a provisional
measure due to insufficient scientific evidence would be deprived of practical effect.614
7.229. Panama submits that the other Members' measures referred to by Costa Rica are not
pertinent to Measure 1. Panama argues that the other Members' measures "enforce, in a generic
way, regulatory responses to instances of MRL non-compliance ".615 Panama asserts that the way
in which developed Members have designed and operate their inspection and control systems, and
their responses to instances of non-compliance, bear no relation to the particular risk that Panama
was trying to assess.616
7.230. For the same reasons as stated in relation to international standards, the Panel considers
that other Members' measures in relation to food safety controls could be pertinent under Article 5.7,
even if they are not specific to the product or risk (pest or contaminant) in question. In particular,
the Panel recalls that, in relation to Measure 1, Costa Rica is challenging Panama's response to the
non-conformity with the MRL for oxamyl in strawberries of two shipments of Costa Rican
strawberries. Therefore, in the circumstances of this case, the pertinent information includes
information on the management of the particular risk situation that Panama claims to address
through the adoption of Measure 1, i.e. non-conformity with the MRL of the importing country.
Moreover, the Panel considers that nothing in Article 5.7 indicates that the level of development of
a Member affects the pertinence of its measures when they address the situation that the importing
Member faces. The Panel observes that Panama itself refers to the measures of other Members in
its arguments.617

7.231. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that the Guidelines and measures of other Members cited
by Costa Rica are pertinent because, although they do not address non-conformity with oxamyl MRLs
for strawberries in particular, they provide guidance on procedures applicable in cases of non-
conformity with food safety requirements. Having determined the relevance of the Codex Guidelines
and the measures of other Members cited by Costa Rica, the Panel will examine whether Panama
adopted Measure 1 on the basis of those same Guidelines and measures.
Whether Measure 1 was adopted on the basis of the Codex Guidelines invoked by
Costa Rica
7.232. The Panel will now examine whether Measure 1 was adopted on the basis of the Guidelines
for the Design, Operation, Assessment and Accreditation of Food Import and Export Inspection and
Certification Systems (Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997) and the Guidelines for Food Import Control
Systems (Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003).618
7.233. Costa Rica submits that, read in conjunction, these Guidelines make clear that in the event
of a non-conformity with food safety requirements, the measures applied by the importing country
must be proportionate and incremental. According to Costa Rica, these Guidelines establish that the
importing country can, as a first step, change the manner in which risk is managed without
suspending imports. Costa Rica argues that measures to change the manner in which risk is
managed could have been holding the food pending a final judgement, enhancing sampling and
testing from the establishment in question, requesting the Costa Rican authorities for information
and cooperation, control programmes implemented by the Panamanian importer or the Costa Rican
exporter to prevent future non-compliance, or even on-site visits.619 Costa Rica adds that, if there
is evidence of systemic problems, the importing country could extend the measures to other
establishments. Costa Rica asserts that the suspension of imports is a last resort, justifiable only "in
the most serious or persistent cases", and once it has been confirmed that previous measures proved
insufficient in ensuring compliance with the safety requirements.620 Costa Rica adds that Panama
"failed to adhere" to these Guidelines, since Panama imposed, as its first and only measure, an
outright prohibition on imports of strawberries from Costa Rica after it detected oxamyl residues at
levels higher than its MRL in two shipments from a Costa Rican producer.621
7.234. Panama argues that, even assuming that these Guidelines are pertinent, the temporary
suspension of imports from the relevant producer or country – which, in its view, overlap in this case
– is one of the measures prescribed by the Codex for the most serious or persistent cases.622
According to Panama, the situation in which Measure 1 was adopted constitutes a "serious" and
"persistent" case of non-conformity.623
7.235. With regard to Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997, the Panel notes that paragraphs 34 and 35, to
which Costa Rica refers, concern the management of situations where "an imported product is found
not to be in conformity" with food safety requirements.624 Paragraph 34 indicates the criteria that
should be taken into account to ensure that any action taken in these situations is "proportionate to
the degree of public health risk, potential fraud or deception of consumers".625 These criteria are:
(a) repeated non-conformity in the same product or in the same category of products; (b) history
of non-conformity of those responsible for handling the products; and (c) reliability of checks made
by the country of origin.626
7.236. Paragraph 35 specifies examples of measures in respect of the non-conforming product and
in respect of future imports. It states that these measures may be cumulative if necessary. The
Panel notes that Costa Rica is not challenging Panama's decision to deny entry into its territory to

imports of Costa Rican strawberries.627 Therefore, the pertinent examples of measures in
paragraph 35 are those that apply to future imports, namely: (a) control programmes implemented
by the importer or exporter to ensure problems do not re-occur; (b) increased intensity of checks
on categories of products identified as being not in conformity and/or the undertakings concerned;
(c) request for information and cooperation on the product or the category of products found not to
be in conformity by the responsible authorities in the country of origin (increased checks at origin);
(d) on-site visits; and, (e) "in the most serious or persistent cases", suspension of imports from
establishments or countries.628
7.237. With regard to Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003629, Costa Rica refers to the appendix thereto,
which provides competent authorities with information to assist them with the design and
implementation of inspection programmes for imported food, based on food safety risks.630
Paragraph 20 of the appendix addresses the scenario of a shipment that fails to meet the food safety
requirements. It states that this situation might, besides other actions, trigger a change in the
manner in which risk is managed by the importing country for the food concerned. According to this
paragraph, this change in risk management could include food being held and enhanced sampling
and testing from the establishment involved, as well as these actions being applied to other exporting
establishments from the same country producing similar foods "where there is evidence of a systemic
problem".631 The suspension of the importation of a food should be reserved only for those situations
"involving a serious food safety risk that has not been managed by other means".632
7.238. The Panel considers that these Guidelines can be read in conjunction, since they both address
non-compliance with food safety requirements. On the basis of a joint reading of the relevant
paragraphs of the Guidelines, the Panel understands that, when a shipment does not meet food
safety requirements, the first line of response in respect of future imports should be limited to actions
such as: the product being held pending final judgment; implementing control programmes;
requesting information from, or increased controls by, the exporting country; increased intensity of
checks by the importing country of the non-conforming product or of the exporter concerned633 and
of other exporters of this food (if there is evidence of systemic problems); and on-site visits.634 The
Panel understands that the measures may be applied cumulatively if necessary, and would need to
be proportionate to the degree of risk, which could depend on whether there is repeated non-
conformity and on the reliability of the importing country's checks.635 The suspension of imports
would be considered only in the "most serious or persistent cases"636 or in situations involving a
"serious food safety risk".637
7.239. Given that Measure 1 seeks to "disallow" imports of Costa Rican strawberries638, this
measure has the effect of suspending these imports. Therefore, the Panel will examine whether
detecting non-compliance with the oxamyl MRL for strawberries, which is the basis for Measure 1,

can qualify as a serious or persistent case of non-conformity or as a serious risk that, according to
the Codex Guidelines, would justify a suspension of imports.
7.240. Costa Rica asserts that Panama's submissions confirm that the non-conformities detected
do not fall into the category of the "most serious or persistent cases" or of a "serious food safety
risk"639, nor do they qualify as "repeated cases of non-conformity".640 According to Costa Rica, in
the case of these non-conformities, the appropriate response would have been to implement
measures to change the manner in which the risk is managed, without suspending imports from the
producer responsible, let alone imports of all Costa Rican strawberries, which is the most stringent
measure possible and not proportionate to the infringement.641
7.241. According to Costa Rica, a case is serious or persistent when an importing country continues
to detect instances of non-compliance after having adopted measures to change the manner in which
the risk is managed, for instance, increased controls for the producer responsible or food being held
pending final judgment.642 Costa Rica adds that these cases are considered serious because the
foreign producer or exporter would not have improved its GAP despite the fact its shipments continue
to be rejected by the importing country and it remains subject to additional control measures.643
Costa Rica asserts that, as indicated by paragraph 24 of Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003, the greater
controls enable a compliance history to be created about the foreign producer in question. For
Costa Rica, this history is important so that the importing country knows whether the exporter in
question continues to be found non-compliant or whether the additional measures can be withdrawn
due to a decrease in infringements.644
7.242. Costa Rica asserts that Panama cannot maintain that the Codex standards justify a
prohibition on imports because precautionary measures would not achieve its ALOP. For Costa Rica,
this argument reflects speculation on the part of Panama regarding what would have happened if
Panama had adopted precautionary measures prior to implementing a "prohibition", which it did not
do.645
7.243. Panama, for its part, submits that the non-conformities detected in shipments of Costa Rican
strawberries under Measure 1 constitute a "serious or persistent" case, for which the
Codex Guidelines would authorize the temporary suspension of imports from the corresponding
country. For Panama, these "repeated cases of non-conformity suggested a systematic problem with
the controls carried out by Costa Rica", meaning that precautionary measures would not reach
Panama's ALOP.646 Panama stresses that, in its view, there was a repeated infringement in relation
to an extremely hazardous substance, coupled with the impossibility of verifying the reliability of the
controls carried out by the exporting country.647 Panama asserts that the ordinary meaning of the
terms "serious" and "persistence" indicates that "serious or persistent" cases are those that involve
repeated non-compliance of great significance. Panama adds that, according to the text and context
of the Codex Guidelines, the seriousness of the risks to human health, repeated non-conformity, in
addition to the reliability and effectiveness of the control systems, are criteria relevant to clarifying
the meaning of "serious or persistent".648

7.244. Panama submits that the authorities of the importing country enjoy wide discretion in
determining how serious the violation is. Panama asserts the Codex Guidelines referred to do not
dictate the order in which risk management measures must be applied, requiring the importing
country to exhaust all other measures before resorting to the suspension of imports. Panama adds
that the degree of deference to the importing authority, in the event of non-compliance, is reflected
in the use of the words "may" and "should" in the Guidelines. For Panama, the text of both Guidelines
makes it clear that an importing country may suspend imports immediately if the case of
non-compliance is serious.649 Panama refers to paragraph 15 of the appendix to
Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 in its argument that the detection of contaminants and harmful residues
in imported food may lead an importing country to suspend the trade in that food until it is confirmed
that corrective measures have been introduced and are being implemented effectively.650
7.245. The Panel will now analyse the meaning and scope of the terms "serious" and "persistent"
in Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and "serious" in Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003. While the
Codex Guidelines do not define these terms, the Panel considers that the text of the relevant
paragraphs of the Guidelines provide useful indications as to the meaning of these terms.651
7.246. Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 indicate that, in the event of non-conformity with food safety
requirements, the measures implemented by the importing country should be proportionate to the
degree of risk and cumulative, if necessary.652 This means that the Guidelines recommend first
implementing a measure and then, if justified, imposing additional measures to increase the degree
of trade restrictiveness.
7.247. Moreover, the fact that Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 refer, first, to enhanced controls of the
establishment involved, beside other actions; second, to controls of other establishments where
there is evidence of a "systemic problem"653; and, third and lastly, to the suspension of the
importation of the food from the country, illustrates the order in which, according to the Codex, the
mitigation measures should be applied. It should be stressed that Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003
indicate that the suspension of imports "should be reserved only" for situations involving a "serious
food safety risk".654 The same Guidelines also state that a "serious … risk" is one that "has not been
managed by other means", since this is the phrase that immediately follows and qualifies "serious
… risk".655 The Panel understands that these considerations also apply when adopting a suspension
in "the most serious or persistent cases" within the meaning of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997, given
that the two Guidelines provide supplementary guidance on the same subject and there is nothing
in them that contradicts this understanding.656
7.248. Panama refers to paragraph 15 of the appendix to Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003, which notes
that, inter alia, the "detection of pathogens, contaminants and harmful residues in imported food ...
may lead an importing country to adjust the nature and frequency of inspection, or in extreme cases,
to suspend the trade in that food until it is confirmed that corrective measures have been introduced
and are being implemented effectively".657 The Panel considers that this paragraph supports the
interpretation that the Guidelines indicate the order in which measures should be adopted once an
