- 104 -
infringement has been detected, starting with an increase in inspections, and proceeding to a
suspension of trade only in serious cases and until assurances are provided the situation has been
resolved.
7.249. In the Panel's view, the context in which the term "grave" ("serious")658 appears in both
Guidelines indicates that a serious case of non-conformity or a "serious food safety risk" would be
an instance where other mitigation measures have not worked. Contrary to what Panama asserts,
the Codex Guidelines note that, for a risk to be qualified as serious and for the importing country to
be able to decide to suspend imports, the country should first attempt to manage the risk through
other measures. In this regard, suspension would be understood as a measure of last resort, as
Costa Rica describes it. The Panel does not consider it necessary to rule on the degree to which the
Guidelines must be followed, based on the use of the terms "may" and "should", in order to
determine whether Measure 1 was adopted on the basis of said Guidelines. While the Guidelines do
provide Panama with flexibility in determining which measure to impose based on its assessment of
the circumstances, in the Panel's view, it is evident from these Guidelines that the suspension of
imports is reserved for "serious" cases or cases involving a "serious risk".659
7.250. In this case, Panama has not responded to the detections of non-conformity by implementing
measures in a incremental and cumulative manner. On the contrary, Panama has resorted to the
most restrictive measure, without first attempting to manage the risk by other means. Panama does
not dispute that Measure 1 was the first and only risk management measure that it adopted when
oxamyl residues were detected at levels higher than its relevant MRL in two shipments of
strawberries from Costa Rica. The Panel considers that the case of non-compliance with the oxamyl
MRL in question does not qualify as "serious" or as involving a "serious risk", within the meaning of
the Guidelines, given that this is the first detection of a non-conformity in two shipments from the
same establishment that was not managed in an incremental manner by, for instance, applying
enhanced controls initially. In this respect, Panama fails to explain how the fact that oxamyl is an
extremely hazardous substance660 could affect the possibility of managing the risk in question by
means other than a suspension of imports. Therefore, the Panel considers that, by adopting a
measure that is tantamount to a suspension of imports of strawberries, Panama failed to follow the
guidance of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 in relation to the management of non-
compliance cases based on the seriousness of risk.
7.251. According to Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997, the suspension of imports may also be adopted in
"persistent" cases. The term "reincidente" ("persistent") is defined as "[q]ue reincide o ha reincidido"
("[t]hat an offence is repeated or has been repeated"), where "reincidir" ("persist") is defined as
"[v]olver a caer o incurrir en un error, falta o delito" ("[t]o repeat or commit an error, offence or
crime again").661 In the light of the criteria for determining risk set out in paragraph 34 of
Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997, the Panel considers that the term "persistent" in paragraph 35 refers
to a "repeated non-conformity in the same product or in the same category of products" or "history
of non-conformity of those responsible for handling the products". Moreover, the Panel finds it
instructive that Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 indicate that, in order to assess the risk associated with

Food Import Control Systems (2006) (Exhibit CRI-127), section 4, para. 22). See also ibid. appendix,
section 4, para. 6.

the imported food when designing a control system, one of the factors to take into account is the
compliance history of the food.662
7.252. As mentioned above, Measure 1 was adopted in response to non-compliance with Panama's
oxamyl MRLs in two shipments from the same exporter (Fresas Gego) from Costa Rica. In
Note AUPSA-AG-051-2020, through which Panama informed Costa Rica of the adoption of
Measure 1, Panama refers to the detection of "various non-conformities in … February 2020".663
However, in this Note, dated 20 February 2020, Panama only presents the non-conformity of the
two shipments from Fresas Gego ascertained on 6 and 18 February, respectively. In the Panel's
view, this non-conformity cannot be considered as "persistent" for the purposes of the relevant
paragraphs of the Guidelines because it concerns only two samples of the shipments of one exporter
that were analysed in the same month and notified together.664 Otherwise, the recommendation of
the Guidelines to adopt measures cumulatively before resorting to the suspension of imports in
serious cases or when non-compliance persists would be rendered meaningless.665
7.253. In communications sent after Note AUSPA-AG-051-2020, Panama reports having detected
other pesticides in shipments from Costa Rica in quantities exceeding Panama's MRLs666 and notes
that it considers these to be "repeated non-conformities detected" in Costa Rican strawberries.667
However, Panama itself explains that the results of these detections were issued after it had
suspended imports of strawberries from Costa Rica.668 Therefore, given that this information was
not available to Panama when it adopted Measure 1 on 20 February 2020, the Panel considers that
these detections cannot be used to consider the non-conformities notified on that date as
"persistent".
7.254. With respect to its compliance history with MRLs, Costa Rica asserts it has been exporting
strawberries to Panama for decades, in accordance at all times with the relevant Panamanian
regulations.669 Costa Rica has submitted a compliance history of its producers with MRLs during the
period 2018-2021, that, according to Costa Rica, was produced on the basis of sampling undertaken
by the SFE, pursuant to the annual plan for monitoring pesticide residues. Costa Rica adds that the
monitoring of Fresas Gego began in 2019, which is when that company was included in the SFE's
register of exporters, packers, and traders in plant products for exportation.670 According to
Costa Rica, its history demonstrates that in that period, the Fresas Gego non-compliance is an
isolated incident.671
7.255. With reference to Costa Rica's history, Panama asserts that Fresas Gego was the only
producer of strawberries whose exports were controlled by the SFE for a period of nearly two
years672, and the result of the first sampling of Fresas Gego carried out on 27 February 2020
revealed non-compliance with the MRL.673 Panama adds that the domestic compliance history of
strawberry producers in Costa Rica from 2018-2019 is not relevant to the assessment of Measure 1,

7.256. The Panel notes that the compliance history of domestic sampling by Costa Rica includes
data of samples taken between October 2018 and November 2021 from three exporters: Plantas y
Flores Ornamentales CABH S.A., Central de Flores RGJ, and Gregory Herrera Siles (Fresas Gego).
Reference is made to 21 samples, four of which were taken from sampling of Plantas y Flores
Ornamentales CABH S.A. and Central de Flores FGJ, and the other samples were taken from
Fresas Gego as from 27 February 2020, after Measure 1 was adopted. The only case of non-
compliance included is that of Fresas Gego on 27 February 2020. In any event, the Panel
understands that the results of Costa Rica's domestic sampling were not available to Panama in
February 2020.
7.257. Panama also submits an exhibit that, in its view, contains the history of compliance with its
MRLs of imports from Costa Rica between 2017 and 2020. Panama asserts that, since Costa Rica
exported hardly any strawberries to Panama up to 2020, there is no MRL compliance history for
strawberry imports prior to 2020. According to Panama, its history indicates that all samples of
strawberries from Costa Rica contained pesticides and 75% of them infringed the MRLs.675
7.258. Costa Rica asserts that Panama's compliance history cannot be considered to be a history of
repeated cases of non-conformity because it does not reveal cases of non-compliance prior to the
two shipments of February 2020 that triggered the adoption of Measure 1.676 Costa Rica argues that
the history submitted by Panama refers to (a) different imports of strawberries for the period 2016-
2019; (b) the infringements that triggered Measure 1; (c) the infringements notified to Costa Rica
on 1 March 2021, with respect to which Panama did not have any analysis results when Measure 1
was adopted; and (d) sample codes without any documentation or information that allows the
shipments corresponding to these samples to be identified.677
7.259. The Panel notes that the entries from 2016 to 2019 in the MRL compliance history submitted
by Panama correspond to products other than strawberries (specifically, mango, chayote, banana,
lettuce, and pumpkin).678 Panama fails to explain the relevance of these entries to the adoption of
Measure 1.

corresponds to the detections notified in March 2021.681 As for sample code LRT-092-2020, this also

- 107 -
corresponds to the detections notified in March 2021.682 The Panel recalls that the results of these
additional detections notified in March 2021 post-date the adoption of Measure 1 on
20 February 2020683, and, therefore, would not form part of the compliance history upon which
Panama could have based that measure.
7.261. In the light of the foregoing analysis, the Panel considers that Panama has failed to
demonstrate that it adopted Measure 1 on the basis of information indicating repeated
non-compliance, because Measure 1 was triggered by a single instance of non-compliance, which
was notified in February 2020. Therefore, the Panel considers that, by adopting a measure that
equates to a suspension of strawberry imports in a case that cannot be considered as "persistent",
Panama has not followed the guidance of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 regarding the management of
cases of non-conformity.
7.262. Moreover, the Panel recalls that Panama argues that this case is a serious or persistent case
because, in addition to the non-compliance in question, there was the "impossibility of verifying the
reliability of the controls carried out by the exporting country".684 In this respect, the Panel considers
that Panama has failed to demonstrate that it was impossible for it to obtain information from
Costa Rica with respect to its controls before Measure 1 was adopted. Accordingly, Panama has failed
to demonstrate that Measure 1 was adopted on the basis of this consideration.
7.263. Given that Measure 1 equates to a suspension of imports of strawberries from Costa Rica
without it having been demonstrated that there were "serious or persistent" cases of non-compliance
or that posed a "serious risk", the Panel concludes that Panama did not adopt Measure 1 on the
basis of Codex Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003.
7.264. The Panel will now examine whether Panama adopted Measure 1 on the basis of information
arising from measures implemented by other Members invoked by the parties.
Whether Measure 1 was adopted on the basis of information arising from the measures of
other Members
7.265. According to Costa Rica, the measures of the European Union, the United States, and
Australia685 support the provisions of the Codex Guidelines, namely that the measures adopted in
response to non-compliance with food safety requirements must be proportionate and incremental,
and that, as a general rule, they do not consist of a "prohibition on the importation" of a product
originating in a specific country.686 Costa Rica asserts that the appropriate measure would be to
change the manner in which the risk is managed through measures such as increasing the frequency
of border controls, requiring a shipment to be accompanied by analysis results confirming compliance
with the MRL, and requiring prior notification on the arrival of the shipment.687 Costa Rica notes that
greater controls allow imports to go ahead, provided the importer demonstrates that the shipment

7.266. Although Panama initially disagrees on the relevance of the measures identified by
Costa Rica, Panama subsequently refers to measures by other Members and asserts that there is no
uniform regulatory response in food safety cases. Panama argues that the pertinent regulations of
the European Union do not exclude the possibility of introducing a prohibition on importation for all
of the exporting country.689 Panama also refers to the practices of Chinese Taipei and the
United States. According to Panama, the importing country has broad discretion over deciding how
serious the violation is, and it is not uncommon for it to adopt a temporary prohibition on imports in
the event of repeated MRL infringements.690
7.267. The Panel will now analyse the measures of the European Union, the United States,
Australia, and Chinese Taipei that are in the record.
7.268. With respect to the European Union's measure, the Panel refers to Regulation No. 178/2002
of 2002 laying down the general principles and requirements of food law. Both parties invoke
Article 53, entitled "Emergency measures for food and feed of Community origin or imported from a
third country".691 This article establishes that "[w]here it is evident that food or feed ... is likely to
constitute a serious risk to human health, animal health or the environment, and that such risk
cannot be contained satisfactorily by means of measures taken by the Member State(s) concerned",
the European Commission shall immediately adopt one or more of the measures listed therein,
"depending on the gravity of the situation". In the case of food or feed from a third country, these
measures include: (a) suspension of imports of the food or feed in question from all or part of the
third country concerned; (b) laying down special conditions for the food or feed in question from all
or part of the third country concerned; and (c) any other appropriate interim measure.692 Article 54,
entitled "Other emergency measures", provides that Member States may adopt "interim protective
measures" where the Commission has not acted in accordance with Article 53. In another
European Union Regulation, it is explained that Articles 53 and 54 "shall apply where, as a result of
new information or of a reassessment of existing information, pesticide residues or MRLs covered ...
may endanger human or animal health requiring immediate action".693
7.269. The record also contains examples of the European Commission imposing special or specific
conditions on the import of certain foods from third countries and an increased level of official
controls pursuant to the aforementioned regulations.694 Regulation No. 2018/1660 imposed special
conditions on the import of vine leaves from Türkiye and pitahaya (dragon fruit) from Viet Nam. The
Regulation refers to a previous increased level of official controls on imports of these products.695 It
notes that, even after increasing the frequency of controls at European Union borders, there was a
"high frequency of non-compliance" with the respective MRLs for both products. In the case of
Türkiye, information was obtained from the official controls, notifications received from
member States, audit reports by the Commission, and reports received from third countries, among
other sources. In the case of Viet Nam, an audit was also carried out that found there was no official
effective pesticide control system for food exported to the European Union and that the authorities
could not ensure compliance of Vietnamese products with MRLs.696 For the European Union, this
provided evidence that the import of vine leaves from Türkiye and of pitahaya from Viet Nam could
constitute "a serious risk to health" that could not be "contained satisfactorily by the measures" in

identity and physical checks on consignments, including sampling and analysis, carried out by the
authorities at the point of entry700, and mentions the possibility of the product being rejected due to
non-compliance with an MRL.701
7.271. The Panel also notes that the European Union regulations reflect the practice of lifting special
conditions where, following their application, the competent authorities do not detect concerning
contaminants in the products in question. Regulation No. 669/2009 provides that, given that the
frequency of the notifications in relation to the presence of certain substances in specific products

were discontinued and, instead, said products were subject to an increased level of controls at the
point of entry into the European Union.702
7.272. With regard to the measure applied by the United States, the Regulatory Procedures Manual
of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) states that if it appears from the examination of a product
or otherwise that the product violates the requirements set out in United States legislation, it can
be refused admission into the country. The Manual recommends detention without physical
examination (DWPE) for future shipments when there exists a history of the importation of violative
products, or products that may appear violative, of United States legislation, or when other
information indicates that future entries of a product may violate that legislation.703 If one sample
of an imported product has been found violative and the violation represents a potentially significant
health hazard, this may support a recommendation for DWPE of products from a specific
manufacturer, shipper, grower, or from a specific growing area or country (if information is sufficient
to establish an appearance that the violation extends to that area/country).704 Where multiple
samples show violations, recommendations for DWPE of a product from a geographic area or country
should consider whether there have been at least 12 detentions in a recent six-month period or less,
whether these detentions represent at least 25% of the total shipments of that product examined in
that time-period, and whether these detentions represent a significant number of firms from the
geographic area or country.705
7.273. To remove a product, exporter, or importer from DWPE, an important element of the FDA's
decision is the extent to which the evidence shows that future shipments will be in compliance with
United States legislation. In the event that illegal pesticide residue has been found in fresh produce,
the DWPE may be removed following five consecutive shipments without violation and if adequate
documentation demonstrates that the residue problem no longer exists, that the shipment has not

7.274. The evidence in the record also indicates that sampling by the United States authorities at
the border targets commodities and countries that might warrant special attention based upon high
violation rates of safety regulations and certain trends.707
7.275. With regard to the measure applied by Australia, a screenshot of the web page of the
Australian Department of Agriculture, Water and the Environment explains that, under the
Imported Food Control Act 1992, "failing foods" are those that have failed analytical testing or
contain non-permitted additives or ingredients. It states that if the failing food is a surveillance food,
a holding order can be applied. This will refer all future consignments of that food for inspection. A
holding order remains in place until a history of compliance can be demonstrated, usually after five
consecutive passes. The screenshot also states that if the failing food is a "risk food", 100% of future
consignments will be referred for inspection until a compliance history is established.708
7.276. Panama also refers to the practice of Chinese Taipei, as described in a report of the Foreign
Agricultural Service of the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA).709 This report states
that, with respect to imported fresh fruit, non-compliance with this Member's pesticide standards
will result in the recall and rejection of the products. It indicates that inspection frequency of the
same commodity imported by the same importer will be elevated to 20-50% from the regular 2-5%
rate after one violation. Following two consecutive violations, inspections will be on a lot-by-lot basis.
If the same cause of violation continues to occur in the same commodity, a plan of improvement
may be required from the source exporter or country. If the plan of improvement is not satisfactory,
imports from that source may be temporarily suspended, which could be extended to the entire
territory of the supplying country.710
7.277. The Panel considers that the aforementioned measures applied by Members that are in the
record demonstrate that, when a shipment is determined to be inconsistent with the relevant MRLs
by means of laboratory tests or otherwise, other Members can reject that violative shipment. With
respect to the measures imposed on future shipments, the Panel notes that each country has its
own procedures for responding to cases of non-compliance with food safety requirements and, as
Panama asserts, the possibility of suspending imports of a product from the entire country of origin
in certain cases is not ruled out. However, the Panel considers that the evidence relating to the
measures taken by other Members supports Costa Rica's assertion that the practices of those
Members consist, initially, of changing the manner in which the risk is managed by imposing control
measures of gradually increasing intensity.
7.278. While European Union legislation provides for the possibility of suspending imports of a food
from all or part of the territory of the country in question, based on the examples in the record, it

does not seem that this is a measure that is imposed as a first response to an MRL infringement. In
the Panel's view, the examples in the record indicate that the European Union gradually increases
the intensity of its controls, up to requiring analysis and sampling at the point of origin and entry in
cases where a continuous high frequency of non-compliance is observed.711 The Panel finds the
explanation of this approach in one of the European Union Regulations, which states that an
increased level of control should allow the known or emerging risk to be countered, and, on the
other hand, the collection of accurate monitoring data on the occurrence and prevalence of
unfavourable results from laboratory analysis.712 Moreover, the Panel notes that for the European
authorities, another way of collecting relevant data is through audits at origin and requests for
information addressed to the country of origin.713
7.279. Likewise, in the case of Chinese Taipei, import controls are gradually intensified as violations
increase, before requiring a plan of improvement if non-compliance persists. Only if that plan is not
satisfactory may imports of the product from the same origin be suspended.714
7.280. With regards to the detention/holding orders of the United States (DWPE) and Australia, the
Panel notes that these practices allow the products of a producer, importer, area, or country to be
monitored, thus establishing a compliance history that builds trust in the conformity of future
shipments. The United States authorities, in particular, permit the importer to prove that the
conditions that gave rise to the violation have been overcome and that the problem has been
addressed in a manner that will prevent future non-compliance.715
7.281. Therefore, the Panel considers that, in cases of non-compliance, the Members mentioned
decide which measures to impose on future consignments based on the seriousness of the situation,
taking into account the risk posed by the contaminant, whether that risk could be controlled
satisfactorily with other measures, as well as the likelihood of persistent non-compliance. For these
Members, the compliance history, including the rate or frequency of non-compliance, appears to be
a deciding factor. Hence, the control measures of these Members seek to obtain data on this matter.
Evidence of the existence or absence of an effective official control system in the country of origin
or that is implemented by the producer in question also appears to be a relevant factor.
7.282. The Panel recalls that Measure 1 seeks to "disallow" imports of strawberries from Costa Rica
in response to the non-compliance with Panama's oxamyl MRLs in two shipments from the same
exporter from Costa Rica (Fresas Gego).716 As mentioned717, Panama does not dispute that this
measure was the first and only risk management measure that it adopted following the detection in
question. Panama did not adopt measures following the incremental approach outlined in the
examples of measures adopted by other Members following a first detection of non-conformity.
7.283. Accordingly, the Panel considers that Panama did not adopt Measure 1 on the basis of
pertinent information derived from measures applied by other Members that are in the record.
Information on Costa Rica's control system

and control system.718 In this respect, the parties disagree as to whether the detections of non-
conformity that Panama notified to Costa Rica on 20 February 2020 pointed to the existence of a
"systemic problem" with Costa Rica's controls.

a "generalized or systematic problem" that affected all Costa Rican strawberry producers, but rather
a risk limited to the Costa Rican producer involved.719 In this respect, Costa Rica asserts that non-
compliance with an MRL in food shipments means that the specific producer responsible for that

7.289. According to the data reported, oxamyl residues in samples of two shipments of strawberries
from the exporter Fresas Gego are 0.056 mg/kg and 0.022 mg/kg. This means that the level of
residues in these samples was 2.2 and 5.6 times higher than Panama's MRL for oxamyl in
strawberries, which was set at 0.01 mg/kg.
7.290. The Panel notes that the evidence in the record suggests that the European Union considers
both the frequency and level of non-compliance as proof that the importation of certain food poses
a risk.731 The Panel understands that, as Panama asserts732, the level of pesticide residue in the food
may be linked to consumer exposure to that pesticide. Accordingly, the Panel considers it reasonable
that, among other factors, the quantity of pesticide residue detected (or the degree to which it
exceeds the relevant MRL) might influence the level of risk posed by the product in question.
However, Panama has failed to explain how the quantities of oxamyl residue detected in the two
Costa Rican shipments in question would affect the relevant risk. Panama only argues that these
quantities show a "systemic problem" with Costa Rica's controls.
7.291. Moreover, the Panel stresses that the detection of oxamyl residues in quantities 2.2
and 5.6 times higher than the MRL corresponds solely to two strawberry shipments from the same
producer made at around the same time. The Panel does not consider that Panama has demonstrated
that its MRL being exceeded in the case in question is, by itself, proof of a systemic problem with
Costa Rica's controls or a serious situation justifying the suspension of all imports of Costa Rican
strawberries.
7.292. The Panel understands that, according to the Codex Guidelines, in cases of non-compliance,
a factor that must be taken into account in order to adopt measures proportionate to the degree of
risk is the reliability of the checks made by the exporting country.733
7.293. However, the Panel finds no evidence of a systemic problem with Costa Rica's controls in
Panama's Note announcing the adoption of Measure 1, or in the relevant laboratory analyses.734 The

checks and at the border in Costa Rica.735 Moreover, Panama does not provide any other details
from which it could be concluded that the non-compliance in question is indicative of a systemic
problem with the controls ensuring the safety of strawberries exported from Costa Rica.
7.294. With regard to the use of oxamyl in Costa Rica, Costa Rica asserts that oxamyl may not be
used in strawberry production within its territory because it is not on the list of pesticides authorized
for that purpose.736 Costa Rica asserts that the fact it does not authorize the use of oxamyl in
strawberry production within its territory demonstrates there is no generalized or systemic problem
with strawberry production in the country.737 Panama does not question this assertion, but responds
that the detection of oxamyl in shipments of Costa Rican strawberries cast doubt on whether the
prohibition on its use in Costa Rica is being implemented effectively, which, in Panama's view,
suggests a greater potential for adverse effects on human health arising from the consumption of
oxamyl in strawberries than is acceptable to Panama.738
7.295. As mentioned in paragraph 7.168 above, the record contains a certified list of pesticides
and related substances authorized in strawberry crops in Costa Rica, which does not include
oxamyl.739 Although this document post-dates the adoption of Measure 1 in February 2020, Panama
confirms that it was aware that Costa Rica did not authorize the use of oxamyl in strawberry
production at that time.740 Furthermore, the Manual on good agricultural and production practices
for strawberry crops, which covers management of the production, packing, and transport of
strawberries in Costa Rica, was available to Panama. Although Panama asserts that it did not possess
information about the implementation of this Manual741, Panama has not argued that any of
Costa Rica's GAP outlined therein742 were insufficient or problematic with regard to pesticide control
in strawberry production in Costa Rica.743
7.296. In relation to the existence or not of a generalized or systemic problem with Costa Rica's
control systems, the parties also disagree on the representativeness of the non-compliant shipments
in question. Panama asserts that Fresas Gego was the only exporter of Costa Rican strawberries to
Panama and that, therefore, its imports would correspond to the total volume of Costa Rican
imports.744 For Panama, this suggests that non-compliance by this producer would immediately
make the problem a systemic one.745 Costa Rica submits that there are four other Costa Rican

the same time, the Panel considers that the authorities of the exporting country are responsible for exercising

Conclusion on whether Panama adopted Measure 1 on the basis of available pertinent
information
7.299. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Measure 1, which consists of
"disallow[ing]" imports of strawberries from Costa Rica, bears no rational and objective relationship
to, and therefore was not adopted on the basis of, the following information: (a) Codex Guidelines
CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 with reference to cases of non-compliance with MRLs; (b) the
measures of the European Union, the United States, Australia, and Chinese Taipei in cases of
non-compliance with MRLs; (c) the fact that Measure 1 was triggered by the detection of
non-compliance in two shipments from the same producer (which is not the only Costa Rican
exporter of strawberries); and (d) the fact that Costa Rica does not authorize the use of oxamyl in
strawberry production in Costa Rica.
7.300. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Panama has not demonstrated that it adopted Measure 1
on the basis of available pertinent information within the meaning of Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement.
7.3.1.2.2.3 Conclusion on Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.301. In paragraphs 7.202 and 7.300 above, the Panel found that Panama has not demonstrated
that it adopted Measure 1 in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence, or on the basis of
available pertinent information, in breach of two of the requirements under Article 5.7. Considering
these findings and the fact that the four requirements of Article 5.7 are cumulative748, the Panel
does not consider it necessary to address the two requirements of Article 5.7 set out in its second
sentence, which concern the maintenance of the measure, in order to resolve this dispute.749
7.302. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 1 does not constitute a provisional
measure covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.

Panel will therefore proceed with the examination of Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1 and 5.2,
which concern the risk assessment and the elements to be taken into account in that assessment,
exporter of strawberries for February 2020 and that it had supplied a quantity equivalent to the total volume of

question No. 115, para. 41). See also Panama's comments on Costa Rica's response to Panel question No. 113,

Panama
7.308. Panama claims that it adopted provisional measures, which included Measure 1, as a
precaution and in response to scientific evidence that called into question its previous conclusions
concerning the sanitary risk posed by imports from Costa Rica of the products at issue.760 Panama
submits that the provisional measures are not inconsistent with Articles 2.2, 5.1, and 5.2 of the

provisional measures justified under Article 5.7 are exempt from the basic obligations set forth in
Article 2.2.761
7.3.1.2.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.309. Article 5.1 reads as follows:
Members shall ensure that their sanitary or phytosanitary measures are based on an
assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of the risks to human, animal or plant
life or health, taking into account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant
international organizations.
7.310. Pursuant to the text of Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement, the evaluation of a claim under
this provision has been understood to mean that a panel is required to examine:
a. whether there is a risk assessment762, as appropriate to the circumstances, taking into
account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant international organizations;
and
b. if there is a risk assessment, whether the SPS measure of the Member concerned is based
on that risk assessment.763
7.311. With regard to the risk assessment, it has been deemed that the review power of a panel is
to determine whether the risk assessment is supported by coherent reasoning and respectable
scientific evidence and is, in this sense, objectively justifiable.764 With regard to the examination of
whether the SPS measure is "based on" the risk assessment, this implies determining whether the
results of that assessment "sufficiently warrant" or "reasonably support" the SPS measure at
issue.765
7.312. Article 5.2, reproduced below, sets forth the elements that must be taken into account when
assessing risks:
In the assessment of risks, Members shall take into account available scientific
evidence; relevant processes and production methods; relevant inspection, sampling
and testing methods; prevalence of specific diseases or pests; existence of pest – or
disease – free areas; relevant ecological and environmental conditions; and quarantine
or other treatment.
760 Panama's first written submission, para. 166.
761 Panama's first written submission, paras. 166 and 435-436.
762 Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement defines a "risk assessment" as follows:
The evaluation of the likelihood of entry, establishment or spread of a pest or disease within the
territory of an importing Member according to the sanitary or phytosanitary measures which
might be applied, and of the associated potential biological and economic consequences; or the
evaluation of the potential for adverse effects on human or animal health arising from the
presence of additives, contaminants, toxins or disease-causing organisms in food, beverages or
feedstuffs.
763 Appellate Body Report, India – Agricultural Products, para. 5.8; and Panel Reports, US – Poultry
(China), para. 7.173; Costa Rica – Avocados (Mexico), para. 7.321.
764 Appellate Body Reports, US/Canada – Continued Suspension, para. 590.
765 Appellate Body Reports, US/Canada – Continued Suspension, para. 591 (referring to Appellate Body
Report, EC – Hormones, para. 193); Panel Report, US – Animals, para. 7.648.

WT/DS599/R
- 118 -
7.313. In previous disputes, a relationship has been found to exist between Articles 5.1 and 5.2 of
the SPS Agreement, in that Article 5.2 sheds light on the elements that are of relevance in the
assessment of risks foreseen in Article 5.1.766 Accordingly, previous panels have considered, in their
analysis under Article 5.1, whether Members considered the elements contained in Article 5.2.767
7.314. Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement reads as follows:
Members shall ensure that any sanitary or phytosanitary measure is applied only to the
extent necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health, is based on scientific
principles and is not maintained without sufficient scientific evidence, except as provided
for in paragraph 7 of Article 5.
7.315. Article 2.2 essentially focuses on the necessity of an SPS measure to be based on scientific
principles and sufficient scientific evidence.768 In previous disputes, the obligation that an
SPS measure not be maintained without sufficient scientific evidence has been understood to mean
that there must be a rational or objective relationship between the SPS measure and the scientific
evidence. Whether there is such a relationship is determined on a case-by-case basis and depends
upon the particular circumstances of the case, including the characteristics of the measure at issue
and the quality and quantity of the scientific evidence.769 As Articles 5.1 and 5.2 of the
SPS Agreement constitute a "specific application"770 of the obligations under Article 2.2, it has been
noted that a finding of a violation of Articles 5.1 and 5.2 may give rise to a rebuttable presumption
of inconsistency with Article 2.2.771
7.316. The Panel shares these assessments of the legal standard applicable under Articles 5.1, 5.2,
and 2.2 made in the aforementioned disputes, and will proceed with its evaluation on that basis.
The Panel now turns to Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2 with respect to
Measure 1.
Whether Measure 1 is based on a risk assessment in accordance with Article 5.1 of the
SPS Agreement
7.317. Panama has confirmed that Measure 1 is not based on a risk assessment, arguing that it is
a provisional measure adopted in circumstances where the body of available scientific evidence did
not allow an appropriate risk assessment to be conducted, as required under Article 5.1 of and as
defined in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.772
7.318. The Panel considers that, as claimed by Costa Rica, a measure cannot be based on a risk
assessment if no such assessment exists. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Measure 1 is inconsistent
with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to
the circumstances, of the risks to human health, taking into account risk assessment techniques
developed by the relevant international organizations.
Whether Panama took into account the elements listed in Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement
in the assessment of risk
7.319. As stated in paragraphs 7.312 and 7.313 above, Article 5.2 is linked to Article 5.1, in that it
sheds light on the elements that are of relevance in the assessment of risks foreseen in Article 5.1.773
Given that, with respect to Measure 1, Panama failed to perform a risk assessment in accordance
with Article 5.1, the Panel finds that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement

Article 5.1. (Panel Report, Australia – Apples, para. 7.211).

Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.627; and Costa Rica – Avocados (Mexico), para. 7.368.

because Panama did not take into account the elements set out under that Article in the assessment
of risks.
Whether Measure 1 is based on scientific principles and is not maintained without
sufficient scientific evidence in accordance with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.320. The Panel recalls that Article 5.1 constitutes a "specific application" of the basic obligations
under Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement.774 The Panel shares the view expressed in previous disputes
that a measure not based on a risk assessment pursuant to Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement can
be presumed not to be based on scientific principles or to be maintained without sufficient scientific
evidence within the meaning of Article 2.2 of the Agreement.775 The Panel considers that the same
can be presumed in this case, as it has found that, in the absence of a risk assessment, Measure 1
is inconsistent with Articles 5.1 and 5.2 of the SPS Agreement. The Panel further notes that Panama
has not presented arguments to rebut this presumption.
7.321. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 1 is not based on a risk assessment, in
breach of Articles 5.1 and 5.2 of the SPS Agreement, and in the absence of a rebuttal by Panama of
the presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2 of the Agreement, the Panel finds that Measure 1
is inconsistent with Article 2.2 because it is not based on scientific principles and is maintained
without sufficient scientific evidence.
7.3.1.2.3.3 Conclusion concerning Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.322. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 1 is inconsistent with the following
provisions:
a. Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement, because it is not based on an assessment, as
appropriate to the circumstances, of the risks to human health, taking into account risk
assessment techniques developed by the relevant international organizations;
b. Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement, because, in the absence of a risk assessment, Panama
did not take into account the elements listed in this provision in the assessment of risks;
and
c. Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, because it is not based on scientific principles and is
maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.

7.323. The Panel notes that the parties disagree on whether Articles 5.6 and 2.2 of the

Panel recalls that it has found that Measure 1 is not a provisional measure under Article 5.7777, and
therefore does not consider it necessary to rule on Panama's argument that Articles 5.6 and 2.2 do
not apply to provisional measures adopted pursuant to Article 5.7. Accordingly, the Panel will now
proceed with its analysis of Costa Rica's claims under these provisions.

7.330. Panama submits that Measure 1 is exempt from the obligations under Articles 2.2 and 5.6

7.3.1.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.3.1.3.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.332. Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement reads as follows:
Without prejudice to paragraph 2 of Article 3, when establishing or maintaining sanitary
or phytosanitary measures to achieve the appropriate level of sanitary or phytosanitary
protection, Members shall ensure that such measures are not more trade-restrictive
than required to achieve their appropriate level of sanitary or phytosanitary protection,
taking into account technical and economic feasibility.3
3 For purposes of paragraph 6 of Article 5, a measure is not more trade-restrictive than required
unless there is another measure, reasonably available taking into account technical and economic
feasibility, that achieves the appropriate level of sanitary or phytosanitary protection and is
significantly less restrictive to trade.

alternative measure that meets three requirements cumulatively796: (a) it is reasonably available

analysis under Article 5.6 therefore involves assessing an alternative measure proposed by the
complainant, which serves as a conceptual tool to be used in comparison with the measure at

7.334. Given that the three requirements in footnote 3 to Article 5.6 are cumulative, the order in
which the Panel assesses them would not have an impact on the final outcome of the assessment.800
Some previous panels have started by assessing whether the alternative measures achieved the
Member's ALOP, as this was the principal point of contention between the parties.801 Others have
followed the order in which the requirements appear in the text of the provision.802
7.335. The first requirement states that the alternative measure must be reasonably available
taking into account technical and economic feasibility. To meet this requirement, an alternative
measure must constitute "an option reasonably available taking into account technical and economic
feasibility in the real world", including "the risk of incorrect enforcement".803 In previous disputes,
consideration has been given to the fact that the respondent's existing use of an alternative measure
proposed by the complainant, even if in a different context, weighs in favour of a finding of
feasibility.804 Moreover, it has been found that an additional administrative burden imposed by an
alternative measure does not per se render the measure infeasible.805
7.336. The second requirement is that the alternative measure achieve the importing Member's
ALOP (i.e. the respondent's ALOP), which must be demonstrated by comparing the importing
Member's ALOP with the level of protection that would be achieved by the alternative measure.806
Therefore, in order to analyse a claim under Article 5.6, a panel would need to identify the level of
protection that the importing Member has set as its ALOP. In that connection, Members have the
"prerogative"807 and the obligation ("implicit[ly]"contained in the Agreement) to set its ALOP.808 In

7.337. The third requirement provides that the alternative measure must be significantly less
restrictive to trade than the challenged SPS measure. To meet this requirement, the complainant
must demonstrate, in the specific case, that the difference in the degree of trade restrictiveness can

meaningfully affect suppliers".812 Any condition upon importation has to be considered significantly
less restrictive than an "outright prohibition".813
7.338. For its part, Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement reads:
Members shall ensure that any sanitary or phytosanitary measure is applied only to the

required to achieve" the importing Member's ALOP, on the other hand.816 In this connection,
Article 5.6 has been considered to elaborate on the more general obligation under Article 2.2.817

7.341. The Panel will first assess whether Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 1 is

7.3.1.3.2.2 Whether Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
The alternative measures proposed by Costa Rica
7.342. Costa Rica states that, in cases of non-compliance with sanitary requirements, alternative
measures to Measure 1 consist of changing the manner in which the relevant risk is managed.819
Costa Rica proposes as alternative measures the type of measures described in paragraph 35 of
Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and in paragraph 20 of the appendix to Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003.820
Costa Rica adds that these measures are applied by other Members.821 For instance, Costa Rica lists
the following alternative measures: (a) control programmes implemented by the importer or
exporter; (b) increased intensity of checks on the product at issue or on a specific undertaking;
(c) request for information and cooperation from the country of origin (in this case, Costa Rica);
(d) on-site visits to the Costa Rican producer concerned; (e) food intended for import to be held
pending a final judgment; and (f) enhanced sampling and testing from the Costa Rican establishment
involved in the non-compliance.822
7.343. Although Panama considers that Measure 1 is exempt from the obligations under Article 5.6,
Panama also asserts that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate the feasibility of the alternative
measures.823
7.344. In determining whether Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.6, under the legal standard
set out above, the Panel will examine whether the alternative measures proposed by Costa Rica
meet the three requirements in footnote 3 to Article 5.6, namely that they: (a) are reasonably
available taking into account technical and economic feasibility; (b) achieve Panama's ALOP; and
(c) are significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 1.824
7.345. The Panel notes that Costa Rica proposes the individual or joint application of alternative
measures, stating that the type and number of alternative measures can be adjusted, at Panama's
discretion to apply only one or several of them cumulatively.825 The Panel considers that, in order to
demonstrate the inconsistency of Measure 1 with Article 5.6, Costa Rica must establish that at least
one of its proposed alternative measures meets the three requirements in footnote 3 to Article 5.6.

Whether the alternative measures are reasonably available
7.346. Costa Rica asserts that the alternative measures are reasonably available in terms of
technical and economic feasibility because the application of this type of measure falls within the
duties assigned to the Panamanian food safety authorities under the Panamanian regulatory
framework. In particular, Costa Rica refers to the duties of two operational directorates of AUPSA,
namely the National Food Import Verification Directorate and the National Imported Food Analysis
and Control Directorate.826 In Costa Rica's view, the fact that a measure is provided for in the
regulatory framework of an importing Member presupposes an internal decision on the feasibility of
that measure, and inevitably tips the balance in favour of finding the measure feasible.827 According
to Costa Rica, an alternative measure that is feasible in practice and does not impose prohibitive
costs or significant technical difficulties should be considered "reasonably available" within the
meaning of footnote 3 to Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.828
7.347. Panama asserts that, in terms of feasibility, the proposed alternative measures (a) are
already covered by Panama's request for cooperation from Costa Rica829; (b) were not feasible during
the COVID-19 pandemic830; or (c) do not apply because the product at issue is fresh fruit.831
7.348. As to whether Panama's legislation provides for alternative measures, as Costa Rica asserts,
the Panel notes that Decree Law No. 11 assigns, inter alia, the following duties to the National Food
Import Verification Directorate: (a) [v]erify compliance with the sanitary requirements that foods
must meet to be introduced into the national territory"; (b) "[c]arry out the actions required as a
result of sanitary and/or phytosanitary measures adopted by the National Standards for Food
Imports Directorate and/or the Scientific and Technical Food Safety Council" of AUPSA; (c) "[i]nspect
foods introduced into the national territory at air terminals, seaports, and land border posts";
(d) [i]nspect … any vehicle … that introduces foods into the national territory"; (e) "[i]mplement the
sanitary measures of retention, treatment, isolation"; and (f) "[i]mplement the sanitary measures
of rejection, return to the country of origin, re-export, seizure, destruction" of foods that represent
a danger.832
7.349. Pursuant to Decree Law No. 11, among the duties of the National Food Import Verification
Directorate are: (a) [t]ak[ing] samples and commission[ing] analyses of foods introduced into the
national territory", including to ensure compliance with sanitary standards and measures; and
(b) "[p]erform[ing] diagnoses, analyses, and technical tests required by other national directorates
of the Authority and/or Scientific and Technical Food Safety Council".833
7.350. In view of the foregoing, the AUPSA Directorates that are responsible for food import controls
to ensure food safety would have the authority to: conduct border inspections; take samples and
perform laboratory testing to ensure compliance with Panamanian sanitary standards and measures;
detain or treat products; and, if necessary, reject or destroy a shipment. Panama does not argue,
and nothing in the record indicates that, in principle, these Directorates could not perform these
duties. The Panel further notes that Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-006-2008, concerning the importation
of strawberries from Costa Rica into Panama, specifies that AUPSA will take samples for
entomological testing upon entry of the food into the country, and reserves the right to take other

7.352. As to whether such alternative measures are reasonably available to Panama, the Panel
notes that, based on the text of footnote 3 to Article 5.6, it follows that the technical and economic
feasibility of the alternative measure affects the determination of whether that measure is
reasonably available.836 In the Panel's view, measures provided for in Panama's regulatory
framework would, in principle, be feasible, given that the regulations themselves provide for such

measures mentioned in the preceding paragraph with a view to ensuring compliance with Panama's

Costa Rica that are provided for under the Panamanian regulatory framework are, in principle,
reasonably available to Panama.
7.353. Nevertheless, the Panel is mindful that there may be circumstances where measures that,
in principle, are available to a Member cannot be carried out. As noted in previous disputes, the
technical and economic feasibility of the alternative measures must be assessed in the context of

measures in question were not feasible under the circumstances surrounding the adoption of
Measure 1.

7.356. The Panel considers that the limitations on the operational capacity of Panamanian agencies

Panel notes that the relevant period for its analysis extends from the date on which Measure 1 was

without physical examination (DWPE)), referred to by Costa Rica as an example849, does apply to
fresh produce.850

period. The Panel recalls that Measure 1 was adopted on 20 February 2020, when flights were still
permitted and, therefore, on-site visits were a feasible alternative to Measure 1. Even if the
resumption of flights did not guarantee that on-site visits would take place immediately thereafter,
rendering the alternative measure unfeasible during that period858, the Panel notes that Panama has
not shown that this measure remained unfeasible as of the date of the Panel's establishment in
September 2021.
7.362. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that the alternative measures proposed by
Costa Rica were reasonably available to Panama. With respect to the alternative measures proposed
by Costa Rica, which consist of (a) increased intensity of checks on the product at issue or on a
specific undertaking; (b) on-site visits to the Costa Rican producer concerned; and (c) enhanced
sampling and testing of the Costa Rican establishment involved, the Panel considers that these
measures were reasonably available to Panama when Measure 1 was adopted. Furthermore,
although these measures were not reasonably available to Panama during certain periods of the
COVID-19 pandemic, the Panel considers that, as of the date the Panel was established, these
measures were again reasonably available to Panama within the meaning of footnote 3 of Article 5.6.
7.363. In relation to the alternative measures proposed by Costa Rica, which are (a) control
programmes implemented by the importer or exporter; (b) request for information and cooperation
from the country of origin (in this case, Costa Rica); and (c) food intended for import to be held
pending a final judgment, the Panel considers that these measures were reasonably available to
Panama throughout the period that Measure 1 was applied and maintained.
7.364. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that alternative
measures are reasonably available taking into account technical and economic feasibility, within the
meaning of footnote 3 to Article 5.6.
7.365. The Panel will now proceed to assess whether the alternative measures identified by
Costa Rica achieved Panama's ALOP.

- 129 -
Whether the alternative measures achieved Panama's ALOP
7.366. Under the legal standard described above859, the Panel will first identify Panama's ALOP, and
subsequently compare it to the level of protection that would be achieved through the alternative
measures proposed by Costa Rica.
Panama's ALOP
7.367. The Panel notes that Panama has submitted three different formulations of its ALOP with
respect to Measure 1. In its first written submission, in the context of Article 5.7, Panama expressed
its ALOP with respect to Measure 1 as an "ALOP with the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on
human health".860 After the first substantive meeting, Panama stated that, in the area of plant
health, its ALOP is to "prevent and control, in a comprehensive manner" and, therefore, to totally
avoid "the introduction, establishment, and spread of pests of plants and plant products in the
territory of the Republic of Panama".861 Lastly, following the second substantive meeting, Panama
clarified that its ALOP in the case of Measure 1 is "to minimize the risks of toxic residues in plants
and the environment", as, in its view, is set out in Executive Decree No. 63 of 1997.862
7.368. Costa Rica stated at the outset that Panama's ALOP should address human health and not
plant health863, and subsequently added that, even after Panama's last clarification, Panama's
definition of its ALOP remains ambiguous. Costa Rica emphasizes that, although Panama
acknowledges that Measure 1 refers to sanitary risks, Panama states that its ALOP is "to minimize
the risks of toxic residues in plants and the environment".864
7.369. The Panel notes that the ALOP could be understood as an "objective" that the SPS measure
seeks to attain865, and recalls that the objective of Measure 1 is to protect human life and health
within Panamanian territory from risks arising from contaminants (oxamyl) in foods
(strawberries).866 The Panel also notes that the definition of ALOP given in Annex A(5) of the
SPS Agreement states that the ALOP must reflect the "level of protection" deemed appropriate by
the Member to achieve its protection objectives (in this case, human life and health). Bearing in
mind that the ALOP is also defined as the "acceptable level of risk"867, Panama's ALOP relevant to
Measure 1 should reflect the level of risk acceptable to Panama with respect to the potential for
adverse effects on human health arising from oxamyl in Costa Rican strawberries.868
7.370. The Panel is aware that general government policy statements on ALOPs in response to
certain risks and documents listing the health protection objectives may indicate the Member's
ALOP.869 In this sense, the Panel considers that the objectives expressed in Executive Decree No. 63
of 1997 could inform Panama's ALOP.
7.371. The preamble of Executive Decree No. 63 of 1997, to which Panama refers, includes the
objective "to have good quality pesticides and fertilizers, as well as additives and technical materials,
which must be used safely and effectively to help minimize the risks of toxic residues in plants and
the environment, safeguarding human health".870 The Panel understands that this preamble

- 130 -
establishes a general policy objective for Panama applicable to the use of pesticides, which is to
minimize the risks of toxic residues, and thus safeguard human health.871
7.372. The Panel recalls that the ALOP should be expressed in a sufficiently clear manner so as to
permit examination of the extent to which any SPS measure achieves that ALOP.872 The use of the
verb "minimize" in Executive Decree No. 63 of 1997 indicates that Panama seeks a very high level
of protection, reducing the relevant risk to a minimum. However, the Panel considers that the Decree
does not clearly establish the extent to which the relevant risks must be minimized in order to
safeguard human health, i.e. the acceptable level of risk.
7.373. In order to obtain further clarity on Panama's ALOP, the Panel will also address the
arguments of the parties and Panama's MRL for oxamyl for strawberries.
7.374. In these proceedings, Panama states that its ALOP with respect to Measure 1 is an "ALOP
with the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on human health".873 Panama further notes that it
applied an MRL for oxamyl in strawberries of 0.01 mg/kg which "corresponds to the lowest possible
residue levels that can be detected using current analytical quantification techniques".874 Panama
adds that the application of the MRL of 0.01 mg/kg for oxamyl in strawberries satisfied "Panama's
ALOP of the lowest possible risk of contamination" because it corresponded to the lower limit of
analytical quantification. Panama argues that this MRL operated in conjunction with the fact that
oxamyl is not generally approved for use in strawberries by Panama's trading partners.875
7.375. The Panel considers that Panama's MRL for oxamyl for strawberries is relevant for the
purpose of identifying Panama's ALOP, since MRLs represent a reference value of the highest
acceptable level of a pesticide residue in a product from a crop to which that pesticide can be
applied.876 In the Panel's view, MRLs indicate the importing Member's tolerance level for adverse
effects arising from the presence of residues of a pesticide in imported food. The Panel considers
that, as noted by Panama877, the MRL may be the instrument by which Members seek to address a
health risk. Although the ALOP normally precedes the establishment of the measure, and the
measure will not always achieve the ALOP878, the Panel notes that, in this case, Panama considers
that the implementation of its MRL satisfied its ALOP.879
7.376. Therefore, in the Panel's view, Panama's MRL of 0.01 mg/kg for oxamyl in strawberries could
be used to indicate the amount of oxamyl residues in Costa Rican strawberries that, for Panama,
constitutes an unacceptable risk to human health within its territory. The Panel notes that Panama
has set the most restrictive MRL possible considering the detection techniques available at that time,
given that this MRL corresponds to the minimum detection threshold.880 In this connection, any
amount of oxamyl detected in Costa Rican strawberries would be inconsistent with Panama's MRL
for this pesticide. In the Panel's view, Panama's ALOP as reflected in its restrictive MRL corresponding

to the minimum detection threshold881 is one that Panama defines as an "ALOP with the lowest
possible risk of adverse effects on human health".882
7.377. As mentioned above, Panama argues that its MRL operated in conjunction with the fact that
oxamyl is not generally approved for use in strawberries by Panama's trading partners.883 In the
Panel's view, even if Panama considered that its ALOP had been achieved because oxamyl was not
approved for use in strawberries in Costa Rica, there is no evidence in the record that the status of
that approval had changed. Panama confirms that in February 2020 it was aware that Costa Rica
did not authorize the use of oxamyl in strawberry production884, and has not demonstrated the
existence of a systemic problem in Costa Rica's controls.885
7.378. Panama confirms that its ALOP has not changed following the adoption of Measure 1886,
which is intended to respond to the finding of non-compliance in two shipments of Costa Rican
strawberries. Accordingly, and considering that there is nothing in the record to suggest that the
fact that oxamyl is not authorized in Costa Rica for use in strawberries had changed, the alternative
measure to Measure 1 should achieve the same ALOP as the MRL for oxamyl in strawberries. Such
an alternative measure should therefore ensure, to the extent possible, that strawberries containing
residues of oxamyl at levels greater than 0.01mg/kg are not imported, given that the ALOP would
be the ALOP with the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on human health.
7.379. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Panama's relevant ALOP with respect
to imports of strawberries from Costa Rica could be formulated as the highest possible level of
protection, which ensures the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on human health arising from
the presence of oxamyl residues in Costa Rican strawberries.
The level of protection that the alternative measures achieve
7.380. The Panel will now proceed to examine whether the alternative measures achieve Panama's
ALOP as identified in paragraph 7.389 below. To that end, the Panel will compare Panama's ALOP
with the level of protection that would be achieved by the alternative measures.
7.381. Costa Rica submits that, regardless of how high or low Panama's ALOP may be, the various
alternative measures can achieve it, as the type and number of measures can be adjusted in
accordance with this ALOP.887 Costa Rica further argues that Panama's level of protection relates to
the protection of human health through imported food controls to ensure compliance with the MRL
for oxamyl in strawberries, which, in its view, is consistent with the objective of Guidelines
CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003.888
7.382. Panama responds that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 establish that the choice of measure(s)
to be applied where an imported product is found not to be in conformity with sanitary requirements
depends on three criteria: (a) repeated non-conformity in the same product or in the same category
of products; (b) history of non-conformity of those responsible for handling the products; and
(c) reliability of checks made by the country of origin. Panama asserts that it had no choice but to
provisionally suspend imports to achieve its ALOP because it was faced with repeated non-conformity
with the relevant MRL for an extremely hazardous substance by a major exporter of strawberries
from Costa Rica, and because it could not verify the effectiveness of Costa Rica's export control
system. Panama adds that paragraph 15 of the appendix to Guidelines CAC/GL 47-2003 justifies the
application of a provisional import prohibition "until it is confirmed that corrective measures have
been introduced and are being implemented effectively". Panama also reiterates that the alternative

measures proposed by Costa Rica could not achieve Panama's ALOP at the time of the onset of
COVID-19.889
7.383. The Panel notes that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 "are intended to assist countries in the
application of requirements and the determination of equivalency, thereby protecting consumers
and facilitating trade in foodstuffs".890 Application of the Guidelines "should help build and maintain
the necessary confidence in the inspection and certification system of an exporting country … taking
account of the expectations of consumers for an appropriate level of protection".891 Guidelines
CAC/GL 47-2003 complement Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and "provide[] a framework for the
development and operation of an import control system to", inter alia, "protect consumers".892
7.384. Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 do not establish a specific ALOP that can
be compared to Panama's ALOP. However, as stated by Costa Rica, the objective of these Guidelines
coincides with Panama's objective of protecting human health. The Guidelines in question seek to
assist Panama in achieving its ALOP by providing a framework so that Panama can establish an
inspection system that ensures the protection of human health from risks associated with imported
food, taking into account its ALOP. This inspection system would ensure the compliance of imported
food with Panama's food safety requirements.
7.385. The measures that Costa Rica suggests as alternatives to Measure 1 are contained in
paragraph 35 of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and in paragraph 20 of the appendix to Guidelines
CAC/GL 47-2003. The Panel recalls that these Guidelines indicate that the measures recommended
in respect of future imports in situations where non-compliance with food safety requirements is
detected include: control programmes implemented by the importer or exporter to ensure problems
do not re-occur; increased intensity of checks on categories of products identified as being not in
conformity and/or the undertakings concerned; request for information and cooperation on the
product or the category of products found not to be in conformity by the responsible authorities in
the country of origin (increased checks at origin); on-site visits893; enhanced sampling and testing
from the establishment involved; and food being held pending final judgment.894 The suspension of
imports would be considered only in the "most serious or persistent cases"895 or in situations
involving a "serious food safety risk".896
7.386. The Panel considers that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 support Costa Rica's argument that
the type and number of measures applied may be adjusted in accordance with Panama's specific
ALOP897 by providing that the measures applied may be "cumulative if necessary" and "may include"
those listed in paragraph 35 of those Guidelines.898
7.387. Panama claims that, in order to achieve its ALOP with respect to imports of Costa Rican
strawberries in the circumstances of this dispute, it had no choice but to resort to the import
suspension measure provided for in the Guidelines.899 Panama maintains that the "repeated

instances of non-conformity suggested a systematic problem with the controls operated by
Costa Rica", meaning that the preventive measures would not achieve Panama's ALOP.900
7.388. These arguments suggest that Panama considered the case of non-conformity targeted by
Measure 1 to be serious and recurrent, so that only the suspension of imports would achieve
Panama's ALOP. The Panel considers that the level of risk or, in this case, the seriousness and
recurrence of non-compliance, may be relevant to the issue of whether a measure achieves a
Member's ALOP.901 In this connection, the Panel recalls that Panama has not demonstrated that the
finding of non-compliance with the MRL for oxamyl in two shipments of Costa Rican strawberries is
a case that qualifies as serious and recurring or as a serious risk within the meaning of Guidelines
CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003.902 The Panel therefore considers that Panama has not
demonstrated that it was necessary to resort to the suspension of imports in order to achieve its
ALOP.
7.389. The Panel also considers that the evidence in the record confirms that at least some of the
measures proposed by Costa Rica achieve Panama's ALOP. The Panel notes that, when notifying
Measure 1 to Costa Rica on 20 February 2020, Panama states that "imports of strawberries from
Costa Rica to the Republic of Panama [are] temporarily disallowed until the health authority of the
sister country indicates the corrective measures implemented".903 Panama confirms that the control
programmes implemented by the exporting country and the request for information and cooperation
from the competent authorities of the country of origin were part of the "corrective measures" that
Panama requested from Costa Rica.904 In the Panel's view, by indicating in its communication of
20 February 2020 that imports of Costa Rican strawberries were disallowed until Costa Rica took
corrective measures, Panama would appear to consider that such corrective measures might be
sufficient to achieve its relevant ALOP. Accordingly, the alternative measures, consisting of the
control programmes implemented by the exporting country and the request for information and
cooperation from the competent authorities of the country of origin, would achieve Panama's
relevant ALOP.
7.390. With respect to increased intensity of checks, including sampling and testing, the Panel notes
that the detection of oxamyl which led to Measure 1 occurred as a result of the checks performed
by the Panamanian authorities. Therefore, in the Panel's view, a measure that consisted of increasing
the intensity of checks would allow Panama to detect non-compliance with the oxamyl MRL, ensuring,
to the extent possible, that strawberries containing oxamyl residues at levels above the MRL of
0.01 mg/kg were not being imported. Even if Panama harboured concerns with respect to
Costa Rica's sanitary controls, this alternative measure would allow it to ensure that its consumers
are exposed to the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on human health arising from the presence
of oxamyl residues in Costa Rican strawberries, thus achieving its ALOP. The Panel recalls that
Panama had considered the possibility of failures occurring in the monitoring of compliance with
GAPs and GMPs during production, harvesting, and packaging of Costa Rican strawberries.905 In this
connection, the Panel also notes that the Resolution governing imports of strawberries from
Costa Rica prior to the adoption of Measure 1 states that AUPSA reserves the right to take additional
samples, upon entry of the food into the country or at any point in the food chain, for analysis,
including for toxic residues.906
7.391. Similarly, should Panama consider it necessary, in addition to increasing the intensity of
checks, Panama could cumulatively apply other alternatives to achieve its ALOP as suggested by
Costa Rica, such as control programmes and cooperation with Costa Rica.

7.392. With respect to Panama's argument that the alternative measures could not achieve its ALOP
at the time of the onset of COVID-19907, the Panel does not consider that these arguments are
different from those made by Panama in relation to the issue of whether alternative measures are
reasonably available. The Panel therefore refers to its explanation under the first requirement of
footnote 3 to Article 5.6 above.908
7.393. As indicated by Costa Rica, the level of protection to be achieved by alternative measures
may vary depending on the type and number of measures applied. However, in the light of the
foregoing, the measures described in the Codex Guidelines in cases of non-compliance with sanitary
requirements would help ensure, to the extent possible, that products that exceed Panama's MRL of
0.01 mg/kg for oxamyl in strawberries are not imported. Accordingly, in the Panel's view, the
measures suggested by Costa Rica on the basis of those Guidelines would enable Panama to achieve
the highest level of protection possible, which ensures the lowest possible risk of adverse effects on
human health arising from the presence of oxamyl residues in Costa Rican strawberries.
7.394. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the measures
included in the provisions of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 cited by Costa Rica,
applied individually or collectively, would achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.395. The Panel will now proceed to examine the third requirement of footnote 3 to Article 5.6,
which is to determine whether the alternative measures identified by Costa Rica are significantly
less restrictive to trade than Measure 1.
Whether the alternative measures are significantly less restrictive to trade
7.396. Costa Rica asserts that the alternative measures it proposes are significantly less restrictive
to trade than Measure 1, given that "an alternative measure that allows imports, albeit with
conditions, 'would be significantly less restrictive to trade than a[] … prohibition'".909 Panama has
not responded to Costa Rica's argument.
7.397. Measure 1 consists of "disallow[ing]" imports of strawberries from Costa Rica into
Panama910, which means that Costa Rican strawberries may not be imported into Panama. As such,
that measure represents the highest possible degree of restrictiveness.911 As the Panel noted above,
in its analysis of Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 and CAC/GL 47-2003 in the context of Article 5.7, the
suspension of imports can be understood as a measure of last resort912, which would indicate that it
would be the most restrictive measure that can be applied to imports in the event of non-compliance
with safety requirements.
7.398. The Panel recalls that, for alternative measures to meet this requirement under footnote 3
to Article 5.6, the difference in the degree of restrictiveness must be significant.913 In previous
disputes, an alternative measure that imposes import conditions but allows trade has been
considered to be significantly less restrictive to trade than a prohibition.914 The Panel concurs with
this statement and will proceed to consider the restrictiveness of the alternative measures proposed
by Costa Rica to determine whether they are "significantly" less restrictive than Measure 1.
7.399. The alternative measures that Costa Rica proposes may have different levels of trade
restrictiveness depending on the number and type of measures adopted. The difference, therefore,
between the restrictiveness of Measure 1 and that of the alternative measures may vary. However,

unlike Measure 1, all of the alternative measures permit trade. In the Panel's view, for exporters,
there would be a significant difference between being unable to export, on the one hand, and being
able to export, on the other hand, even if exports are subject to measures such as control
programmes or enhanced inspections and sampling to verify compliance with the relevant sanitary
requirements. The Panel considers that this would be the case, even if a specific consignment that
fails to comply with the relevant sanitary requirements may be held and, if necessary, rejected or
destroyed. The Panel further recalls that Guidelines CAC/GL 26-1997 "are intended to assist
countries in the application of requirements and the determination of equivalency, thereby protecting
consumers and facilitating trade in foodstuffs".915
7.400. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the alternative
measures are significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 1.
7.3.1.3.2.3 Conclusion on Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
7.401. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the
alternative measures to Measure 1 that it proposes meet the three cumulative requirements of
footnote 3 to Article 5.6, given that they are reasonably available taking into account technical and
economic feasibility, they achieve Panama's ALOP, and they are significantly less restrictive to trade.
7.402. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 1 is inconsistent
with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement because it is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve
Panama's ALOP.
7.3.1.3.2.4 Whether Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.403. The Panel shares the observation made in previous disputes that a finding that a measure
is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve the Member's ALOP, in breach of Article 5.6, leads
to a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with the obligation under Article 2.2 that SPS measures
should be applied only to the extent necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health.916
The Panel considers that this rebuttable presumption can be made in this case because of its finding
that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.917 Panama has not presented
arguments to rebut this presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2.
7.404. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the
SPS Agreement, and in the absence of a rebuttal by Panama of the presumption of inconsistency
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 because it is not applied only to the extent necessary to
protect human health.
7.3.1.3.2.5 Conclusion on Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.405. In the light of the above, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 1
is inconsistent with Article 2.2 because it is not applied only to the extent necessary to protect
human health.
