to find that Panama acted inconsistently with Article 7 as a consequence of the inconsistency with

7.421. The Panel recalls that, in its preliminary ruling of 5 April 2023, it found that Costa Rica's

of reference.941 Therefore, its assessment in this section will address only the claims that Costa Rica
included in its panel request, namely, the claims under Article 7 and Annex B(1).
7.422. Article 7 of the SPS Agreement reads as follows:
Members shall notify changes in their sanitary or phytosanitary measures and shall
provide information on their sanitary or phytosanitary measures in accordance with the
provisions of Annex B.

of Annex B" in Article 7 qualifies both of the preceding obligations, namely, to notify changes in

demonstrate that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 7 and Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement with
respect to the publication obligations established thereunder.
7.3.1.5.3 Conclusion on Article 7 and Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement
7.425. In view of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that
Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 7 and Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement.
7.3.1.6 Claims under Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement
7.3.1.6.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.426. Costa Rica claims that Measure 1 is inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the
SPS Agreement because the measure is inconsistent with other provisions of the SPS Agreement. In

requests the Panel to find that Panama has, consequently, acted inconsistently with its obligations

not demonstrated that Measure 1 is inconsistent with the provisions of the SPS Agreement.946

7.428. Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement provides as follows:
This Agreement applies to all sanitary and phytosanitary measures which may, directly

in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.

Members have the right to take sanitary and phytosanitary measures necessary for the
protection of human, animal or plant life or health, provided that such measures are
not inconsistent with the provisions of this Agreement.

7.456. The Panel will begin its assessment by examining whether Costa Rica has proved that
Panama did indeed revoke Costa Rica's sanitary/animal health eligibility for all the products at issue,
given that it is this aspect of the measure on which the parties disagree1021, before continuing with
the aspect concerning the renewal requests for the sanitary approvals in question.
7.4.1.2.1 Whether Panama maintains an import prohibition on the products at issue by
virtue of the revocation of Costa Rica's sanitary/animal health eligibility
7.457. In its assessment of this issue, the Panel will examine the evidence in the record, which
includes the relevant Panamanian regulatory framework, the submissions of the Panamanian and
Costa Rican sanitary authorities on the enforcement of these regulations, and the commitments
made at the Paso Canoas meeting.
7.4.1.2.1.1 Relevant Panamanian regulatory framework
7.458. As provided for in the relevant regulatory framework, two types of cumulative eligibility are
needed to import into Panama products of animal origin that pose both sanitary risks (to human
health) and animal health risks (for example, through the transmission of an animal disease).1022
On the one hand, the party interested in exporting products of animal origin for human and/or animal
consumption to Panama must ascertain whether the product of animal origin it wishes to introduce
into Panama is subject to import requirements issued by AUPSA. If there are import requirements,
the interested party must ascertain whether the sanitary eligibility of countries, regions, zones and
districts and/or the approval of the establishment, production chain and/or processing plant is/are
required.1023 On the other hand, the importer must also obtain an animal health import licence,
which recognizes the animal health eligibility of the country, area or region and the establishment
in question.1024

7.459. Regarding the products of animal origin at issue, Panama has specific sanitary import
requirements. These requirements for certain meat and dairy products are in the record.1025 The
resolutions containing these requirements are not the same for all the products. However, they all
allude to country-level sanitary eligibility and the sanitary approval of establishments to export food
products to Panama. One of the recitals common to these resolutions notes AUPSA's competence to
"approve the sanitary and phytosanitary eligibility of countries, regions, zones, production chains
and/or plants to export foodstuffs" to Panama.1026 These resolutions also include the requirements
that: (a) the country of origin be "recognized as eligible by [AUPSA]" to export the products in
question1027; and (b) the products be worked or processed in an establishment authorized for export
purposes by the exporting country and approved by AUPSA.1028 For some products, authorization for
the introduction of foodstuffs depends on the status of bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE) in
the country, zone or district.1029 Although these resolutions do not refer to obtaining country-level
animal health eligibility or to an establishment's animal health licence, the Panel understands that
these requirements are applicable for importing the products at issue into Panama.1030
7.460. The Panel notes that, pursuant to the aforementioned regulations, in order to export to
Panama the products at issue posing both sanitary and animal health risks, Costa Rica's sanitary
and animal health eligibility must be recognized and all the Costa Rican establishments must have
sanitary and animal health approval for the products at issue. As mentioned previously, the main
point of contention between the parties is whether Panama revoked Costa Rica's sanitary/animal
health eligibility for all the products at issue when processing the renewal requests for the sanitary
approvals of the 18 Costa Rican establishments in question.

7.461. Regarding sanitary and animal health eligibility1031, the relevant regulations are set out in
the following instruments and provisions:
a. Article 6(5) of Law No. 23 of 15 July 1997 "approving the Marrakesh Agreement
Establishing the World Trade Organization; Panama's Protocol of Accession to that
Agreement, together with its Annexes and Schedules of Commitments; adapting domestic
legislation to international norms and introducing other provisions", which appointed the
National Animal Health Directorate (DINASA) of the Ministry of Agricultural Development
(MIDA) as the competent authority to "[a]pprove the animal health eligibility of zones,
countries and regions that export by-products to Panama".1032
b. Article 62 of Law No. 23 of 15 July 1997, pursuant to which MIDA "approves regions,
countries, zones and/or processing plants or other facilities related to livestock production
located therein, as eligible, from an animal health standpoint, to export the goods they
produce to Panama".1033

7.462. Regarding the relationship between sanitary eligibility and animal health eligibility, the
preliminary questionnaire to determine a country's animal health eligibility that Panama sent to
Costa Rica in February 2021, after the Paso Canoas meeting, contains the following clarification in
one of its introductory paragraphs:
If the country interested in exporting animals and products and by-products of animal
origin to Panama is declared eligible in terms of animal health, on account of it
complying with the level of protection required by Panama's sanitary laws and
regulations, only then will it be possible to continue with the process of determining
equivalence in respect of the safety of foodstuffs for human consumption, which falls
within the competence of the Food Safety Department of the Ministry of Health (MINSA)
of Panama and the Panamanian Food Safety Authority (AUPSA).1042
7.463. The Panel understands that, in accordance with the aforementioned regulations and the
introductory paragraph to the questionnaire, in order to import certain products into Panama,
including the dairy and meat products at issue that pose sanitary and animal health risks, the
exporting country's animal health eligibility and sanitary eligibility must have been approved for the
product concerned.
7.464. The AUPSA Manual on sanitary eligibility establishes the procedures to be followed by
countries to obtain sanitary eligibility1043, by product or animal species, in order to export their
products to Panama.1044 The Manual notes that the party interested in exporting products to Panama
may proceed to the establishment approval1045 stage if the country, region, zone or district is
authorized; that is, if it has sanitary eligibility.1046 If sanitary eligibility has not been registered,
therein are described the steps to be taken for the Panamanian authorities to perform a risk analysis
and decide whether to grant eligibility. Eligibility is declared through a resolution by the Scientific
and Technical Food Safety Council, which is approved by AUPSA and subsequently published and
included in the database on the AUPSA website.1047
7.465. Pursuant to Decree-Law No. 11 of 2006 and in relation to

human consumption to Panama; and (b) update to the Annex to Decree-Law No. 11 of 20061049
granting sanitary eligibility to Costa Rica to export some of the products at issue to Panama.1050

7.466. Although Costa Rica claims that Panama revoked its sanitary eligibility, there is no evidence
in the record that the instruments through which Panama granted sanitary eligibility to Costa Rica
to export the products at issue to Panama have been formally modified or revoked. The Panel notes
that, according to Panama, the aforementioned instruments remain valid and have not been
amended expressly to revoke Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility.1051
7.467. The Panel also notes that Panama's regulations provide that AUPSA may only suspend or
revoke a country's sanitary eligibility when: (a) there is evidence of substantial changes in its
sanitary status, in accordance with the notification mechanisms established by international
regulations; or (b) there is scientific or technical evidence conclusively demonstrating
non-compliance with Panama's sanitary regulations.1052
7.468. The Panel finds nothing in the record showing that Panama notified Costa Rica that the cases
provided for in Panama's regulations for the suspension or revocation of sanitary eligibility had
arisen. Neither party states that Costa Rica's sanitary status has changed substantially, or that there
is evidence demonstrating a failure to comply with Panama's sanitary regulations for the dairy and
meat products at issue. In the Panel's view, the foregoing supports the conclusion that Panama has
not revoked Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility.
7.469. In respect of animal health eligibility, the Panel notes that the parties have not presented
such detailed evidence as that concerning sanitary eligibility. Therefore, the record does not indicate
when animal health eligibility was granted to Costa Rica for the products at issue or the Costa Rican
establishments in question. However, it seems clear from the aforementioned regulations that, in
order to import the products at issue into Panama, Costa Rica and its establishments had to have
that eligibility. Therefore, it could be assumed that Panama granted animal health eligibility for these
products at least during the period when these establishments were able to export the products at
issue to Panama. In line with what the Panel has noted so far in respect of sanitary eligibility, there
is nothing in the record to indicate that Panama revoked this animal health eligibility.1053
7.470. In addition to the regulatory framework for sanitary and animal health eligibility, the Panel
also finds elements relevant to its analysis in the regulatory framework for the sanitary approval of
establishments. In particular, the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments sets out the
procedure to be followed by establishments, production chains and/or processing plants to obtain
approval to export their products of animal origin for human and/or animal consumption to Panama
and, if applicable, subsequent renewals of sanitary approvals.1054
(2009) (Exhibit PAN-1)). The record does not contain evidence of the granting of sanitary eligibility to
Costa Rica for the following products mentioned by Costa Rica in its first written submission: meat products
(sausages) of poultry; aquatic animal (fish) food; and pet (dog) food containing protein of ruminant origin.
(Costa Rica's first written submission, para. 41).
However, as noted below, the record contains evidence that the sanitary approval of Biomar and
Central Veterinaria was effective until the date of the establishment of the Panel for shrimp and fish food, and
pet food, respectively. (AUPSA, Approval of processing plants: Costa Rica (July 2022) (Exhibit CRI-217)).
Similarly, the sanitary approvals of at least three establishments exporting meat products of poultry were
effective until 30 June 2020 (see Cinta Azul Pipasa No. 5, Planta proceso posterior (Pipasa) No. 30E and
Sigma Alimentos No. 7 in AUPSA, Approval of processing plants: Costa Rica (July 2022) (Exhibit CRI-217)). It
follows that Panama recognized Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility for these products.

7.474. As provided for in the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments, once the

AUPSA may request relevant information from the competent authority of the exporting country on

establishments for the products in question was based on this provision.1061 Costa Rica disagrees

7.476. The Panel notes that, as indicated by Panama, the AUPSA Manual for the approval of
establishments provides that, in the process of renewing sanitary approvals, AUPSA may request
information on "the sanitary status … of the exporting country", as well as information on the sanitary
status of the establishment; the characteristics of the products it would like to continue to introduce
into the country; and the experience with, knowledge of and confidence in the food inspection and
certification systems of the exporting country and the establishment, production chain or processing
plant in question.1063 In the Panel's view, the scope of Panama's requests for information from
Costa Rica in the context of the renewal requests does not, in itself, determine whether Panama
revoked Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility for the products in question, given that Panama could
request excessive information as part of the renewal process for sanitary approvals without this
leading to the revocation of sanitary eligibility.

Prior to sending this communication, AUPSA had made no similar mention in the communications in
the record of the requirements for renewing the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican
establishments.1074
7.481. About one month later, on 21 August 2020, AUPSA sent SENASA a communication referring
to the renewal requests for the sanitary approvals of a number of Costa Rican establishments
interested in exporting dairy and meat products to Panama. AUPSA informed SENASA that "pursuant
to the procedure established in Panama's sanitary legislation to process these requests recognition
of Costa Rica's animal health eligibility is required beforehand[, for] which reason it is necessary to
initiate the animal health evaluation process in order to determine its eligibility to export animals
and products and by-products of animal origin to Panama".1075
7.482. The Panel notes that, in its communication of 21 August 2020, AUPSA refers for the first
time to the necessity of granting Costa Rica recognition of its animal health eligibility before
concluding the renewal of the sanitary approvals. As mentioned above, Panama submits that animal
health eligibility is different from sanitary eligibility.1076 The Panel considers that Panama's assertion
that sanitary eligibility and animal health eligibility are different concepts finds support in the
regulations set out in paragraph 7.461 above. As Panama notes, the granting of animal health
eligibility is governed by Executive Decree No. 49 of 16 April 2001, and both this Decree and Law
No. 23 of 15 July 1997 authorize MIDA to approve the animal health eligibility of a country that is
going to export to Panama. Sanitary eligibility, for its part, is granted pursuant to Resolution AUPSA-
DINAN-092-2007, Decree-Law No. 11 of 2006, and the AUPSA Manual on sanitary eligibility, which
authorize AUPSA to grant sanitary eligibility.1077 The Panel considers that the foregoing appears to
indicate that, despite the institutional cooperation provided for in Decree-Law No. 11 of 20061078F,
AUPSA would not, under Panamanian regulatory framework, be competent to grant animal health
eligibility. In this regard, AUPSA could be understood to have acted in the exercise of its authority
to request up-to-date information on, inter alia, the sanitary status of the exporting country, as
provided for in paragraph 7 of section VII of the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments.
7.483. On 9 October 2020, almost two months after its communication of 21 August 2020, AUPSA
sent a communication to SENASA reiterating what it had said in the earlier communication. More
specifically, AUPSA stated that it "[was] waiting for your reply to be able to initiate the animal health
evaluation process for Costa Rica that would make it possible to determine its eligibility to export
animals and products and by-products of animal origin to Panama through the procedures carried
out by the competent Panamanian authorities".1079

Paso Canoas included two points of relevance to Measure 2: (a) approving the Biomar and
Central Veterinaria plants to export products not intended for human consumption before
19 February 2021; and (b) "perform[ing] an evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary system" in
respect of products of trade interest. The agreement states that this evaluation would take place

7.485. Also on 12 February, SENASA sent a communication to AUPSA in which, in accordance with
the agreement reached at Paso Canoas, it reiterated "the approval request for the establishments
that prepare products of animal origin intended for human consumption".1082 SENASA further stated
that it would be grateful to receive a "communication indicating the date on which the process is to
be initiated, in order to prepare the information required".1083
7.486. Three days later, on 15 February 2021, AUPSA requested that SENASA send "as soon as
possible the relevant note granting [them] [its] consent to initiate the animal health eligibility
process".1084
7.487. The following day, on 16 February 2021, SENASA reiterated its request that AUPSA indicate
the date on which Panama intended to initiate the evaluation process for the Costa Rican sanitary
system, so that SENASA could prepare the information required.1085
7.488. The aide-mémoire of the first session of the Bilateral Technical Commission, held on
18 February 2021, almost a week after the meeting in Paso Canoas, shows that Costa Rica agreed
to Panama carrying out the evaluation of its sanitary system, which would take place within
30 working days. The parties also agreed that Panama would inform Costa Rica, no later than
22 February 2021, of the information it required for "the evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary
system with a view to reauthorizing the plants of interest mentioned at the meeting of
12 February".1086
7.489. Four days after the first session of the Bilateral Technical Commission, on 22 February 2021,
AUPSA sent a communication to SENASA stating that, in the light of SENASA's communications of
12 and 16 February, it considered that this Costa Rican authority "g[ave] its consent to the
competent Panamanian sanitary authorities to carry out the evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary
system".1087 AUPSA added that, in line with SENASA's consent, it hereby notified SENASA of "the
formal initiation of the animal health evaluation procedure for Costa Rica in order to determine its
sanitary eligibility to export products and by-products of animal origin (dairy products) to
Panama".1088 AUPSA also attached a questionnaire regarding imports of dairy products (concerning
the Dos Pinos company, an exporter of these products) that SENASA was to respond to in full,
providing "specific documentation, evidence and sources" to support its replies.1089 AUPSA noted
that the on-site audits provided for in Panamanian legislation would be performed at a later stage.1090
7.490. Two days later, on 24 February 2021, SENASA informed AUPSA that it had consented to
Panama carrying out an evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary system, but it had never agreed to
Panama performing a country-level sanitary eligibility process. SENASA noted that, as the
questionnaire sent on 22 February 2021 concerned determining sanitary eligibility, not evaluating
the sanitary system, it considered it essential to distinguish between the two concepts. After

exporting dairy products covered thereby. SENASA also requested that questionnaires similar to the

7.491. The following day, on 25 February 2021, during the second meeting of the Bilateral Technical
Commission, the parties registered their disagreement regarding the nature of the sanitary
evaluation procedure that Panama was going to initiate. However, it was noted that Costa Rica would
send the information requested by Panama before 1 March 2021.1093
7.492. The Panel notes that, on 12 February 2021, at the Paso Canoas bilateral meeting, the parties
referred to an "evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary system".1094 This language differs from the
references to the recognition of Costa Rica's animal health eligibility that AUPSA included in its
aforementioned communications prior to this date. The Panel also notes that there is nothing in the
record that clarifies the use of different language at the Paso Canoas meeting on the one hand, and
in AUPSA's previous communications on the other.
7.493. In addition, the Panel notes that the communications following the Paso Canoas meeting1095
reflect the disagreement between the parties regarding the nature of the procedure that Panama
was undertaking as part of the process of renewing the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican
establishments in question. This disagreement concerns whether Panama initiated an animal health
evaluation procedure for Costa Rica in respect of dairy products as part of the process of renewing
the relevant sanitary approvals, as Panama claims, or whether it revoked country-level
sanitary/animal health eligibility for all the products at issue, as Costa Rica caliams.
7.494. Based on the relevant communications examined in this section, the Panel understands that
Panama was not consistent in indicating to Costa Rica the information it required to conclude the
process of renewing the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments. When AUPSA
contacted SENASA regarding the approval of two Dos Pinos establishments, on 2 January 2020,
AUPSA requested that certain information concerning Costa Rica's sanitary system be updated, but
did not refer to the necessity of initiating a review of Costa Rica's animal health status, as mentioned
in its subsequent communications.1096
7.495. Six months later, on 10 July 2020, AUPSA informed SENASA that, owing to the COVID-19
pandemic, in order to extend the sanitary approvals of Costa Rican establishments that had expired
on 30 June 2020, these establishments could "be subject to a new procedure for their approval".
This new procedure would take place once the Panamanian authorities were in a position to initiate
the processes for the "evaluation and recognition of the Costa Rican sanitary system" and audits at
origin.1097 A month later, on 21 August 2020, AUPSA informed SENASA that recognition of

that it was no longer eligible. (Memorandum SENASA-DG-191-2021 (Exhibit CRI-58), p. 2).

processed.1098 As of this date, the Panamanian authorities were consistent in indicating that carrying
out an animal health evaluation of Costa Rica was necessary in order to renew the sanitary approvals
of the Costa Rican establishments. For their part, the Costa Rican authorities were consistent in
stating that they accepted an evaluation of Costa Rica's sanitary system, but not a new process to
determine country-level sanitary eligibility.
7.496. In its communications to SENASA, AUPSA does not explain what is entailed by the "animal
health evaluation of Costa Rica" it intended to carry out. In its communication of 22 February 2021,
AUPSA sent SENASA a questionnaire entitled "Preliminary questionnaire to determine a
country's animal health eligibility in order to verify whether its measures achieve the same level of
protection established by the relevant laws and regulations of the Republic of Panama".1099 This
questionnaire focused on dairy products. There is nothing in the record to show that, as of the date
of the establishment of the Panel, Panama had sent questionnaires to Costa Rica about the other
products at issue.1100 In these proceedings, Panama explained to the Panel that sending this
questionnaire did not mean that sanitary eligibility had been revoked.1101 Panama also clarified that
it had sent the questionnaire to gather animal health information about milk and dairy products in
accordance with what was agreed in Paso Canoas.1102 Costa Rica, for its part, understood that the
questionnaire only applied when a country was interested in exporting to Panama for the first time,
which was not the case.1103
7.497. The Panel considers that there is no indication in the communications sent by AUPSA or the
commitments made at the Paso Canoas meeting that Panama revoked or ceased to recognize
Costa Rica's sanitary or animal health eligibility for all the products at issue.1104 Despite the possible
ambiguities arising from the differences in terminology used therein and the title of the questionnaire
sent by AUPSA to SENASA in February 2021, it follows from the examination of all the
communications and the rest of the evidence in the record that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate
that Panama revoked its sanitary or animal health eligibility for all the products at issue. In fact, the
record contains no evidence of the conclusion of the formal animal health evaluation process that
AUPSA indicated it should carry out to renew the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican
establishments in question.1105 In the Panel's view, the fact that such a procedure may be ongoing
does not support the conclusion that Costa Rica's country-level sanitary or animal health eligibility
has been revoked, and Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate otherwise.
7.498. In addition, the Panel observes that Panama acknowledges that it imports live animals from
Costa Rica.1106 Although under Panamanian regulations country-level sanitary eligibility and animal
health eligibility are granted for each product1107, the fact that Panama imports live animals strongly
suggests that it has not revoked Costa Rica's sanitary or animal health eligibility. This is due to the
fact that, by importing these animals, Panama accepts the risks related to the diseases that may be
transmitted by live animals.1108 If Panama accepts these risks, and in the absence of any evidence
to the contrary, it could be understood that Panama also accepts the risks of importing the products

7.499. In summary, as explained above, in the Panel's view: (a) the formal animal health evaluation
process has not concluded in a way that would have implied the revocation of Costa Rica's animal
health eligibility or sanitary eligibility; (b) there is no formal act in the record revoking
Costa Rica's sanitary or animal health eligibility for all the products at issue; (c) Panama did not
notify Costa Rica that it had concerns justifying the suspension or revocation of Costa Rica's sanitary
or animal health eligibility, as provided for in Panamanian regulations; and (d) Panama has
continued to import live animals, indicating that Panama accepts the risks of importing the inputs to
manufacture the products of animal origin at issue. The Panel also notes that, as part of the process
of renewing the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments, Panama requested
information on dairy products, not on all the products at issue.
7.500. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Panama
effectively revoked or ceased to recognize Costa Rica's sanitary or animal health eligibility for all the
products at issue.1109 Therefore, Costa Rica has failed to discharge its burden of proving that
Measure 2 consists of an import prohibition on the products at issue maintained by virtue of
Panama's decision to revoke Costa Rica's sanitary/animal health eligibility for these products.
7.4.1.2.2 Whether Panama maintains an import prohibition on the products at issue by
virtue of the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of 18 Costa Rican establishments
7.501. The Panel will now examine whether Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 2 consists
of an import prohibition on the products at issue maintained by virtue of the non-renewal1110 of the
sanitary approvals of 18 Costa Rican establishments.
7.502. The Panel notes that the sanitary approvals granted to the 18 Costa Rican establishments in
question were issued between 2014 and 2016, for a duration of three or five years.1111 As shown in
the record, SENASA requested the renewal of the sanitary approvals of 16 Costa Rican
establishments between 2017 and 2018, and of another two in 2019 and 2020, respectively, and
repeatedly reiterated these requests until 2020.1112 The record also shows that representatives of
one of the establishments, Biomar, contacted AUPSA officials to process the extensions of the
sanitary approval of this establishment between December 2017 and October 2019.1113
7.503. The Panel also notes that AUPSA granted (a) a "two-month extension" for "the expired
[sanitary approvals of] Costa Rican establishments that export food products" to Panama, from

7.4.1.2.3 Conclusion regarding the content of Measure 2
7.506. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that
Panama revoked or ceased to recognize Costa Rica's sanitary/animal health eligibility for the
products in question. Therefore, Costa Rica has failed to discharge its burden of proving that
Measure 2 consists of an import prohibition maintained by virtue of Panama's decision to revoke this
eligibility. In addition, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 2 consists of
an import prohibition on dairy and meat products from Costa Rica maintained by virtue of the
non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican establishments in question.
7.507. Accordingly, the Panel's analysis below is limited to Measure 2, as indicated in the preceding
paragraph.
7.4.2 Claims under the SPS Agreement
7.508. Having determined the content of Measure 2, the Panel will now examine Costa Rica's claims
under the SPS Agreement with respect to this measure. The Panel will first assess whether Measure 2
is an SPS measure that falls within the scope of the SPS Agreement.
7.4.2.1 Whether Measure 2 is a sanitary or phytosanitary measure that falls within the
scope of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.1.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.4.2.1.1.1 Costa Rica
7.509. Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 falls within the scope of the SPS Agreement, as it is an
SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1)(b) and (c) to the Agreement and directly affects
international trade within the meaning of Article 1.1.1121
7.510. Costa Rica argues that the objective of Measure 2 relates to both: (i) the protection of human
or animal life or health from risks arising from foods within the meaning of Annex A(1)(b)1122; and
(ii) the protection of human life or health from risks arising from diseases carried by animals through
animal products within the meaning of Annex A(1)(c).1123 Costa Rica submits that Measure 2 falls
within the scope of Annex A(1)(b) because it is a consequence of Panama's refusal to renew the
sanitary approvals of Costa Rican establishments that export foods (meat and dairy products) for
human and animal consumption, as a result of the alleged loss of Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility.1124
Moreover, Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 falls within the scope of Annex A(1)(c) because an
animal health evaluation is also required for the sanitary eligibility granted by Panama, the loss of
which Costa Rica deems unjustified.1125
7.511. Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 directly affects international trade because Panama's
non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments prevents products from
those establishments from being imported into Panama.1126

7.4.2.1.1.2 Panama
7.512. Panama does not dispute that Measure 2 is an SPS measure in accordance with the definition
in Annex A(1) and Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement, to which the SPS Agreement applies.
7.4.2.1.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.4.2.1.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.513. The Panel will next examine whether, in accordance with the legal standard described in
section 7.3.1.1.2.1 above, the SPS Agreement is applicable to Measure 2. In particular, the Panel
will consider whether, as established in Articles 1.1 and 1.2 of the Agreement, Measure 2:
(a) constitutes an SPS measure in accordance with the definition given in Annex A to the
SPS Agreement; and (b) may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade.
7.4.2.1.2.2 Whether Measure 2 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1) to the SPS Agreement
7.514. The Panel recalls that Measure 2 consists of an import prohibition on dairy and meat products
maintained by virtue of the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of 16 Costa Rican establishments
that export the products at issue.1127 Bearing in mind the legal standard outlined above, the task of
this Panel is to determine whether Measure 2 has been implemented in order to achieve at least one
of the objectives listed in Annex A(1)(a)-(d).1128
7.515. Given that Measure 2 concerns the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of 16 Costa Rican
establishments, the Panel considers that the objective of Measure 2 relates to the purpose of those
sanitary approvals.
7.516. As explained in section 7.4.1.2.1.1 above, to be able to export food products of animal
origin to Panama, Costa Rican establishments must have a valid sanitary approval. In addition, the
country of origin must have had its sanitary eligibility approved for the products concerned.1129
Panama notes that two manuals provide the legal basis for the procedures governing a country's
sanitary eligibility and the sanitary approval of establishments for the export of foods to Panama:
(a) the AUPSA Manual on sanitary eligibility and (b) the AUPSA Manual for the approval of
establishments.1130 Both manuals make reference to the importation or introduction of foods and
cite the Codex Guidelines or Principles as relevant regulations.1131 AUPSA, which is the authority
responsible for the relevant import authorization process, is the Panamanian Food Safety Authority.
Its equivalent in Costa Rica, SENASA, is the authority that provides the responses to two AUPSA
questionnaires: one to verify the country's sanitary status1132 and one concerning Costa Rica's
1127 See para. 7.506 above.

7.517. The two aforementioned AUPSA manuals and the questionnaires sent by Panama to
Costa Rica indicate that the purpose of the sanitary approval renewal procedures is to ensure the
safety of food imported into Panama by checking the sanitary conditions in Costa Rica. In this
respect, the objective of Measure 2 is in line with the objective of "protect[ing] human or animal life
or health within the territory of the Member from risks arising from additives, contaminants, toxins
or disease-causing organisms in foods, beverages or feedstuffs", set forth in Annex A(1)(b).
7.518. In addition, the two AUPSA manuals make reference to the International Office of Epizootics
(OIE)1134 and its Terrestrial Animal Health Code.1135 Moreover, the AUPSA Manual for the approval
of establishments defines "sanitary eligibility" as "the recognition by [AUPSA] of the existence of
countries, regions, zones or districts free from the disease under consideration, as defined by the
competent official authority of the exporting country, for the importation or transit of foodstuffs",
which confirms the animal health dimension of this measure.1136 Some of the communications
between the parties' authorities concerning Measure 2 also make reference to matters of an animal
health nature. In its communications, SENASA makes reference to the OIE and its Terrestrial Animal
Health Code, as well as to diseases such as swine fever and BSE.1137 For its part, AUPSA refers to
the necessity of conducting an animal health evaluation process for Costa Rica to determine its
eligibility to export animals and animal products and by-products to Panama1138 and sent a
questionnaire to SENASA for determining a country's animal health eligibility.1139 The questionnaire
mentions MIDA's power to approve countries' animal health eligibility.1140 It also makes reference to
the aim of protecting human and animal health and life against animal diseases that are
transmissible to humans (zoonoses).1141 With regard to SENASA's responses to the questionnaire,
the authorities of Panama expressed public health concerns about zoonoses such as brucellosis and
bovine tuberculosis.1142
7.519. In view of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Measure 2 also has an animal health
objective that is in line with the objective of "protect[ing] human life or health within the territory
of the Member from risks arising from diseases carried by animals, plants or products thereof, or
from the entry, establishment or spread of pests", set forth in Annex A(1)(c).
7.520. Therefore, the Panels finds that the objective of Measure 2 corresponds to the objectives set
forth in Annex A(1)(b) and (c).
7.521. With regard to the type of measure, as explained in the section on the content of
Measure 21143, Panama's decision not to renew the sanitary approvals by virtue of which Measure 2
is maintained is related to the procedure established in Panama's regulations for the granting of
such approvals. Therefore, it is the Panel's view that Panama's decision not to renew the sanitary
approvals by virtue of which the import prohibition on Costa Rican dairy and meat products is
maintained could qualify as a procedure for the purposes of Annex A(1). Moreover, as outlined
above, even where a measure is not expressly included in the list of measures in Annex A(1), it may
be considered an SPS measure if it is applied to achieve one of the objectives listed in that provision.

7.4.2.1.2.3 Whether Measure 2 may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade
7.522. The Panel notes that previous panels have concluded that administrative procedures that
can delay or deny entry of products into a Member may affect international trade.1144 Measure 2, by
its very nature, directly affects international trade by preventing the importation into Panama of the
dairy and meat products at issue, as a consequence of the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of
16 Costa Rican establishments.1145
7.4.2.1.3 Conclusion on the applicability of the SPS Agreement to Measure 2
7.523. Having determined that Measure 2 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1)(b) and (c) to the SPS Agreement and that it directly affects international trade within
the meaning of Article 1.1 of the Agreement, the Panel finds that the SPS Agreement is applicable
to this measure.
7.524. The Panel will proceed by examining whether, as Panama claims1146, Measure 2 is exclusively
subject to the disciplines under Article 8 and Annex C of the SPS Agreement, or whether, as
Costa Rica asserts, it is also subject to other obligations under the Agreement.1147

7.4.2.2.2 Main arguments of the third parties
7.532. Australia and Canada disagree with Panama's assertion that approval procedures are
exclusively subject to Article 8 and Annex C of the SPS Agreement.1159 In particular, Canada notes
that Article 8 does not preclude other provisions of the SPS Agreement from applying to approval
procedures. In this regard, Canada states that, in addition to requiring Members to observe the
provisions of Annex C in the operation of these procedures, Article 8 requires Members to "ensure
that their procedures are not inconsistent with the provisions of this Agreement". Canada adds that
there is nothing in the SPS Agreement that precludes the other provisions of the Agreement from
applying to an approval procedure that constitutes an SPS measure. Canada refers to
US – Poultry (China) and Korea – Radionuclides as examples of disputes in which obligations other
than those established in Annex C have been applied to procedural measures.1160
7.533. The United States also notes that the final clause of Article 8 establishes that Members shall
"otherwise ensure that their procedures are not inconsistent with the provisions of this Agreement".
According to the United States, this clause clarifies that a Member's procedures, which include
approval procedures, shall be consistent not only with Annex C, but also "otherwise … with the
provisions of this Agreement".1161
7.534. The European Union considers that Costa Rica's claim is similar to that submitted by the
European Union in Russia – Pigs (EU), in which it challenged a European Union-wide prohibition that
Russia described as "provisional compliance" with the bilateral veterinary certificates as a result of
the impossibility of complying with the content of the certificates. According to the European Union,
the panel in that case agreed with the European Union. The European Union considers that such an
import prohibition may fall within the scope of various provisions of the SPS Agreement, in particular
those related to risk assessment and non-discrimination.1162
7.4.2.2.3 Assessment by the Panel
7.535. As outlined above1163, Costa Rica submits that all of the provisions in respect of which it
submitted claims are applicable to Measure 2.1164 Panama, for its part, argues that, as Measure 2 is
a procedure and not a substantive SPS measure, only Article 8 and Annex C(1) are applicable to
it.1165
7.536. The parties' arguments concerning the provisions applicable to Measure 2 are closely linked
to the matter of whether Measure 2 constitutes a control, inspection and approval procedure within
the meaning of Article 8 and Annex C(1). The Panel therefore deems it appropriate to first examine
this matter in order to determine which of the provisions of the SPS Agreement invoked by
Costa Rica are applicable to Measure 2.
7.4.2.2.3.1 Relevant legal standard
7.537. Article 8 of the SPS Agreement stipulates as follows:
Members shall observe the provisions of Annex C in the operation of control, inspection
and approval procedures, including national systems for approving the use of additives
or for establishing tolerances for contaminants in foods, beverages or feedstuffs, and

7.538. The relevant subparagraphs of Annex C to the SPS Agreement, entitled "Control, Inspection
and Approval Procedures", establish the following:
Members shall ensure, with respect to any procedure to check and ensure the fulfilment
of sanitary or phytosanitary measures, that:
(a) such procedures are undertaken and completed without undue delay and in no less
favourable manner for imported products than for like domestic products;
…
(c) information requirements are limited to what is necessary for appropriate control,
inspection and approval procedures, including for approval of the use of additives or for
the establishment of tolerances for contaminants in food, beverages or feedstuffs[.]
7.539. Therefore, Article 8 and Annex C are applicable to "control, inspection and approval
procedures".1166 Annex C(1) identifies the types of procedures to which the obligations contained in
subparagraphs (a) to (i) apply, namely, "any procedure to check and ensure the fulfilment of [SPS]
measures". The text of Annex C(1) therefore covers any procedure that: (a) is aimed at "checking
and ensuring the fulfilment of [SPS] measures"; and (b) is undertaken in the context of "control,
inspection, or approval".1167
7.540. For a procedure to fall within the scope of Article 8 and Annex C, there must be a link
between the procedure and the SPS measure whose fulfilment the Member seeks to check and
ensure.1168 In previous disputes, Annex C(1) has been considered to "cover a broad array of
procedures" and "does not specify or exclude any type of procedures from its application".1169

Annex C(1) to the SPS Agreement
7.541. The parties agree that Measure 2 constitutes an approval procedure within the meaning of
Annex C(1) of the SPS Agreement.1170 Nevertheless, the Panel will examine this matter, in
accordance with its duty under Article 11 of the DSU to make an objective assessment of the matter
before it.
7.542. As Costa Rica is the party claiming that Measure 2 is subject to Annex C(1), it falls on
Costa Rica to demonstrate that the procedure to grant and renew the sanitary approvals of
Costa Rican establishments is one: (a) that is aimed at "checking and ensuring the fulfilment of
[SPS] measures"; and (b) that is undertaken in a context of "control, inspection, or approval".1171
7.543. With respect to whether Measure 2 is aimed at checking and ensuring the fulfilment of
SPS measures, the Panel recalls that, according to the Panamanian regulations outlined in
section 7.4.2.1.1.2 above, the aim of the procedures for renewing sanitary approvals for the
importation into Panama of the products at issue is to check that establishments wishing to export
those products meet the relevant sanitary requirements. The Panel considers that, as argued by

7.544. Furthermore, as the procedures relate to the renewal of the sanitary approvals of
Costa Rican establishments seeking to export the products at issue to Panama, they are undertaken

7.545. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that

7.4.2.2.3.3 Provisions of the SPS Agreement invoked by Costa Rica and applicable to
Measure 2
7.546. The Panel notes that Panama's main argument is that, as Measure 2 is an approval
procedure, it is exclusively subject to the disciplines under Annex C(1) and Article 8 of the SPS
Agreement. In the Panel's view, and as submitted by Costa Rica and several third parties, the very
terms of Article 8 contradict Panama's argument.
7.547. The final clause of Article 8 of the SPS Agreement stipulates that Members shall "otherwise
ensure that their [control, inspection and approval] procedures are not inconsistent with the
provisions of this Agreement". In the Panel's view, this obligation implies that any approval
procedure must be consistent with the obligations set forth in the other provisions of the
SPS Agreement. Nevertheless, the Panel considers that the applicability of each provision will depend
on the obligations it contains and the nature of the measure concerned.
7.548. The Panel recalls that Measure 2 consists of an import prohibition on dairy and meat products
from Costa Rica that is maintained by virtue of the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of 16 of
the 18 Costa Rican establishments in question1174, which constitutes an approval procedure within
the meaning of Article 8 and Annex C(1) of the SPS Agreement.1175 The Panel will now assess
whether the obligations in the provisions invoked by Costa Rica are applicable to Measure 2.
7.549. With respect to the obligations concerning the scientific basis of SPS measures set forth in
Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, the Panel recalls Costa Rica's position that the
claim in respect of those obligations relates to the revocation of Costa Rica's sanitary/animal health
eligibility. Given that the Panel has found that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Panama
revoked Costa Rica's sanitary or animal health eligibility for all products concerned, the Panel will
not address the claim under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 with respect to Measure 2. Moreover, the
Panel notes that Costa Rica has not developed arguments relating to the absence of a scientific basis
for the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.550. With respect to the non-discrimination obligations set forth in Articles 2.3 and 5.5 of the
SPS Agreement, the Panel agrees with the findings of the panel in US – Poultry (China), which saw
no reason to consider that the SPS Agreement prohibits discrimination only in relation to one type
of SPS measure.1176 The Panel therefore considers that it would be appropriate to examine the claims

7.553. In view of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Measure 2 is subject to the obligations set

7.4.2.3.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
Costa Rica
7.560. Costa Rica submits that Panama incurred undue delays in undertaking and completing the

7.4.2.3.2.2 Main arguments of the third parties
7.579. Canada, Brazil, and the European Union state that the examination under Annex C(1)(a) to
the SPS Agreement should be conducted on a case-by-case basis, focusing on whether there is a
justification for the delay, rather than on the length of the delay.1222
7.580. In particular, the European Union states that the contentious period between the parties is
that after 15 October 2020, when Costa Rica lifted its travel restrictions.1223 The European Union
considers that the Panel, in its analysis, must take into account not only Costa Rica's official travel
restrictions but also the actual travel possibilities of Panama's inspectors during the relevant period.
In that regard, in the European Union's view, the Panel should assess whether the inspectors were
prevented from travelling because of COVID-19-related reasons during the relevant period.1224 The
European Union also invites the Panel to assess whether the information requested by Panama was
of a relevant nature for the assessment of the 16 Costa Rican establishments, as, if it was not
relevant, it could be concluded that there was an undue delay.1225
7.4.2.3.2.3 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.581. The first clause of Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement requires Members to ensure that
procedures to check and ensure the fulfilment of SPS measures are "undertaken and completed
without undue delay". The wording of Annex C(1)(a) has been interpreted as a good faith obligation
requiring Members to proceed with their approval procedures as promptly as possible, taking account
of the need to check and ensure the fulfilment of their relevant SPS requirements.1226
(Exhibit CRI-58); Memorandum SENASA-DG-340-2021 (Exhibit CRI-60); and
Memorandum SENASA-DG-725-2021 (Exhibit CRI-63)).

Note AUPSA-AG-571-2020 (Exhibit CRI-55)).

- 174 -
7.582. The Panel notes that the phrase "without undue delay" has been interpreted as relating to
both the undertaking and the completion of the procedure and, therefore, if a Member causes undue
delay at any stage in an approval procedure, this would constitute a breach of the first clause of
Annex C(1)(a).1227
7.583. It follows from the text of the provision that the analysis of a claim under the first clause of
Annex C(1)(a) comprises two steps: first, the complainant must establish that there has been a
delay in the undertaking or completion of the procedure; and second, the complainant must establish
that the delay was undue.1228
7.584. The concept of "delay" has been interpreted as "(a period of) time lost by inaction or inability
to proceed".1229 In turn, the concept of "undue" has been interpreted as "going beyond what is
warranted" or "unjustifiable".1230 Thus, it is understood that Annex C(1) requires that approval
procedures be undertaken and completed with no unjustifiable loss of time.1231 In this respect, what
matters is whether there is a legitimate reason, or justification, for a given delay, not the length of
a delay as such.1232
7.585. The determination of whether a procedure has been undertaken or completed "without
undue delay" must be made on a case-by-case basis1233, in light of the nature and complexity of the
procedure to be undertaken and completed.1234 In addition, Members have been deemed not legally
responsible for delays which are not attributable to them. Hence, delays attributable to action, or
inaction, of a complainant must not be held against a Member when a determination is made
regarding whether that Member has undertaken or completed approval procedures "without undue
delay".1235 On the other hand, if the Member did not proceed as expeditiously as could be expected
of it in the specific circumstances of the case, the delay caused in this way would be "undue".1236
7.586. The Panel shares these assessments of the legal standard applicable under Annex C(1)(a)
made in the aforementioned disputes and will proceed with its own assessment on that basis.
Whether Panama incurred an undue delay in undertaking or completing the renewal of
the sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican establishments in question
7.587. The Panel notes that, with regard to Costa Rica's claim under Annex C(1)(a), the parties
disagree on three main issues: (a) the time-period that the Panel should examine; (b) whether
Panama incurred delays; and (c) whether the alleged delays incurred by Panama were undue. The
Panel will analyse each of these three issues in the light of the aforementioned legal standard.
The relevant time-period
7.588. Costa Rica claims that Panama incurred delays between 2017, when it submitted its initial
sanitary approval renewal requests, and 27 September 2021, the date on which the Panel was
established.1237 Costa Rica notes that Panama carried out automatic extensions that were not
notified at the time, and that were merely posted on the AUPSA web page. According to Costa Rica,
the fact that AUPSA had granted automatic, informal extensions of the sanitary approvals, such as
that granted until 30 June 2020 for various Costa Rican establishments, does not remedy the delays
incurred during those procedures prior to the expiry of the sanitary approvals.1238 Moreover,
Costa Rica submits that the fact that the automatic, informal extensions had enabled trade in meat
and dairy products between the countries to continue between 2017 and 2020 does not mean that

AUPSA had correctly processed and completed the procedures to renew the sanitary approvals of
the Costa Rican establishments.1239
7.589. Panama submits that it completed the procedures relating to the sanitary approval renewal
requests submitted by Costa Rica between 2017 and 2020 by extending the validity of those
approvals to 30 June 2020 for the Costa Rican establishments in question.1240 In this respect,
Panama also notes that, on the date the Panel was established, the sanitary approvals of the
establishments of the companies Central Veterinaria and Biomar were valid, and enabled them to
export animal products up to 19 September 2022 and 30 June 2023, respectively.1241
7.590. Accordingly, Panama submits that the Panel should only examine the alleged delays that
were incurred during the procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican
establishments in the period between the expiry of those approvals (30 June 2020) and the
establishment of the Panel (27 September 2021).1242
7.591. The Panel notes that the parties disagree on whether, as a matter of fact, as at 30 June 2020,
Panama had completed the procedures concerning the sanitary approval renewal requests that
Costa Rica had submitted from 2017 onwards. Contrary to what has been stated by Panama1243,
Costa Rica has not acknowledged that Panama completed the renewal procedures by extending the
validity of the sanitary approvals to 30 June 2020.1244 This matter is central to determining the
period to be examined, as, if Panama completed the renewal procedures on 30 June 2020, the only
period that the Panel should evaluate is that after that date. Otherwise, the Panel should also
examine events prior to 30 June 2020.
7.592. To resolve this issue, the Panel will first examine Panama's regulatory framework to identify
when the renewal procedure is considered to have been completed. In this respect, the relevant
provisions of the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments stipulate that, as part of the
approval and renewal procedure, an approval resolution is published and the establishment is added
to the database on the AUPSA web page.1245
7.593. The Panel recalls the stages of the sanitary approval renewal procedure mentioned in
paragraph 7.471 . Pursuant to the Manual, this procedure comprises the following stages: (a) the
request from the interested party; (b) the documentary analysis of the sanitary evaluation; (c) the
audit at origin or on site (where applicable); (d) the evaluation by the Institutional Technical
Commission (CTI); (e) the issuance of a resolution approving or refusing the renewal concerned;
and, once the renewal has been approved, (f) the publication of the approval resolution; and (g) the
inclusion of the establishment on the AUPSA web page.1246 Accordingly, the renewal process begins
with the request from the party interested in exporting, which, in this case, was sent by SENASA to
AUPSA.
7.594. Panama's regulations state that on-site audits may form part of the sanitary approval
renewal procedure. In this respect, the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments stipulates
that "AUPSA reserves the right to undertake audits and inspections at origin for the renewal of
establishments, based on its decision in the relevant risk analysis".1247 As mentioned in
paragraph 7.473 above, the Manual also stipulates that AUPSA may request documentary
information from the exporting country on the sanitary conditions of the establishment as part of

7.595. According to the Manual, a resolution requires a prior evaluation by CTI to check compliance
with the relevant sanitary requirements. Once approval has been granted (or renewed) for the
establishment concerned, Office of the Legal Counsel of AUPSA proceeds to draft the resolution,
which is signed by the Directors and Administrator General of AUPSA.1249 The Panel notes that all
the resolutions in the record for the 16 Costa Rican establishments issued between 2014 and 2016
with a validity of between three and five years share the following features described in the AUPSA
Manual for the approval of establishments: (a) they were issued by AUPSA; (b) they were signed by
the Administrator General and Directors of AUPSA; and (c) before they were issued, on-site audits
were undertaken when so decided by AUPSA.1250 In addition, the aforementioned resolutions state
that "AUPSA appointed a team of auditors to undertake the official inspection of the plant".1251 This
took place irrespective of whether it concerned resolutions granting or renewing the sanitary
approval.1252
7.596. In the Panel's view, the aforementioned resolutions are indicative of the form and content
that a resolution must have for the purposes of completing the procedure to grant or renew a
sanitary approval, based on the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments. Therefore, the
Panel understands that a sanitary approval resolution based on the Manual is one that is issued by
AUPSA expressly granting sanitary approval to the establishment in question, irrespective of whether
it is a renewal of the approval or an initial approval.
7.597. With regard to the establishment approval procedures initiated by Costa Rica between 2017
and 2020, SENASA sent AUPSA the sanitary approval renewal requests of 16 Costa Rican
establishments before the expiry of those approvals. In those communications, it also requested
that Panama indicate the dates on which the on-site audits would be undertaken.1253
7.598. Panama claimed that the on-site audits were necessary to proceed with the renewal of the
sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments, as, in Panama's view, a documentary
review of the establishments was insufficient to achieve its ALOP.1254 For its part, Costa Rica
recognizes that, as part of the sanitary approval renewal procedure, Panama usually requires on-

are not a mandatory part of the procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of establishments,
Panama deemed it appropriate to require such audits for the renewal of the sanitary approvals of
the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.599. The Panel notes that Article 4 of AUPSA Resolution No. 145-AG-2019, extending the validity
of the sanitary approvals of various Costa Rican establishments, stipulates the need to draw up a
timetable for the on-site audits of the establishments listed therein.1256 Moreover, AUPSA made
reference to the need to conduct audits when it granted extensions to various Costa Rican
establishments through Note AUPSA-AG-399-2019 of 15 October 2019.1257 In the Panel's view, the
above demonstrates that AUPSA determined that undertaking on-site audits was part of the
procedure in order to complete the sanitary approval renewal requests for the 16 Costa Rican
establishments.
7.600. Accordingly, the Panel considers that the following stages must be carried out for the
procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of each of the Costa Rican establishments to be deemed
to have been completed: (a) AUPSA undertakes a documentary analysis of the sanitary evaluation;
(b) AUPSA and other sanitary authorities of Panama undertake on-site audits; (c) the CTI evaluates
and approves the renewal request after checking compliance with the relevant requirements;
(d) AUPSA issues and publishes a resolution renewing the sanitary approval of the establishment
concerned; and (e) AUPSA includes the establishment concerned on its web page.
7.601. With respect to recording the sanitary approval extensions of the establishments awaiting
renewal on the AUPSA web page, Costa Rica considers that publishing this information does not
signify that AUPSA had processed and completed the sanitary approval renewal procedures for the
Costa Rican establishments. Costa Rica adds that, under Panama's regulatory framework,
completing the procedure requires an approval resolution to be issued, which did not happen.1258 In
this respect, Panama considers that the renewal procedure in question was completed each time the
approval of each of the 16 Costa Rican establishments was extended.1259
7.602. The evidence in the record indicates that AUPSA recorded the 2019 and 2020 extensions for
the 16 Costa Rican establishments on its web page.1260 However, the record does not include
documents that confirm that the Panamanian authorities completed the other stages1261 of the
procedure. The Panel does not consider the inclusion of the establishments on the AUPSA web page
to be sufficient to consider the sanitary approval renewal process to have been completed. In
accordance with the Panamanian regulatory framework, the technical evaluation of the substance of
the renewal request is a fundamental component of this procedure. Accordingly, simply recording
the extensions issued on a web page, without providing an examination of the merits of the request,
cannot be considered sufficient to conclude that the procedure in question has been completed. In
the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that, as at 30 June 2020, Panama had not completed
the procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican establishments in
question.
7.603. Bearing in mind that Panama had not completed the renewal procedures as at 30 June 2020,
and given that the obligation under Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement covers all stages of a
procedure1262, the Panel will now examine the events referred to by Costa Rica from its initial renewal
requests in 2017 to the date of the establishment of the Panel on 27 September 2021.

Whether Panama incurred delays in undertaking and completing the renewal of the
sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican establishments in question
7.604. The Panel will now analyse whether, during the aforementioned period, Panama incurred
any delay in the procedures to renew the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
The Panel recalls that there is deemed to be a delay in the procedure when there is "(a period of)
time lost by inaction or inability to proceed".1263
7.605. The Panel will examine the time that elapsed between the submission of the sanitary
approval renewal requests for the 16 Costa Rican establishments and the establishment of the Panel.
The Panel recalls that, in accordance with the provisions of the AUPSA Manual for the approval of
establishments, the renewal procedure begins with the request from the party interested in

7.606. As can be observed, between two years and nine months and four years and eight months
elapsed between the sanitary approval renewal requests of the Costa Rican establishments, which
marked the start of the renewal procedure, and the establishment of the Panel. The evidence in the
record indicates that, during that time, Panama: (a) did not undertake on-site audits of these
16 Costa Rican establishments; and (b) did not issue and publish resolutions renewing the sanitary
approvals of each of those Costa Rican establishments.1281
7.607. Regarding the scheduling of the on-site audits, AUPSA sent two types of communications to
SENASA. On the one hand, on 9 August 2017, AUPSA sent SENASA a general communication
concerning the scheduling of the on-site audits for the sanitary approval renewal for establishment
No. 7 of the company Sigma Alimentos and the other establishments for which SENASA had
requested the renewal of their sanitary approvals. Therein, AUPSA indicated that "given the limited
number of auditors and the large volume of requests, the planned date of the visit will be slightly
postponed".1282
7.608. On the other hand, there were communications concerning the audits of three
establishments of the company Dos Pinos between January and February 2020.1283 On
9 March 2020, AUPSA informed SENASA that it was postponing the on-site audits of those three
establishments, which had been scheduled for the week of 9 to 13 March 2020, as a result of the
COVID-19 pandemic.1284 The record does not indicate that AUPSA rescheduled the on-site audits for
a later date or eventually carried out the audits of the three establishments of the company Dos
Pinos. There is no evidence in the record relating to the scheduling of on-site audits by AUPSA other
than those mentioned here.
7.609. Regarding the issuance and publication of the resolutions renewing the sanitary approvals
of the 16 Costa Rican establishments, the Panel refers to AUPSA Resolution No. 145-AG-2019.1285
Panama submits that, pursuant to that Resolution, the procedure to renew all of the requested
sanitary approvals was completed on that date by extending their validity.1286 The Panel disagrees
and considers that this Resolution did not complete the renewal procedures initiated by SENASA for
the 16 Costa Rican establishments, as indicated in Table 7 above. In the Panel's view, this Resolution
extended the validity of the sanitary approvals of 17 Costa Rican establishments (including the
16 establishments under assessment) for a period of six months, from 1 January to 30 June 2020,

- 180 -
but did not renew the approvals. Article 1 of AUPSA Resolution No. 145-AG-2019 states that an
"extension of the authorization" is granted to the relevant establishments, which is different from
the language used in: (a) the AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments, which sets out the
procedure for "renewing the approval of an establishment"1287; and (b) the resolutions renewing the
approvals of establishments prior to 2017, as was the case for establishment No. 40 of the company
El Ángel and establishments Nos. 45 and 47 of the company Sigma Alimentos, which state that it
has been decided to "[approve] the [r]enewal of the [e]stablishment".1288
7.610. Moreover, the fourth recital of AUPSA Resolution No. 145-AG-2019 mentions the need, once
the documentary formalities are completed, for audits of the establishments to be undertaken by
the Panamanian authorities. Article 4 of that same Resolution indicates that a timetable for on-site
audits will be drawn up for establishments whose sanitary approval has been extended.1289 This, in
the Panel's view, supports its conclusion that, as at the date on which the Resolution was issued,
the procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments had not been
completed, as the on-site audits of the establishments mentioned in the Resolution were still
pending.1290 The failure to schedule or undertake on-site audits was confirmed by Panama itself with
respect to 12 of these establishments, and there is no evidence in the record that indicates that on-
site audits were carried out for the other establishments.1291
7.611. As mentioned above, for the sanitary approval renewal procedure to be deemed to have
been completed, on-site audits must be undertaken and a resolution confirming the renewal must
be issued, events that did not occur with respect to any of the 16 Costa Rican establishments. In
the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that the time that elapsed between the request to
renew the sanitary approvals of each of the establishments and the establishment of the Panel is
sufficiently long to conclude that Panama failed to proceed in the review of the merits of the sanitary
approval renewal requests or in scheduling or undertaking on-site audits. Accordingly, the Panel
considers that Panama incurred delays within the meaning of Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement
with respect to the procedures to renew the sanitary approvals of 16 of the 18 Costa Rican
establishments in question.
Whether the delays were "undue"
7.612. The second issue that the Panel will assess in order to determine the consistency of
Measure 2 with Annex C(1)(a) is whether the delays identified in the previous section were undue,
in the sense that there was no justification for them. The Panel recalls that a delay is undue when it
is unjustifiable1292, and that what matters is whether there is a legitimate reason, or justification,
for a given delay, not the length of a delay as such.1293
7.613. Costa Rica identifies three reasons why the delays incurred by Panama are unjustifiable:
(a) the on-site audits were not undertaken for reasons attributable to Panama; (b) the logistical
constraints relating to the COVID-19 pandemic did not prevent Panama from undertaking the on-site
audits before March 2020 or after October 2020; and (c) requiring Costa Rica to complete a new
country sanitary eligibility procedure is not a legitimate justification for the delay.1294

- 181 -
7.614. For its part, Panama considers that the evidence in the record shows that it was not inactive
prior to 30 June 2020.1295 Panama also claims that the alleged delays prior to that date are justified
by the COVID-19 pandemic and Costa Rica's refusal to provide information on its animal health
system.1296 In particular, Panama adds that Costa Rica admits that it provided the information
requested by Panama on 24 March and 3 June 2021, which means, according to Panama, that any
alleged delay up to that date would be attributable to Costa Rica.1297
7.615. The Panel will now analyse the delays during the period prior to the onset of the COVID-19
pandemic in Costa Rica in March 2020, followed by the events that occurred after the start of the
pandemic up to the establishment of the Panel.
Delays prior to the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in Panama and Costa Rica in
March 2020
7.616. The Panel recalls that the main reason why the procedures to renew the sanitary approvals
did not proceed during this period was the failure to conduct the on-site audits. Costa Rica claims
that these audits were not undertaken for reasons attributable to Panama.1298 For its part, Panama
asserts that it had completed the procedures by 30 June 2020, and that the evidence in the record
shows that, in the cases where Costa Rica provided information, it carried out its initial documentary
analysis and took measures to schedule the on-site audits.1299
7.617. The Panel considers that the evidence in the record confirms Costa Rica's position. First, as
mentioned above, AUPSA sent a series of communications concerning the scheduling of the on-site
audits for the establishments with respect to which SENASA had requested the renewal of their
sanitary approvals.1300 In those communications, AUPSA indicates that it is impossible to schedule
or undertake the on-site audits for reasons that, in the Panel's view, do not seem legitimate to justify
AUPSA's inaction between the date of its communications, in August 2017 and February 2020, and
the date of the establishment of the Panel.1301
7.618. In particular, on 9 August 2017, AUPSA indicated that "given the limited number of auditors
and the large volume of requests, the planned date of the visit will be slightly postponed" for the
Costa Rican establishments that had, by that date, requested the renewal of their respective sanitary
approvals.1302 While the Panel understands that the national authorities acted within the means of
their available resources, especially where their human resources specialized in certain tasks are
limited, it does not seem justifiable that the scheduling and subsequent undertaking of the on-site
audits of the 16 Costa Rican establishments was delayed for a period of more than two and a half
years, between August 2017 and March 2020.
7.619. In the case of establishments Nos. 46A, 46B and 46C of the company Dos Pinos, AUPSA
indicated in three communications that the on-site audits would be undertaken on various dates,
between January and March 2020.1303 In the most recent of those communications, dated
27 February 2020, AUPSA changed the date of the on-site audits to the week of 9 to 13 March 2020
as a result of "delays in the administrative process of issuing the passport of one of our auditors".1304
This last-minute change, after giving different dates for the audits on three occasions, is attributable
to Panama and contrasts with the interest that the company Dos Pinos showed in the on-site audits
being undertaken. In its renewal request, SENASA asked, on behalf of that company, for the on-site

audits to be undertaken1305 and showed that it stood ready to cooperate with the Panamanian
authorities during the scheduling of the three on-site audits that, in the end, were not carried out.
In addition, SENASA provided responses to the questionnaire sent by AUPSA in January 2020.1306
The fact that AUPSA did not react in any way to these responses also points to AUPSA's inaction
regarding the on-site audits.
7.620. With regard to the other Costa Rican establishments, Panama did not take any action to
effectively undertake the on-site audits requested, even though Costa Rica requested them for all
of the establishments in question. As mentioned above, Panama merely announced a timetable for
the on-site audits in Article 4 of AUPSA Resolution No. 145-AG-2019 of 26 December 2019.
Moreover, in the case of establishments Nos. 8 and 8C of the company Coopemontecillos, Panama
stated that "while the renewal audit of the two establishments listed is still pending ... those
establishments may continue to export to Panama".1307 The Panel notes that merely stating that the
on-site audits would be undertaken, without scheduling them, equates to inaction on the part of the
Panamanian authorities in terms of proceeding with the sanitary approval renewal procedure for the
establishments.
7.621. Accordingly, the Panel concurs with Costa Rica1308 in that the delays in undertaking the
on-site audits prior to the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, around March 2020, are not justified.
Panama has not demonstrated that AUPSA's efforts to coordinate the audits justified the time that
elapsed between the submission of the renewal requests and the introduction of measures related
to the COVID-19 pandemic that prevented Panama's inspectors from undertaking the audits.
7.622. Therefore, the Panel finds that the delays incurred by Panama prior to the onset of the
COVID-19 pandemic in Panama and Costa Rica in March 2020 are undue delays within the meaning
of Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement. Although this finding is sufficient to find a violation of
Annex C(1)(a) with respect to the 16 Costa Rican establishments, the Panel will continue with its
assessment of the subsequent events in order to cover the entire time-period identified by Costa Rica
and, thus, help to secure a positive solution to this dispute.
7.623. The Panel recalls that Panama argues that, with respect to the period after the onset of the
COVID-19 pandemic, the delays in undertaking the on-site audits and in completing the sanitary
approval renewal procedures are justified for two reasons: (a) the measures related to the COVID-19
pandemic; and (b) Costa Rica's refusal to provide the information necessary to complete those
procedures.1309
Delays brought about by the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in Panama and Costa Rica
before the establishment of the Panel
7.624. Panama argues that the measures it adopted in connection with the COVID-19 pandemic
prevented the scheduling of on-site audits of the Costa Rican establishments, and that these
measures, as well as the COVID-19 infections of their staff, substantially hampered their ability to
complete all administrative procedures.1310 Panama considers that even after the lifting of
restrictions on travel to Costa Rica, the situation of uncertainty arising from the COVID-19 pandemic
made it quite difficult to conduct on-site audits after October 2020.1311 Panama adds that a
documentary review did not allow it to achieve its ALOP.1312
7.625. Costa Rica, for its part, argues that, between March and June 2020, Panama could have
replaced on-site audits with a documentary review of the relevant information such as that accepted
for establishments in Peru, New Zealand, and Mexico.1313 Costa Rica also claims that Panamanian
