7.627. From the foregoing, the Panel first notes that Panama, in its communication of 9 March 2020,
informed SENASA of its decision to postpone for the fourth time the on-site audit of the
establishments of the Dos Pinos company due to the yellow alert issued by Costa Rica on account of
the COVID-19 cases in the country.1342 This postponement was clearly justified by a global health
emergency without precedent in recent history. The Panel therefore considers that the sanitary
situation in connection with the COVID-19 pandemic and the response measures taken in
March 2020 did not allow Panama to conduct on-site audits.
7.628. The events described above, related to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on Panama's
administrative capacity, show that at least between March and October 2020, the time at which the
last suspension of international flights in Panama expired and Costa Rica authorized flights from
Central America and Panama, it was impossible for the Panamanian authorities to carry out on-site
audits of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.1343 Accordingly, during the period March-October 2020,
the delays incurred by Panama in conducting on-site audits at the 16 Costa Rican establishments
would be justified for legitimate reasons and would therefore not be undue.
7.629. Nonetheless, the Panel considers that Panamanian inspectors would have been able to travel
to Costa Rica to conduct on-site audits as of October 2020, when Costa Rica lifted travel restrictions.
As indicated in the table above, the evidence in the record shows that flights between Panama and
Costa Rica resumed in October 2020.1344 In this regard, Panama asserts that, even if they could
travel, its auditors did not wish to do so, given the ongoing uncertainty generated by the COVID-19
pandemic.1345 The Panel understands that the resumption of flights may not have been sufficient to
enable on-site audits to take place, given that willingness to travel in the context of the pandemic is
a difficult matter to test and evaluate. However, Panama made no attempt to coordinate on-site
audits for almost a year between October 2020 and September 2021. In the Panel's view, this delay
of more than a year would be unjustifiable in the absence of legitimate reasons justifying it in the
record.

below, indicates that administrative activity was resuming, allowing AUPSA to progress with the

Costa Rica's alleged failure to provide necessary information
7.636. As noted above, Panama claims that, between the expiry of the sanitary approvals of the
Costa Rican establishments on 30 June 2020 and Costa Rica's request for the establishment of a
panel on 20 August 2021, its ability to complete the procedures for the renewal of sanitary approvals
was severely affected not only by the COVID-19 pandemic, but also by Costa Rica's failure to provide
information on its animal health system.1358 Costa Rica rejects this argument.1359
7.637. The record shows that Panama requested information from Costa Rica through
communications that it sent on 2 January 20201360, 22 February 20211361, and 1 April 2021.1362 In
response to these communications, Costa Rica sent information to Panama on three occasions:
19 February 20201363, 24 March 20211364, and 3 June 2021.1365
7.638. According to Panama, the information provided by Costa Rica was insufficient to enable
completion of the procedures for renewing the sanitary approvals.1366 Costa Rica responds that the
information requested was excessive.1367 As explained in detail in section 7.4.2.3.3 below, the Panel
considers that Panama requested more information from Costa Rica than it needed in order to be
able to complete the procedures for renewing the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican
establishments. In this regard, the Panel recalls that any request for excessive information entails
an undue delay.1368
7.639. Accordingly, the Panel considers that Panama incurred delays by taking more than a year to
inform Costa Rica that further information was required in order to complete the procedure for
renewing the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments in question. The Panel finds no
legitimate reasons in the record to justify such a delay beyond the period from March to June 2020,
during which measures relating to the COVID-19 pandemic significantly limited the operational
capacity of the Panamanian authorities.

7.4.2.3.2.4 Conclusion on Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement
7.640. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that the evidence in the record
demonstrates that Panama incurred delays in the process of renewing the sanitary approvals of the
16 Costa Rican establishments between 2017 and September 2021, when the Panel was established.
7.641. These delays arise from three main aspects: (a) delays in clarifying the information that
Panama required in order to process the renewals, and in conducting the corresponding documentary
review, (b) the failure to conduct on-site audits, which in the case at hand form part of the sanitary
approval renewal procedure, and (c) the failure to issue rulings on the substance of the applications
to renew the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.642. With respect to the delays in clarifying the information that Panama required and in
conducting the corresponding documentary review, the Panel considers that these delays were
undue within the meaning of Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement during the periods
January 2020-March 2020 and June 2020-February 2021, as there is no reason why Panama should
not have informed Costa Rica of the missing information it needed. The Panel further considers that
there are no reasons to justify, during the above periods, the failure to undertake a documentary
review of the information Costa Rica sent to Panama in February 2020.
7.643. With respect to the failure to conduct on-site audits, the Panel considers that these delays
were undue within the meaning of Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement during the periods
January 2017-March 2020 and October 2020-September 2021, as there is no legitimate justification
for not having carried out the respective audits. The Panel, however, considers that Panama's delays
between March and October 2020 are not undue, as these delays are justified by the measures taken
to address the COVID-19 pandemic.
7.644. With regard to AUPSA's failure to issue rulings, the Panel considers that these delays were
undue within the meaning of Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement during the periods
January 2017-March 2020 and June 2020-September 2021, taking into account that deadlines in
administrative procedures were partially resumed as of June 2020, following a temporary suspension
between March and June 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
7.645. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Annex
C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement, because Panama incurred undue delays in the course of the
procedures for renewing the sanitary approvals of 16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.4.2.3.3 Panama's invocation of Article 23 of the draft articles of the International Law
Commission (ILC) on responsibility of States for internationally wrongful acts in relation
to Annex C(1)(a)
7.4.2.3.3.1 Main arguments of the parties
Panama
7.646. Panama submits that if the Panel finds that Panama has not completed the approval
procedures relating to Measure 2 without undue delay, in a manner inconsistent with Article 8 and
Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement, the wrongfulness of such action would be excluded on grounds
of force majeure pursuant to Article 23 of the draft ILC articles on responsibility of States for
internationally wrongful acts (draft articles on State responsibility).1369 In particular, Panama
submits that the COVID-19 pandemic is a circumstance that meets the three requirements of
Article 23 of the draft articles on State responsibility in order to be considered a situation of
force majeure, namely: (i) it is an act caused by an irresistible force or an unforeseen event; (ii) it
is beyond Panama's control; and (iii) it made it materially impossible, in the circumstances, to
perform the obligation to complete the approval procedures contained in Annex C(1)(a). Panama
submits that such justification applies to any violation of an obligation arising from a rule of
international law, such as a treaty, and that it constitutes "a relevant form of international law" that
1369 Panama's second written submission, paras 11-14.

WT/DS599/R
- 189 -
the Panel is required to apply pursuant to Article 3.2 of the DSU and Article 31(3)(c) of the Vienna
Convention on the Law of Treaties.1370
Costa Rica
7.647. Costa Rica submits that Article 23 of the draft articles on State responsibility is not a covered
agreement and thus not part of the WTO's body of rules, and that Panama has no legal basis to
require the Panel to apply it. Costa Rica adds that, even assuming that Article 23 was applicable,
the concept of force majeure would not provide any additional element to the interpretation and
application of the term "undue delay" in Annex C(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement. According to the
meaning of that term, a panel could consider any circumstances beyond the control of the importing
country, such as the COVID-19 pandemic. For this reason, Costa Rica views it as a pointless exercise
to examine in addition whether, under Article 23 of the draft articles on State responsibility, the
effect of a situation of force majeure such as the COVID-19 pandemic would be considered.1371
7.4.2.3.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.648. The Panel notes that Panama's arguments under Article 23 of the draft articles on
State responsibility1372 focus on events relating to the COVID-19 pandemic. The Panel recalls that in
its assessment of Costa Rica's claim under Annex C(1)(a), it has already thoroughly examined how
the events of the COVID-19 pandemic affected the performance of Panama's obligations under that
provision. In this regard, the Panel concluded that the measures identified by Panama in relation to
the COVID-19 pandemic demonstrate that, between March 2020 and October 2020, the capacity of
Panamanian government officials to conduct the relevant administrative procedures was significantly
affected by the pandemic.1373
7.649. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that its findings under Annex C(1)(a) are
sufficient to assist the DSB in making recommendations and rulings that achieve a satisfactory
settlement of the matter.1374 Accordingly, the Panel will not examine Panama's invocation of
Article 23 of the draft articles on State responsibility in this dispute.

7.4.2.3.4.2 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.658. According to Annex C(1)(c) of the SPS Agreement, Members shall ensure that "information
requirements are limited to what is necessary for appropriate control, inspection and approval
procedures". This obligation, which applies with respect to any procedure to check and ensure the
fulfilment of SPS measures, entails determining the objective of the procedure in question and the
type of information necessary for that procedure.1389 If the evidence in the record demonstrates that
the respondent's requests for information are excessive for the purposes of the procedure in
question, the respondent could be found to have acted inconsistently with Annex C(1)(c).1390
7.659. In the light of the legal standard described above, the Panel will proceed to examine whether
Panama requested more information from Costa Rica than was necessary for the purposes of the
procedure to renew the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
Whether Panama requested more information than was necessary for the relevant
procedure
7.660. Costa Rica claims that Panama's request for information was excessive because a review of
Costa Rica's sanitary eligibility is not necessary in order to process the sanitary approval renewal
applications for its establishments.1391 Panama, for its part, submits that its regulations require the
sanitary authority to evaluate the sanitary system of the exporting country when processing an
application for the renewal of sanitary approvals.1392
7.661. In accordance with the legal standard described above, the Panel begins its assessment by
determining the objective of the renewal procedure and the information necessary for the purposes
of that procedure, in accordance with the applicable Panamanian regulations. The Panel recalls that
the process for renewing sanitary approvals is governed by section VII, paragraph 7, of the
AUPSA Manual for the approval of establishments. This paragraph provides the following:
AUPSA may renew the approval of an establishment, production chain or processing
plant, based on the following aspects, for which relevant information may be requested
from the competent authority of the exporting country and/or the interested party:
• Sanitary and/or phytosanitary status of the exporting country.
• Sanitary status of the establishment and characteristics of the products it would
like to continue to introduce into the country.
• Experience with, knowledge of and confidence in the food inspection and
certification systems of the exporting country and the establishment, production
chain or processing plant in question.

AUPSA reserves the right to carry out audits and inspections at origin as part of the
renewal process for establishments, production chains or processing plants, basing its
decision on the relevant risk analysis.1393
7.662. In addition, the Panel recalls that the purpose of the establishment approval procedure is to
ensure the safety of the food imported into Panama by checking the sanitary conditions in Costa Rica
and to protect human and animal life and health from animal diseases that are transmissible to
humans (zoonoses).1394
7.663. Accordingly, in the case of sanitary approval renewals, the procedure in question provides
that AUPSA shall base its decision to renew the sanitary approval of an establishment on up-to-date
information concerning: (a) the sanitary status of the exporting country; (b) experience with,
knowledge of and confidence in the food inspection and certification systems of the exporting country
and of the establishment, production chain, or processing plant in question; and (c) the record of
compliance of previous conformity assessments. As part of the renewal process, AUPSA may also
request relevant information from the competent authority of the exporting country or conduct audits
and inspections at origin.1395 It follows from the text of section VII, paragraph 7, of the Manual for
the approval of establishments itself that, as claimed by Panama1396, AUPSA has the authority to
request updated information from Costa Rica on its sanitary and safety conditions for the purpose
of processing sanitary approval renewal applications.
7.664. The parties agree1397 that AUPSA acted in accordance with its powers to request updated
information when, on 2 January 2020, it sent Costa Rica the "Questionnaire verifying the sanitary
status of a country requesting the renewal of eligibility or recognition of an area, place or site of
production free from a quarantine disease or with a low prevalence thereof, for the export of dairy
products to the Republic of Panama".1398 This questionnaire was sent in the context of the scheduling
of on-site audits for two establishments of the Dos Pinos company exporting milk to Panama. In this
questionnaire, Panama requests updated information covering the period 2014-2018 on the subject
matter described below.1399
7.665. Costa Rica replied to this questionnaire on 19 February 2020.1400 The record does not show
that Panama commented on Costa Rica's replies. However, almost 14 months later, on
22 February 2021, Panama sent Costa Rica the "preliminary questionnaire to determine a country's
animal health eligibility in order to verify whether its measures achieve the same level of protection
established by the relevant laws and regulations of the Republic of Panama".1401 In the

7.666. Comparing the January 2020 questionnaire with that of February 2021, the Panel notes first

implementation of GAPP in relation to dairy products.1406

five thematic areas: (a) general country information; (b) assessment of the competent
authority and technical capacity of the country's veterinary services; (c) the country's

7.668. The comparison demonstrates that, in the questionnaire sent in February 2021, Panama
requested information that was much more detailed than the information it had requested in
January 2020. In particular, there are six thematic areas on which Panama requests additional
information that had not been requested in January 20201445, as well as some common thematic
areas in respect of which more details are requested.1446 These differences are reflected in the
structure of the questionnaires.
7.669. The Panel will now assess whether AUPSA needed the additional information requested from
Costa Rica in February 2021 in order to rule on the substance of the renewal applications for the
sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.670. At an early stage of these proceedings, Panama stated that the applicable questionnaire for
evaluating Costa Rica's sanitary system, in the context of renewing the sanitary approvals of
Costa Rica's meat and dairy establishments, was the questionnaire sent on 2 January 2020.1447
Subsequently, Panama explained that the questionnaire sent on 22 February 2021 sought
information that would allow for an evaluation of the production of milk and dairy products in
Costa Rica from an animal health standpoint.1448 Panama also submitted three model questionnaires,
which it claims it uses during the renewal procedures for sanitary approvals1449 for meat1450,
dairy1451, and aquaculture products.1452 The record contains no evidence that Panama sent these
questionnaires to Costa Rica as part of the process of renewing the sanitary approvals for its
establishments.
7.671. Regarding the questionnaire sent in January 2020, the Panel recalls that both parties agree
that this questionnaire refers to the information required to process the renewal of the sanitary
approvals.1453 The fact that Panama indicated that this was the applicable questionnaire in this
context, suggests that AUPSA did not need any additional information beyond what was specified in
the questionnaire. The Panel will assess the other relevant evidence in order to confirm whether the

- 200 -
information that AUPSA requested from Costa Rica in January 2020 was indeed sufficient to process
the renewal of the sanitary approvals of its establishments or whether, on the contrary, Panama
needed the additional information referred to in the questionnaire sent in February 2021. To do so,
the Panel considers it appropriate to compare the information requested in the model questionnaires
submitted by Panama for dairy and meat products (Exhibits PAN-132 and PAN-131, respectively),
on the one hand, and the information requested in the questionnaires sent in January 2020 (Exhibit
CRI-38) and in February 2021 (Exhibit CRI-268), on the other.1454
7.672. When comparing the content of the model questionnaires on dairy and meat products with
the questionnaire sent to Costa Rica on 2 January 20201455, the first observation is that the
three questionnaires share the same thematic structure, which is divided into three main sections:
(a) information to review the sanitary conditions of the exporting country (Section I); (b) information
to review the organization and operation of the sanitary services in relation to the product in question
(Section II); and (c) information to review the application of GAPP (Section III). The title of
Section III in the model questionnaires also mentions GMP, hygiene procedures, and safety. The
second observation is that, for most questions involving a time-frame, the model questionnaires ask
for information for the previous three years. As mentioned above, most of the January 2020
questionnaire requests updated information for the 2014-2018 period.
7.673. Regarding Section I, which pertains to information needed to review the sanitary conditions
of the exporting country, the Panel notes the following:
a. The two sets of questionnaires contain two questions regarding general information on the
country (name and geography of the country).1456
b. The model questionnaires contain additional questions regarding whether the country is a
member of the OIE and, if so, since when; and regarding production (over the previous
three years), the proportion of production carried out in establishments for export and
under veterinary supervision, a list of exporting establishments, and the number of
establishments overseen by the competent authority for the inspection of the products.1457
7.674. Regarding Section II, which pertains to information needed to review the organization and
operation of the sanitary services, the Panel notes that most of the subsections are the same across
all three questionnaires. These subsections cover the following: (a) a description of the location of
offices, laboratories, entry points and control posts; (b) the organization, structure, and level of
efficiency/training of the exporting country's official sanitary services; (c) sanitary legislation relating
to the product in question that is wished to be introduced; (d) surveillance systems; and
(e) laboratory services.1458 The only subsection under Section II that is found in the model
questionnaires but not in the questionnaire sent in January 2020 pertains to relations with
independent scientific experts.1459 Regarding this Section, the Panel notes the following:
a. In the subsection pertaining to the location of offices, laboratories, entry points and control
posts, the two sets of questionnaires contain the same question.1460
1454 The Panel notes that the questionnaire sent in February 2021 (Exhibit CRI-268) contains questions
on dairy and meat products. Accordingly, the Panel considers it appropriate to compare the two model
questionnaires on these products (Exhibits PAN-131 and PAN-132).

b. In the subsection pertaining to the organization, structure, and level of efficiency/training
of the exporting country's official sanitary services, the two sets of questionnaires contain
the same questions regarding the organizational chart and responsibilities of sanitary
services at the national and local levels, and whether there are other services responsible
for public health and safety in respect of milk and dairy products.1461 The model
questionnaires contain more detailed questions on the human resources available to the
competent animal health authority (four questions) and safety (four questions), and
request details concerning the number of inspectors, technical assistants and core staff
members. These questionnaires also request further information on the financial
management of official sanitary services (five additional questions), administrative data
(three additional questions), financial data regarding means of transport (one additional
question), and details of veterinary training (one additional question).1462
c. In the subsection pertaining to sanitary legislation, the two sets of questionnaires contain
the same questions on: legislation in force on sanitary matters; and the enforcement of
laws and penalties in cases of non-compliance.1463 The January 2020 questionnaire
contains questions on the following topics that were not included in the model
questionnaires: judicial actions and results of actions taken by the veterinary services
regarding food safety in the previous three years; and information on regulations on the
implementation of quality systems in plants wishing to export milk and dairy products,
and certification and recertification requirements for these plants.1464 Lastly, the model
questionnaires contain additional questions that ask for further details on existing sanitary
regulations and other related issues.1465
d. In the subsection pertaining to surveillance systems, the questionnaire sent in
January 2020 requests more information than the model questionnaires. The
questionnaire sent in 2020 contained 51 questions, seven of which were of a general
nature, while the rest focused on the surveillance and control programmes for bovine
brucellosis and bovine tuberculosis. In contrast, the model questionnaires contain
13 general questions on diseases and there are no requests for details of the two bovine
diseases mentioned in the questionnaire sent in January 2020.1466
e. In the subsection pertaining to laboratory services, the model questionnaires expand on
two questions contained in the January 2020 questionnaire regarding information on
diagnostic and research laboratories. They do not contain certain questions on the national
programme on toxic residues in dairy products, compliance history, and the existence of
an official pathogen monitoring programme.1467

WT/DS599/R
- 202 -
a. Both sets of questionnaires contain similar questions on the following thematic areas:
hygiene and biosecurity measures in respect of the location and construction of livestock
production plants; measures to assess and ensure water quality in livestock production
plants; measures relating to the supply and quality of feed in livestock establishments;
animal welfare standards in livestock production; good practices in respect of the use of
veterinary drugs; good practices in respect of the use of pesticides and chemical
substances; the transport of live animals; and the recording of activities to comply with
good practices.1468
b. The model questionnaires contain three additional questions on: the humane treatment of
animals during transportation; good livestock practices regarding the hygienic production
of fresh milk; and any legal actions and sanctions in respect of food safety in the previous
three years.1469
7.676. A comparison of these questionnaires reveals that, according to the model questionnaires
submitted by Panama to the Panel, Panama usually asks for more detailed information on certain
aspects relating to the processing of the products concerned and on the resources allocated to
sanitary services (including laboratories). The model questionnaires contain some questions on the
application of GAPP and GMP in relation to hygiene and safety. It should also be recalled that the
information requested in the model questionnaires usually covers a time-frame comprising the
previous three years.
7.677. The questionnaire that Panama sent to Costa Rica in January 2020, for its part, contained
many more questions on the status of bovine tuberculosis and bovine brucellosis, along with requests
for information spanning a broader time-frame (the previous five years), which appears to align with
the dates when Costa Rica last underwent a sanitary evaluation, in 2014. Although the January 2020
questionnaire asks for less detail than the model questionnaires, except in respect of the
two diseases already mentioned, it addresses the same thematic areas as these questionnaires and
contains many of the same questions.
7.678. In the light of this comparison, the Panel considers that the information that Panama
requested in January 2020 corresponds to what it usually requests in its model questionnaires.
Accordingly, the information that AUPSA requested from SENASA in January 2020 was sufficient for
Panama to evaluate Costa Rica's sanitary status in order to complete the sanitary approval renewal
process for the establishments seeking to export dairy products to Panama.
7.679. The Panel will now proceed, nevertheless, to compare the content of the model
questionnaires with that of the questionnaire sent to Costa Rica on 22 February 20211470 in order to
confirm whether the information requested by Panama in the latter questionnaire was excessive.
7.680. The Panel notes that, unlike the January 2020 questionnaire, the February 2021
questionnaire does not follow the same structure as the model questionnaires.1471 The February
2021 questionnaire is more extensive than the model questionnaires, in terms of both topics covered
and the number of questions posed, as described below.

e. Information on livestock traceability
i. The model questionnaires have no section on this subject, but do contain a question
on the methodology for recording data on compliance with GAPP, and data that would
allow an animal's history to be traced from livestock farms to immediate recipient
(traceability).1492
ii. The February 2021 questionnaire requests much more detailed information on this
topic by including questions on the regulatory framework for livestock traceability; the
existence of a traceability system that has been or is being implemented; the
registration system for products, farms, establishments, carriers and vehicles engaged
in the transport of animals; and the individual official identification system for each
animal species.1493
f. Information on the quarantine system
i. The model questionnaires have no section on this subject, but do contain a question
on approved points of entry for inspection (air, maritime, and land) and internal
quarantine movement checkpoints.1494

the quarantine system. In particular, it covers the following subject matter (including
references to legal basis and organizational structure): (a) a description of the
quarantine system for the prevention and control of animal diseases; (b) quarantine
controls applied for the surveillance of transboundary diseases in seaports, airports,
and border zones; (c) treatment at points of entry and responsibility for carrying it
out; (d) demarcation of the country's animal health zones; (e) control within animal
health zones; and (f) sanitary requirements for internal control of the movement of
animals between animal health zones, and of diseases subject to mandatory
control.1495

methods used for animal diseases; (d) the existence of international certification for

7.683. The Panel draws two main conclusions from the above comparison. The first is that the model
questionnaires include, in some areas, more detailed questions on topics that are also referred to in
the February 2021 questionnaire. The second is that, in the February 2021 questionnaire, AUPSA
requested information that was much more detailed than the information it usually requests in the
model questionnaires for certain areas, while for others, it requested additional information not
included in the model questionnaires. In the Panel's view, the fact that much of the additional
information requested does not appear in the model questionnaires typically used by Panama,
suggests that this information was not necessary for the purpose of renewing the sanitary approvals.
7.684. The Panel further notes that, in its communications to Costa Rica, AUPSA did not explain
why it needed this information. However, during the current proceedings, Panama stated that the
information requested in February 2021 was necessary owing to the animal health risks posed by
dairy products, such as the transmission of foot and mouth disease. Panama also stated that in 2014
it had requested the Costa Rican authorities responsible for animal health to evaluate these types
of risks, so it should not have come as a surprise to those authorities that animal health information
was requested during a renewal procedure.1516
7.685. The Panel does not consider that Panama's explanations justify the need to request such
detailed information on general aspects of the sanitary system such as those referred to in
paragraph 7.679 above. It is the Panel's understanding that this would be information that was not
clearly related to the animal health concerns raised by Panama in a context where it had a history
of trade with Costa Rica in multiple products and was familiar with the sanitary and veterinary
authorities of its trading partner. Furthermore, as mentioned above, the questionnaire sent on
2 January 2020 contained 51 specific questions on two bovine diseases, and other more general
questions regarding the management of zoonoses and the animal health situation in Costa Rica.
7.686. As explained above1517, the Panel considers that Panamanian law authorizes AUPSA to
request updated information on the sanitary situation of the exporting country, which may include
information about the animal health situation.
7.687. Furthermore, it should be recalled that there is nothing in the record to show that AUPSA
commented on the information that SENASA sent it on 19 February 2020 in response to the
questionnaire of 2 January 2020. If AUPSA believed that the information it requested more than a
year later, in February 2021, was necessary for its evaluation, it could have notified SENASA much
more promptly.1518 AUPSA's lack of action in this respect would tend to confirm that the additional
information requested in February 2021 was not necessary.

7.4.2.3.4.3 Conclusion on Annex C(1)(c) to the SPS Agreement
7.690. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Annex C(1)(c)
to the SPS Agreement, because Panama required more information from Costa Rica than was
necessary for the purpose of completing the procedure for renewing the sanitary approvals of the
16 Costa Rican establishments.
7.4.2.3.5 Whether Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 8 of the SPS Agreement
7.691. Costa Rica requests the Panel to find a violation of Article 8 of the SPS Agreement because
Panama has incurred undue delay and has required more information than is necessary for the
procedures at issue, in breach of Annex C(1)(a) and Annex C(1)(c) to the SPS Agreement.1521
Panama, for its part, contends that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Measure 2 violates
Article 8 of the SPS Agreement.1522
7.692. The Panel notes that Annex C(1) to the SPS Agreement gives meaning and content to
Article 8 of the Agreement.1523 Article 8 establishes the obligation to observe the provisions of
Annex C in the operation of control, inspection and approval procedures. Thus, a failure to observe
the provisions of Annex C implies a breach of Article 8.1524
7.693. The Panel has found that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement
because Panama incurred undue delays in the course of the procedures for renewing the sanitary
approvals of 16 Costa Rican establishments.1525 The Panel has also found that Measure 2 is
inconsistent with Annex C(1)(c) to the SPS Agreement because Panama required more information
from Costa Rica than was necessary for the purpose of completing these procedures.1526
7.694. Consequently, the Panel finds that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 8 of the
SPS Agreement, because Panama failed to comply with the provisions of Annex C(1)(a) and
Annex C(1)(c) to the Agreement.

7.4.2.3.6 Conclusion on Article 8 of the SPS Agreement
7.695. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 2 is inconsistent with
Annex C(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement, because Panama incurred undue delays in the course of the
procedures for renewing the sanitary approvals of 16 Costa Rican establishments1527; and with Annex
C(1)(c) to the SPS Agreement, because Panama required more information from Costa Rica than
was necessary for the purpose of completing these procedures.1528 Consequently, Measure 2 is also
inconsistent with Article 8 of the SPS Agreement.
7.4.2.4 Claims under Articles 2.3 and 5.5 of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.4.1 Order of analysis
7.696. In its panel request, Costa Rica submits a claim that all of the measures at issue are
inconsistent with Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement, together with a consequential violation of
Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement.1529 Subsequently, Costa Rica proposes that the Panel first address
its claims under Article 2.3 "because of the broader nature of the non-discrimination obligations"
contained therein, "as opposed to the focus of Article 5.5 on discrimination that may arise in respect
of distinctions in levels of protection".1530 Furthermore, Costa Rica considers that the Panel could
exercise judicial economy with respect to the claims under Article 5.5 if it were to find that
Measures 2 and 3 were inconsistent with Article 2.3.1531 For its part, Panama does not object to
Costa Rica's suggestion, and asserts that the claims under Articles 2.3 and 5.5 are only relevant in
the context of Measure 3, as Measure 2 is exclusively concerned with establishment approval
procedures.1532
7.697. The Panel recalls that Article 5 of the SPS Agreement elaborates on the basic obligations
under Article 2 of the Agreement.1533 Previous panels have considered that Article 5.5, which
requires Members to avoid arbitrary or unjustifiable distinctions in the ALOPs they consider to be
appropriate in different situations, when such distinctions result in discrimination or a disguised
restriction on international trade, elaborates on the non-discrimination obligation under
Article 2.3.1534 It follows that in previous disputes where claims were made under both provisions,
panels have sometimes considered Article 5.5 first and concluded that their findings applied to the
claim under Article 2.3.1535 In those disputes where the claim under Article 5.5 was presented as an
alternative claim, or where arguments under Article 2.3 were made first, because of the broader
nature of its obligations, the panels chose to start their assessments with Article 2.3 and exercised
judicial economy with respect to Article 5.5 by finding a violation of Article 2.3.1536
7.698. Given the parties' positions on the order of analysis as stated above1537 and the possibility
of exercising judicial economy, the Panel considers it appropriate to begin its analysis with Costa
Rica's claims under Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement.

7.4.2.4.2 Claims under Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.4.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
Costa Rica
7.699. Costa Rica claims that Panama arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminates between Members
where identical or similar conditions prevail, contrary to the provisions of the first sentence of
Article 2.3. In particular, Costa Rica submits that Panama refused to extend the sanitary
authorizations of its establishments based on a documentary review without a prior on-site audit,
while it granted an extension in the same conditions to similar establishments in Peru, Mexico, and
New Zealand.1538
7.700. Costa Rica also states that Panama revoked its sanitary eligibility, even though its sanitary
status had not changed, while it did not revoke the sanitary eligibilities of Peru, Mexico, and
New Zealand, whose sanitary status, like that of Costa Rica, had remained unchanged. Costa Rica
asserts that, consequently, it is obliged to go through a new sanitary eligibility process in order to
resume trade.1539
7.701. Costa Rica submits that the conditions in Peru, Mexico, and New Zealand are identical or
similar to those in Costa Rica1540 because:
a. they have sanitary eligibility approved by Panama, and their sanitary status remains
unchanged;
b. they have establishments that export products of animal original to Panama for human or
animal consumption; and
c. they took steps to renew the sanitary approvals of their respective establishments.1541
7.702. Costa Rica further submits that the similarity between the conditions prevailing in Peru,
Mexico, and New Zealand, on the one hand, and Costa Rica, on the other, is based on the fact that
Panama should have granted the same type of extension to the establishments in these countries
at the same time (July 2020). At that time, there were ongoing logistical challenges and border
restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic.1542
7.703. Costa Rica contends that the treatment accorded to its establishments is discriminatory
compared to that accorded to establishments in Peru, Mexico, and New Zealand.1543 For Costa Rica,
the discrimination stems from Panama's refusal to extend the sanitary approvals of Costa Rica's
establishments, while Panama granted this type of extension to establishments in Peru, Mexico, and
New Zealand which were in identical or similar circumstances.1544 Moreover, unlike these other
Members, Panama prevented Costa Rica from undertaking the process to renew the sanitary
approvals of its establishments on the basis of documentary review and without disrupting trade.1545
Costa Rica adds that, in the case of Peru, the discrimination is especially clear1546, as Panama used
the health crisis caused by the COVID-19 pandemic as a reason to grant the extension of the
approvals to the Peruvian establishments on a purely documentary basis, while that same reason
was used by Panama to reject Costa Rica's request to extend the approvals of its establishments in

7.4.2.4.2.2 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.709. Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement reads as follows:
Members shall ensure that their sanitary and phytosanitary measures do not arbitrarily
or unjustifiably discriminate between Members where identical or similar conditions
prevail, including between their own territory and that of other Members. Sanitary and
phytosanitary measures shall not be applied in a manner which would constitute a
disguised restriction on international trade.
7.710. It is clear from the wording of the provision that Article 2.3 contains two obligations, each
of which corresponds to one of the sentences of Article 2.3:
a. Under the first sentence, Members must ensure that their SPS measures do not arbitrarily
or unjustifiably discriminate between Members where identical or similar conditions
prevail, including between their own territory and that of other Members.
b. Under to the second sentence, Members must ensure that their SPS measures are not
applied in a manner which would constitute a disguised restriction on international
trade.1560
First sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
7.711. In the light of the first sentence of Article 2.3, the complainant is required to demonstrate
three cumulative elements to establish a violation of this obligation:
a. that the SPS measure discriminates between the territories of Members other than the
Member imposing the measure, or between the territory of the Member imposing the
measure and that of another Member;
b. that the discrimination is arbitrary or unjustifiable; and
c. that identical or similar conditions prevail in the territory of the Members compared.1561
7.712. These three elements inform each other. While it has not been deemed that the analysis of
these elements should follow a particular order1562, in previous disputes, panels have begun their
analysis by identifying the relevant conditions, and assessing whether they are identical or similar,
as this is considered to provide a good starting point for an analysis under the first sentence of
Article 2.3.1563
7.713. With respect to the prevalence of identical or similar conditions in the Members compared,
it should be noted that the relevant conditions must be identified, taking into account the specific
nature of the measure at issue and the specific circumstances of the case. This may require
consideration of the SPS measure's stated Annex A(1) "protected interest"1564 or of the specific
SPS risk at issue1565, which relates to an objective pursued under the SPS Agreement. This objective
may inform the "conditions" that must be "identical or similar" under the first sentence of
1560 Panel Reports, Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.1296; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.388; and
Costa Rica – Avocados (Mexico), para. 7.2144.
1561 This approach has been followed by previous panels. See e.g. Panel Reports, Russia – Pigs (EU),
para. 7.1292; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.389; US – Poultry (China), para. 7.317; US – Animals,
para. 7.571; and Australia – Salmon (Article 21.5 – Canada), para. 7.111.

omitted))). See also Appellate Body Report, Korea – Radionuclides, para. 5.58.

Article 2.3.1566 In this connection, previous disputes have considered the presence of a disease within
a territory and the concomitant risk associated with that disease to be relevant conditions.1567
7.714. The conditions referred to in Article 2.3 may include those found both in the products at
issue and in the territory of the exporting or importing Member. They also include those territorial
conditions that may not yet have manifested in products, but that are relevant in the light of the
regulatory objective and specific SPS risks at issue.1568
7.715. With respect to the existence of discrimination, previous panels have been guided by the
interpretation of the term "discrimination" in the context of other agreements, and in particular, the
chapeau of Article XX of the GATT 1994.1569 Accordingly, discrimination in the context of Article 2.3
has been interpreted to exist when different treatment is accorded to Members where identical or
similar conditions prevail.1570 Other provisions that have been referred to when interpreting the term
"discrimination" are Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 and Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement.1571 Thus,
a measure has been considered to discriminate if it "alters the conditions of competition to the
detriment of products originating in the territories of Members other than the Member imposing the
measure".1572 It has also been held that prohibiting imports of relevant products of one Member,
while permitting imports of relevant products presenting an equivalent level of risk from other
Members, is a "warning signal" of the existence of discrimination, as under Article 5.5 of the
SPS Agreement.1573
7.716. With respect to the arbitrary or unjustifiable nature of discrimination, previous panels have
also relied on interpretations given to the terms in the context of Article XX of the GATT 1994. As
such, it has been determined that the analysis of this issue "should focus on the cause of the
discrimination, or the rationale put forward to explain its existence".1574 The analysis would therefore
focus on whether the measure at issue bears a "rational connection to" its stated objective.1575
Second sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
7.717. To interpret the scope of the obligation contained in this sentence of Article 2.3, that
SPS measures are not applied in a manner which constitutes a disguised restriction on international
trade, previous panels have looked to the interpretation of these terms in the context of Article XX
1566 See also Appellate Body Report, Korea – Radionuclides, para. 5.59.
1567 Panel Reports, Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.1311; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.460; and
Australia – Salmon (Article 21.5 – Canada), para. 7.113.
1568 Appellate Body Report, Korea – Radionuclides, paras. 5.63-5.65.
1569 Panel Reports, Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.1313; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.400.
The chapeau of Article XX of the GATT 1994 reads:
Subject to the requirement that such measures are not applied in a manner which would
constitute a means of arbitrary or unjustifiable discrimination between countries where the same
conditions prevail, or a disguised restriction on international trade, nothing in this Agreement
shall be construed to prevent the adoption or enforcement by any [Member] of measures.
In SPS disputes, it has been observed that there are a number of similarities between the language of
Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement and the chapeau of Article XX of the GATT 1994. (Panel Report, Russia – Pigs
(EU), para. 7.1316 (quoting Panel Report, US – Animals, para. 7.570)). Furthermore, the eighth recital of the
preamble of the SPS Agreement states that Members desire to "elaborate rules for the application of the
provisions of GATT 1994 which relate to the use of sanitary or phytosanitary measures, in particular the
provisions of Article XX(b)".
1570 Appellate Body Report, US – Shrimp, para. 165.
1571 The Appellate Body in EC – Hormones confirmed that Article 2.3 is an important part of the context
of Article 5.5, and that, when read together with Article 2.3, Article 5.5 "mark[s] out and elaborat[es] a
particular route leading to the same destination set out in Article 2.3". See Appellate Body Report, EC –
Hormones, para. 212.
1572 Panel Report, US – Animals, para. 7.573 (referring to Appellate Body Reports, EC – Asbestos,
paras. 98-99; Dominican Republic – Import and Sale of Cigarettes, para. 96; and Philippines – Distilled Spirits,
para. 256).
1573 Panel Report, US – Animals, para. 7.605.
1574 Panel Report, Russia – Pigs (EU), paras. 7.1321 (quoting Appellate Body Report, Brazil – Retreaded
Tyres, paras. 225-226)-7.1323.
1575 Panel Report, Russia – Pigs (EU), paras. 7.1321 (quoting Appellate Body Report, Brazil – Retreaded
Tyres, para. 227). This approach was adopted in the context of the SPS Agreement by panels in a number of
disputes. (See Panel Reports, Russia – Pigs (EU), para. 7.1323; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.429; and
US – Animals, para. 7.574).

- 215 -
of the GATT 1994.1576 In that context, the terms "arbitrary discrimination", "unjustifiable
discrimination", and "disguised restriction on international trade" have been considered to impart
meaning to one another.1577
7.718. A disguised restriction on international trade has also been interpreted to encompass all
measures that constitute arbitrary or unjustifiable discrimination.1578 Accordingly, considerations
made in the context of an assessment of whether or not there is arbitrary or unjustifiable
discrimination will be relevant for determining whether a measure constitutes a disguised restriction
on international trade within the meaning of the second sentence of Article 2.3.
7.719. It should also be recalled that it has been held that when an SPS measure is not based on a
risk assessment, this is a strong indication that the measure "is not really concerned with the
protection of human, animal or plant life or health but is instead a trade-restrictive measure taken
in the guise of an SPS measure, i.e. a 'disguised restriction on international trade'".1579
7.720. The Panel agrees with the legal standard set out above and will proceed with its examination
of Costa Rica's claim under Article 2.3 on this basis. In this connection, the Panel observes that, in
its panel request, Costa Rica claimed that Measure 2 was inconsistent with Article 2.3, by referring
to both sentences of that provision.1580 However, in the course of these proceedings, Costa Rica has
only made arguments relating to a possible inconsistency of that measure with the first sentence of
Article 2.3.
7.721. Accordingly, in the absence of any argumentation, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has not
made a prima facie case of inconsistency of Measure 2 with the second sentence of Article 2.3 of the
SPS Agreement. The Panel will now proceed to examine Costa Rica's claim under the first sentence
of that provision.
Whether Measure 2 is inconsistent with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the
SPS Agreement
7.722. Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement, since
Panama arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminates between Members where identical or similar
conditions prevail, contrary to the first sentence of Article 2.3.1581
7.723. Before beginning its analysis, the Panel recalls that, according to Panama, Measure 2 is not
subject to the obligations of Article 2.3 because the measure is an approval procedure within the
meaning of Annex C.1582 In this connection, the Panel concluded that the non-discrimination
obligation under Article 2.3 extends to all types of SPS measures, and that it finds no reason why
that provision would not apply to Measure 2.1583 Accordingly, the Panel will now proceed to analyse
Costa Rica's claim under the first sentence of Article 2.3.

7.724. As in previous disputes1584, the Panel, in its analysis under the first sentence of Article 2.3,
will first assess whether the conditions identified by Costa Rica are identical or similar in the Members
concerned, within the meaning of that provision.
Whether identical or similar conditions prevail in territories of the Members in question
7.725. Under the legal standard set out above, one of the issues that Costa Rica must demonstrate
as the complainant is that the conditions prevailing in Costa Rica are identical or similar to those
prevailing in Peru, Mexico, and New Zealand for the purposes of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement.
In this connection, the Panel considers that the relevant conditions to be compared must first be
determined.
7.726. The conditions identified by Costa Rica as identical or similar with respect to Peru, Mexico,
and New Zealand are the following:
a. the validity of the sanitary eligibility;
b. the types of establishments and products in question;
c. the steps taken by the countries to request renewal of the sanitary approvals;
d. the type of extension that should have been granted; and
e. when AUPSA decided to reject or approve the requested extensions.1585
7.727. As indicated above1586, the particular "conditions" that must be shown to be "similar or
identical" under that provision, should be informed by the nature of SPS measures and the particular
circumstances of the case. Thus, "conditions" relating to the particular objective pursued (i.e. the
protected interest as set out in Annex A(1) to the SPS Agreement) and the risks addressed by
Measure 2 would be relevant for the Panel's analysis of whether identical or similar conditions prevail
between the Members.1587
7.728. The Panel recalls that Measure 2 is maintained by virtue of the non-renewal of the sanitary
approvals of 16 Costa Rican establishments to import dairy and meat products into Panama.1588
The Panel concluded that Measure 2 aims (a) to ensure the safety of food imported into Panama by
checking the sanitary conditions in Costa Rica, and (b) to protect animal and human life and health
against animal diseases transmissible to humans (zoonoses).1589
7.729. Regarding the risk addressed by Measure 2, the Panel notes that, with respect to food safety,
Measure 2 seeks to protect animal or human health or life from risks arising from additives,
contaminants, toxins, or disease-causing organisms in dairy and meat products.1590 In turn, with
respect to diseases transmissible to humans (zoonoses), Measure 2 seeks to protect human life or
health in Panama from diseases carried by by animals or products thereof.1591
7.730. Considering the above-mentioned objectives and risks, the Panel considers that all of the
conditions identified by Costa Rica are relevant, because they are all closely linked to the different
stages of the sanitary approval renewal procedure provided for in Panamanian regulations or those
applied by Panama. In this connection, it should be noted that:

7.733. Costa Rica maintains that the conditions in the countries being compared are only those
"relevant" for the purposes of Article 2.3. According to Costa Rica, the territorial conditions of the
countries compared are not relevant conditions in the light of the measure at issue. Costa Rica also

7.734. In the Panel's view, under the legal standard described above, the conditions identified by
Panama are also relevant to the present case.1603 The Panel understands that Costa Rica also agrees
that at least the condition relating to the health risks of the products from the different countries is
relevant, and is a condition of sanitary eligibility that Costa Rica identified within the relevant

7.735. Although Panama has not clearly identified the territorial conditions that could affect the
products in question, the Panel considers that, as this is a comparison of conditions that could affect
food products, these conditions would be those relating to the sanitary status of the countries
compared.1604 This sanitary status is closely linked to the health risks mentioned by Costa Rica with
respect to the importation of the products from the countries compared. In particular, these health
risks relate to the prevalence of animal diseases that may affect the products at issue. Accordingly,
the Panel will examine the two conditions identified by Panama in the context of the sanitary status
of the countries compared, which Costa Rica has referred to as sanitary eligibility.
7.736. Having determined the relevance of the conditions identified by the parties, the Panel will
now proceed to assess whether the Members in question were in similar situations with respect to
each of these conditions.

7.744. The Panel observes that, in the context of the exchanges of information relating to
Measure 2, on 12 July 20211618, DINASA sent comments to SENASA on the information that SENASA
had sent to AUPSA on 2 June 2021.1619 In particular, DINASA referred to the evaluation of the
Sanitary and Phytosanitary Evaluation Unit (UNESYF) of the information it received from SENASA.
According to UNESYF, there was some concern about the capacity and reliability of sanitary controls
in Costa Rica in the following areas: (a) insufficient information; (b) with respect to bovine
traceability, lack of progress in the individual identification of animals; (c) delegation of internal
animal movement controls to institutions not specialized in the area of animal health; (d) worrying
prevalence of bovine brucellosis and tuberculosis; and (e) Costa Rican standards and technical
regulations that contravene Panamanian regulations.1620
7.745. Costa Rica points out that its territory is recognized by the OIE as free from classical swine
fever and that, with respect to BSE, Panama and Costa Rica have the same sanitary status of
negligible BSE risk.1621 The evidence in the record also indicates that, in 2014, Panama classified
Costa Rica as a country with a "controlled BSE risk", while New Zealand was classified as a country
with a "negligible BSE risk".1622
7.746. Notwithstanding the different risk classification, the Panel considers that, given that sanitary
eligibility is a precondition for imports which presupposes a review of the sanitary status of the
country in question1623, the fact that Panama granted that eligibility demonstrates that Panama
considers the risks arising from the importation of the products for which eligibility was granted to
be acceptable. In this context, the fact that the sanitary eligibility of the three countries compared
was valid for the products in question demonstrates that they were in a similar situation from the
perspective of the health risks involved in importing these products into Panama.
7.747. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
conditions in Costa Rica, on the one hand, and in Peru and New Zealand, on the other hand, are
similar with respect to the granting of their sanitary eligibilities and the absence of changes in their
sanitary statuses since that sanitary eligibility was granted.

The moment when AUPSA ruled on the renewal or extension requests

Conclusion on whether identical or similar conditions prevail in the territories of the
Members in question
7.765. The Panel concludes that, for the reasons set out above, Costa Rica has demonstrated that
similar conditions prevail in the territories of the Members in question, namely Costa Rica, Peru, and
New Zealand, with respect to the procedure for renewing the sanitary approvals of their
establishments.
7.766. The Panel will now proceed to examine whether Measure 2 discriminates against the
16 Costa Rican establishments compared to the treatment accorded to the establishments in Peru
and New Zealand.
Whether Measure 2 discriminates against the establishments in Costa Rica compared to
the establishments in Peru and New Zealand
7.767. Costa Rica considers that Panama's discriminatory treatment of the Costa Rican
establishments is demonstrated by two facts:
a. that Panama granted the extension of sanitary approvals for establishments in Peru and
New Zealand after expiry1656, but not for establishments in Costa Rica; and
b. that Panama allowed the process to renew the sanitary approvals of the establishments in
Peru and New Zealand to begin with a documentary review, leaving the on-site audit of
the establishments to a later date, while it did not do the same for the Costa Rican
establishments, despite being in similar conditions.1657
7.768. Panama submits that no discrimination occurred, as AUPSA "extended the [sanitary]
approvals for Costa Rican establishments for transitional periods ranging from 60 days to 6 months"
on 15 October 2019 and 26 December 2019.1658 According to Panama, by extending the sanitary
approvals of the establishments in Peru and New Zealand, it afforded them the "same opportunity"
that the Costa Rican establishments had benefited from at the end of 2019.1659 Panama stresses
that the longest extension that it granted, of six months, was to the Costa Rican establishments.1660
In the case of New Zealand, Panama claims that the extension granted was of the same type as that
which all Costa Rican dairy and meat product establishments had received in October and December
2019.1661 Panama adds that: (a) the extensions of all establishments are subject to the fulfilment of
certain conditions by the importers; (b) in all cases, it was established that AUPSA would determine
a schedule for on-site audits prior to the expiry of the extension; and (c) all the extensions had the

- 225 -
same legal basis, namely Decree Law No. 11 of 2006 and the resolutions granting sanitary eligibility

7.769. The Panel recalls that a central aspect of the analysis of whether there is discrimination is
determining whether the measure at issue alters the conditions of competition to the detriment of
products originating in the territories of Members other than the Member imposing the measure.1663
7.770. In this dispute, the extension granted by Panama to the sanitary approvals of the
establishments in Peru and New Zealand resulted in those establishments being able to export their
products to Panama after their sanitary approvals had expired, which was between October 2019
and June 2020.1664 In contrast, the 16 Costa Rican establishments affected as a result of their
sanitary approvals not being renewed or extended have not been able to export their products to
Panama since the expiry of those approvals on 30 June 2020.1665
7.771. Panama argues that the treatment was not discriminatory because the Costa Rican
establishments benefited from two extensions to their approvals, granted in October and December
2019. In Panama's view, the granting of these two extensions to the Costa Rican establishments
means that the Costa Rican establishments received the same treatment as the establishments in
Peru and New Zealand.1666 For Costa Rica, however, the fact that Panama granted those extensions
in 2019 does not remedy the discrimination experienced when, in July 2020, Panama did not extend
the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments unlike the establishments in Peru and New
Zealand. Costa Rica asserts that discriminatory treatment cannot be offset by other instances in
which no discrimination or even more favourable treatment occurred, as established under
Article III:2 of the GATT 1994.1667
7.772. The Panel notes, first, that the discrimination challenged by Costa Rica under Article 2.3
does not relate to the extensions of the sanitary approvals granted in December 2019, but to
Panama's decision not to extend the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments when
they expired in June 2020. The Panel recalls that, when examining the relevant conditions, it focused
on the time-period between the expiry of the sanitary approvals of the 16 Costa Rican establishments
in June 2020, and the rejection of the request to extend or renew those approvals, in July 2020.
Accordingly, the Panel also frames its analysis within that time-period when assessing the situation
of the establishments in Peru and New Zealand, which, at the end of July 2020, saw their approvals
extended for 90 days1668 and until 30 January 20211669, respectively.
7.773. Second, the Panel recalls that, despite having requested this information from Panama1670,
the record does not show whether the establishments in Peru and New Zealand benefited from any
extension prior to the July 2020 extension, as the Costa Rican establishments had in 2019.
Nevertheless, the Panel does not consider this issue to be dispositive because, as Costa Rica
indicates, offsetting less favourable treatment with more favourable treatment does not nullify the
discriminatory nature of a situation.1671

two instances of discrimination identified by Costa Rica with respect to the treatment that Panama

a. In July 2020, Panama extended the sanitary approvals of the establishments in Peru and
New Zealand for a duration of 90 days and 6 months, while it did not do the same for the
establishments in Costa Rica, in July 2020, when the sanitary approvals of the

first instance, to be followed by an on-site audit at a later stage, allowing the authorities
in Peru and New Zealand to certify the country's sanitary status on the basis of
documentation.1690 In contrast, Panama rejected Costa Rica's request to extend its
sanitary approvals following that same procedure, and required that, as part of the
sanitary approval procedure for its establishments, an evaluation of the Costa Rican

7.776. As a result of the foregoing, Panama permits the importation of products from the
establishments in Peru and New Zealand, but not from those in Costa Rica. This difference in
treatment constitutes discriminatory treatment for the purposes of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement,
insofar as Panama permitted the importation of products from Peru and New Zealand, while it did
not do the same for those from Costa Rica, by not extending the sanitary approvals of the
16 Costa Rican establishments.1692 In this case, the distinction in treatment results from the manner
in which Panama has handled the requests to extend those sanitary approvals.
Conclusion on whether Measure 2 discriminates against the Costa Rican establishments
compared to the establishments in Peru and New Zealand
7.777. The Panel therefore concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that Panama accorded
discriminatory treatment to the detriment of Costa Rica's establishments compared to Peru's
establishments between July 2020 and October 2020, and New Zealand's establishments between
July 2020 and January 2021.
7.778. Having found that there is discrimination to the detriment of the Costa Rican establishments,
the Panel will now proceed to examine whether such discrimination is arbitrary or unjustifiable within
the meaning of the first sentence of Article 2.3.
Whether Measure 2 discriminates arbitrarily or unjustifiably
7.779. Costa Rica submits that, if the objective of Measure 2 is to ensure food safety, there is no
reason or basis for denying Costa Rica an extension and the documentary review afforded to Peru
and New Zealand.1693 Costa Rica adds that Panama cited the COVID-19 pandemic as a reason to
justify both the granting of the extension to the Peruvian establishments and the non-renewal of the
sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments.1694
7.780. Panama submits that, as an approval procedure, Measure 2 is not covered by Article 2.3.1695
Accordingly, Panama did not develop any arguments on whether Measure 2 discriminates arbitrarily
or unjustifiably. However, in order to justify the difference in treatment, Panama initially argued that
the extension of the sanitary approvals of the establishments in Peru without on-site audits was
because those establishments were authorized to export meat not intended for human consumption
(pet food and aquatic food).1696 Panama subsequently clarified that those establishments produced
products of animal origin for human consumption, and indicated that this fact should not change the
Panel's analysis.1697
7.781. The Panel notes that AUPSA explained to SENASA that it could not accept its request to
extend its sanitary approvals on a documentary basis followed by a on-site audit at a later date, for
the following reasons: (a) the documentary assessment alone did not satisfy Panamanian sanitary
regulations or ensure compliance with them by the Costa Rican establishments; and (b) the
Costa Rican plants and sanitary system had not been subject to a comprehensive evaluation by the
Panamanian authorities since 2014.1698 In its first written submission, Panama also asserted that
"the … circumstances of COVID-19 required even greater certainty on the matter of compliance with
Panama's sanitary requirements" and, accordingly, the Costa Rican establishments could be subject

of Costa Rica's animal health system, as well as the physical audit of the plants.1699
7.782. The Panel recalls that the analysis of whether discrimination is arbitrary or unjustifiable
requires an examination of "the cause of the discrimination, or the rationale put forward to explain
its existence"1700, taking into account both the provisions relating to the measure's application and
its actual application. The Panel's analysis will therefore focus on whether the discrimination
stemming from Measure 2 bears a "rational connection to" its objective of protecting human or
animal life or health from risks associated with food safety and zoonosis.1701
7.783. The Panel will now proceed to examine whether any of the reasons put forward by Panama
to justify its refusal to extend the sanitary approvals of the Costa Rican establishments on the basis
of an initial documentary assessment, followed by an on-site audit, have a rational connection to
the objective of Measure 2 and the risk addressed by that measure.
The justification that a documentary assessment does not satisfy or ensure compliance
with Panamanian sanitary regulations
7.784. The first reason put forward by Panama relates to compliance with its sanitary regulations.
In its communication of 10 July 2020, in which it rejected Costa Rica's request to extend the sanitary
approvals on the basis of a documentary assessment of the 16 Costa Rican establishments, Panama
stated the following: "[a] documentary assessment alone does not satisfy or ensure compliance with
the requirements stipulated in Panama's sanitary legislation to be met by these establishments".1702
However, Panama has not explained why it considers that "a purely documentary review would not
achieve Panama's level of sanitary protection".1703
7.785. In this connection, the Panel notes that Costa Rica did not request a "documentary
assessment alone", as indicated by Panama in its communication to Costa Rica of 10 July 2020.
Costa Rica requested that its establishments be evaluated and approved on a documentary basis,
pending on-site audits at a later date.1704 This same type of evaluation was applied by Panama to
the extensions granted to establishments in Peru and New Zealand.1705
7.786. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Panama has not provided a convincing
explanation as to why it was possible to accept imports from Peru and New Zealand on the basis of
a documentary assessment and not imports from Costa Rica, taking into account the objective of
Measure 2. In conclusion, the Panel considers that there is no rational connection between the
differential treatment accorded to Costa Rican establishments compared to those in Peru and
New Zealand, on the one hand, and the protection of human or animal life or health from risks
associated with food safety and zoonosis, on the other.
The justification that there had not been a comprehensive evaluation of Costa Rican plants
and the Costa Rican health system since 2014
7.787. The second reason put forward by Panama for rejecting the documentary assessment of the
Costa Rican establishments is that, as AUPSA pointed out to SENASA, the "plants and the Costa
Rican sanitary system ha[d] not been subject to a comprehensive evaluation … since 2014".1706 In
its submissions to the Panel, Panama explains that the "comprehensive evaluation" of 2014 to which
it alludes in its communication to Costa Rica of 10 July 2020 refers to the establishment approval

procedures carried out by AUPSA between 2011 and 2014 in relation to 13 of the 16 Costa Rican

7.788. The Panel highlights the following points relating to the evidence on this issue:
a. The comprehensive evaluations of 2014, referred to by Panama, are approval procedures
for 13 establishments in accordance with the provisions of section VII of the AUPSA Manual
for the approval of establishments. As mentioned above, these procedures consist of the
following stages: (i) the request from the interested party; (ii) the documentary analysis
of the sanitary evaluation; (iii) the audit at origin or on site (where applicable); (iv) the
evaluation by the CTI; (v) the issuance of a resolution approving or refusing the renewal
concerned; and, in the case of approval of the renewal, (vi) the publication of the approval
resolution; and (vii) the inclusion of the establishment on the AUPSA web page.1708 This is
indicated in the resolutions approving the 13 establishments, and confirmed by Panama
itself.1709
b. The five Costa Rican establishments affected by Measure 2 that do not appear on the list
of establishments that were the subject of a comprehensive evaluation in 2014 submitted
by Panama are Dos Pinos (No. 46B), El Ángel, Nutrilac, and Sigma Alimentos (No. 45 and
No. 47):
i. The approvals of Dos Pinos (No. 46B) and Nutrilac also date from 2014, following an
approval procedure virtually identical to the procedures of the 13 establishments
identified by Panama.1710 This indicates that these establishments were also subject to
a comprehensive evaluation in 2014, in the sense reported by Panama.
ii. The approvals of El Ángel and the two establishments of Sigma Alimentos took place
in 2016 through approval procedures virtually identical to the procedures of the
13 establishments identified by Panama.1711 This would seem to indicate that these
establishments were also subject to a comprehensive evaluation in 2016, in the sense
reported by Panama.
c. In line with the above, Panama submits that the Costa Rican establishments were the
subject of an evaluation of a similar nature in 2014 or 2015.1712
7.789. The aforementioned evidence indicates that the comprehensive evaluation of 2014, referred
to by Panama, consisted of the usual establishment approval procedures provided for in the AUPSA
Manual for the approval of establishments, and that these same procedures were applied to all

establishments affected by Measure 2 between 2014 and 2016, and not only to the
13 establishments identified by Panama.
7.790. Furthermore, the non-renewal of the sanitary approvals of Costa Rican establishments after
they expired on 30 June 2020 affects both establishments that were the subject of the
comprehensive evaluation in 2014 and others that, according to Panama, were not (e.g. El Ángel or
Nutrilac).
7.791. The record does not show whether the establishments in Peru and New Zealand were the
subject of a "comprehensive evaluation" in the sense indicated by Panama and, if so, when.
However, in response to a question from the Panel, Panama indicates that the scope of the evaluation
of the establishments in these two countries was the same as the scope of the AUPSA's evaluation
of the Costa Rican establishments.1713 The Panel understands that the evaluations that led to the
granting of the sanitary approvals of the establishments of these three Members are
contemporaneous, given that these sanitary approvals expired on approximately the same date
(October 2019 to June 2020). The fact that the comprehensive evaluation of 2014, referred to by
Panama, consists of the usual establishment approval procedure set out in the AUPSA Manual for
the approval of establishments, and Panama's confirmation that this was the case, indicates that the
establishments in Peru and New Zealand were the subject of a similarly comprehensive evaluation
when they obtained their approvals.
7.792. Given that the establishments in Costa Rica, Peru, and New Zealand were subject to the
same comprehensive evaluation at approximately the same time, the Panel considers that Panama's
argument would also not justify the difference in treatment to the detriment of the establishments
in Costa Rica. In the Panel's view, this difference in treatment does not reflect a rational connection
to the protection of human or animal life or health from risks associated with food safety and
zoonosis.
The justification of the COVID-19 pandemic
7.793. Lastly, Panama relies on the COVID-19 pandemic to make the completion of approval
procedures contingent upon the evaluation of Costa Rica's animal health systems and the physical
audit of its plants.1714 In this connection, Panama stated that "the … circumstances of COVID-19
required even greater certainty on the matter of compliance with Panama's sanitary
requirements".1715 Panama submits that this explanation was made in the context of AUPSA's
communication of 10 July 2020, which was in response to SENASA's request to extend the sanitary
approvals of the Costa Rican establishments. According to Panama, its refusal to grant an extension
on the basis of a documentary assessment and no on-site audits, merely sought to enforce
Panamanian law with a consistent level of sanitary protection. For Panama, the COVID-19 pandemic
was not a reason for importing countries to stop applying its SPS measures or reduce its level of
sanitary protection. Panama adds that it responded in a impartial manner to similar requests from
Peru and New Zealand, by requiring that on-site audits be scheduled once "th[e] documentary
process [is] complete".1716
7.794. Notwithstanding Panama's assertion, the Panel notes that, in the case of the extension
granted to the Peruvian establishments, AUPSA took into account the international health situation
caused by the COVID-19 pandemic1717 precisely in order to grant the extensions to those
establishments through a documentary process (followed by an on-site audit at a later date). The
resolution granting the extension to the establishments in New Zealand does not expressly refer to
the COVID-19 pandemic, but it is clear from the text that the pandemic was not an obstacle to the
processing and approval of the extension.
7.795. Accordingly, given that Panama took into account the COVID-19 pandemic both to grant
extensions based on a documentary assessment and to reject the same type of extensions, the Panel
considers that the COVID-19 pandemic does not justify the difference in treatment to the detriment
of the Costa Rican establishments. In this connection, the Panel considers that this difference in
