Conclusion on whether Measure 2 discriminates arbitrarily or unjustifiably
7.796. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that there is no reason or basis to rationally
link the objectives of Measure 2 to the discriminatory treatment accorded to the establishments in
Costa Rica, compared to the treatment accorded to the establishments in Peru and New Zealand.
Conclusion on Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
7.797. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
Measure 2 is inconsistent with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement, because it
arbitrarily and unjustifiably discriminates against 16 establishments in Costa Rica compared to the
establishments in Peru and New Zealand.
7.798. The Panel also finds that, owing to a lack of argumentation, Costa Rica has not demonstrated
that Measure 2 is inconsistent with the second sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement.
7.4.2.4.3 Claims under Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.4.3.1 Main arguments of the parties
Costa Rica
7.799. Costa Rica claims that Panama acted inconsistently with Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement
by applying distinct ALOPs in different but comparable situations, given that these distinctions are
arbitrary and unjustifiable1718, and result in discrimination or a disguised restriction on international
trade.1719
Panama
7.800. Panama submits that Measure 2 is not covered by the disciplines of Article 5.5 of the
SPS Agreement because it constitutes "approval procedures", which fall exclusively within the scope
of Article 8 and Annex C(1) of the SPS Agreement.1720
7.4.2.4.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.801. Article 5.5 provides, in relevant part, that:
With the objective of achieving consistency in the application of the concept of
appropriate level of sanitary or phytosanitary protection against risks to human life or
health, or to animal and plant life or health, each Member shall avoid arbitrary or
unjustifiable distinctions in the levels it considers to be appropriate in different
situations, if such distinctions result in discrimination or a disguised restriction on
international trade
7.802. As explained above1721, this provision elaborates on the non-discrimination obligations set
out in Article 2.3.1722
7.803. The Panel recalls that Costa Rica considers that the Panel could exercise judicial economy
with respect to the claims under Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement after having found that Measure 2

is inconsistent with Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement.1723 Panama does not object to this assessment
by Costa Rica.1724
7.804. The Panel considers that its findings under the first sentence of Article 2.3 are sufficient to
enable the DSB to make recommendations or rulings aimed at achieving a satisfactory settlement
of the matter.1725 While it has not made findings under the second sentence of Article 2.3 and,
therefore, has not concluded whether Measure 2 is a disguised restriction on trade, the Panel
considers that an additional finding on this issue would not affect the manner in which Panama
should adjust Measure 2 to comply with the findings under Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement, and in
that sense would not affect the recommendations of the DSB in this respect. Accordingly, the Panel
decides to exercise judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under Article 5.5 of the
SPS Agreement in respect of Measure 2.
7.4.2.5 Claims under Articles 5.6 and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.5.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.4.2.5.1.1 Costa Rica
7.805. Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
because it is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP. Costa Rica considers
that there is an alternative measure reasonably available to Panama that achieves its ALOP and is
significantly less restrictive to trade. Costa Rica also claims that, consequently, Measure 2 is
inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, as it is not applied only to the extent
necessary.1726
7.806. Costa Rica argues that Panama could have, as an alternative measure to Measure 2,
conducted a documentary assessment of Costa Rica's sanitary system and the Costa Rican
establishments, without suspending the trade in dairy and meat products of these
establishments.1727 According to Costa Rica, this was the treatment that Panama accorded Peru,
Mexico and New Zealand, by granting extensions to their establishments and agreeing to process
the renewal of their sanitary approvals on a documentary basis, pending the completion of on-site
audits after the expiry of the extension.1728
7.807. Costa Rica submits that the alternative measure fulfils the three requirements under
footnote 3 to Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.1729
7.808. First, Costa Rica argues that the alternative measure is reasonably available to Panama
taking into account technical and economic feasibility, given that Panama has renewed the sanitary
approvals of establishments in Peru, Mexico, and New Zealand where, according to Costa Rica, the
prevailing conditions were the same as in the Costa Rican establishments.1730

7.811. Panama submits that Measure 2 is not covered by the disciplines of Articles 2.2 and 5.6 of
the SPS Agreement because it constitutes "approval procedures" that fall exclusively within the

the extension was granted to the Peruvian and Mexican establishments without on-site audits as

New Zealand dairy product establishment, just as it did to all the Costa Rican dairy and meat product
establishments in October and December 2019.1737
7.4.2.5.3 Assessment by the Panel
7.812. The Panel recalls that, in response to Panama's argument that Measure 2 is not subject to
the obligations of Article 5.6 as it is an approval procedure within the meaning of Annex C1738, it
concluded that the obligation contained therein also applies to Measure 2.1739 Accordingly, the Panel
will proceed with its analysis of Costa Rica's claim under Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.
7.4.2.5.3.1 Relevant legal standard
7.813. To determine whether Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 5.6, in accordance with the legal
standard set out above1740, the Panel will examine whether the alternative measure proposed by
Costa Rica: (a) is reasonably available taking into account technical and economic feasibility;
(b) achieves Panama's ALOP; and (c) is significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 2. The
Panel will examine Measure 2 on the basis of these elements.

7.4.2.5.3.2 Whether Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
The alternative measure proposed by Costa Rica
7.817. Costa Rica proposes an alternative measure to Measure 2 that consists of assessing its
sanitary system and its establishments on a documentary basis, subject to subsequent on-site audits
being undertaken. This alternative measure, which Panama applied in respect of the establishments
in Peru, Mexico and New Zealand, would not imply the suspension of trade.1742
7.818. Panama maintains that it did not accord different treatment to the establishments in Peru,
Mexico and New Zealand compared with those in Costa Rica, as Costa Rica asserts. Accordingly, in
Panama's view, Costa Rica's claim under Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement should fail, given that it
is based exclusively on such difference in treatment.1743
7.819. The Panel recalls that the Costa Rican establishments that export products of animal origin
for animal consumption (Biomar and Central Veterinaria) are not covered by Measure 2.1744
Therefore, the Panel does not consider it relevant to include Mexico and the Mexican establishment
that exports products for animal consumption in its assessment. Accordingly, the Panel's analysis is
limited to assessing the availability of the alternative measure identified by Costa Rica that Panama
applied to the establishments in Peru and New Zealand.
Whether the alternative measure is reasonably available
7.820. Costa Rica claims that the alternative measure is reasonably available, as Panama has
accepted it in the case of Peru and New Zealand.1745
7.821. Costa Rica argues that it is in the same situation as Peru and New Zealand given that: (a) the
Costa Rican establishments export products of animal origin for human consumption to Panama;
(b) the Costa Rican authorities requested an extension from Panama so that the establishments
could continue to export during the approval renewal process, and asked that the renewal process
be of a documentary nature, with the possibility of performing the on-site audits at a later date; and

7.835. The Panel will now assess whether the alternative measure identified by Costa Rica achieves

Whether the alternative measure achieves Panama's ALOP
7.836. Under the legal standard described above1776, the Panel will first identify Panama's ALOP and
subsequently compare it with the level of protection that would be achieved through the alternative
measure proposed by Costa Rica.
Panama's ALOP
7.837. Panama notes that its ALOP for animal health measures is "the prevention, […] control, and
eradication of animal diseases and/or pests, in order to protect the country's animal heritage and
contribute to public health and environmental protection". Panama indicates that this ALOP is set
out in Article 1 of Law No. 23 of 1997. According to Panama, Article 12 of Executive Decree No. 49
of 2001 implements this highly restrictive ALOP by providing that the Panamanian sanitary authority
shall issue an opinion on sanitary eligibility "if the animal health evaluation does not identify any
animal health hazards or risks".1777 Panama also states that Article 5.1 of Decree-Law No. 11 of
2006 establishes that Panama's ALOP for food safety is "[t]o prevent the introduction and
establishment of pests and diseases that are transmissible through food and might affect human
health and the country's animals and plants, using strictly scientific-technical criteria".1778
7.838. Costa Rica acknowledges that Panama provided its ALOP for dairy and meat products.1779
7.839. The Panel recalls that an ALOP could be understood as an "objective" that the SPS measure
seeks to attain.1780 Moreover, Members have the "prerogative" to determine their ALOP, provided
that this can be distinguished with sufficient precision as to enable the application of the relevant
provisions of the SPS Agreement, for example, based on documents setting out health protection

objectives.1781 The regulations quoted by Panama indicate that the objective of Panama's sanitary
requirements is to protect the country's animal heritage and contribute to public health by ensuring
the prevention, control, and eradication of animal diseases and/or pests.1782 In addition, these
requirements seek to prevent "the introduction and establishment" of pests and diseases that are
transmissible through food, and that might affect human health and the country's animal and plant
heritage.1783 The Panel recalls that the objective of Measure 2 is to check the fulfilment of the
sanitary requirements for the importation of dairy and meat products into Panama. Accordingly, the
relevant procedures would seek to contribute to this objective.
7.840. The Panel notes that the sanitary protection objective in respect of Measure 2 is expressed
in general terms and does not mention specific diseases or pests. Although an ALOP can be described
more precisely, it has been considered that, as expressions of a general objective, ALOPs are often
set forth in a qualitative and generic manner.1784
7.841. According to Panama, its ALOP is "highly restrictive".1785 The Panel notes that the record
contains evidence relating to Measure 2 that allows Panama's protection objectives and, accordingly,
its ALOP to be identified. The AUPSA Manuals establishing the procedures for country-level sanitary
eligibility and the approval of establishments indicate that the AUPSA's objectives include
"[e]nsur[ing] an appropriate level of protection of human health and the country's agricultural and
livestock assets, in relation to imported food, taking into account the diversity and quality of
supplies".1786 The AUPSA Manual on sanitary eligibility provides a framework for developing and
operating a regulatory system for the introduction of food, "with the aim of protecting human life or
health and the country's agricultural and livestock assets, as well as facilitating trade in food".1787 In
addition, the communications from Panama's authorities to Costa Rica's authorities refer to the
objective of "guarantee[ing] food safety and human, animal and plant health; in order to guarantee
an appropriate level of sanitary protection"1788; and to ensuring the fulfilment of Panama's sanitary
requirements.1789
7.842. Compliance with the approval and renewal procedures for the sanitary approvals of
establishments is a precondition for importing into Panama. According to Panama, if the renewal
procedures are not complete when the approval expires, imports from such establishments will be
suspended until these procedures have been completed.1790 This reflects a high-level protection
approach that requires the fulfilment of Panama's sanitary requirements in order to ensure the
prevention, control, and eradication of animal diseases and pests, as well as to prevent the
introduction and establishment of pests and diseases that are transmissible through food and might
affect human health and the country's animal and plant heritage. However, it is not a level of
protection entailing zero tolerance to risk because, on the basis of the documents examined, it can
be inferred that the sanitary and animal health protection objectives coexist with trade facilitation
objectives.
7.843. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Panama's relevant ALOP could be
formulated as a high level of protection, focused on ensuring the prevention, control, and eradication
of animal diseases and pests, as well as preventing the introduction and establishment of pests and
diseases that are transmissible through food and might affect human health or the country's animal
heritage.

The level of protection achieved by the alternative measure proposed by Costa Rica
7.844. Costa Rica submits that the alternative measure would enable Panama to achieve its ALOP,
given that Panama has adopted this measure in respect of establishments in Peru and New Zealand
where the prevailing conditions were similar to those in Costa Rica1791 and which were subject to the
same ALOP.1792
7.845. Panama confirms that it applies the same ALOP in the case of sanitary measures concerning
dairy and meat products from Costa Rica, Peru and New Zealand.1793 Panama also confirms that its
ALOP for imports of dairy and meat products from Costa Rica has not changed as a result of
Measure 2.1794 However, Panama submits that carrying out the renewal procedure through a purely
documentary review of the pertinent information would fail to achieve its ALOP1795 given that it
"neither satisfies nor ensures the fulfilment of the requirements" of its sanitary legislation.1796
Panama does not explain why this would be the case, but focuses on the fact that, despite the
extensions, on-site audits were considered a requirement for obtaining further extensions.1797
7.846. Costa Rica's argument that the alternative measure would achieve Panama's ALOP for
imports of dairy and meat products from Costa Rica is based on a comparison of
Costa Rica's conditions on the one hand, and those of Peru and New Zealand on the other.1798
Costa Rica's comparison of the conditions in these countries implies a comparison of the risk posed
by the corresponding imports.
7.847. As mentioned above, the Panel considers that the level of risk is relevant for determining
whether a measure would achieve a Member's ALOP.1799 In this case, if the risk posed by imports
from Costa Rica is greater than that posed by imports from Peru and New Zealand, the same
measure could not achieve the same ALOP.
7.848. Costa Rica refers to the arguments that it uses in the context of its claim of discrimination
under Article 2.3 to maintain that its prevailing conditions are identical or similar to the conditions
in those Members.1800 As mentioned in paragraph 7.726 above, under Article 5.6, Costa Rica refers
to identical or similar conditions with regard to Peru and New Zealand in respect of: (a) the validity
of the sanitary eligibility; (b) the types of establishments and products in question (products of
animal origin for human consumption); (c) the steps taken by the countries to request renewal of
the sanitary approvals; (d) the type of extension that should have been granted; and (e) when
AUPSA decided to reject or approve the requested extensions.1801
7.849. After assessing the evidence concerning these aspects, the Panel concluded with regard to
the claim under Article 2.3 that Costa Rica has demonstrated that its prevailing conditions are similar
to those in Peru and New Zealand in respect of the risk posed by its imports of products of animal
origin for human consumption that are subject to the measure at issue. The similarity in the

7.850. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the alternative
measure that it proposes would achieve Panama's ALOP.
Whether the alternative measure is significantly less restrictive to trade
7.851. Costa Rica submits that the alternative measure is significantly less restrictive to trade than
Measure 2, as it would enable trade to resume, while Measure 2 is "as trade-restrictive as can be"1802
given that it is an "import prohibition".1803 Costa Rica notes that, under the alternative measure, its
establishments would have obtained an extension of their sanitary approvals that would have
enabled them to continue to export to Panama.1804 Costa Rica adds that the possibility of performing
on-site audits at a later date would ensure that trade is not suspended owing to a lack of time to
carry out this part of the procedure.1805
7.852. The Panel recalls that Panama's actions under this measure have made it impossible to
import Costa Rican dairy and meat products into Panama.1806
7.853. As mentioned above, in previous disputes, an alternative measure that imposes import
conditions but allows trade has been considered to be significantly less restrictive to trade than a
measure that does not allow it, like a prohibition.1807 Although conducting the procedure in the
manner proposed by Costa Rica does not guarantee the final outcome, extending the sanitary
approvals of the establishments would ensure that imports are not interrupted during this process.
7.854. Accordingly, the Panel considers that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the alternative
measure would be significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 2.
7.4.2.5.3.3 Conclusion on Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
7.855. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the
alternative measure to Measure 2 that it proposes meets the three requirements in footnote 3 to
Article 5.6, as it is reasonably available taking into account technical and economic feasibility,
achieves Panama's ALOP, and is significantly less restrictive to trade.
7.856. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the
SPS Agreement because it is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.4.2.5.3.4 Whether Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.857. The Panel concurs with what was said in previous disputes in that the finding that a measure
is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve the Member's ALOP, in breach of Article 5.6, leads
to a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with the obligation under Article 2.2 that SPS measures
should only be applied to the extent necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health.1808
The Panel considers that this rebuttable presumption arises in the present case because of its finding
that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.1809 Panama has failed to
present arguments to rebut this presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2.

7.4.2.6 Claims under Article 7 and Annex B of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.6.1 Main arguments of the parties

7.861. In its first written submission, Costa Rica submits that Panama acted inconsistently with the

7.867. In view of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that
Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 7 and Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement.
7.4.2.7 Claims under Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement
7.4.2.7.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.868. Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the
SPS Agreement, as the measure is inconsistent with the provisions of the SPS Agreement. In the
light of all the inconsistencies that, according to Costa Rica, it has demonstrated, Costa Rica requests
the Panel to find that Panama has, consequently, acted inconsistently with its obligations under
Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement.1819
7.869. Panama submits that Costa Rica was unable to establish its claims under Articles 1.1 and
2.1, as it has failed to demonstrate that Measure 2 is inconsistent with the provisions of the
SPS Agreement.1820
7.4.2.7.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.870. The Panel recalls the general obligation under Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement
that any SPS measure must be consistent with the provisions of the Agreement. The Panel also
recalls that previous panels found a measure at issue to be inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1
after finding that measure to be inconsistent with one of the obligations under the
SPS Agreement.1821
7.871. The Panel has found that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Articles 2.2, 2.3, 5.6, and 8 and
Annex C(1)(a) and Annex (C)(1)(c) of the SPS Agreement. Accordingly, the Panel finds that
Measure 2 is inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement because the measure is
inconsistent with other provisions of the SPS Agreement.

7.4.3.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.4.3.1.1 Costa Rica

Costa Rica submits that the measure: (a) belongs to the group of "rules and formalities in connection
with importation" covered by this Article; (b) provides an advantage to other Members (i.e. Peru,
Mexico, and New Zealand); (c) affects like products, as it results in discrimination based exclusively

7.873. Costa Rica also claims that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994

challenged under Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994; and (b) amounts to an import prohibition, since
Panama's decision to suspend imports of dairy and meat products constitutes "a legal ban on the

7.874. Furthermore, Costa Rica claims that Measure 2 is inconsistent with Article 4.2 and footnote 1
of the Agreement on Agriculture. According to Costa Rica, the Agreement on Agriculture is
applicable to Measure 2, as dairy and meat products are agricultural products classified under

or similar border measures on agricultural products, set forth in Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the

7.875. Panama submits that Costa Rica "has not established its claims" under Article XI:1 of the
GATT 1994 and Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the Agreement on Agriculture.1825
7.4.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.876. With respect to the claims under the GATT 1994, the Panel observes that prior panels have
recognized that the relationship between the SPS Agreement and the GATT 1994 could lead to
circular reasoning that does not contribute to securing a positive resolution to the dispute. These
panels have considered that any findings of inconsistency with GATT 1994 provisions would also
require an examination of whether the measure was justified under Article XX(b) of the GATT 1994,
which refers to measures necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health.1826 The Panel
agrees with this reasoning and recalls that the SPS Agreement elaborates on Article XX(b) of the
GATT 1994.1827
7.877. The Panel also recalls that it has found Measure 2 to be inconsistent with Articles 2.2, 2.3,
5.6, and 8, and Annex C(1)(a) and Annex C(1)(c) of the SPS Agreement.1828 These findings relate
both to the delays and the necessity of information requirements for the purposes of the relevant
procedures, and to the discriminatory nature, necessity and degree of trade-restrictiveness of
Measure 2.
7.878. The Panel considers that, under the circumstances of this dispute, the findings above are
sufficient to enable the DSB to make recommendations or rulings that achieve a satisfactory
settlement of the matter and that are sufficiently precise to ensure prompt compliance therewith.1829
Accordingly, the Panel does not deem it necessary to address Costa Rica's claims under the
GATT 1994, and decides to exercise judicial economy in respect of these claims.1830

7.5 Measure concerning the importation of pineapples (Measure 3)
7.880. The Panel will now examine Costa Rica's claims in respect of Measure 3. As indicated in the
section on the order of analysis, the Panel will begin its analysis by examining Costa Rica's claims
under the SPS Agreement. First, the Panel will examine the applicability of that Agreement to
Measure 3 and, in the event that it is applicable, will continue with its analysis of the claims under
the relevant provisions of the SPS Agreement. If necessary, the Panel will subsequently address the
claims under the GATT 1994 and the Agreement on Agriculture.
7.5.1 Claims under the SPS Agreement
7.5.1.1 Whether Measure 3 is an SPS measure within the scope of the SPS Agreement
7.5.1.1.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.5.1.1.1.1 Costa Rica
7.881. Costa Rica claims that Measure 3 is within the scope of the SPS Agreement because it is an
SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1)(a) to the Agreement and it directly affects
international trade, within the meaning of Article 1.1.1833 Costa Rica submits that the apparent
objective of Measure 3 is to protect plant life or health in Panama from the risks arising from the
introduction of the pink mealybug pest.1834 Costa Rica also asserts that Measure 3 affects trade in
the most direct way possible by prohibiting the entry of fresh pineapples from Costa Rica into the
Panamanian market.1835
7.5.1.1.1.2 Panama
7.882. Panama does not dispute that Measure 3 is an SPS measure in accordance with the definition
in Annex A(1) and Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement, to which the SPS Agreement applies.

7.5.1.1.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.5.1.1.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.883. The Panel will now examine whether, in accordance with the legal standard described in
section 7.3.1.1.2.1 above, the SPS Agreement is applicable to Measure 3. In particular, the Panel
will consider whether, as provided for in Articles 1.1 and 1.2 of that Agreement, Measure 3:
(a) constitutes an SPS measure in accordance with the definition given in Annex A to the
SPS Agreement; and (b) may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade.
7.5.1.1.2.2 Whether Measure 3 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1) to the SPS Agreement
7.884. As mentioned in the descriptive part, Measure 3 inactivates the resolution containing
Panama's PIR for Costa Rican pineapple.1836 Taking into account the legal standard described above,
the task of this Panel is to determine whether Measure 3 has been applied to achieve at least one of
the objectives listed in paragraphs (a)-(d) of Annex A(1), which are set out above.1837 In this respect,
the Panel notes that, in Note AUPSA-AG-032-2019, whereby Panama informed Costa Rica of
Measure 3, Panama stated that measure was a "preventive measure".1838 The Note indicates that
the measure would be maintained until Costa Rica provided "further information … on the
phytosanitary status of pineapple production areas, in reference to the pink mealybug pest ... and
other pests indicating their … status" at that time.1839
7.885. Note AUPSA-AG-032-2019 identifies the pest of concern, namely the pink mealybug. It
follows from the Note that Panama associates the risk of this pest with pineapple imports from
Costa Rica, given that Panama inactivated the resolution containing the PIR for pineapple from
Costa Rica after verifying its phytosanitary status in relation to imports of that product.1840 In its
response to this Note, the SFE of Costa Rica confirms that it understands that the measure is the
result of an assessment that determined a risk consisting of the possible presence of pink mealybug
in Costa Rican pineapples.1841 In this regard, the Panel recalls that the pink mealybug is a highly
polyphagous insect that affects a wide variety of plants1842, including pineapple.1843
7.886. In the Panel's view, the notification of Measure 3 to Costa Rica indicates that the measure is
applied because of the risk posed, according to Panama, by Costa Rican pineapples of the
pink mealybug entering Panama.
7.887. Accordingly, the Panel considers that the objective of Measure 3 relates to the objective of
"protect[ing] … plant life or health within the territory of the Member from risks arising from the
entry, establishment or spread of pests", set out in Annex (A)(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement.
7.888. With regard to the type of measure, the Panel considers that Measure 3, which consists of
inactivating the resolution containing the PIR for Costa Rican pineapple, makes it impossible to
import this product. Therefore, in the Panel's view, Measure 3 could qualify as a requirement for the
purposes of Annex A(1).1844 Moreover, as indicated above, even if it is not expressly included in the
list of measures in Annex A(1), a measure may be considered an SPS measure if it is applied to
achieve one of the objectives listed in that provision. Measure 3 meets this requirement and,

therefore, may be considered an SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1) to the

7.5.1.1.2.3 Whether Measure 3 may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade
7.889. The Panel considers that, given the nature of the measure, and as explained above in relation
to Measure 11845, Measure 3 directly affects international trade by making it impossible to import
Costa Rican pineapples into Panama as a result of the inactivation of the resolution containing the
relevant PIR.
7.5.1.1.3 Conclusion on the applicability of the SPS Agreement to Measure 3
7.890. Having determined that Measure 3 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement and that it directly affects international trade within the
meaning of Article 1.1 of the Agreement, the Panel finds that the SPS Agreement does apply to this
measure.
7.891. Accordingly, the Panel will examine Costa Rica's claims regarding the scientific basis of the
measure under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.
7.5.1.2 Claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.892. Costa Rica asserts that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the
SPS Agreement because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of
the risks to plant life or health. Costa Rica claims that, consequently, Measure 3 is also inconsistent
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, given that it is not based on scientific principles and is
maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.1846 Moreover, Costa Rica considers that Measure 3
is not covered by the exemption provided for under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement because it
does not meet the requirements of this provision.1847 Costa Rica adds that Panama has not rebutted
its prima facie case of the inapplicability of Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement, nor has it met its
burden of proving that all the requirements of this provision have been met.1848
7.893. Panama, for its part, asserts that Costa Rica has failed to establish a prima facie case that
Panama does not meet the four requirements set out in Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.1849
Panama requests the Panel to conclude that Measure 3 is a provisional measure covered by
Article 5.7 and that, consequently, Panama has not acted inconsistently with Articles 2.2, 5.1, 5.2,
and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement.1850
7.894. According to the allocation of the burden of proof under Article 5.7 set forth in
paragraph 7.121 above, the Panel will first examine whether Measure 3 qualifies as a provisional
measure within the meaning of Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. Depending on its findings with
respect to that issue, the Panel will decide whether it is necessary to address Costa Rica's claims
under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement. In this regard, the Panel recalls that
Article 5.7 has been considered a qualified exemption from the obligation under Article 2.2 to not
maintain an SPS measure without sufficient scientific evidence and, by extension, from those under
Article 5.1. Accordingly, in the event that Measure 3 were covered by the exemption under
Article 5.7, the obligations under Articles 2.2, 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 would not be applicable.1851

7.5.1.2.1.2 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.912. As mentioned in respect of Measure 1, for an SPS measure to be considered covered by
Article 5.7, the Member that adopts the measure must meet four cumulative requirements: adopting
the measure (a) in a situation where "relevant scientific evidence is insufficient"; and (b) "on the
basis of available pertinent information"; (c) "seek[ing] to obtain the additional information
necessary for a more objective assessment of risk"; and (d) "review[ing] the … measure accordingly
within a reasonable period of time".1896

7.913. The Panel will now examine whether Measure 3 is covered by Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement. As with Measure 1, the Panel will first examine whether Measure 3 was adopted in
a situation where relevant scientific evidence was insufficient.
Whether Panama adopted Measure 3 in a situation where relevant scientific evidence was
insufficient
7.914. The Panel recalls that it concurs with the interpretation in previous disputes that there is a
situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence within the meaning of Article 5.7 where
deficiencies in the body of available scientific evidence do not allow a Member, in quantitative or
qualitative terms, to perform an adequate assessment of risks as required under Article 5.1 and as
defined in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.1897
7.915. Costa Rica submits that Panama has not demonstrated that Measure 3 was adopted in a
situation where the relevant scientific evidence was insufficient for an assessment of the risk in
question.1898 According to Costa Rica, Panama had sufficient information on the pink mealybug pest
and the phytosanitary status of Costa Rica with respect to that pest.1899 Panama, for its part, asserts
that Costa Rica has not demonstrated that the scientific evidence was sufficient for an assessment
of the risk.1900
7.916. The Panel will now examine whether the scientific evidence available to Panama at the time
Measure 3 was adopted would have allowed it to carry out the risk assessment. The Panel will begin
its analysis by considering the issue of relevant risk, followed by the information that Panama needed
to assess the risk in question.
The relevant risk
7.917. Panama has indicated that the risk that it seeks to address by adopting Measure 3 is the risk
of entry, establishment1901 or spread of the pink mealybug through imports of fresh pineapples from
Costa Rica.1902
7.918. Costa Rica states that "Panama had to evaluate the probability of entry, establishment and
spread of this pest within Panamanian territory through pineapples imported from Costa Rica".1903
In Costa Rica's view, the relevant information available at the time Measure 3 was adopted indicated
the absence of that risk.1904
7.919. The Panel considers that these statements by the parties do not reflect a disagreement over
the relevant risk, but rather the likelihood of that risk materializing. Accordingly, the Panel considers
that the assessment to be carried out by Panama, concerning the risk of entry, establishment or
spread of pink mealybug through imports of fresh pineapples from Costa Rica, corresponds to the
first sentence of Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement. That sentence refers to "the likelihood of entry,
establishment or spread of a pest ... within the territory of an importing Member according to the
sanitary or phytosanitary measures which might be applied, and of the associated potential biological
and economic consequences".
Information necessary for the assessment of the risk in question

is determined by the information that is necessary to assess the risk in question.1905 Therefore, the

7.921. Panama asserts that it needed "further information … on the phytosanitary status of
pineapple production areas [in Costa Rica], in reference to the pink mealybug pest (Maconellicoccus
hirsutus) and other pests indicating their current status".1906 Costa Rica, in turn, claims that Panama
had sufficient information for the assessment of the risk in question because it had: (a) information
on the pink mealybug pest (morphology, symptoms, detection, reproduction system, survival
conditions, natural enemies, control methods, etc.); and (b) information on
Costa Rica's phytosanitary situation in respect of that pest (Costa Rica's phytosanitary status as
notified to Panama and the IPPC in May 2014, no instances of the pest having been intercepted in
pineapple shipments from Costa Rica to Panama, and pineapple imports from Costa Rica into
Panama continuing as usual).1907
7.922. The Panel considers that ISPM 11, on pest risk analysis for quarantine pests1908, provides
valuable guidance in this regard. The Panel finds this ISPM relevant, given that: (a) it provides
details for the conduct of a "pest risk assessment" as part of the PRA for quarantine pests1909; (b) the
pink mealybug is considered "a pest of quarantine concern" owing to its wide injurious range1910;
(c) Panama informed Costa Rica that the pink mealybug is "a pest of quarantine concern" for
Panama1911; and (d) Panamanian regulations provide that the Pest Risk Analysis and Phytosanitary
Requirements Section of the Directorate of Plant Health (DNSV) will prepare PRA studies in
accordance with the stages set out in ISPM 11, among others.1912
7.923. ISPM 11 indicates that the "pest risk assessment" stage (stage 2 of a PRA) consists of
three interrelated steps: (a) pest categorization; (b) assessment of the probability of introduction
and spread; and (c) assessment of potential economic consequences (including environmental
impacts).1913 The Panel will focus on the information necessary for assessing the probability of
introduction and spread of the pink mealybug, given that Panama argues that there is a situation of
insufficiency with regard to this step of the risk assessment.1914
7.924. ISPM 11 identifies three components as part of the assessment of the probability of
introduction and spread of a pest: (a) probability of entry; (b) probability of establishment; and
(c) probability of spread after establishment. The ISPM outlines the information that could be
considered to assess each of these probabilities. However, ISPM 11 does not require the country
conducting the risk assessment to request all the information specified therein or to follow each and
every one of the steps described therein. As is evident from the text of the ISPM itself, which often
introduces factors to be considered with terms such as "factors to consider", "examples of factors to

Probability of entry of a pest1917
7.925. In this section, ISPM 11 addresses the following five issues:
a. Identification of pathways for a PRA initiated by a pest
i. ISPM 11 states that "[p]est interception data may provide evidence of the ability of a
pest to be associated with a pathway and to survive in transport or storage".1918 It also
states that entry by natural means should also be assessed.1919
b. Probability of the pest being associated with the pathway at origin
i. The ISPM provides that "[t]he probability of the pest being associated, spatially or
temporally, with the pathway at origin should be estimated" and, to that end, it
includes a non-exhaustive list of factors to consider, namely: (a) prevalence of the
pest in the source area; (b) occurrence of the pest in a life stage that would be
associated with commodities, containers or conveyances; (c) volume and frequency of
movement along the pathway; (d) seasonal timing; and (e) pest management,
cultural and commercial procedures applied at the place of origin (application of plant
protection products, handling, culling, roguing, grading).1920
c. Probability of survival during transport or storage
i. ISPM 11 identifies the following factors to consider: (a) speed and conditions of
transport and duration of the life cycle of the pest in relation to time in transport and
storage; (b) vulnerability of the life stages during transport or storage; (c) prevalence
of pest likely to be associated with a consignment; and (d) commercial procedures
(e.g. refrigeration) applied to consignments in the country of origin, country of
destination, or in transport or storage.1921
d. Probability of pest surviving existing pest management procedures
i. According to ISPM 11, assessing this probability means evaluating "[e]xisting pest
management procedures (including phytosanitary procedures)[,] applied to
consignments against other pests from origin to end use, ... for effectiveness against
the pest in question".1922 It also mentions the need to estimate "[t]he probability that
the pest will go undetected during inspection or survive other existing phytosanitary
procedures".1923
e. Probability of transfer to a suitable host
i. ISPM 11 lists a number of factors to consider: (a) dispersal mechanisms; (b) whether
the imported commodity is to be sent to a few or many destination points in the
PRA area; (c) proximity of entry, transit and destination points to suitable hosts;
(d) time of year at which import takes place; (e) intended use of the commodity (e.g.

Probability of establishment1925
7.926. ISPM 11 states that "reliable biological information (life cycle, host range, epidemiology,
survival, etc.) should be obtained from the areas where the pest currently occurs", and that "[c]ase
histories concerning comparable pests can be considered".1926
7.927. Among the examples of the factors to be considered, the ISPM includes the following:
(a) availability, quantity and distribution of hosts in the PRA area; (b) environmental suitability in
the PRA area; (c) potential for adaptation of the pest; (d) reproductive strategy of the pest;
(e) method of pest survival; and (f) cultural practices and control measures.1927
7.928. In the section on probability of establishment, ISPM 11 addresses the following four issues:
a. Availability of suitable hosts, alternate hosts and vectors in the PRA area
i. In this regard, factors to consider according to the ISPM are: (a) whether hosts and
alternate hosts are present and how abundant or widely distributed they may be;
(b) whether hosts and alternate hosts occur within sufficient geographic proximity to
allow the pest to complete its life cycle; (c) whether there are other plant species,
which could prove to be suitable hosts in the absence of the usual host species;
(d) whether a vector, if needed for dispersal of the pest, is already present in the
PRA area or likely to be introduced; and (e) whether another vector species occurs in
the PRA area.1928
b. Suitability of environment
i. ISPM 11 states that factors in the environment (e.g. suitability of climate, soil, pest
and host competition) that are critical to the development of the pest, its host and if
applicable its vector, and to their ability to survive periods of climatic stress and
complete their life cycles, should be identified.1929
c. Cultural practices and control measures
i. According to ISPM 11, where applicable, practices employed during the
cultivation/production of the host crops should be compared to determine if there are
differences in such practices between the PRA area and the origin of the pest that may
influence its ability to establish.1930
ii. Pest control programmes or natural enemies already in the PRA area which reduce the
probability of establishment may also be considered, as may the availability, or lack,
of suitable methods for eradication.1931
d. Other characteristics of the pest affecting the probability of establishment

7.929. In order to estimate the probability of spread of the pest, reliable biological information
should be obtained from areas where the pest currently occurs. The situation in those areas can
then be compared with that in the PRA area. ISPM 11 also notes that case histories concerning
comparable pests can usefully be considered.1936
7.930. Examples of the factors that ISPM 11 indicates should be considered are: (a) suitability of
the natural and/or managed environment for natural spread of the pest; (b) presence of natural
barriers; (c) the potential for movement with commodities or conveyances; (d) intended use of the
commodity; (e) potential vectors of the pest in the PRA area; and (f) potential natural enemies of
the pest in the PRA area.1937
7.931. Having identified the information that, according to ISPM 11, Panama might need to assess
the risk of entry, establishment and spread of the pink mealybug through imports of Costa Rican
pineapples, the Panel will now assess whether Panama had sufficient relevant scientific evidence for
such an assessment at the time Measure 3 was adopted.
Relevance and sufficiency of the scientific evidence available at the time Measure 3 was
adopted
7.932. The information that the parties have identified as available to Panama at the time Measure 3
was adopted can be grouped into two categories: (a) information on the pink mealybug pest; and
(b) information on Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of this pest.1938

parties do not disagree over the fact that these two categories of information may be relevant to
the risk in question.1939 The Panel agrees with the parties that the information on both the pink
mealybug and Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of this pest may constitute scientific
evidence relevant to the risk of entry, establishment or spread of the pest through imports of fresh
pineapples from Costa Rica. This is confirmed by ISPM 11, given that it indicates that information
on both the pest in general and the phytosanitary status and regulations of the place of origin of the
product to be imported (Costa Rica) may be necessary in order to perform a risk assessment.1940

of the risk in question, the Panel recalls that what is relevant in the context of the first requirement
under Article 5.7 is that the body of available scientific evidence does not allow, in quantitative or

7.935. The Panel notes that Panama has identified the following situations of insufficient scientific
evidence with respect to the assessment of the risk of entry, establishment or spread of pink
mealybug through imports of fresh pineapples from Costa Rica:
a. Situation of insufficient scientific evidence resulting from the discovery in 2018 by the
Panamanian authorities of scientific evidence from 20111942 reporting on the presence of
the pink mealybug pest on pineapple in Fiji.
b. Situation of insufficient scientific evidence regarding the phytosanitary status of pineapple
production areas in Costa Rica in respect of the pest and, more specifically, the following
aspects: (i) technical evidence concerning the phytosanitary status of pineapple
production areas in Costa Rica in reference to the pink mealybug; (ii) surveillance and
specific phytosanitary control of the pest in pineapple production areas; (iii) survey
procedures; and (iv) current mitigation or risk management strategies.1943
7.936. The Panel will now examine these two situations of alleged insufficient scientific evidence
identified by Panama.
Alleged situation of insufficient scientific evidence in relation to the pest
7.937. With regard to the 2011 scientific publication that Panama claims to have discovered in
December 2018, Panama notes that, when it adopted the PIR for Costa Rican pineapple in July 2008,
the available scientific evidence did not suggest that pineapples were hosts of the pink mealybug.1944
Panama contends that the discovery of this publication was one of the triggers for the adoption of
Measure 3, by calling into question its previous conclusions as to the risk associated with the
transmission of the pink mealybug in pineapple.1945
7.938. Costa Rica submits that the 2011 scientific publication simply confirmed that pineapple may
be another of the hundreds of potential hosts of the pink mealybug. Costa Rica notes that Panama
has not indicated what it was that, according to Panama, was "called into question" following the
publication of this scientific evidence in 2011.1946 Costa Rica adds that Panama should have been
aware that pineapple appears as a host of the pink mealybug in scientific journals and databases
such as those of CABI and EPPO, since it asserts that it develops its PIR using those resources.1947
7.939. The Panel notes that, following the first substantive meeting, Panama has not developed its
argument regarding the 2011 publication, instead it has focused exclusively on the situation of
insufficient scientific evidence regarding Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of the pest.
Panama itself appears to confirm this by asserting that "the only issue before the Panel is whether
the Panamanian authorities had sufficient information on Costa Rica's status to perform a risk
assessment".1948 However, as part of its objective assessment of the matter before it, the Panel will
examine whether Panama's discovery of that publication in 2018 created a situation of insufficient
relevant scientific evidence.

7.940. The Panel agrees with Costa Rica that Panama has not identified the "previous conclusions"
or the specific aspects that the 2011 publication calls into question. It seems to follow from
Panama's argument that the discovery reported in the publication is the fact that pineapple is a
potential host of the pink mealybug pest. The 2011 publication indicates that the pest was detected
in Fiji for the first time, but it does not indicate that this was the first time that this pest was detected
on pineapple. In this regard, the Panel notes that the CABI datasheet on the pink hibiscus mealybug
identifies pineapple as a host of the pest, and links this information not only to the 2011 publication
identified by Panama, but also to a publication from 2004.1949 The fact that, in 2004, scientific
evidence linked the pest in question with pineapple would call into question the discovery that,
according to Panama, was presented in the 2011 publication by indicating that pineapple could be a
host of the pest.
7.941. Having examined the 2011 publication, the Panel notes that, of the 30 pages that make up
the publication, only a few lines are devoted to the pink mealybug. These lines report the presence
of the pink mealybug in Fiji for the first time, once on hibiscus and once on pineapple.1950 The rest
of the publication mainly deals with the discovery of new species of mealybug in Fiji. The authors
recall that this polyphagous pest is widespread throughout southern Asia, Australia and Africa, and
that it has spread to many geographic areas in recent years. The authors state that it is a potential
invasive pest of several crops, particularly pineapple.1951 The publication includes a list of mealybug
species recorded in Fiji that indicates that the pink mealybug was found on two of Fiji's islands on
the following host plants: Ficus sp.1952, Hibiscus rosa-sinensis1953 and Ananas comosus
(pineapple).1954
7.942. The authors of this publication do not indicate that their findings question existing scientific
evidence; they merely state that, during their "small survey"1955, they recorded the pink mealybug
pest in Fiji for the first time and that the pest could be potentially very important ecologically and
economically.1956 In the Panel's view, this publication does not question previous scientific evidence
on hosts of the pest, instead it complements existing information on the pest by reporting its
presence on pineapple in Fiji. Given the "highly polyphagous"1957 nature of the pest, detected in
more than 300 plant genera in over 85 families1958, the fact that a scientific publication reports the
detection of the pest on pineapple in Fiji does not appear to call into question the scientific evidence
on the matter, but rather to confirm the pest's polyphagous nature.
7.943. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Panama has not demonstrated that,
at the time Measure 3 was adopted, there was a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence
in relation to pineapple as a host of the pest stemming from the 2011 publication that Panama claims
to have discovered in 2018.
7.944. With regard to the rest of the relevant scientific evidence on the pest, for which Panama has
not identified any situation of insufficiency, the Panel notes that Costa Rica has included in the record
a broad sample of the extensive literature that exists on the pest. That literature includes (a) CABI

7.945. In the Panel's view, this scientific evidence on the pest addresses in sufficient detail the
issues that, according to ISPM 11, should be considered in a risk assessment as part of a PRA. These
issues include topics such as the pest's biological information, hosts, dispersal mechanisms,
environmental suitability, reproductive strategy and method of survival.1964 Accordingly, the Panel
considers that Panama has not demonstrated that there is a situation of insufficient scientific
evidence about the pest that prevents it from assessing the risk in question.
Alleged situation of insufficient scientific evidence concerning the phytosanitary status of
pineapple production areas in Costa Rica in respect of the pest
7.946. The Panel will now examine the second situation of alleged insufficient scientific evidence
identified by Panama, which concerns Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of the pink
mealybug.
7.947. As mentioned above, Panama asserts that it could not assess the risk of entry, establishment
or spread of the pink mealybug through fresh pineapple imports from Costa Rica because, at the
time Measure 3 was adopted, it did not have "further information … on the phytosanitary status of
pineapple production areas" in reference to the pest.1965 Specifically, this information refers to
(a) technical evidence concerning the phytosanitary status of pineapple production areas in
Costa Rica in reference to the pink mealybug; (b) information on the surveillance and specific
phytosanitary control of the pest in production areas; (c) survey procedures; and (d) current
mitigation or risk-management strategies.1966
7.948. For its part, Costa Rica asserts that Panama could have assessed the risk in question with
the available scientific evidence on Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in relation to the pink
mealybug, namely: (a) Costa Rica's notifications to the IPCC and Panama in May 2014 reporting
that the pink mealybug was present in its territory, not widely distributed and under official
control1967; (b) the fact that pineapple imports from Costa Rica into Panama continued after these

7.949. The Panel observes that, in its communications to Costa Rica following the adoption of
Measure 3 and during these proceedings, Panama has identified, with varying degrees of detail, the
information that it considered necessary to carry out the assessment of the risk of entry,
establishment or spread of the pink mealybug through imports of fresh pineapples from Costa Rica.
Initially, when it notified Costa Rica of Measure 3 in January 2019, Panama requested "further
information … on the phytosanitary status of pineapple production areas, in reference to the pink
mealybug pest ... and other pests indicating their current status ".1970 More than two years later, on
1 March 2021, Panama reiterated its request for "further information on the status of the pest ... in
pineapple production areas" and clarified that it needed (a) "information on, inter alia, the specific
phytosanitary surveillance of the pest in production sites in areas where the pest is present, pest-
free areas, and survey procedures"; (b) "the list of exporting companies subject to official control
by the SFE, interested in exporting fresh pineapple fruit" to Panama; and (c) "the mitigation
measures used for the pest, during the cultivation stages and in packing centres".1971 Ten days after
reiterating its request for further information, Panama sent a detailed list of the information required
in response to a request from Costa Rica.1972
7.950. Subsequently, in its written submissions to the Panel, Panama indicated that, in order to
assess the risk in question, it required "technical evidence concerning the phytosanitary status of
pineapple production areas in Costa Rica in reference to M. hirsutus; information on the surveillance
and specific phytosanitary control of the pest in production areas; survey procedures; and current
mitigation or risk management strategies".1973 Lastly, in its responses after the second substantive
meeting, Panama provided a table comparing the information that it had requested from Costa Rica
on 10 March 2021 with the information that ISPM 11 considers necessary to conduct an assessment
of the risk in question.1974 In this table, Panama included fewer issues than those listed in its
communication of 10 March 2021.1975

7.951. Aside from the more or less detailed formulation chosen by Panama to refer to the
information that it requested from Costa Rica on its phytosanitary status in respect of the pest, the
Panel sees no contradictions in the information required by Panama. The Panel notes that information
concerns pineapple production areas in Costa Rica, as Panama itself indicated to Costa Rica when
informing it of the adoption of Measure 31976, and repeated in its submissions to the Panel.1977
7.952. On that basis, the Panel will now examine whether, at the time Measure 3 was adopted,
Panama had sufficient information on the phytosanitary status of pineapple production areas in
Costa Rica in respect of the pest to be able to assess the risk in question.
7.953. In this regard, the Panel observes that the information set forth in ISPM 11 in relation to the
assessment of the probability of entry, establishment and spread of a pest can be grouped into three
categories: (a) information on the pest1978; (b) information on the PRA area (Panama)1979; and
(c) information "from the areas where the pest currently occurs" or "the origin of the pest".1980 The
Panel recalls that Costa Rica informed Panama and the IPPC in May 2014 that the pest is present in
Costa Rica, but not in pineapple production areas. Specifically, the pest appears to be linked to a
single host (the hibiscus plant), not widely distributed (in Río Oro de Santa Ana in the province of
San José) and under official control.1981 Panama confirms that, when it adopted Measure 3, it verified
the presence of the pink mealybug in Costa Rica and knew that Costa Rica's status had not changed
since the notification of the pest in 2014, as confirmed by Costa Rica's reports to the IPPC.1982
7.954. In respect of the necessary information to assess the probability of pest entry, the Panel
notes that Panama was aware of Costa Rica's pest status (absent from pineapple production areas),
as well as the fact that it had not detected the pest in shipments of Costa Rican pineapples, which
continued as usual between May 2014 and the adoption of Measure 3 in January 2019. The Panel
considers that, in the light of in the provisions of ISPM 111983, this information allowed Panama to
assess the probability of entry and, in particular, the probability of the pest being associated with
the pathway, without needing to request further information on the phytosanitary status of pineapple
production areas in Costa Rica in respect of the pest.
7.955. Panama also had information on pest management in the OIRSA region, as both Panama
and Costa Rica are part of this organization. OIRSA published a technical report in 2018, shortly
before the adoption of Measure 3, which sets out control strategies in the OIRSA region and its
member countries. In the case of Costa Rica, it is reported that "[i]n 2012 and 2013, parasitoids
were exported to Costa Rica following the detection of the pest in the province of San José and
neighbouring areas in Alajuela".1984 In respect of the OIRSA region, the Organization's members
reaffirmed "the importance of the biological control programme against the PMP [pink mealybug
pest] since its inception, enabling the continued protection of the OIRSA region from the negative

effects of the pest over the past 18 years of operation".1985 The report also indicates that, "as a

first-hand information on how the pest was handled in the region, including in Costa Rica.
7.956. In any event, the Panel considers that the information referred to in ISPM 11 does not cover
detailed information on the management of the pest in pineapple production areas in Costa Rica,
given that, as Panama acknowledges, there is no record of the pest being present in those areas
and, therefore, it is not appropriate to request information on the management of a pest in an area
where the pest is not present.
7.957. In respect of the necessary information to assess the probability of establishment of the
pest, the Panel notes that ISPM 11 provides that "reliable biological information ... should be
obtained from the areas where the pest currently occurs"1987, as should information on the PRA area
(in this case, Panama).1988 Therefore, Panama did not need additional information on the
phytosanitary status of the pest in pineapple production areas in Costa Rica, in which the pest is not
present, to perform this assessment.
7.958. Lastly, with regard to the probability of spread of the pest, ISPM 11 indicates that "reliable
biological information should be obtained from areas where the pest currently occurs" so that the
status of these areas can then be compared with that of the PRA area (in this case, Panama).1989
Again, as there is no record of the pest being present in pineapple production areas in Costa Rica,
ISPM 11 does not support the relevance of the information on these areas requested by Panama to
assess the probability of spread of the pest.
7.959. Therefore, the examination of the information necessary to assess the risk in question in the
light of in the provisions of ISPM 11 does not support Panama's argument that it needed "further
information … on the phytosanitary status of pineapple production areas, in reference to the pink
mealybug pest"1990, beyond what it already had at the time Measure 3 was adopted, namely:
a. information on the phytosanitary status of Costa Rica in respect of the pest, in the sense
that the pink mealybug in Costa Rica was a quarantine pest, not widely distributed and
under official control, notified to Panama and the IPPC in 2014, and absent from pineapple
production areas;
b. information derived from the fact that Costa Rican pineapple imports into Panama
continued as usual from the notification of the pest in 2014 until Measure 3 was adopted
in January 2019 (e.g. the information required under the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica
regarding the production, harvesting, packaging or packaging; or information concerning
ports of entry and means of transport used as documented in the customs
declarations)1991; and
c. the absence of pink mealybug interceptions by Panama in pineapple shipments from
Costa Rica, which, according to ISPM 11, "may provide evidence of the ability of a pest to
be associated with a pathway and to survive in transport or storage".1992

7.960. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Panama has not demonstrated that it
adopted Measure 3 in a situation of insufficient scientific evidence with respect to the phytosanitary
status of pineapple production areas in Costa Rica in respect of the pest that prevented it from
performing an assessment of the probability of entry, establishment and spread of the pest in
Panama through pineapples from Costa Rica.
Conclusion on whether Panama adopted Measure 3 in a situation where the relevant
scientific evidence was insufficient
7.961. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Panama has not demonstrated that, at the
time Measure 3 was adopted, there was a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence in
relation to (a) information concerning the pink mealybug pest, or (b) the phytosanitary status of
pineapple production areas in Costa Rica in respect of this pest, which prevented it from assessing
the risk of entry, establishment and spread of the pink mealybug pest in Panama through imports
of pineapples from Costa Rica.
7.962. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Panama has not demonstrated that Panama adopted
Measure 3 in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence within the meaning of Article 5.7
of the SPS Agreement.
7.963. As mentioned in respect of Measure 1, this finding would be sufficient to conclude that
Measure 3 is not covered by Article 5.7, given that the requirements of Article 5.7 are cumulative.1993
However, the Panel considers it appropriate to also address the second requirement of Article 5.7,
which, like the first, concerns the adoption of the measure.
Whether Panama adopted Measure 3 on the basis of available pertinent information
7.964. In accordance with the legal standard described above1994, the Panel will now examine
whether Panama's decision to "temporarily inactivate Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008"1995
establishing the PIR for fresh pineapple from Costa Rica was based on the pertinent information
available to Panama on 29 January 2019. The Panel recalls that, in order to meet this requirement,
Panama must demonstrate that there is a "rational and objective relationship"1996 between
Measure 3 and the pertinent available information regarding the risk of entry, establishment and
spread of the pink mealybug pest in Panama through pineapple imports from Costa Rica. The Panel
will address the different categories of information identified by the parties, summarized in the table
below, to determine, first of all, whether they are pertinent and whether they were available to
Panama at the time of the adoption of Measure 3 in January 2019. If that is the case, the Panel will
assess whether Panama adopted the Measure on the basis of that information.

pest were intercepted in a shipment of Costa Rican pineapples before adopting a provisional

7.974. The Panel considers that all the information on Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect
of the pink mealybug, including that concerning the absence of interceptions, is pertinent, because
it is directly related to the risk in question, namely the risk of entry, establishment and spread of
the pest in Panama through imports of pineapples from Costa Rica. The Panel refers to ISPM 11,
according to which "[p]est interception data may provide evidence of the ability of a pest to be
associated with a pathway and to survive in transport or storage".2024 Accordingly, the Panel sees
no reason to deny the pertinence of the information concerning interceptions of the pest.
7.975. Furthermore, Panama had the information on Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of
the pink mealybug pest at the time Measure 3 was adopted. With regard to whether Measure 3 was
adopted on the basis of this information, Panama itself indicates that this was not the case, by
asserting that it adopted the measure on the basis of two pieces of information concerning Costa
Rica's phytosanitary status in respect of the pest: (a) the presence of the pest in Costa Rica; and
(b) the absence of information on the phytosanitary status of pineapple-producing areas in Costa
Rica.2025 According to Panama, the fact that the pest was present in Costa Rica implied a "risk that
the pink mealybug will continue to spread to other areas of Costa Rica in other hosts, including
pineapple".2026 Therefore, and given the absence of "further technical information"2027 that,
according to Panama, would have enabled it to assess that risk, Panama decided to suspend imports
of fresh pineapple from Costa Rica.
7.976. The Panel notes that Panama decided to adopt Measure 3, which prohibits imports and is
considered under ISPM 11 to be "of last resort"2028, despite knowing that the pink mealybug is a
pest with limited distribution in Costa Rica, under official control, that has not been detected in
pineapple-producing areas of Costa Rica, and that has not been intercepted. Moreover, Panama itself
has confirmed that it knew the status of the pest in Costa Rica had not changed since it was notified
in 2014.2029
7.977. The Panel finds nothing in the record to suggest that the information on Costa Rica's
phytosanitary status in respect of the pest would justify the decision to inactivate the PIR for
pineapple from Costa Rica, thereby preventing the importation of this product into Panama.
7.978. With regard to the lack of further technical data on the phytosanitary status of
pineapple-producing areas in Costa Rica in reference to the pink mealybug, the Panel refers to the
conclusions in the previous section on the necessary information according to ISPM 11.2030 As
explained in the examination of the first requirement of Article 5.7, this ISPM does not support the
need to have such information in order to assess the risk in question and to adopt the measure
appropriate to the level of risk determined when the pest has not been found to be present in
pineapple-producing areas.
7.979. In the Panel's view, the body of information on Costa Rica's phytosanitary status in respect
of the pest available to Panama in January 2019 does not support the adoption of Measure 3.
Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Panama has failed to demonstrate that it adopted Measure 3

ISPM 2 and ISPM 20
7.980. The third category of information that Costa Rica considers pertinent consists of ISPM 2 and
ISPM 20. For Costa Rica, these standards are pertinent because "they refer to 'emergency' actions
– which may lead to the adoption of provisional measures pending technical justification – and
establish when such measures may be taken".2031 Panama disagrees with Costa Rica on the
pertinence of these two ISPM, since they do not address the phytosanitary risk in question and, in
its view, accepting their pertinence would mean imposing the particular ALOP reflected in these
standards on all WTO Members, contrary to Article 3.3 of the SPS Agreement.2032
7.981. With regard to Panama's argument that the ISPM are not pertinent because they do not
address the phytosanitary risk in question, the Panel recalls that, with respect to Measure 1, it has
concluded that pertinent information within the meaning of Article 5.7 does not refer exclusively to
information that addresses the particular risk in question.2033 As explained above2034, in the Panel's
view, Article 5.7 would not a priori exclude the pertinence of international standards of a general
nature that address the management of a particular situation (e.g. non-conformity), even if such
standards do not specifically refer to the product or risk in question.
7.982. Pursuant to Annex A(3) to the SPS Agreement, the IPPC Secretariat is a relevant
international organization in matters of plant health. In addition, the Panel notes that, unlike the
provisions of Annex A(3)(a) to the SPS Agreement on Codex standards, Annex A(3)(c) refers in
generic terms to "international standards, guidelines and recommendations developed under the
auspices of the Secretariat of the [IPPC]", without referring to any particular aspect of the field of
"plant health".2035 In the Panel's view, this language confirms that the pertinence of such standards
is not contingent on their addressing particular pests or products, as implied by Panama's argument.
Accordingly, the Panel considers that the ISPM, as standards developed under the auspices of the
IPPC Secretariat, constitute information "from" that organization that could be considered
"pertinent" for the purposes of Article 5.7.
7.983. With regard to the pertinence of the ISPM in question, the Panel observes that Costa Rica
refers to the following passages from ISPM 2 on the "Framework for pest risk analysis" and ISPM 20
on "Guidelines for a phytosanitary import regulatory system":
ISPM 2
1.1.3. Review of phytosanitary policies
… For existing trade, no new measures should be applied until the revision or new PRA
has been completed, unless this is warranted by new or unexpected phytosanitary
situations that may necessitate emergency measures.2036
ISPM 20
5.1.6. Non-compliance and emergency action
5.1.6.2 Emergency action
Emergency action may be required in a new or unexpected phytosanitary situation, such
as the detection of quarantine pests …

2032 Panama's first written submission, para. 303.

…
5.1.6.4 Withdrawal or modification of regulations
In the case of repeated non-compliance, or where a significant non-compliance or
interception warranting emergency action occurs, the NPPO of the importing contracting
party may withdraw the authorization (e.g. permit) allowing import, modify the
regulation, or institute an emergency or provisional measure with modified entry
procedures or a prohibition.2037
7.984. These sections of the ISPM address emergency actions and measures aimed at preventing
the entry, establishment or spread of a pest in a new, unexpected or unforeseen situation not
addressed by existing phytosanitary measures.2038
7.985. ISPM 2 provides a framework that describes the PRA process within the scope of the IPPC.
ISPM 20, meanwhile, describes the structure and operation of a phytosanitary import regulatory
system and the rights, obligations and responsibilities which should be considered in establishing,
operating and revising the system.2039
7.986. The Panel agrees with Costa Rica that ISPM 2 and ISPM 20 are pertinent insofar as they
provide guidance on the type of situations that may require emergency actions leading to the
adoption of provisional measures such as is the case of Measure 3, according to Panama. ISPM 2
reserves emergency measures for "new or unexpected phytosanitary situations", while ISPM 20
provides that an "[e]mergency action may be required in a new or unexpected phytosanitary
situation".2040
7.987. Costa Rica asserts that, when Measure 3 was adopted, no "new", "unexpected" or
"unforeseen" event had occurred with respect to imports of Costa Rican pineapples, such as, for
instance, an interception of the pest or significant non-compliance, to justify the application of
emergency provisional measures.2041
7.988. Panama contends that "nothing in ISPM 20 restricts the adoption of emergency measures to
cases of non-compliance or interception of a pest in a shipment of a commodity".2042 In this regard,
Panama indicates that the situation set forth in ISPM 20 of "[p]ests not regulated for a particular
pathway" is "the case at hand", since Panama temporarily suspended imports of pineapples from
Costa Rica in response to the presence of the pink mealybug in Costa Rica and scientific evidence
that called into question its previous conclusions as to the transmission of the pest through pineapple
imports from Costa Rica.2043 Panama also contends that Article 5.7 does not require the prior
detection of a pest for the adoption of a provisional measure such as Measure 3.2044
7.989. ISPM 2 does not provide guidance as to what may be considered a new, unexpected or
unforeseen situation. Conversely, the Panel notes that ISPM 20, when addressing emergency action
in section 5.1.6.2, refers to examples of the detection of pests. In the first paragraph of that section,
ISPM 20 refers explicitly to "a new or unexpected phytosanitary situation, such as the detection of
quarantine pests or potential quarantine pests".2045 Furthermore, in the subsequent paragraphs of
that section, which are reproduced below, ISPM 20 describes three "[c]ommonly encountered
