situations requiring emergency action".2046 In the three scenarios, reference is made explicitly or
implicitly to the detection of pests.
7.990. In the first scenario, which concerns "[p]ests not previously assessed", ISPM 20 states that,
"[a]t the time of interception, they may be categorized as regulated pests on a preliminary basis
because the NPPO has a cause to believe they pose a pest risk".2047 In the second scenario, which
deals with "[p]ests not regulated for a particular pathway", ISPM 20 refers implicitly to the
interception of the pest by stating that such pests "should be included on the appropriate list or
within other measure if it is determined that the occurrence of the pest in the same and similar
circumstances may be anticipated in the future".2048 In the Panel's view, this language indicates that
the emergency phytosanitary actions referred to in this scenario may be applied to non-regulated
pests in respect of particular pathways where it is determined that they are present in a shipment.
Lastly, the third scenario, which addresses the "[l]ack of adequate identification" of a pest, also
explicitly links emergency actions to pest detection by establishing how to proceed "[w]here pests
are routinely detected in a form that does not allow for adequate identification".2049
7.991. The context provided by other sections of ISPM 20, and by other ISPM and the IPPC, which
will be discussed below, appears to confirm this understanding that the concept of new, unforeseen
or unexpected situations is "commonly"2050 linked to the detection of pests in imported
consignments. Section 5.1.6.4 of ISPM 20 on "Withdrawal or modification of phytosanitary
regulation" provides that the NPPO of the importing contracting party may institute an emergency
or provisional measure in "the case of repeated non-compliance, or where a significant
non-compliance or interception warranting emergency action occurs".2051 In this regard, it should be
noted that the detection of regulated pests may be considered a significant instance of
non-compliance, as indicated in ISPM 13 on "Guidelines for the notification of non-compliance and
emergency action".2052
7.992. This same ISPM provides guidance for the notification of emergency measures related to the
failure of an imported consignment to comply with requirements or the detection of pests or
organisms therein. As indicated by Costa Rica2053, ISPM 13 establishes that emergency actions are
taken "on the detection in an imported consignment of … regulated pests" or "organisms posing a
potential phytosanitary threat".2054 The Panel also agrees with Costa Rica on the pertinence of
Article VII.6 of the IPPC2055, which confirms the above by establishing the right of any contracting
party to "tak[e] appropriate emergency action on the detection of a pest posing a potential threat
to its territories or the report of such a detection".2056
7.993. Having said this, the Panel agrees with Panama that the scenarios described in
section 5.1.6.2 of ISPM 20 do not constitute an exhaustive list of situations that may warrant the
adoption of an emergency action. This is evident from the use of expressions like "a new or
unexpected phytosanitary situation, such as"2057 and "[c]ommonly encountered situations requiring
emergency action include".2058 Therefore, the Panel does not rule out the possibility of scenarios not
explicitly envisaged in ISPM 20 that are not linked to the detection of a pest and that require the
adoption of emergency measures. Nevertheless, the scenarios discussed above, which are explicitly
identified in section 5.1.6.2 of ISPM 20, are linked to the detection of a pest. Since Panama asserts
that Measure 3 fits into one of these scenarios, namely that of "[p]ests not regulated for a particular
pathway", Measure 3 should result from the detection of the pest. However, the record contains no
evidence of the pest having been intercepted in shipments of Costa Rican pineapples.2059

7.994. Moreover, the Panel considers that Panama has failed to demonstrate the existence of a
new, unexpected or unforeseen phytosanitary situation that could warrant the adoption of Measure
3. In this regard, the Panel notes Panama's assertion that it "temporarily suspended imports of
pineapples from Costa Rica in response to the presence of M. hirsutus in Costa Rica and evidence
that called into question its previous conclusions as to the transmission of the pest through imports
of Costa Rican pineapples".2060 In the Panel's view, the two elements that Panama mentions, namely
the presence of the pink mealybug in Costa Rica and the 2011 publication reporting the presence of
the pest in pineapple in Fiji, do not constitute new, unexpected or unforeseen situations, given that:
(a) Panama knew since 2014 that the pest was present in Costa Rica because it was notified by
Costa Rica2061; and (b) the 2011 publication was included in the CABI and EPPO datasheets on the
pest, which are updated periodically, and which Panama consults regularly. Furthermore, the
CABI datasheet mentions a 2004 publication in relation to pineapple as a host of the pest.2062
7.995. The foregoing leads the Panel to conclude that Panama has not demonstrated that it adopted
Measure 3 on the basis of ISPM 2 and ISPM 20, since there is no evidence in the record of the pink
mealybug having been intercepted in shipments of Costa Rican pineapples. Panama has also failed
to demonstrate the existence of other kinds of new, unexpected or unforeseen situations that might
justify the adoption of Measure 3.

SPS Agreement. The Panel will first examine whether these measures, summarized in the table
below, constitute pertinent information for the purposes of Article 5.7 and, if so, whether Measure 3
was adopted on the basis of the information derived therefrom.

7.997. The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 allows Members to adopt SPS measures provisionally "on
the basis of available pertinent information, including that from the relevant international
organizations as well as from sanitary or phytosanitary measures applied by other Members".2068
The Panel therefore considers that the measures adopted by other Members identified by Costa Rica
could be considered pertinent information for the purposes of the second requirement of Article 5.7
of the SPS Agreement. While Article 5.7 does not refer explicitly to measures of the Member adopting
the provisional measure, the measures adopted by Panama may also be pertinent to the risk in
question. The fact that the list of available pertinent information in Article 5.7 is not exhaustive, as
denoted by the use of the term "including", would permit the consideration of not only the categories
of information mentioned therein but also other information deemed pertinent to the risk in question
that is available to the Member adopting the provisional measure.
7.998. Costa Rica considers that these measures are pertinent, even if they relate to fruit other
than pineapples, because they concern risks arising from the pink mealybug pest.2069 For its part,
Panama does not consider them pertinent because, in its view, they are not related to the
phytosanitary risk in question or were adopted in circumstances different from those of the present
case.2070
7.999. With respect to Panama's argument that the measures of other Members applied to products
other than pineapple are not pertinent because they are not related to the phytosanitary risk in
question2071, the Panel agrees with Costa Rica that the measures concerning the importation of
avocados are pertinent, as both pineapple and avocado are considered to be hosts of the pest.2072
As a result, countries that import these products face the risk of transmission of the pest through
imports of those products. In addition, the Panel observes that the scientific literature on the pest
does not distinguish between hosts when addressing issues such as the detection and identification
of the pest, spread pathways, or control methods.2073
7.1000. The Panel also disagrees with Panama's position that some measures of other Members
and of Panama itself are not pertinent because they were adopted in circumstances different from
those of the present case with regard to the risk assessment in question. Panama refers specifically
to the fact that "[t]he measures applicable to avocado imports [in Panama and Peru] stem from an
objective assessment of phytosanitary risk based on the information enabling such an
assessment"2074, unlike Measure 3. The Panel considers that the text of Article 5.7 does not support
Panama's approach, as it refers to "[SPS] measures applied by other Members". Accordingly, the
language of the provision does not allow any measure applied by a Member to be excluded a priori.
In the Panel's view, the pertinence of the measure in question should be determined on a
case-by-case basis, and in the light of all relevant facts and circumstances.
7.1001. In addition, the evidence in the record lends partial support to Panama's assertion, since it
shows only that risk assessments were conducted for the measures applied by Peru to avocado and

imports from Colombia, Panama's responses to the Panel's questions on this matter are inconclusive.
In response to the Panel's question as to whether Panama's PIR for avocado from Colombia are
based on a risk assessment within the meaning of Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement, Panama replied
that these PIR "are based on a bilateral phytosanitary protocol between Colombia and Panama, and
on the ICA resolution establishing provisions for the registration and management of farms
producing fresh fruit for export and the registration of exporters".2076 According to Panama, this
bilateral phytosanitary protocol and the ICA resolution constitute the risk assessment, which "was
characterized by the exchange and analysis of phytosanitary information, following which mutually
agreed mitigation measures were established".2077
7.1002. The Panel observes that Panama adopted the PIR for avocado from Colombia in
January 20072078 and modified them in December 2009.2079 The bilateral phytosanitary protocol to
which Panama refers is dated 16 December 20082080, and the ICA resolution, September 2004.2081
Accordingly, the Panel understands that, when Panama asserts that the protocol and the resolution
constitute the risk assessment for the PIR, it is referring to the 2009 PIR. Regarding these PIR,
Panama asserts that it did not assess the risk associated with the pink mealybug in avocado from
Colombia because, when it adopted the 2007 PIR, it already knew that the pest had been present in
Colombia since 2003, and it had classified the pink mealybug as a pest of interest to Panama.2082
The absence from the record of a risk assessment with respect to the pink mealybug leads the Panel
to consider that Panama has failed to demonstrate that the measures it applies to avocado imports
from Colombia are based on an objective assessment of the risk associated with the pink mealybug
and that they were thus adopted in circumstances different from those of Measure 3 with regard to
the risk assessment.
7.1003. Furthermore, the Panel observes that Panama presented a similar argument about the
different circumstances affecting pineapple imports from the United States and Colombia. Initially,
Panama suggested that the situation with regard to imports of pineapples from Costa Rica was
different to that concerning imports of pineapples from Colombia and the United States because,
"[u]nlike with these countries, Panama was unable to perform a more objective assessment of the
risk in relation to Costa Rican pineapples because the technical information required was not
available".2083 However, Panama subsequently clarified that it had not performed an assessment of
the risk of transmission of the pink mealybug through imports of fresh pineapples from these
two countries.2084
7.1004. Having addressed Panama's arguments rejecting the pertinence of the measures identified
by Costa Rica, the Panel will now consider the similarities between the measures of other Members
and Panama, on the one hand, and Measure 3, on the other, which, in the Panel's view, would justify
considering these measures as pertinent information for the purposes of Article 5.7. First, the Panel
notes similarities between the products imported in the sense that the measures of Panama and
Peru with regard to Colombian avocados concern the importation of a product (avocado) that, like
pineapple, is considered a host of the pink mealybug pest.2085 Second, the Panel observes similarities
between the phytosanitary status of the exporting Members in respect of the pink mealybug. The
pest is considered a quarantine pest and is present to a limited degree in both Colombia and

7.1005. With respect to the exemption that the European Union grants to pineapple imports from
any third country, including Costa Rica, which obviates the need for consignments to be accompanied
by a phytosanitary certificate, the Panel observes that this exemption is provided for in Part C of
Annex XI to Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2019/2072 of 28 November 2019.2088
Panama adopted Measure 3 in January 2019, months prior to the entry into force of the Regulation
providing for the exemption, and thus did not have this information at the time of the adoption of
the measure. Accordingly, the Panel considers that the measure applied by the European Union to
pineapples from Costa Rica does not constitute pertinent information for the purposes of the second
requirement of Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.
7.1006. In relation to Panama's measures concerning the importation of pineapple from Colombia
and the United States, the Panel notes significant factual discrepancies between the parties regarding
the very existence of such imports.
7.1007. Costa Rica asserts that, "after adopting the prohibition on the importation of pineapples
from Costa Rica on account of the pink mealybug, Panama has continued to import fresh pineapple
from countries where the pest is also present"2089, in particular Colombia and the United States.2090

the United States to Panama in 2020 and 2022 were also of, or included, fresh pineapple.2092
Costa Rica also submitted USDA data on pineapple exports over the period 2018-2022.2093 For
Costa Rica, these data show that Panama imported fresh pineapple from Colombia and the

United States and Colombia, given that Panama imported pineapple only from these two

data.2097 Panama considers that Costa Rica has failed to meet the burden of proof with respect to its

7.1009. The INEC and USDA data on pineapple imports into Panama submitted by Costa Rica do
not differentiate between fresh and dried pineapple from 2014 onwards.2099 These data show that,
from the adoption of Measure 3 in January 2019 until the establishment of this Panel in September
2021, Panama imported pineapple from the United States in 2019 and 2020 and from Colombia in
2020; however, they do not specify whether it is fresh or dried pineapple. In the Panel's view, the
fact that Panama imported fresh pineapple from the United States in the period 2010-2013, as
indicated by the INEC data2100, does not necessarily mean that, in subsequent years, Panama
continued to import fresh pineapple from this origin. The Panel considers that, on the basis of the
data on pineapple imports submitted by Costa Rica, which do not distinguish between imports of
fresh pineapple and dried pineapple for the period 2019-2021, it cannot be concluded that Costa Rica
has demonstrated that Panama imported fresh pineapple from the United States and Colombia
following the adoption of Measure 3.
7.1010. Furthermore, the Panel observes that three of the four customs declarations submitted by
Panama for the period 2020-20212101, which account for 93.70% of the total c.i.f. value of pineapple
imports from the United States and Colombia during this period, indicate that imports into Panama
were of dried or processed pineapple.2102 The other customs declaration, which concerns a pineapple
shipment from the United States in 2020 with a very low c.i.f. value of PAB 363.172103, refers simply
to "pineapples".2104 The Panel recalls Panama's assertion that there are no PIR for fresh pineapple
from the United States and Colombia2105 and that, in the absence of PIR, which "is the official
document declaring the exporting country eligible to export the product of interest to Panama"2106,
importers cannot submit food import notifications.2107

inputs must comply with the requirements established by the [DNSV]. These

7.1012. Furthermore, the Panel notes that Decree-Law No. 11 of 2006 establishing AUPSA provides
that AUPSA and the National Directorate of Food Import Standards are responsible for "establishing
the sanitary and/or phytosanitary requirements that foodstuffs must meet in for their introduction"
into the national territory.2110
7.1013. The Panel considers that this piece of Panamanian legislation confirms Panama's position
that the importation of a plant product, such as pineapple in the case at hand, is contingent on
compliance with the PIR established by the competent Panamanian authorities.
7.1014. There is no evidence in the record of the existence of PIR for fresh pineapple from the
United States and Colombia. These PIR, which, if they exist, should be publicly available, as
established by the Panamanian regulatory framework2111, have not been provided by Costa Rica in
support of its assertion that the importation of fresh pineapple from these two countries is permitted,
nor do they appear on the website where Panama's PIR for food products are available.2112
Accordingly, it can be assumed that the imports of "pineapples" from the United States with a c.i.f.
value of PAB 363.17 in the 2020 customs declaration did not refer to imports of fresh pineapples.
7.1015. The Panel has compared the customs declarations submitted by Panama for the period
2020-2021 with the data on pineapple imports from Panama's INEC database and notes that the
c.i.f. values in the customs declarations match those reported by INEC. While it is true that the gross
weight values do not match, the fact that the c.i.f. values match is a strong indication that the
information contained in the customs declarations is accurate. In this regard, the Panel notes that
Costa Rica expresses doubts as to whether the customs declarations submitted by Panama "duly
demonstrate that all the pineapple that has entered the Panamanian market since 2014 has been
dried pineapple".2113 Costa Rica cites as an example a discrepancy for 2015 between the quantities
of imports reflected in the customs declaration, on the one hand, and in the official INEC data, on
the other.2114 However, Costa Rica does not explain why this discrepancy invalidates the data in the
customs declarations on the imported product, nor does it provide any other reasons why the Panel
should not consider this evidence.
7.1016. The Panel recalls that the burden of proof in respect of this issue lies with Costa Rica, which
is the party asserting that fresh pineapple was imported from the United States into Panama after
the adoption of Measure 3. In this regard, the Panel considers that Costa Rica has not demonstrated
that Panama imported fresh pineapple from the United States and Colombia after the adoption of
Measure 3.
7.1017. A review of the Members' measures described above shows that Peru has not suspended
imports of avocados and pineapples from Colombia, even though they are fresh plant products and
hosts of the pink mealybug from a country, Colombia, where the pest is present. Similarly, Panama
itself allows avocado imports from Colombia, with there being no evidence that Panama has
conducted an assessment of the risks associated with the pink mealybug. The Panel therefore
considers that Measure 3, insofar as it prevents the importation of fresh pineapples from Costa Rica
into Panama, bears no rational and objective relationship to the information derived from the
measures applied by Peru and by Panama itself that were identified by Costa Rica.
7.1018. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Panama did not adopt Measure 3 on the basis of the
pertinent information derived from the measures applied by Peru to imports of avocados and
pineapples from Colombia, and by Panama itself to imports of avocados from Colombia.

- 278 -
Conclusion on whether Panama adopted Measure 3 on the basis of available pertinent
information
7.1019. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Measure 3, which inactivates the
resolution containing the PIR for Costa Rican pineapples in Panama2115, bears no rational and
objective relationship to, and therefore was not adopted on the basis of, the following information:
(a) general information concerning the pink mealybug pest; (b) information on Costa Rica's
phytosanitary status in respect of the pink mealybug pest; (c) ISPM 2 and ISPM 20; and (d) the
measures applied by Peru and by Panama itself.
7.1020. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Panama has not demonstrated that it adopted Measure 3
on the basis of available pertinent information within the meaning of Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement.
7.5.1.2.1.3 Conclusion on Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.1021. In paragraphs 7.962 and 7.1019 above, the Panel found that Panama has not demonstrated
that it adopted Measure 3 in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence, or on the basis of
available pertinent information, in breach of two of the requirements under Article 5.7. Considering
these findings and the fact that the four requirements of Article 5.7 are cumulative2116, the Panel
does not consider it necessary to address the two requirements of Article 5.7 set out in the second
sentence, which concern maintaining the measure, in order to resolve this dispute.2117
7.1022. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 3 does not constitute a provisional
measure covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.
7.5.1.2.2 Claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1023. As the Panel has found that Measure 3 is not a provisional measure under Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement, Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement are applicable to this measure.
The Panel will therefore proceed with the examination of Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2,
and 5.3, which concern the assessment of risk and the elements to be taken into account in that
assessment, and under Article 2.2, relating to the obligation to ensure that the SPS measure is based
on scientific principles and is not maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.
7.5.1.2.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
Costa Rica
7.1024. Costa Rica claims that, since Panama has not conducted a risk assessment that provides a
scientific basis for Measure 3, Panama has acted inconsistently with the obligation under Article 5.1
of the SPS Agreement to base its phytosanitary measure on an assessment of the risk as appropriate
to the circumstances.2118 Costa Rica considers that the absence of a risk assessment results, in turn,
in a breach of Articles 5.2 and 5.3. As a result of the failure to comply with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3
of the SPS Agreement, Costa Rica claims that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 2.2, since it is
not based on scientific principles nor is it maintained with sufficient scientific evidence.2119
7.1025. Costa Rica argues that Measure 3 was adopted without any scientific basis or underpinning
risk analysis, even though Panama referred to a "risk assessment" in its communication of

7.1027. Costa Rica submits that Panama's communication of 29 January 2019 does not contain,

Costa Rica adds that ISPM 11, which sets out the elements to be reflected in any "appropriate" risk

whether there is any risk of introduction of the pest by means of the pathway in question.2128
Panama
7.1028. Panama asserts that it adopted provisional measures, including Measure 3, as a precaution,
in response to scientific evidence that called into question its previous conclusions concerning the

given that it acted strictly within the limits of its rights under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement and

Relevant legal standard
7.1030. As mentioned under Measure 12132, Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement requires the Panel to
examine two aspects:
a. whether there is a risk assessment2133, as appropriate to the circumstances, taking into
account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant international organizations;
and
b. if there is a risk assessment, whether the SPS measure of the Member concerned is based
on that risk assessment.2134
7.1031. It was also noted that Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement sets forth elements that must be
taken into account when assessing risks. Similarly, Article 5.3 of the SPS Agreement, which is
reproduced hereunder, and pursuant to which Costa Rica submits claims with respect to Measure 3,
lays down four economic factors to be taken into account in assessing the risk to plant life or health:
In assessing the risk to animal or plant life or health and determining the measure to
be applied for achieving the appropriate level of sanitary or phytosanitary protection
from such risk, Members shall take into account as relevant economic factors: the
potential damage in terms of loss of production or sales in the event of the entry,
establishment or spread of a pest or disease; the costs of control or eradication in the
territory of the importing Member; and the relative cost-effectiveness of alternative
approaches to limiting risks.
7.1032. The Panel agrees with the interpretation of Articles 5.2 and 5.3 that these provisions shed
light on the elements that are of relevance in the assessment of risks foreseen in Article 5.1.2135
Accordingly, previous panels have considered, in their analysis under Article 5.1, whether Members
took into account the elements contained in Articles 5.2 and 5.3.2136
7.1033. With respect to Article 2.2, the Panel recalls that a finding of a violation of Articles 5.1, 5.2,
and 5.3 may give rise to a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2.2137 This is
because Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement constitute a "specific application"2138 of the
obligation under Article 2.2 to ensure that an SPS measure is based on scientific principles and
sufficient scientific evidence.
2132 See para. 7.310 above.
2133 Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement defines a "risk assessment" as follows:
The evaluation of the likelihood of entry, establishment or spread of a pest or disease within the
territory of an importing Member according to the sanitary or phytosanitary measures which
might be applied, and of the associated potential biological and economic consequences; or the
evaluation of the potential for adverse effects on human or animal health arising from the
presence of additives, contaminants, toxins or disease-causing organisms in food, beverages or
feedstuffs.

to be taken into account when conducting the relevant risk assessment.

7.1034. The Panel will now consider Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 with
respect to Measure 3.
Whether Measure 3 is based on a risk assessment in accordance with Article 5.1 of the
SPS Agreement
7.1035. Panama has confirmed that Measure 3 is not based on a risk assessment, arguing that it is
a provisional measure that was adopted in a situation in which the body of available scientific
evidence did not allow an appropriate assessment of the risks to be performed, as required under
Article 5.1 and as defined in Annex A to the SPS Agreement.2139
7.1036. The Panel considers that, as claimed by Costa Rica, a measure cannot be based on a risk
assessment if no such assessment exists. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent
with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement in that it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to
the circumstances, of the risks to plant life or health, taking into account risk assessment techniques
developed by the relevant international organizations.
Whether Panama took into account the elements and factors listed in Articles 5.2 and 5.3
in the assessment of the risk
7.1037. As mentioned in paragraph 7.1032 above, Articles 5.2 and 5.3 are linked to Article 5.1, in
that they shed light on the elements and factors that are of relevance in the assessment of risks
foreseen in Article 5.1. Given that, in the case of Measure 3, Panama failed to conduct an assessment
of the risk, the Panel finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Articles 5.2 and 5.3 of the
SPS Agreement, as Panama did not take into account the elements and factors contained therein in
the assessment of the risks.
Whether Measure 3 is based on scientific principles and is not maintained without
sufficient scientific evidence in accordance with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1038. The Panel recalls that Article 5.1 constitutes a "specific application" of the basic obligations
under Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement.2140 The Panel shares the view expressed in previous
disputes that where a measure is not based on a risk assessment pursuant to Article 5.1 of the
SPS Agreement, it can be presumed not to be based on scientific principles or to be maintained
without sufficient scientific evidence within the meaning of Article 2.2.2141 The Panel considers that
this presumption can be made in the present case because of its finding that, in the absence of a
risk assessment, Measure 3 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3.2142 The Panel further notes
that Panama has failed to submit arguments to rebut this presumption.
7.1039. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 3 is not based on an assessment of the
risk, in breach of Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement, and in the absence of a rebuttal
by Panama concerning the presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2 of the Agreement, the Panel
finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 because it is not based on scientific principles
and is maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.
7.5.1.2.2.3 Conclusion on Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1040. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent with the following
provisions:

7.5.1.3.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.1048. As explained in the context of Measure 2, Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement contains two
different obligations. The first sentence of Article 2.3 requires that SPS measures do not arbitrarily
or unjustifiably discriminate between Members where identical or similar conditions prevail, including
between their own territory and that of other Members. The second sentence of Article 2.3 requires
that SPS measures shall not be applied in a manner which would constitute a disguised restriction
on international trade.2161
7.1049. The Panel recalls that, to establish a violation of the first sentence of Article 2.3, Costa Rica
must demonstrate three cumulative elements:
a. the measure discriminates between the territories of Members other than Panama, or
between Panama's territory and that of another Member;
b. the discrimination is arbitrary or unjustifiable; and
c. identical or similar conditions prevail in the territory of the Members compared.2162
7.1050. With respect to the second sentence of Article 2.3, the Panel recalls that a disguised
restriction on international trade has been considered to encompass measures that constitute
arbitrary or unjustifiable discrimination.2163
7.5.1.3.2.2 Whether Measure 3 is inconsistent with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the
SPS Agreement
7.1051. As it did for Measure 2, the Panel will analyse Costa Rica's claim under the first sentence
of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement by first examining whether the conditions identified by
Costa Rica are identical or similar in the Members in question, within the meaning of that
provision.2164
Whether identical or similar conditions prevail in the territories of the Members in
question
7.1052. In order to ascertain whether identical or similar conditions prevail in the territories of the
Members in question, the Panel will first examine the relevance of the conditions identified by
Costa Rica. Subsequently, the Panel will address the similarity of the conditions it has deemed
relevant.
7.1053. Costa Rica submits that the relevant phytosanitary conditions are those relating to the
presence of the pink mealybug.2165 Panama, for its part, does not address the issue of the relevance
of the conditions identified by Costa Rica.
7.1054. The Panel agrees with previous panels that both the objective of the measure and the risk
it addresses are relevant aspects when it comes to identifying the relevant conditions for the
purposes of Article 2.3.2166 In this respect, the Panel has found that the objective of Measure 3
corresponds to the objective set out in Annex A(1)(a), namely "to protect ... plant life or health
within the territory of the Member from risks arising from the entry, establishment or spread of
pests".2167 With regard to the risk addressed by Measure 3, the Panel recalls that the parties do not

and the risk associated with that pest are relevant conditions for an analysis under Article 2.3.2169
The Panel therefore agrees with Costa Rica with respect to the relevance of the conditions relating

7.1056. Costa Rica asserts that the conditions relating to the presence of the pink mealybug are

present with limited distribution in these three countries.2170 The information in the record confirms

7.1057. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that the conditions of Costa Rica are similar to
those of the United States and Colombia as regards its phytosanitary status with respect to the
pink mealybug.

7.1058. The Panel will now analyse whether Measure 3 discriminates against fresh pineapples from

of Costa Rican pineapples, the importation of which is prohibited, in comparison with the treatment
granted to pineapples from the United States and Colombia, and to avocados from Colombia, the
importation of which is permitted.2172
7.1059. Regarding the treatment Panama grants to fresh pineapples from the United States and

imported into Panama.2173 Moreover, after examining the INEC and USDA data on pineapple imports
into Panama submitted by Costa Rica and the customs declarations for 2020-2021 submitted by
Panama, the Panel concluded that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Panama imported fresh
pineapples from the United States and Colombia after adopting Measure 3.2174

7.1066. The Panel identifies three main issues in the arguments of the parties, which it will now
address. These issues concern: (a) the differences in the PIR for avocado from Colombia and for
pineapple from Costa Rica in relation to the mitigation measures for the pink mealybug; (b) the
existence or not of assessments of the risk of entry, establishment and spread of the pink mealybug
via avocado imports from Colombia and pineapple imports from Costa Rica; and (c) compliance or
not with Panama's ALOP with respect to imports of these two products.
7.1067. With respect to the first issue, relating to the mitigation measures for the pink mealybug
set out in the relevant PIR, the Panel observes that Panama focuses on the fact that, unlike the PIR
for pineapple from Costa Rica, the PIR for avocado from Colombia provide for a mitigation measure
for the pink mealybug. This measure requires that the shipment has a phytosanitary certificate
issued by the Colombian Agricultural Institute (ICA) containing an additional declaration certifying
that the shipment is free from pink mealybug.2184
7.1068. Panama justifies this difference in the PIR for avocado and the PIR for pineapple on the
grounds that, when it adopted the PIR for avocado in 2007, it was aware of the presence of the pest
in Colombia since 2003, and considered that the risk of pest transmission was greater in avocado
imports from Colombia compared to pineapple imports from Costa Rica. Conversely, Panama notes
that, when it adopted the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica in 2008, it was not aware of the presence
of the pest in Costa Rica given that it was notified for the first time several years later, in 2014.2185
7.1069. The Panel notes that the reason given by Panama for having included a mitigation measure
for the pink mealybug in the PIR for avocado is linked solely to its knowledge of Colombia's
phytosanitary status with respect to the pest, and not to other aspects such as detections of the
pest in shipments of avocados from Colombia2186 or the potential higher risk posed by avocado as a
main host of the pest.2187 This fits with Panama's explanation of how it drafts its PIR for foods of
plant origin intended for consumption. The Panel recalls that Panama has indicated that, in order to
draft these PIR, AUPSA "verified the pests of quarantine concern reported officially by the [NPPOs]
of each country of origin and compared them with the list of pests of Panama's NPPO, as well as its
ability to follow the pathway"2188, and that "[t]his verification was Panama's risk analysis, but it was
not documented as such."2189
7.1070. Panama has confirmed that it was the presence of the pest in Colombia that prompted it to
include a mitigation measure for this pest in its PIR for avocado. However, when Panama was notified
about the presence of the pest in Costa Rica in May 2014, it did not modify the PIR for pineapple
from Costa Rica to include a mitigation measure, as it had done for avocados from Colombia. Panama
has reiterated on various occasions that it was the discovery in 2018 of a publication from 2011,
which reported the presence of the pest in pineapple in Fiji, together with the presence of the pest
in Costa Rica, which cast doubt on its previous conclusions on the risk associated with the pest.2190
However, as previously mentioned, the publication in question from 2011 is included in the main
databases that Panama consulted to draft its PIR, such as those of CABI and EPPO2191, as is another
publication from 2004 that links pineapple to the pink mealybug.2192 If, as Panama asserts, the
reason for including a mitigation measure for the pink mealybug in the PIR for avocado from
Colombia in 2007, and subsequently retaining that measure in the 2009 version of the PIR, was that
it was aware of the presence of the pest in that country, when it became aware that the pest was

7.1071. Accordingly, the Panel considers that the fact that Panama permits avocado imports from
Colombia while not permitting pineapple imports from Costa Rica is not justified by the existence of
a mitigation measure for the pink mealybug with respect to avocado from Colombia, but not with
respect to pineapple from Costa Rica.
7.1072. With respect to the second issue, relating to the existence or not of risk assessments, the
Panel recalls that, under Article 5.7, it considered that Panama had failed to demonstrate that the
measures it applies to avocado imports from Colombia are based on an objective assessment of the
risk associated with the pink mealybug.2194 In turn, Panama has not submitted any risk assessment
that formed the basis for its PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica since, as Panama states, its
"verif[ication]" of the pests of quarantine concern reported officially by Costa Rica's NPPO, which it
subsequently compared with its NPPO's list of pests, was the risk analysis, but was not documented
as such.2195
7.1073. The Panel therefore considers that the fact Panama permits avocado imports from Colombia
while not permitting pineapple imports from Costa Rica cannot be justified by the existence of a risk
assessment in the case of the former, but not the latter. Moreover, in response to Panama's
argument that it had sufficient information to conduct the risk assessment for avocado, but not for
pineapple, the Panel refers to its finding under Article 5.7 that Panama was not in a situation of
insufficient relevant scientific evidence that prevented it from conducting the risk assessment in
question.2196
7.1074. With respect to the third and final issue, relating to compliance with Panama's ALOP2197,
Panama asserts that avocados comply with its ALOP "on the basis of the bilateral phytosanitary
protocol between Colombia and Panama, and the ICA resolution establishing provisions for the
registration and management of farms producing fresh fruit for export and the registration of
exporters".2198 With respect to pineapple from Costa Rica, Panama asserts that the 2008 PIR
complied with its ALOP until a publication indicating that the pink mealybug had been detected in
pineapple in Fiji and the presence of the pest in Costa Rica called into question its previous
conclusions.2199 Panama adds that it adopted Measure 3 because it was not possible to assess
whether the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica achieved Panama's ALOP.2200
7.1075. The Panel disagrees with Panama that it could not reassess whether the 2008 PIR for
pineapple from Costa Rica met its ALOP. As the Panel found under Article 5.7, when Panama adopted
Measure 3 in 2019, it had sufficient information to perform a risk assessment that would have
allowed it to determine whether the existing PIR still met its ALOP, despite the discovery of the 2011
publication reporting the presence of the pink mealybug in pineapple in Fiji and the presence of the
pest in Costa Rica since May 2014. Moreover, the Panel recalls that Panama has not demonstrated
that its PIR for avocado from Colombia are based on a risk assessment relating to the pink
mealybug.2201 Accordingly, the fact Panama permits avocado imports from Colombia while not
permitting pineapple imports from Costa Rica does not appear to be justified by Panama's assertion

that, in the case of avocados from Colombia, it was able to determine that the ALOP was met, while
this was not possible for pineapple from Costa Rica because it did not have sufficient information.
7.1076. An additional issue raised by Costa Rica is whether the fact that they are different hosts
justifies in any way the discriminatory treatment of pineapple from Costa Rica. In this respect,
Costa Rica asserts that the fact that pineapple is not considered a main or primary host of the pest,
unlike avocado, "means that, technically, avocado has a higher risk of being associated with the pink
mealybug".2202 As mentioned in the context of the claims under Article 5.7, both avocado and
pineapple are considered hosts of the pink mealybug.2203 Some sources classify avocado as a main
or primary host of the pink mealybug.2204 However, the scientific literature on the pest does not
distinguish between hosts when addressing issues such as the detection and identification of the
pest, spread pathways, and control methods.2205 It can therefore be argued that, in relation to
products from areas with the same phytosanitary status for the pest, avocado poses at least as high
risk of being associated with the pest as pineapple does. Accordingly, the foregoing also does not
justify a discriminatory treatment to the detriment of pineapple from Costa Rica.
7.1077. In the previous paragraphs, the Panel has determined that: (a) both avocados and
pineapples are host fruits of the pest that come from Members where the pest is present, with limited
distribution; and (b) their respective PIR have been drafted by AUPSA following the same procedure,
which seeks to verify the pests of quarantine concern reported officially by the relevant NPPOs and
to compare them with the list of pests of Panama's NPPO, and its ability to follow the pathway.
Moreover, it should be recalled that the Panel found under Article 5.7 that Panama had sufficient
information to conduct an assessment of the risk in question, accordingly Panama could have
assessed whether the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica still met its ALOP following incidents that,
according to Panama, called into question its previous conclusions on the risks associated with the
pink mealybug. Accordingly, the Panel finds nothing in the record that justifies Panama's
discriminatory treatment of pineapple from Costa Rica compared to avocado from Colombia.
7.1078. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that the discriminatory treatment under
Measure 3 of pineapple from Costa Rica compared to avocado from Colombia, whereby imports of
the former are prohibited and imports of the latter are permitted, is arbitrary or unjustifiable given
that it bears no rational connection to the stated objective of Measure 3 of protecting plant life and
health in Panama's territory from risks arising from the entry, establishment or spread of the pink
mealybug.
7.5.1.3.2.3 Conclusion on the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
7.1079. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
Measure 3 is inconsistent with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement, because it
discriminates arbitrarily or unjustifiably against pineapple from Costa Rica compared to avocado
from Colombia.
7.1080. The Panel also finds that Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Measure 3 is inconsistent
with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement because it discriminates arbitrarily and
unjustifiably against pineapple from Costa Rica compared to pineapple from the United States and
Colombia.

7.5.1.5.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.5.1.5.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1091. Costa Rica alleges that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
because it is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP. Costa Rica considers
that there are alternative measures that are reasonably available to Panama, that achieve its ALOP,
and are significantly less restrictive to trade. Costa Rica also alleges that, consequently, Measure 3
is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, as it is not applied only to the extent necessary
to protect plant life or health.2216
7.1092. Costa Rica identifies two alternative measures that Panama could have applied to achieve
its ALOP against the pink mealybug pest which are significantly less restrictive to trade than a "total
prohibition" such as Measure 3.2217 These two measures consist of: (a) applying the PIR of Resolution
AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 concerning the importation of pineapples, which Panama inactivated in
January 2019 without any new, unexpected or urgent phytosanitary situation having arisen2218; and
(b) requiring that shipments of Costa Rican pineapples be certified as free from the pink mealybug,
or as originating in areas, places or sites of production free from the pink mealybug.2219
7.1093. Costa Rica submits that these alternative measures fulfil the three requirements under
footnote 3 to Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2220
7.1094. First, Costa Rica argues that the alternative measures are reasonably available taking into
account technical and economic feasibility, because Panama has already applied them, and continues
to apply them, to pineapples from Costa Rica and to other imported products such as avocados from
Colombia and almonds from China.2221
7.1095. Second, Costa Rica submits that the alternative measures would achieve Panama's ALOP,
even if it were very high, because they provide a high level of protection from the pest, thus
preventing its introduction into Panama.2222 In this regard, Costa Rica notes that the specific
certification requirements with respect to the pink mealybug already achieve Panama's ALOP for
shipments of products from countries where the pest is present (namely, avocados from Colombia
and almonds from China).2223
7.1096. Third, according to Costa Rica, the alternative measures are significantly less
trade-restrictive than Measure 3, as they would allow Costa Rican pineapples access to the
Panamanian market.2224

that the two reports cited by the panel in Korea – Radionuclides – namely, Japan – Apples
(Article 21.5 – US) and India – Agricultural products – also do not support its assertion.2246
7.1111. As explained in paragraphs 7.832-7.833 above, the Panel sees no reason not to take into
consideration the panel's findings in Korea – Radionuclides concerning the use of a measure by the
importing country and its implications when analysing the availability of an alternative measure to
that Member. In addition, the Panel understands that Panama's central argument is that a measure
that is technically and economically feasible in one context may not be so in another. However,
Panama has applied the first alternative measure in the same context (namely, to regulate the
importation of fresh pineapple from Costa Rica) for more than 10 years, until the adoption of
Measure 3 in January 2019, without any evidence that there had been a change in the circumstances
relating to the importation of this product.
7.1112. Accordingly, and in the absence of counter-arguments from Panama, the Panel considers
that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the first alternative measure was reasonably available to
Panama both at the time Measure 3 was adopted and later, during the period it was in force.
Second alternative measure: specific PIR for the pink mealybug
7.1113. Costa Rica notes that Panama already requires "pest-free shipment" or "shipment from
pest-free areas, places or sites of production" certification for imports of avocados from Colombia
and almonds from China, respectively.2247 Costa Rica also argues that ISPM 12 provides that
phytosanitary certificates may include an additional declaration.2248 Panama, for its part, has not
advanced any reason why the second alternative measure proposed by Costa Rica would not be
technically or economically feasible, within the meaning of footnote 3 to Article 5.6.
7.1114. The first example of specific PIR for the pink mealybug relates to the resolution concerning
the importation of fresh avocados from Colombia. This resolution establishes that shipments must
be covered by a phytosanitary certificate issued by the ICA. This certificate must include, inter alia,
an additional declaration certifying that "[t]he consignment is free from ... Maconellicoccus hirsutus"
and of live insects, contaminants and leaves.2249 The second example of specific PIR for the pink
mealybug relates to the resolution concerning the importation of dried almonds from China. This
resolution requires that shipments of almonds be covered by a phytosanitary certificate issued by
the NPPO of China. That certificate must include an additional declaration specifying, inter alia, that
"[t]he commodity comes from areas, places or sites of production that have been officially recognized
or accredited as free from the pink mealybug (Maconellicoccus hirsutus)", and that the commodity
is free from pests of quarantine interest for Panama, such as those listed in the resolution.2250 In
connection to these two examples, Costa Rica also refers to the requirement laid down in the
2244 Panel Report, Korea – Radionuclides, para. 7.144 (quoting Panel Reports, India – Agricultural
Products, paras. 7.541-7.542; and Japan – Apples (Article 21.5 – US), para. 8.187). See also paras. 7.832 -

resolution inactivated by Panama in January 2019, which requested that the phytosanitary certificate
certify that the consignment is free from the pest Paracoccus marginatus.2251
7.1115. The Panel considers that the examples mentioned by Costa Rica demonstrate that Panama
requires phytosanitary certificates issued by the NPPOs of exporting countries in order to ensure
that fruits imported into Panama comply with its PIR. Panama also requires that these certificates
include specific declarations that shipments are free from a certain pest or come from pest-free
areas, places or sites of production. Panama even uses these requirements in relation to the pink
mealybug for the importation of host fruits of this pest, such as avocados.2252 Similarly, Panama has
required phytosanitary certificates for the importation of Costa Rican pineapple, albeit it to certify
the absence of another pest in the shipment.2253
7.1116. The Panel observes that ISPM 12, entitled "Phytosanitary certificates", provides model
phytosanitary certificates that may be used for reference purposes. These certificates are used to
attest that consignments meet PIR2254 and may include an additional declaration.2255 The fact that
this measure is envisaged in ISPM 12 is indicative that this type of measure may be feasible for
trade in plant products.
7.1117. Accordingly, and in the absence of counter-arguments from Panama, the Panel considers
that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the second alternative measure was reasonably available to
Panama both at the time Measure 3 was adopted and later, during the period it was in force.
7.1118. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the two alternative
measures it proposes are reasonably available taking into account technical and economic feasibility,
within the meaning of footnote 3 to Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.
7.1119. The Panel will now proceed to analyse whether the two alternative measures identified by
Costa Rica achieve Panama's ALOP.
Whether the alternative measures achieved Panama's ALOP
7.1120. In accordance with the legal standard described above2256, the Panel will first identify
Panama's ALOP and then compare it with the level of protection that would be achieved through the
alternative measures.
Panama's ALOP
7.1121. Panama notes that its ALOP in the area of plant health is to "prevent and control, in a
comprehensive manner" and, therefore, to avoid entirely "the introduction, establishment, and
spread of pests of plants and plant products in the territory of the Republic of Panama".2257 According
to Panama, this ALOP is set forth in Law No. 47 of 9 July 1996 and is specifically incorporated in the
applicable PIR.2258 Panama confirms that this ALOP applies to imports of fresh pineapple from
Costa Rica, and that it has not changed since the adoption of Measure 3.2259

7.1122. Costa Rica does not dispute Panama's definition of its ALOP.2260 Nevertheless, it asks the
Panel, when assessing or determining Panama's ALOP, to consider that Panama's conduct is not
compatible with the objective of minimizing negative trade effects, pursuant to Article 5.4 of the
SPS Agreement.2261
7.1123. The Panel recalls that the ALOP can be understood as an "objective" that SPS measures
seek to attain2262, and that the objective of Measure 3 is to protect Panama from the risk of entry,
establishment and spread of the pink mealybug.2263 The Panel also recalls that the definition of ALOP
in Annex A(5) of the SPS Agreement indicates that the ALOP must reflect the "level of protection"
deemed appropriate by the Member to attain its sanitary or phytosanitary protection objectives.2264
Bearing in mind that the ALOP is also defined as the "acceptable level of risk"2265, Panama's ALOP
relevant to Measure 3 should reflect Panama's acceptable level of risk with respect to the introduction
of the pink mealybug into its territory through pineapple imports from Costa Rica.2266
7.1124. Panama asserts that its ALOP is set out in Law No. 47 of 1996. This Law states that its
provisions "regulate all actions relating to the protection of plants of national agricultural heritage,
with the primary objective of preventing and controlling phytosanitary issues in a comprehensive
manner and achieving phytosanitary quality in plants and plant products".2267 The Law also states
that one of Panama's fundamental objectives from a phytosanitary perspective is to "prevent the
introduction, establishment and spread of pests of plants and plant products".2268
7.1125. Given that the ALOP can be understood as an "objective" that the SPS measure seeks to
attain2269, the relevant provisions of the Law in question may inform Panama's relevant ALOP.2270
However, this must be expressed clearly enough to allow analysis of whether a particular
SPS measure reaches this level of protection.2271
7.1126. Panama refers to a generic ALOP for preventing the entry, establishment and spread of
pests of plants and plant products. In the case of Measure 3, the aim would be to prevent the entry,
establishment and spread of the pink mealybug. For its part, Costa Rica considers that Panama's
ALOP is to "prevent the introduction of [the pink mealybug] in its territory".2272
7.1127. The Panel considers that this definition of Panama's ALOP is consistent with its assertion
that it did not have to wait for the risk of the pink mealybug being introduced in its territory to
materialize in order to adopt a measure that addressed that risk.2273 The Panel understands that
Panama's ALOP for the pink mealybug is high, given that its aim is to prevent the entry of the pest
into its territory, but it is not zero-risk, since Panama permits the importation of products that host

7.1128. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Panama's relevant ALOP could be
framed as a high, but not zero-risk, level of protection that avoids to the extent possible the
introduction of the pink mealybug into Panamanian territory through the importation of Costa Rican
pineapple. Accordingly, and taking into account the risk that Panama seeks to address, Panama's
ALOP can be defined as the prevention of the entry, establishment and spread of the pink mealybug
in its territory.
The level of protection that the alternative measures achieve
7.1129. The Panel will now proceed to assess whether the alternative measures proposed by
Costa Rica achieve Panama's ALOP. To that end, the Panel will compare Panama's ALOP with the
level of protection that would be achieved by those alternative measures.
First alternative measure: application of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008
7.1130. Costa Rica submits that the first alternative measure achieves Panama's ALOP with respect
to the pink mealybug, given that:
a. The pink mealybug is not present in pineapple production areas in Costa Rica, which is
why the requirements applied previously would be sufficient, since they required,
inter alia, that every shipment of Costa Rican pineapples to Panama: (i) be inspected
and/or tested to determine that they are free from the quarantine pests specified by
Panama; and (ii) arrive "free from live insects of any type", which would include the pink
mealybug.2275
b. Exports of fresh pineapple from Costa Rica to Panama continued as ususal, without
Panama reporting any incidence, after Costa Rica informed Panama and the
IPPC Secretariat of the presence of the pink mealybug in its territory in May 2014.2276
Costa Rica adds that, when Measure 3 was adopted in January 2019, there had been no
change in its territory with respect to the pink mealybug.2277
c. Panama does not apply any PIR relating to the pink mealybug to fresh pineapple imports
from the United States or Colombia, despite both countries having reported the presence
of this pest in their territories.2278
7.1131. Panama responds that it cannot assess whether this alternative measure can achieve its
ALOP with respect to Costa Rican pineapples because the scientific evidence is insufficient to perform
a risk assessment.2279
7.1132. As mentioned above, the level of risk is relevant for determining whether a measure would
achieve a Member's ALOP.2280 In this case, before the adoption of Measure 3, the first alternative
measure achieved Panama's ALOP. In order to determine whether this alternative still achieves this
ALOP after the adoption of Measure 3, the Panel considers it necessary to examine whether the level
of risk in question is the same or whether, on the contrary, it has increased. In the event that the

- 298 -
level of risk has not changed, this would allow us to conclude that the alternative measure continues
to achieve Panama's ALOP as it did before the adoption of Measure 3.
7.1133. The Panel observes that Costa Rica is assuming that the pest is not present in Panamanian
territory and that Panama's ALOP is to prevent the entry of the pest into its territory. For Costa Rica,
every alternative that has demonstrably prevented the entry of the pest into Panama achieves
Panama's ALOP.2281 The Panel concurs with Costa Rica's assertion, given that the record does not
show that there were any changes in Costa Rica's phytosanitary situation that would impact the level
of risk posed with respect to the introduction of the pink mealybug into Panama through imports of
Costa Rican pineapples.
7.1134. As regards Panama's phytosanitary situation with respect to the pest, the record contains
no evidence of any change since the inactivation of the resolution. The pest remains absent in
Panamanian territory, meaning that it has not been introduced into Panama2282, despite more than
10 years2283 of Costa Rican pineapple imports regulated by the PIR of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-
116-2008. The Panel recalls that, for four and a half years of this period of more than 10 years2284,
pineapples were imported from Costa Rica when Panama already knew that the pink mealybug was
present in Costa Rica, with limited distribution, under official control, and without its presence having
been detected in pineapple production areas.
7.1135. As regards Costa Rica's phytosanitary situation with respect to the pest, the record also
does not contain any evidence of changes since Costa Rica notified Panama and the IPPC Secretariat
of the presence of the pest in its territory in May 2014.2285 The pest continues to be present in its
territory, with limited distribution and under official control.2286 The record contains no evidence that
the pest has been introduced to pineapple production areas since May 2014, so such areas are
considered free from the pest. As Costa Rica notes, and Panama confirms, the pest has not been
detected in shipments of Costa Rican pineapples to Panama.2287
7.1136. According to Panama, the PIR of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 preceded the
scientific evidence that confirmed that pineapple is a host of the pink mealybug, and the first
detection of the pest in Costa Rica.2288 Panama asserts that these developments called into question
its previous conclusion that the phytosanitary requirements of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008
were sufficient to meet Panama's ALOP with respect to imports of Costa Rican pineapples2289, and
that this led it to adopt Measure 3.2290 This seems to indicate that Panama perceived an increased
risk as a result of these developments. However, the Panel observes that Panama did not assess the
risk despite the fact that, as noted in reference to Article 5.7, it was not in a situation of insufficient
relevant scientific evidence.
7.1137. In addition, as the Panel mentioned above in relation to Article 5.72291, the two elements
that Panama mentions, namely, the presence of the pink mealybug in Costa Rica and the 2011
publication reporting the presence of the pest in pineapple in Fiji, do not constitute new, unexpected
or unforeseen situations that required the adoption of measures different from those provided for in
Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008. This conclusion is based on the fact that: (a) Panama had
known since 2014 that the pest was present in Costa Rica because Costa Rica notified this fact to

7.1138. In the light of these facts, and in the absence of other elements in the record that indicate
the contrary, the Panel does not consider that a change occurred in the level of risk in January 2019
that suggested that the PIR of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 would not have achieved

7.1139. The Panel observes that, according to Costa Rica, imports of fresh pineapple from the
United States and Colombia are not subject to any requirements, which "confirms still further" that
the first alternative measure achieves Panama's ALOP.2295 The Panel recalls that, with respect to
Articles 5.7 and 2.3, it concluded that Costa Rica has not demonstrated: (a) that Panama imported
fresh pineapple from the United States and Colombia after the adoption of Measure 32296; and (b)
that Panama permits fresh pineapple imports from the United States and Colombia, since there is
no evidence that Panama has adopted the corresponding PIR.2297 Accordingly, the Panel dismisses
the relevance of Costa Rica's arguments related to imports of fresh pineapple from the United States
and Colombia for the purposes of determining whether the first alternative measure would achieve
Panama's ALOP. Nevertheless, this does not undermine the Panel's previous conclusions on the fact
that the application of the PIR of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 would achieve Panama's ALOP,
as no change had occurred in Costa Rica's phytosanitary situation that led to a change in the existing
level of risk with respect to the introduction of the pink mealybug through imports of Costa Rican
pineapples.
7.1140. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Costa Rica has demonstrated that its
first alternative measure would achieve Panama's ALOP with respect to the pink mealybug.
Second alternative measure: specific PIR for the pink mealybug
7.1141. Costa Rica submits that, if shipments of avocado from Colombia and almonds from China
achieve Panama's ALOP when they are certified as free from the pink mealybug or as originating in
places free from that pest, shipments of pineapple from Costa Rica certified in the same way should
also achieve Panama's ALOP.2298
7.1142. Panama responds that it cannot assess whether this alternative measure can achieve its
ALOP with respect to Costa Rican pineapples because the scientific evidence is insufficient to perform
a risk assessment.2299
7.1143. The Panel recalls that the relevant ALOP for Measure 3 applies to plants and plant
products2300, so it would be the same in the case of imports of avocado from Colombia and almonds
from China. Regarding the level of risk of imports of these products compared to that of imports of
pineapple from Costa Rica, the Panel recalls that the risk assessment that Panama asserts it performs
in order to prepare its PIR consists of verifying the pests of quarantine concern officially reported by
the NPPO of each country of origin and comparing them to the list of pests of Panama's NPPO, as
well as verifying the pest's ability to follow the pathway.2301 The Panel understands that Panama was
in a position to carry out this type of assessment for imports of avocado from Colombia, almonds
from China and pineapple from Costa Rica.

7.1144. Avocado and pineapple are both hosts of the pest2302, and they come from countries with
the same phytosanitary status with respect to the pink mealybug, namely, the pest is present and
of limited distribution.2303 Therefore, they would pose a similar level of risk with respect to the pink
mealybug.2304 In the case of almonds from China, the Panel notes that China's phytosanitary status
is the same as that of Costa Rica with respect to the pest.2305 However, there is nothing in the record
that shows that almond is a host of the pest.2306 Accordingly, imports of almonds from China and
pineapples from Costa Rica would not pose a similar risk.
7.1145. The Panel considers that if a specific mitigation measure, such as the additional declaration
included in the PIR for avocado from Colombia, meets Panama's ALOP, as Panama itself has
confirmed2307, the inclusion of a similar measure in the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica should
also achieve it, as the two fruits are hosts of the pest from countries where the pest is present with
a limited distribution. In the case of avocado from Colombia, the record is silent on whether the pest
is present in avocado production areas in Colombia. In any case, irrespective of whether the pest is
present in those areas, the fact that the pest is not present in pineapple production areas in
Costa Rica reinforces the Panel's conclusion that the inclusion of an additional declaration in the PIR
for pineapple from Costa Rica could achieve Panama's ALOP, in the same way that it is achieved in
the case of avocado imports from Colombia. Moreover, if, as the Panel believes, applying the PIR of
Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 achieves Panama's ALOP, applying those same PIR and
including a specific mitigation measure for the pink mealybug should achieve it even more
comfortably.
7.1146. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Costa Rica has demonstrated that its
second alternative measure would achieve Panama's ALOP with respect to the pink mealybug.
7.1147. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the two alternative
measures that it has identified would achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.1148. The Panel will now proceed to analyse the third requirement of footnote 3 to Article 5.6,
which is to determine whether the alternative measures identified by Costa Rica are significantly
less restrictive to trade than Measure 3.
Whether the alternative measures are significantly less restrictive to trade
7.1149. Costa Rica asserts that the first alternative measure is significantly less restrictive to trade
than Measure 3 because compliance with the requirements applied previously, including the
phytosanitary certificate issued by the NPPO of Costa Rica, would allow "considerably greater access
to the market" than Measure 3, which Costa Rica characterizes as "Panama's outright ban".2308
Costa Rica adds that phytosanitary certificates are the standard phytosanitary measure
recommended by the IPPC, as reflected in ISPM 12.2309 Costa Rica puts forward the same arguments
with respect to the second alternative measure.2310 Panama does not comment on this element of
the legal standard.

- 301 -
7.1150. The Panel recalls that Measure 3 consists of the "inactivation" ("inhabilitación") of
Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-116-2008 establishing the PIR for pineapple from Costa Rica2311, which
makes it impossible to import Costa Rican pineapples into Panama. As such, the measure represents
the highest possible degree of restrictiveness.2312 As the Panel indicated above in its analysis with
respect to Article 5.7, ISPM 11 considers prohibiting the importation of a product as a "measure of
last resort", which should be considered in light of the anticipated efficacy, and is applicable in
situations where no satisfactory measure to reduce risk to an acceptable level can be found.2313 This
indicates that Measure 3 would be the most restrictive measure that may be applied to pineapple
imports.
7.1151. Both applying the PIR that were in force until January 2019 and which require, inter alia, a
certificate issued by the NPPO of the importing country, and including an additional declaration in
the phytosanitary certificate on the absence of the pest in the shipment or the area, place or site of
production of the product, are measures that would allow Costa Rican pineapple to be imported.
7.1152. As mentioned above, in order for alternative measures to meet the requirements of
footnote 3 to Article 5.6, they must be "significantly" less restrictive to trade.2314 In the Panel's view,
the two alternative measures proposed by Costa Rica would be significantly less restrictive to trade
than Measure 3, which prevents the importation of pineapple from Costa Rica. This is confirmed by
ISPM 12, which asserts that "[p]hytosanitary certification … facilitates international trade in … plant
products … by providing an internationally agreed document and related procedures".2315
7.1153. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the two alternative
measures are significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 3.
7.5.1.5.2.3 Conclusion on Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
7.1154. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
the alternative measures to Measure 3 that it proposes meet the three cumulative requirements of
footnote 3 to Article 5.6, given that they are reasonably available taking into account technical and
economic feasibility, they achieve Panama's ALOP, and they are significantly less restrictive to trade.
7.1155. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the
SPS Agreement because it is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.5.1.5.2.4 Whether Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement.
7.1156. The Panel concurs with the view expressed in previous disputes that the finding that a
measure is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve the Member's ALOP, in breach of
Article 5.6, leads to a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with the obligation under Article 2.2
to apply the SPS measure only to the extent necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or
health.2316 The Panel considers that this rebuttable presumption can be made in this case because
of its finding that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement. Panama has not
advanced arguments to rebut this presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2.
7.1157. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the
SPS Agreement, and in the absence of a rebuttal by Panama of the presumption of inconsistency
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, the Panel finds that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 2.2
because it does not apply only to the extent necessary to protect plant life or health.

7.5.1.6 Claims under Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement
7.5.1.6.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.5.1.6.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1159. Costa Rica claims that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement
because it is not based on ISPM 2 and ISPM 20.2317 Costa Rica considers that these standards are
relevant because Measure 3 is a phytosanitary measure, and in accordance with Annex A(3)(c) to
the SPS Agreement, international standards, guidelines or recommendations for plant health are
those developed under the auspices of the Secretariat of the IPPC.2318
7.1160. Costa Rica asserts that ISPM 2 provides that, in the event of a national review of
phytosanitary regulations, requirements or operations, "[f]or existing trade, no new measures
should be applied until the revision or new PRA has been completed, unless this is warranted by new
or unexpected phytosanitary situations that may necessitate emergency measures".2319 Costa Rica
adds that, according to ISPM 20, "[e]mergency action may be required in a new or unexpected
phytosanitary situation, such as the detection of quarantine pests"; and that the prohibition of import
"should be used when no alternatives for pest risk management exist", to be resorted to only "[i]n
the case of repeated non-compliance, or where a significant non-compliance or interception
warranting emergency action occurs".2320
7.1161. Costa Rica claims that Panama did not base Measure 3 on these standards, since it adopted
a "prohibition on the import of fresh pineapple from Costa Rica".2321 According to Costa Rica,
Measure 3 is not the measure envisaged by ISPM 2 and ISPM 20 for situations such as that of
Panama in January 2019 because: (a) trade in pineapple already existed between Costa Rica and
Panama; (b) no "new", "unexpected" or "unforeseen" situation had arisen in relation to the trade in
fresh pineapple with Costa Rica; and (c) Panama had not reported any "detection", "repeated
non-compliance" or "significant non-compliance" in shipments of this product from Costa Rica.2322
Costa Rica submits that the adoption of an import prohibition in cases where the international
standard provides for the continuation of trade is equivalent to contradicting the international
standard, so logically the measure is not based on that international standard.2323

7.1162. Panama submits that Costa Rica cannot establish a violation of Article 3.1 of the

especially the case when scientific information that a Member needs to perform a risk assessment

7.5.1.6.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.1163. The Panel recalls that Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement provides that "Members shall base
their sanitary or phytosanitary measures on international standards, guidelines or recommendations,
where they exist, except as otherwise provided for in this Agreement, and in particular in
paragraph 3".
7.1164. The Panel has found that Measure 3 is not covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
and, as part of its analysis under that provision, the Panel concluded that Panama did not adopt
Measure 3 on the basis of ISPM 2 and ISPM 20.2326 The Panel has also found that Measure 3 is
inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, 2.3, and 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2327 These findings
relate both to the scientific basis for Measure 3 and to the discriminatory nature, necessity and
degree of trade-restrictiveness of the measure.
7.1165. Accordingly, the Panel considers that, in the circumstances of this dispute, the findings
above are sufficient to enable the DSB to make recommendations or rulings that achieve a
satisfactory settlement of the matter and that are sufficiently precise to ensure prompt compliance
therewith.2328 Accordingly, the Panel does not deem it necessary to address Costa Rica's claims
under Article 3.1 with respect to Measure 3, and decides to exercise judicial economy in respect of
those claims.

7.5.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.5.2.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1178. Costa Rica claims that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article I:1 of the GATT 1994.
Costa Rica submits that this measure: (a) belongs to the group of "rules and formalities in connection
with importation" covered by this Article; (b) provides an advantage to other Members (i.e. the
United States and Colombia); (c) affects like products, as it results in discrimination based
exclusively on the origin of the products; and (d) the advantage granted to other Members is not
accorded immediately and unconditionally to the products from Costa Rica.2342
7.1179. Costa Rica also claims that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994
because this measure: (a) falls within the broad residual category of "other measures" that may be
challenged under Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994; and (b) amounts to an import prohibition, since
Panama's decision to inactivate imports of pineapple constitutes "a legal ban on the trade or
importation of a specified commodity".2343

Agreement on Agriculture. According to Costa Rica, the Agreement on Agriculture applies to
Measure 3, as pineapple is an agricultural product classified under Chapter 8 of the
Harmonized System. Costa Rica argues that a violation of Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994 implies a
violation of the obligation not to resort to quantitative import restrictions or similar border measures
on agricultural products, set forth in Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the Agreement on Agriculture.2344
7.5.2.1.2 Panama

7.5.2.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.1182. With respect to the claims under the GATT 1994, the Panel observes that previous panels
have recognized that the relationship between the SPS Agreement and the GATT 1994 could lead to
circular reasoning that does not contribute to securing a positive solution to the dispute. These
panels have considered that any findings of inconsistency with GATT 1994 provisions would also
require an examination of whether the measure was justified under Article XX(b) of the GATT 1994,
which refers to measures necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health.2346 The Panel
agrees with this reasoning and recalls that the SPS Agreement elaborates on Article XX(b) of the
GATT 1994.2347
