7.6.1 Claims under the SPS Agreement
7.6.1.1 Whether Measure 4 is a sanitary or phytosanitary measure that falls within the
scope of the SPS Agreement
7.6.1.1.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.6.1.1.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1187. Costa Rica submits that Measure 4 falls within the scope of the SPS Agreement because it
is an SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1)(a) to the Agreement and it affects international
trade within the meaning of Article 1.1.2353
7.1188. Regarding the objective of Measure 4, Costa Rica asserts that an objective reading of the
notes sent by Panama on 10 and 25 October 2019 reveals that the PIR for plantain and banana from
Costa Rica were being inactivated due to a "technical review process" of the relevant resolutions
(including, in the case of plantains, the approval of packing plants) that would culminate in the
approval of new requirements.2354 Costa Rica submits, therefore, that the objective of Measure 4 is
to protect plant life and health in Panama from potential phytosanitary risks.2355
7.1189. Costa Rica claims that, following the adoption of Measure 4, Panama gave two new reasons
to justify the measure.2356 The first reason was the national state of alert prompted by the fungus
Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense Tropical Race 4 (Foc TR4). Panama notified this reason to
Costa Rica two months after communicating the measure.2357 The second reason, which only
concerns bananas, was the detection of residues of the pesticide chlorpyrifos in banana samples in
levels higher than the MRL established in Panamanian law. Panama notified this reason to Costa Rica
one year and four months after communicating the measure.2358 Costa Rica emphasizes that these
reasons pre-dated the adoption of Measure 4, so Panama could have mentioned them in its notes of
October 2019 informing Costa Rica of the measure, and yet it did not.2359
7.1190. Costa Rica asserts that knowing the reason for an SPS measure at the time of its adoption
is of critical importance, especially when there has been no risk assessment (which, if performed,
would have enabled the measure's objective to be identified).2360 Costa Rica submits that a Member
must not be allowed to adopt an SPS measure on phytosanitary grounds and then present additional
reasons over time. For Costa Rica, this way of acting undermines the rights and expectations of
exporting countries in a manner inconsistent with the object and purpose of the SPS Agreement.2361

7.1191. Costa Rica requests the Panel to take into account, in its examination of Measure 4, how
the measure was originally notified to Costa Rica, and not the reasons given by Panama
a posteriori.2362 For Costa Rica, the explanation of the reasons given subsequently constitutes an
ex post facto justification or rationalization of the measure.2363 Costa Rica considers that such
rationalizations should be rejected, in line with the practice of previous panels.2364 Costa Rica submits
that it does not see, and Panama did not explain, the relationship between the various reasons,
namely, the Foc TR4 pest, the approval of packing plants, and an alleged failure to comply with the
MRL for the pesticide chlorpyrifos.2365
7.1192. In relation to what Panama refers to as the "context" of Measure 4, Costa Rica asserts that,
although the objective of a measure may be understood from the circumstances relating to its
application, these circumstances should be objective and unambiguous and not leave any doubt as
to the objective of the measure. For Costa Rica, this is not the case in this dispute.2366 According to
Costa Rica, Panama cannot reasonably argue that Costa Rica should have understood from the
"context" that the market closure was due to Foc TR4, given that:
a. this pest is absent in Costa Rica2367;
b. the pest "does not prevent and has not prevented" the trade in plantain and banana fruit,
because the fruit are not the main transmission pathway of Foc TR42368;
c. Panama's measures and actions related to Foc TR4, set out in its Resolutions OAL-001-
ADM-2015, OAL-102-ADM-2019, and OAL-106-ADM-2019, did not indicate that Panama
would stop trade in plantains and bananas. Costa Rica adds that in 2015, pursuant to
Resolution OAL-001-ADM-2015, Panama had already declared Foc TR4 a quarantine pest
in Panama, and the provisions of both that Resolution and Resolution OAL-102-ADM-2019

7.6.1.1.2.2 Whether Measure 4 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1) to the SPS Agreement
7.1203. As mentioned in the descriptive part, Measure 4 consists of the inactivation of the
resolutions containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and bananas.2389 Taking into account the
legal standard described above, the task of this Panel is to determine whether Measure 4 has been
applied to achieve at least one of the objectives listed in paragraphs (a)-(d) of Annex A(1) to the
SPS Agreement.2390
7.1204. The Panel recalls that Panama has not contested Costa Rica's assertions that Measure 4
constitutes an SPS measure adopted to protect plant life or health in Panama within the meaning of
Annex A(1)(a) of the SPS Agreement. However, the parties disagree over whether this measure was
adopted to protect plant life or health due to: (a) a technical review process of the PIR for plantain
and banana from Costa Rica, and the need for plantain packing plants to be approved, as argued by
Costa Rica; or (b) the risk of introduction2391 of Foc TR4 within the territory of Panama, as argued
by Panama.
7.1205. The Panel observes that Costa Rica determines the objective of Measure 4 on the basis of
the notes through which Panama informed it of the measure.2392 Panama, for its part, requests the
Panel to take into consideration additional factual elements which, in its view, "are inextricably part
of the circumstances surrounding the application of the measure" and which, contrary to Costa Rica's
claim, should not be considered "an ex post rationalization"2393. These elements include the presence
of Foc TR4 in Colombia, OIRSA's phytosanitary alert due to Foc TR4 and Panama's Foc TR4
resolutions.2394 Panama also refers to communications regarding Measure 4 that Costa Rica sent
after the adoption of this measure.2395
7.1206. The Panel recalls that it agrees with the approach taken in previous disputes regarding the
determination of the objective of a measure. The Panel recalls that it has been found that "the
purpose of a measure is to be ascertained on the basis of objective considerations"2396, and that
scrutiny of the circumstances must reveal "a clear and objective relationship between that measure
and the specific purposes enumerated in Annex A(1)".2397 In this respect, it has been noted that this
may be ascertained not only from the objectives of the measure as expressed by the respondent,
but also from the text and structure of the measure, its surrounding regulatory context, and the way
in which it is designed and applied.2398 Panels have considered a wide range of factual elements,
both before and after the adoption of the measure at issue, in order to determine its objective.2399
7.1207. Accordingly, the Panel will begin its assessment by examining the text of the notes through
which Panama notified Measure 4 to Costa Rica in order to determine whether, as Costa Rica claims,
the text of the notes itself indicates the reason why Panama adopted the measure.2400

The notes through which Panama informed Costa Rica of Measure 4
7.1208. As noted above2401, Panama informed Costa Rica of Measure 4 in October 2019 through
Notes AUPSA-AG-392-2019 (concerning plantains) and AUPSA-AG-424-2019 (concerning
bananas).2402
7.1209. Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019 of 10 October 2019 (concerning plantains) reads, in the relevant
part, as follows:
[W]e hereby take the opportunity to inform you that we have initiated a technical review
process of the published resolutions where we have noted that Article 3, paragraph 3.2
of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-106-2009 concerning the importation of plantains
(Musa paradisiaca) from Costa Rica establishes the following:
"Plantains (Musa paradisiaca) must come from areas or places of
production and packing plants subject to inspection and registration by the
[NPPO] of the country of origin, and packing plants approved by [AUPSA]".
In view of the above, and taking into account that the Authority has not carried out the
approval of packing plants, the requirements for the exportation of plantains originating
in Costa Rica to ... Panama have been temporarily inactivated until the NPPO (Plant
Health - MIDA) reviews Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-106-2009 and determines the actions
to be taken.2403
7.1210. For its part, Note AUPSA-AG-424-2019 of 25 October 2019 (concerning bananas) reads, in
the relevant part, as follows:
[W]e hereby take the opportunity to inform you that we have initiated a technical review
process of Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-019-2012 concerning the importation of banana
(Musa spp.) fruit originating in Costa Rica.
In this regard, we inform you that the Authority has temporarily inactivated the
requirements for the importation of bananas from Costa Rica to Panama until the NPPO
of Panama (Plant Health - MIDA) reviews the resolution and determines the actions to
be taken.2404
7.1211. In the Panel's view, contrary to what Panama asserts2405, the text of these two notes refers
explicitly to the reason for the inactivation of the resolutions containing the PIR for Costa Rican
plantains and bananas. Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019 states that what triggered the adoption of this
measure was the fact that the Panamanian authorities undertook a technical review of the resolution
containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and realized that one of the PIR had not been fulfilled,
namely, that packing plants be approved by AUPSA.2406 In the Panel's view, the expressions "in view
of the above" and "taking into account" relate the adoption of Measure 4 to the review of the relevant
resolution and the lack of approval of the plantain packing plants. With respect to Note AUPSA-AG-
424-2019, the text of the Note itself also expressly indicates that the inactivation of the relevant
resolution is due to the initiation of a process for the technical review of that resolution. The adverbial
locution "in this regard" in this Note serves to relate the adoption of Measure 4 to the review of the
relevant resolution.
2401 See section 2.1.5 above.

7.1212. The notes through which Panama informed Costa Rica of Measure 4 merely notify the above
and do not mention any specific pest, such as Foc TR4, nor do they refer to any event that may be
related to that pest or the management of the concomitant risk. As noted, the only documents
referred to in the notes of October 2019 are Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-106-2009 concerning the
importation of plantains and Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-019-2012 concerning the importation of
bananas. These Resolutions include the requirement that the consignment be free from live insects
of any type, as well as other biological, chemical and physical contaminants, including soil.2407
Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-106-2009 also requires that the phytosanitary certificate include an
additional declaration certifying that the fruit have been subject to a specific fruit fly quarantine
treatment.2408 Neither of these two Resolutions mentions any specific requirements related to Foc
TR4. In the absence of any reference to this pest in the PIR and the notes through which their
inactivation was notified, it is not plausible to interpret Measure 4 as being related to a change in
Panama's risk management of this pest.
7.1213. Therefore, the Panel considers that the notes through which Panama informed Costa Rica
of Measure 4 indicate that the adoption of this measure was prompted by the initiation of the review
of the resolutions containing the relevant PIR and the approval of the plantain packing plants.
Accordingly, the Panel is of the view that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect against the risks
that Panama sought to address through this review and approval.
7.1214. Having examined the notes through which Panama informed Costa Rica of Measure 4 and
having concluded that these notes detail the reason for the adoption of the measure, the Panel will
proceed to assess whether, as Panama claims, some evidence in the record shows that the objective
of Measure 4 is to protect against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4.
Evidence which, according to Panama, demonstrates that the objective of Measure 4 was
to protect against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4
7.1215. The Panel will now assess the factual elements which, according to Panama, demonstrate
that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect against the risk of entry of Foc TR4.2409
7.1216. Panama identifies the following elements:
a. Events prior to the adoption of Measure 4 that Panama qualifies as "context"2410:
i. On 25 June 2019, the ICA declared the phytosanitary quarantine of a farm in
Colombian territory due to the presence of symptoms associated with Foc TR4.2411

7.1217. With regard to the events related to Foc TR4 in Colombia, namely, the declaration of the
phytosanitary quarantine of a farm in Colombia due to the suspected presence of Foc TR4 in
June 2019, and the subsequent confirmation of the presence of this pest in Colombia and the
declaration of a state of phytosanitary emergency in August 2019, the Panel does not consider that
these events are in themselves indicative of the objective of Measure 4, adopted in October 2019.
In the Panel's view, the adoption of Measure 4, which relates to imports of plantains and bananas
from Costa Rica, does not appear to be linked to these events, which concern the phytosanitary
status of Foc TR4 in another country, Colombia.
7.1218. With regard to OIRSA's phytosanitary alert of July 2019, the Panel observes that OIRSA
refers to the suspected presence of Foc TR4 in Colombia, and warns the countries of the region to

number of actions for immediate implementation at points of entry. These actions focus on (a) the
inspection of luggage, cargo, packages, mail, and passengers from countries where the pest is
present; (b) the treatment of means of transport with disinfectant solution and the placement of
disinfectant mats; (c) requesting travellers and transporters not to bring Musaceae into the country;
and (d) international waste management measures.2420 The Panel fails to see how the OIRSA alert
and the actions suggested by that institution to prevent the introduction of Foc TR4 indicate a link

between the adoption of Measure 4 and the risk of entry of Foc TR4 into Panama. In particular, the
Panel recalls that none of OIRSA's recommendations are to suspend the importation of plantain and

inability to import plantain and banana fruit from Costa Rica, where the pest is not present.
7.1219. The statements made by some OIRSA members2421 and other members of the region also
do not support Panama's position that Costa Rica should have been "absolutely clear" that a measure
such as Measure 4, which prevents the importation of bananas and plantains from a territory where
Foc TR4 is not present, was adopted in the "context" of the confirmed presence of Foc TR4 in the
region2422 and regional alerts due to Foc TR4.2423 In this regard, the Panel notes that, at a meeting
of the agricultural authorities of Belize, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Cuba, the
Dominican Republic, Ecuador, Guatemala, Mexico, Nicaragua, Panama, Paraguay, and Peru, held in
Quito on 5 August 2019, it was recognized that the threat of the possible entry of Foc TR4 into
Latin America and the Caribbean was a matter of "high priority". The participating countries at that
meeting issued a declaration which included decisions to prevent the introduction of Foc TR4 to
American crops, including: (a) a "recommendation to ensure Musaceae (banana plants) from outside
Latin American and Caribbean countries are imported only in 'extremely necessary' cases, in glass
containers and with a guarantee of being free from the fungus"; and (b) the decision that studies,
diagnostics, and surveillance and quarantine measures, as well as technical and financial
cooperation, should be strengthened.2424 This declaration does not refer to the need to prevent the
importation of plantain and banana fruit from territories where Foc TR4 is not present.2425
7.1220. With respect to the Resolutions on Foc TR4 issued by Panama, as mentioned above, Panama
refers to Resolution OAL-102-ADM-2019 of 12 September 2019 and Resolution OAL-106-ADM-2019
of 1 October 2019.2426 Costa Rica also considers Resolution OAL-001-ADM-2015 of 6 January 2015
to be relevant, through which Panama had already declared Foc TR4 a quarantine pest in its territory
in 2015.2427
7.1221. The Panel observes that, in these proceedings, Panama first asserted that it banned imports
of plantains and bananas of all origins pursuant to the declaration of the state of alert in Resolution
OAL-102-ADM-2019.2428 However, Panama later asserted that the specific legal basis on which
measures were adopted to mitigate the entry of Foc TR4 into Panama was
Resolution OAL-106-ADM-2019. Panama indicates that this Resolution "establishes a Technical
Support Commission to prevent the entry of Foc TR4 into Panama, to protect and safeguard banana
and plantain production, and to adopt any measure that ensures the national territory remains free
from Foc TR4".2429
7.1222. Costa Rica, for its part, contends that Panama has not submitted any government or official
decisions that prohibit imports of plantain and banana fruit of all origins.2430 For Costa Rica,
Measure 4 is not consistent with Panama's own legislation.2431 Costa Rica asserts that
Resolution OAL-102-ADM-2019 reflects the findings of a risk analysis, and that it follows from that
Resolution that Panama considers plants, reproductive material and plant material as pathways for
the transmission of Foc TR4, and prohibits their entry when they come from areas where the pest

has been reported.2432 Costa Rica notes that none of Panama's three resolutions concerning Foc TR4
mentioned above established that Panama should stop the trade in plantain and banana fruit due to
the threat of entry of Foc TR4, which is what Panama did with respect to Costa Rican plantains and
bananas by inactivating the PIR for these products.2433
7.1223. With respect to Panama's three Resolutions concerning Foc TR4, the Panel observes the
following:
a. Pursuant to Resolution OAL-001-ADM-2015, of 6 January 2015, Panama declared Foc TR4
a quarantine pest in the national territory, and prohibited "the entry into the country of
plants, reproductive material (shoots, rhizomes and meristem cultures) and plant material
(such as leaves, bark and tissue) of Musaceae and Heliconia from areas where the pest
has been reported".2434 The Resolution also stated that the DNSV and the
Executive Directorate of Agricultural Quarantine (DECA) would adopt "all phytosanitary
and quarantine measures necessary to prevent the introduction of the pest into the
national territory", including: (i) requiring certification of the absence of the pest in
shipments containing plant material from areas "not reported to have" Foc TR4;
(ii) inspecting and sampling shipments at points of entry to verify their phytosanitary
status; (iii) destroying or returning the consignment in the event that Foc TR4 is detected;
and (iv) carrying out inspections at origin when deemed appropriate.2435
b. Pursuant to Resolution OAL-102-ADM-2019, of 12 September 2019, Panama declared a
"state of national alert due to the risk of introduction of [Foc TR4], a dangerous quarantine
pest, into the national territory"; and prohibited "the entry into the country of plants,
reproductive material … and plant material … of Musaceae and Heliconia from areas where
the pest has been reported", in line with the provisions of Resolution OAL-001-ADM-2015
of 6 January 2015.2436 In addition, Panama established "an action plan for the prevention
of the introduction of [Foc TR4], [to] be developed by the [DNSV], in coordination with
[DECA], by adopting all necessary phytosanitary and quarantine measures".2437 The Panel
notes that this Resolution makes reference to Panama's concern due to the presence of
Foc TR4 in Colombia.2438
c. Pursuant to Resolution OAL-106-ADM-2019, of 1 October 2019, Panama created the
Technical Support Commission "to prevent the entry of [Foc TR4] into the Republic of
Panama, in order to protect and safeguard banana and plantain plantations". This
Commission was responsible for implementing a number of measures, including
(a) training and disseminating information on Foc TR4 to State entities, producers and the
general population, and at airports; (b) inspecting parcels and mail; (c) establishing risk
profiles for the selective inspection of passengers; (d) including, in passengers' goods
declarations, information on the entry of plantain and banana plant material; (e) treating
means of transport with disinfectant solution; (f) rolling out the use of disinfectant mats;
(g) launching a communication and disclosure programme, to encourage travellers, crews
and transporters not to bring in plantain and banana products and by-products;
(h) checking international waste; (i) monitoring shipments from countries with Foc TR4;
(j) carrying out patrols to detect plantain and banana plant material; and (k) detecting
organic plant material at airport exit points.2439 It was also established that the

Commission could adopt "any other measure that ensures the national territory remains
free from [Foc TR4]".2440
7.1224. On the basis of the foregoing, the Panel observes that Resolutions OAL-001-ADM-2015 and
OAL-102-ADM-2019 explicitly establish a prohibition on the importation of plant material from areas
where Foc TR4 has been reported, the intended use of which would be sowing or planting (in
particular, plants, reproductive material, and plant material). However, these Resolutions include no
requirements relating to plantain and banana fruit. It should also be noted that
Resolution OAL-106-ADM-2019 indicates that the pest is spread through the movement of planting
material, soil, tools, vehicles, and water2441, and does not mention spread by fruit. Accordingly, the
Resolutions in the record do not stipulate inactivating the resolutions containing the PIR for fresh
plantain and banana or otherwise prevent the importation of such fruit as a measure to prevent the
entry of Foc TR4.
7.1225. As mentioned above, Resolution OAL-001-ADM-2015 states that the DNSV and DECA would
also adopt other necessary phytosanitary and quarantine measures to prevent the introduction of
the pest into the national territory, and Resolution OAL-106-ADM-2019 grants the Technical Support
Commission the power to implement any other measure that ensures Panamanian territory remains
free from Foc TR4. However, the Panel finds no evidence in the record that demonstrates that
Panama's adoption of Measure 4 was a result of the DNSV and DECA or the Commission exercising
their powers to adopt and implement measures to prevent the introduction of Foc TR4 into Panama.
In addition, the record does not include the "action plan for the prevention of the introduction of
[Foc TR4], [to] be developed by the [DNSV], in coordination with [DECA]", provided for in
Resolution OAL-102-ADM-2019. Therefore, nothing in the record shows that Measure 4 was adopted
as part of the implementation of that plan.
7.1226. In connection with its argument that it banned imports of plantains and bananas of all
origins, Panama provides the notes through which it notified the suspension of the PIR for plantain
from Ecuador and Guatemala on 10 October 2019, the same day that AUPSA informed the SFE of
the inactivation of the resolution concerning the importation of plantains from Costa Rica.2442 The
Panel observes that these notes contain, in essence, the same language as the notes through which
Panama notified Measure 42443, thus they do not support Panama's argument that the objective of
Measure 4 was to protect against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into Panama.2444

- 318 -
7.1227. In the light of all of the above, the Panel considers that the events and actions that Panama
qualifies as "context", namely, the events related to the presence of Foc TR4 in Colombia, OIRSA's
alert, and Panama's resolutions concerning Foc TR4, together with Costa Rica's phytosanitary status
with respect to Foc TR4, do not make it possible to link the adoption of Measure 4 to the objective
of preventing the entry of this pest into Panamanian territory.
7.1228. As for the communications sent by Panama to Costa Rica following the adoption of
Measure 4, the Panel recalls that, according to Panama, these communications clarified the reason
for the measure. Panama adds that Costa Rica explicitly recognized the reason for the adoption of
the measure and gave a commitment to the Panamanian authorities to carry out the necessary steps
to comply with the approval procedure for plantain and banana packing plants.2445 According to
Panama, Costa Rica recognized "the serious economic consequences that [Foc TR4] may cause to
banana and plantain production", and "sought to assure Panama that 'Costa Rica is making its best
efforts to prevent the introduction of Foc TR4'"2446. For Costa Rica, while Panama mentioned its
concern about Foc TR4 for the first time on 18 December 2019 (two months after the adoption of
the measure), this concern was linked to the need for packing plants to be approved, and the
Costa Rican authorities did not understand that Foc TR4 was the reason for the adoption of
Measure 4. Costa Rica adds that, on 22 February 2021, Panama also mentioned the alleged
non-compliance with the MRL for chlorpyrifos, which left the Costa Rican authorities "totally
confused" about the reason for the measure.2447
7.1229. The Panel observes that Panama's communication to Costa Rica of 18 December 2019, sent
two months after the adoption of Measure 4 and after Panama had received two communications
from Costa Rica, including a note from the Minister of Foreign Trade, is the first in which Panama
mentions Foc TR4.2448 In this communication, Panama's Minister of Trade and Industry indicated
that he had been informed by the relevant Panamanian authorities that Panama had declared
"a national state of alert due to the risk of introduction of [Foc TR4]" on 12 September 2019. The
Minister added that, "[i]n the light of this emergency situation, it became evident from the relevant
controls that the phytosanitary requirements for bananas and plantains must be separated, resulting
in their inactivation during the review and updating process".2449 The Minister also mentioned that
he hoped the new requirements would be in force in January 2020 and that a risk analysis would
need to be carried out, including the assessment of "processing plants and establishments, which
had never been performed before in Costa Rican establishments".2450
7.1230. In its subsequent communications dated 22 February, 1 March, and 13 March 2021,
Panama referred to OIRSA's phytosanitary alert due to Foc TR4, the presence of Foc TR4 in Colombia
and the alert issued by the ICA, Panama's declaration of a phytosanitary alert due to Foc TR4,
Panama's prevention action plan, and the creation of its Technical Support Commission to prevent
the entry of Foc TR4.2451 After summarizing these events, in its communication of 22 February 2021,
Panama asserted that it had instituted the "review process of 'phytosanitary and safety policies'" for
the development and implementation of the action plan to prevent the entry of Foc TR4.2452 Panama
also mentioned its Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019 of 10 October 2019, indicating that this Note stated
that the packing plants for the export of plantains from Costa Rica "do not have inspection reports

7.1231. For the Panel, mere references in these communications to events and actions that Panama
qualifies as "context", even if they preceded the adoption of Measure 4, do not explain how
Measure 4 is linked to the objective of protecting against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4. The
Panel considers that the content of these subsequent submissions is consistent with the objective
stated in the notes through which Panama notified Measure 4. In particular, the Panel observes that
Panama again referred to the need to assess Costa Rican packing plants and review the PIR for
plantain and banana. Panama did not ask Costa Rica at any point for information on Foc TR4; Costa
Rica provided such information in August 2021 on its own initiative, after Panama had mentioned
Foc TR4 in its communications of December 2019 and of 2021.2454
7.1232. Similarly, the Panel notes that Panama has not provided additional evidence regarding the
application of Measure 4 that links it to the objective of protecting against the risk of introduction of
Foc TR4.
7.1233. For the Panel, the text of Panama's subsequent communications, together with the time
that elapsed between the notification of Measure 4 (in October 2019) and those communications (in
December 2019 and in 2021), do not make it possible to conclude that the objective of this measure
is to protect against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into Panama.
7.1234. In conclusion, the Panel considers that neither the events which, in Panama's view,
constitute the "context" of Measure 4, nor Panama's communications after the adoption of
Measure 4, demonstrate that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect against the risk of introduction
of Foc TR4 into Panamanian territory. This finding is without prejudice to Panama's intention to
protect plantains and bananas in its territory from the introduction of this pest.
Whether the objective of Measure 4 corresponds to one of those listed in Annex A(1)
7.1235. The Panel has found that, as communicated to Costa Rica in the notes of October 2019,
the reason why Panama adopted Measure 4 was the initiation of the review of the resolutions
containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and bananas and the approval of plantain packing plants.
In this regard, the Panel considers that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect against the risks that
Panama sought to address through this review and approval.
7.1236. The Panel will now examine whether the objective that it determined is pursued by
Measure 4 corresponds to any of those listed in Annex A(1) of the SPS Agreement. In this regard,
the Panel observes that the notes through which Panama notified Measure 4 in October 2019 do not
specifically mention what it seeks to protect, nor from what risk. However, the Panel observes that
both notes from October 2019 are addressed to the SFE (State Phytosanitary Service of Costa Rica)
and mention that the Panamanian body responsible for the review of the inactivated resolutions is
its national plant protection organization, namely Plant Health - MIDA. This is the same authority
that sent the SFE three communications relating to Measure 4 after its adoption.2455 Furthermore,
the Panel observes that the resolutions in question establish the PIR (phytosanitary import

"it carries out activities to prevent the introduction of this pest, in accordance with the provisions of
international standards and without interrupting trade with those countries whose pest status is also absent".
(Memorandum DSFE-0533-2021 (Exhibit CRI-113), p. 1). (underlining original). In addition, it should be noted
that, in its communication of 22 February 2021, after referring to Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019, Panama clarified

(italics added)). The Panel understands that the technical procedure corresponds to the technical review and it
is indicated that the review has a general objective of protecting plantains and bananas, without this objective
being specifically related to Foc TR4.

WT/DS599/R
- 320 -
requirements) for plantains and bananas from Costa Rica.2456 Moreover, as mentioned above, in its
communication of 22 February 2021, after referring to Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019, Panama clarified
that the "technical procedure" referred to covered "all aspects associated with the level of protection
required to safeguard Musaceae production activities" in Panama.2457 The foregoing frames
Measure 4 in the context of plant health protection, and, specifically, the protection of plantains and
bananas in Panama. The Panel further notes that the resolutions indicate, inter alia, that AUPSA's
main objective is to protect human health and the animal and plant heritage of the country, and that
the country, place and site of production must have been recognized as areas free from pests of
quarantine interest. Therefore, in the Panel's view, the measure is related to protection against pests
that may affect plantains and bananas.
7.1237. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel considers that Measure 4 was applied with the
objective of protecting plantains and bananas within the territory of Panama from risks arising from
the entry, establishment or spread of pests. Therefore, the Panel finds that the objective of
Measure 4 is aligned with the objective set forth in Annex A(1)(a).
7.1238. With regard to the type of measure, the Panel considers that Measure 4, which consists of
inactivating the resolution containing the PIR for plantain and banana, makes it impossible to import
these products. Therefore, in the Panel's view, Measure 4 could qualify as a requirement for the
purposes of Annex A(1).2458 In any event, the Panel recalls that, although it is not specifically
included in the list under Annex A(1), a measure may be considered an SPS measure if it is applied
to achieve one of the objectives listed in that provision.2459 Measure 4 meets this requirement and,
therefore, may be considered an SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1) to the
SPS Agreement.
7.6.1.1.2.3 Whether Measure 4 may, directly or indirectly, affect international trade
7.1239. The Panel considers that, owing to the type of measure, and as explained above2460,
Measure 4 directly affects international trade by making it impossible to import Costa Rican plantains
and bananas into Panama as a result of the inactivation of the resolutions containing the PIR for
these products.
7.6.1.1.3 Conclusion on the applicability of the SPS Agreement to Measure 4
7.1240. Having determined that Measure 4 constitutes an SPS measure within the meaning of
Annex A(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement and that it directly affects international trade within the
meaning of Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement, the Panel finds that the SPS Agreement applies to this
measure.
7.1241. Accordingly, the Panel will now examine Costa Rica's claims under the SPS Agreement with
respect to Measure 4, starting with those related to the measure's scientific basis under Articles 5.1,
5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.7 of the Agreement.
7.6.1.2 Claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.1242. Costa Rica claims that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the
SPS Agreement because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of
the risks to plant life or health. Costa Rica claims that, accordingly, Measure 4 is also inconsistent
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, given that it is not based on scientific principles and is
maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.2461 Costa Rica also contends that Measure 4 is not

covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement, since it does not comply with the requirements of this

7.1243. For its part, Panama asserts that Costa Rica has failed to establish a prima facie case that
Panama does not meet the four requirements under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. Panama
requests the Panel to conclude that Measure 4 is a provisional measure covered by Article 5.7 and
that, consequently, Panama has not acted inconsistently with Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the
SPS Agreement.2463
7.1244. As with Measures 1 and 3, the Panel will first examine whether Panama has demonstrated
that Measure 4 constitutes a provisional measure under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement. The Panel
recalls that, in the event that Measure 4 were covered by the exemption under Article 5.7, the
obligations under Articles 2.2, 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 would not be applicable to the measure.2464 If need
be, the Panel will then examine the claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 2.2.
7.6.1.2.1 Whether Measure 4 is an SPS measure covered by Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement
7.6.1.2.1.1 Main arguments of the parties
Costa Rica
7.1245. Costa Rica submits that Panama has failed to demonstrate that each of the four cumulative
requirements of Article 5.7 has been met pursuant to the burden of proof that it bears.2465
7.1246. With regard to the first requirement of Article 5.7, Costa Rica submits that Panama has
failed to demonstrate that, when Panama adopted Measure 4 in October 2019, it was in a situation
where there was insufficient relevant scientific evidence to enable it to conduct a sufficiently
objective assessment of the risk to the life or health of plantains and bananas.2466
7.1247. Costa Rica submits that "the technical review process" related to Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-
106-2009 concerning plantains (including "approvals for [plantain] packing plants") and Resolution
AUPSA-DINAN-019-2012 concerning bananas is the matter in respect of which it should be
determined whether there was, in October 2019, "some evidentiary basis indicating the possible
existence of a risk, but not enough to permit the performance of a risk assessment".2467 In this
regard, Costa Rica maintains that, in October 2019, there was no evidentiary basis indicating the
possible existence of a risk associated with this technical review.2468 Costa Rica asserts that imports
of a product cannot be prohibited only because a technical review process for the PIR has been
initiated.2469 Costa Rica considers that Panama was not in a situation "where little, or no, reliable
evidence was available on the subject matter at issue".2470 According to Costa Rica, Panama had
data on historical trade in plantains and bananas with Costa Rica and knew that Panama (like other
countries) had imported these fruit from Costa Rica without any phytosanitary risk having arisen.2471
Costa Rica adds that, while plantain packing plants should have been approved, the lack of approval

Member has refused to provide it.2542
7.6.1.2.1.2 Assessment by the Panel
Relevant legal standard
7.1267. As noted above, for an SPS measure to be considered covered by Article 5.7, the Member
adopting the measure must meet four cumulative requirements: adopt the measure (a) in cases
where "relevant scientific evidence is insufficient"; and (b) "on the basis of available pertinent
information"; (c) "seek to obtain the additional information necessary for a more objective
assessment of risk"; and (d) "review the … measure accordingly within a reasonable period of
time".2543
7.1268. As with Measures 1 and 3, the Panel will first examine whether Panama adopted the
measure in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence.
Whether Panama adopted Measure 4 in a situation where relevant scientific evidence was
insufficient
7.1269. The Panel notes that Costa Rica presents two lines of argument, contending that Panama
changed its narrative with respect to the objective of Measure 4. Costa Rica's main line of argument
revolves around the technical review of the PIR for plantain and banana as the reason for Measure 4.
However, Costa Rica has also developed another line of argument in the event that the Panel
considers that the adoption of Measure 4 is linked to the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into
Panamanian territory, as claimed by Panama.
7.1270. With regard to there being a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence, Costa Rica
maintains that the technical review of the PIR is the matter in respect of which it should be
determined whether there was, in October 2019, "some evidentiary basis indicating the possible
existence of a risk, but not enough to permit the performance of a risk assessment". In this regard,
Costa Rica submits that there was no evidentiary basis indicating the possible existence of a risk
associated with the technical review of the PIR.2544 Costa Rica considers that imports of a product
cannot be prohibited only because a technical review process for the PIR has been initiated.2545
7.1271. Should the Panel consider that the reason for Measure 4 is Foc TR4, Costa Rica claims that
Panama had sufficient scientific evidence to perform a risk assessment when it adopted the
measure2546, since Foc is one of the most studied pests in the world2547, and OIRSA had prepared a
PRA for Foc TR4.2548
7.1272. For its part, Panama focuses on the alleged insufficiency of scientific evidence concerning
the potential risk of Foc TR4 infecting plantain and banana fruit that, in its view, triggered the review
of the PIR.2549 Panama also indicates that it needed information verified by the Panamanian
authorities on the phytosanitary conditions in plantain and banana establishments in Costa Rica.2550

7.1273. The Panel recalls that Article 5.7 covers situations where deficiencies in the body of
scientific evidence do not allow, in quantitative or qualitative terms, a Member to perform an
adequate assessment of risks.2551 Moreover, the provision refers to the goal of performing "a more
objective assessment of risk". Therefore, the risk that the Member is seeking to address has an
impact on the assessment that the Member must undertake and the information necessary for that
purpose.
7.1274. In this respect, the Panel recalls that the risk that the Member imposing the measure is
seeking to address also forms part of the determination of the objective of the measure. The Panel
also recalls its finding in paragraph 7.1213 above that, as communicated to Costa Rica in the notes
of October 2019, the reason why Panama adopted Measure 4 was the initiation of the review of the
resolutions containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and bananas and the approval of plantain
packing plants. In this regard, the Panel considered that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect
against the risks that Panama sought to address through this review and approval. In the Panel's
view, the evidence in the record does not demonstrate that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect
against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into Panamanian territory. In view of these findings on
the objective of Measure 4, the Panel will focus its analysis under Article 5.7 on the objective of
phytosanitary protection against the risks that Panama was seeking to address through the review
of the relevant resolutions and the approval of plantain packing plants. Accordingly, the Panel will
not address the parties' arguments regarding the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into Panamanian
territory.
7.1275. The Panel notes that Panama does not identify any deficiencies in the scientific evidence
regarding the technical review process for the PIR. As explained below, in its argumentation, Panama
links the absence of information verified by its authorities on packing plants in Costa Rica with the
risk of introduction of Foc TR4. Thus, Panama's entire argumentation under Article 5.7 revolves
around Foc TR4, and the exclusion of arguments related to Foc TR4 covers all of Panama's arguments
under Article 5.7. In this regard, the Panel recalls that, in Note AUPSA-AG-392-2019 informing Costa
Rica of Measure 4 concerning plantains, Panama mentions that, when the technical review process
for the PIR was initiated, it was noted that AUPSA had not carried out the approvals of packing plants
provided for in the inactivated resolution.2552 Subsequently, Panama also referred to the need to
approve banana packing plants.2553 However, in the course of these proceedings, Panama asserted
that the lack of approval of the plants by its authorities was worrying in the light of the national
state of alert for Foc TR4, its corresponding high ALOP, and the fact that it shares a border with
Costa Rica.2554 Panama added that its "highly restrictive ALOP" for Foc TR4 did not allow it to proceed
on the basis of assumptions that cleaning and packing processes in exporting countries were
sufficient.2555
7.1276. Panama has not presented arguments to rebut Costa Rica's claim that there was no
situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence with respect to the technical review of the PIR,
should the Panel not accept that the reason for Measure 4 was Foc TR4.
7.1277. In this context, in the absence of arguments by Panama that rebut Costa Rica's claim that
there was no situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence with respect to the technical review
of the PIR, the Panel will assess whether Costa Rica discharged its burden of proof under Article 5.7.
As noted above, the Panel considers that Costa Rica, in raising the inapplicability of Article 5.7 in its
panel request, must discharge its initial burden of demonstrating that Measure 4 is not covered by

Article 5.7. Costa Rica must therefore demonstrate that Measure 4 fails to meet at least one of the
cumulative requirements set out in Article 5.7.2556
7.1278. The Panel will now proceed to address Costa Rica's arguments as to whether Panama had
sufficient relevant scientific evidence related to the technical review of the resolutions containing the
PIR for plantain and banana.
7.1279. Costa Rica submits that, in October 2019, Panama was not in a situation of insufficient
relevant scientific evidence, given that it had data on historical trade in plantains and bananas with
Costa Rica. According to Costa Rica, Panama knew that, like other countries, it had imported these
fruit from Costa Rica without any phytosanitary risk having arisen.2557 Concerning the information
on historical trade in plantains and bananas with Costa Rica, Costa Rica asserts that Panama knew
that all exports from Costa Rica were accompanied by an official phytosanitary export certificate
confirming that shipments had been inspected and/or tested to ensure they were free from the
quarantine pests specified by Panama.2558
7.1280. Costa Rica provides trade data from Panama's INEC showing that there were imports of
fresh plantains from Costa Rica into Panama in 2014 and 20192559, and of fresh bananas in 2000
and the period 2017-2019.2560 The Panel agrees with Costa Rica that prior imports of plantains and
bananas, including in the years immediately preceding the adoption of Measure 4, provide pertinent
information on the risks that such imports pose. In particular, in accordance with the applicable
PIR2561, Costa Rica submitted phytosanitary certificates that included information on the exporter
and the place of origin, among other details, and certified that the products "have been inspected …
and are considered to be free from the quarantine pests specified by [Panama] and to conform with
the current phytosanitary requirements of [Panama], … and they are deemed to be practically free
from other pests".2562
7.1281. Costa Rica considers that it should be determined whether, in respect of the technical
review of the PIR, there was, in October 2019, "some evidentiary basis indicating the possible
existence of a risk, but not enough to permit the performance of a risk assessment".2563 In this
regard, Costa Rica maintains that, in October 2019, there was no evidentiary basis indicating the
possible existence of a risk associated with this review. Costa Rica submits that the review of
phytosanitary policies does not give rise to the emergence of phytosanitary risks, and that imports
of a product cannot be prohibited only because a technical review process for the PIR has been
initiated.2564 The Panel understands Costa Rica's position to be that, in the absence of any new
scientific evidence related to the risk associated with the importation of Costa Rican plantains and
bananas, there is no situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence.
7.1282. The Panel notes that, according to Panama, when AUPSA was developing the PIR for
plantain and banana, it performed a verification of the pests of quarantine concern notified by the

NPPOs of countries of origin, which "was Panama's risk analysis, but it was not documented as
such".2565 Therefore, prior to the imposition of Measure 4, the importation of the products concerned
was authorized in accordance with the PIR established on the basis of a pest verification that Panama
considers to be a risk assessment sufficient to achieve its ALOP.
7.1283. In these circumstances, the Panel understands that, as maintained by Costa Rica, for the
scientific evidence that Panama considered sufficient to become insufficient to perform a risk
assessment, there must be some new scientific evidence that calls into question what was previously
known about the risk concerned. The Panel considers that, in the absence of any change in the body
of relevant scientific evidence available to Panama in this case, it cannot be maintained that the
situation of this relevant scientific evidence has shifted from a state of sufficiency to one of
insufficiency. In any event, the Panel recalls that the evolution of scientific evidence does not
necessarily imply that the relevant scientific evidence that had so far been sufficient becomes
insufficient; rather, it may be an indication that the existing risk assessment should be reviewed or
that a new assessment should be undertaken.2566
7.1284. Costa Rica refers to ISPM 2 in asserting that, although countries may undertake "a national
review of phytosanitary regulations, requirements or operations", "[f]or existing trade, no new
measures should be applied until the revision or new PRA has been completed, unless this is
warranted by new or unexpected phytosanitary situations that may necessitate emergency
measures".2567
7.1285. In the Panel's view, by referring to this guidance from ISPM 2, Costa Rica is proposing that
new measures may be applied: (a) when a new or revised PRA has been completed, or (b) when
there are new or unexpected phytosanitary situations that may necessitate emergency measures.
In the context of the IPPC and the ISPM, emergency measures are applied as a matter of urgency
in new or unexpected phytosanitary situations and may or may not be provisional measures.2568
Accordingly, the ISPM also support the proposition that, if a risk assessment is not conducted, there
must be a change in the situation for a new measure, whether provisional or not, to be adopted.
7.1286. The Panel recalls that the ISPM that may provide context for ISPM 2 link the adoption of
emergency measures or actions to prior detection of a pest.2569 There is no evidence in the record
of any new or unexpected phytosanitary situations having arisen with respect to the importation of
Costa Rican plantains and bananas. Panama provides no argument or evidence regarding the
detection of pests (whether Foc TR4 or another pest) in Costa Rican plantain and banana shipments
in relation to the adoption of Measure 4. Even taking into account that detections at origin may
constitute a new or unexpected phytosanitary situation2570, Panama does not assert that Costa Rica's
phytosanitary status in respect of a pest of quarantine concern that affects plantains or bananas
(whether Foc TR4 or another pest) changed in such a way as to result in the adoption of Measure 4.
In addition, bearing in mind that new or unexpected situations may not be limited to a detection2571,
the Panel notes that Panama does not put forward any argument or evidence regarding the existence
of new or unexpected situations associated with the importation of Costa Rican plantains and
bananas.2572
7.1287. The only detection in shipments of the products concerned that Panama mentions is of the
pesticide chlorpyrifos at levels above the relevant MRL, in a shipment of Costa Rican bananas on

26 September 2019.2573 According to Panama, this detection "raised new concerns about
phytosanitary conditions for banana production in Costa Rica".2574 Nevertheless, Panama confirmed
that the detection of chlorpyrifos in Costa Rican bananas during the national alert for Foc TR4 did
not prompt the adoption of Measure 4, and that, therefore, in its analysis under Article 5.7, the Panel
should not consider whether the scientific evidence was insufficient with respect to the risks
associated with chlorpyrifos in Costa Rican bananas.2575 The Panel also notes that a violation of the
relevant MRL constitutes a breach of safety requirements and does not concern the sufficiency of
relevant scientific evidence for an assessment of the risk of entry, establishment or spread of pests
or diseases and of the associated potential biological and economic consequences.2576
7.1288. For the foregoing reasons, the Panel considers that there is no evidence in the record that
a new or unexpected phytosanitary situation occurred with respect to the importation of plantains
and bananas originating in Costa Rica in the context of the process of review of the relevant
resolutions.
7.1289. In addition to arguing that, in the absence of new or unexpected phytosanitary situations,
there was no risk related to the importation of Costa Rican plantains and bananas, Costa Rica claims
that the lack of approval of plantain packing plants also did not indicate the possible existence of a
risk in respect of plantain imports. Costa Rica adds that the absence of this approval had never
constituted a reason for Panama to prevent the entry of Costa Rican plantains into its market.2577
7.1290. In this regard, the Panel observes that, as is apparent from Panama's communication to
Costa Rica of 10 October 2019, Panama became aware of a failure to comply with a requirement of
the regulations in force prior to the adoption of Measure 4.2578 According to the requirement in
question, it must be stated, on the phytosanitary certificate accompanying imports of Costa Rican
plantains, that the plantains come from areas and places of production and packing plants subject
to inspection and registration by the NPPO of the country of origin and approval of packing plants
by AUPSA.2579 As mentioned above, the importation of the products concerned into Panama was
authorized in accordance with the PIR established on the basis of a pest verification that Panama
considers to be a risk assessment sufficient to achieve its ALOP.2580 Furthermore, as Costa Rica
observes, the lack of approval of packing plants had not previously prevented the importation of
Costa Rican plantains and bananas in accordance with the applicable PIR, and, in October 2019, no
new or unexpected phytosanitary situation had arisen in relation to any pest in plantains and
bananas from Costa Rica. Therefore, in the Panel's view, the fact that Panama noticed a failure by
its own authorities to comply with this requirement does not in itself demonstrate that the relevant
scientific evidence was insufficient to conduct an assessment of the risks to plant life or health within
the meaning of Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement.
Conclusion on whether Panama adopted Measure 4 in a situation in which the relevant
scientific evidence was insufficient
7.1291. In view of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated, and
Panama has failed to rebut, that, when Measure 4 was adopted in October 2019, there was no
situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence concerning the risks that Panama was seeking to
address through the review of the resolutions containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and
bananas and the approval of plantain packing plants.

7.1292. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Costa Rica discharged its burden of demonstrating that
Measure 4 does not meet at least one of the cumulative requirements set out in Article 5.7 and has
therefore demonstrated that Measure 4 does not constitute a provisional measure under Article 5.7
of the SPS Agreement.
7.6.1.2.1.3 Conclusion on Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement
7.1293. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 4 does not constitute a provisional
measure under Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement, because Costa Rica has demonstrated, and
Panama has failed to rebut, that Panama did not adopt Measure 4 in a situation of insufficient
relevant scientific evidence.
7.6.1.3 Claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1294. Given that the Panel has found that Measure 4 is not a provisional measure under Article 5.7
of the SPS Agreement, Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement are applicable to this
measure. As a result, the Panel will proceed to examine Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2,
and 5.3, which concern the assessment of risk and the elements and aspects to be taken into account
in such assessments, and Article 2.2, which requires that an SPS measure be based on scientific
principles and not be maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.
7.6.1.3.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.6.1.3.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1295. Costa Rica claims that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the
SPS Agreement, given that it is not based on a risk assessment, and, consequently, that it is also
inconsistent with Article 2.2, as it is not based on scientific principles and is maintained without
sufficient scientific evidence.2581
7.1296. Costa Rica submits that Panama has not carried out a risk assessment, within the meaning
of Annex A(4) to the SPS Agreement, that provides a scientific basis for Measure 4, thus Panama
has acted in a manner inconsistent with the obligation set out in Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement
to base its measure on an assessment of the risks, as appropriate to the circumstances.2582
7.1297. Costa Rica argues that, in the communications through which Panama informed it of the
adoption of Measure 4, Panama made no reference to any risk assessment that would sufficiently
warrant or reasonably support this measure.2583 According to Costa Rica, the content of these
communications does not constitute, in itself, a "risk assessment" within the meaning of Annex A(4)
to the SPS Agreement2584, since merely referring to the initiation of a process for the technical review
of the resolutions does not qualify as a "scientific process" characterized by "systematic, disciplined
and objective enquiry and analysis".2585 Costa Rica adds that there is absolutely no reasoning based
on scientific grounds, nor any analysis of probabilities with respect to the entry, establishment or
spread of pests through the importation plantains or bananas from Costa Rica.2586
7.1298. Costa Rica adds that Panama has expressly recognized that Measure 4 is not based on a
risk assessment.2587 According to Costa Rica, Panama closed its market without having any scientific
or technical basis that would provide grounds for such a closure for the duration of an alleged

7.1300. Panama asserts that it adopted provisional measures, including Measure 4, as a precaution,

7.6.1.3.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.6.1.3.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.1301. As mentioned in the context of Measure 12592, Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement requires
the Panel to consider two aspects:
a. whether there exists a risk assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, taking into
account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant international organizations;
and
b. if there is a risk assessment, whether the SPS measure of the Member concerned is based
on that risk assessment.
7.1302. The Panel recalls that Articles 5.2 and 5.3 shed light on the elements and factors that are
of relevance to the assessment of risks foreseen in Article 5.1; accordingly, these elements and
factors have been assessed as part of the analysis under that provision.2593 Furthermore, the Panel
recalls that a finding of a violation of Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 may give rise to a refutable
presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2.2594
7.1303. The Panel will next examine Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 with
respect to Measure 4.
7.6.1.3.2.2 Whether Measure 4 is based on a risk assessment in accordance with
Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement
7.1304. Panama has confirmed that Measure 4 is not based on a risk assessment, arguing that it is
a provisional measure adopted in a situation where the body of available scientific evidence did not

7.1305. The Panel considers that, as Costa Rica asserts, a measure cannot be based on a risk
assessment if no such assessment exists. The Panel therefore finds that Measure 4 is inconsistent
with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to

developed by the relevant international organizations.
7.6.1.3.2.3 Whether Panama took into account the elements and factors enumerated in
Articles 5.2 and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement when assessing the risk
7.1306. As mentioned above, Articles 5.2 and 5.3 are linked to Article 5.1, as they shed light on
the elements and factors that are of relevance to the assessment of risks foreseen in Article 5.1.2596
In the absence of a risk assessment, the Panel finds that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Articles 5.2
and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement because Panama did not take into account the elements and factors
set out therein when assessing the risks.
7.6.1.3.2.4 Whether Measure 4 is based on scientific principles and is not maintained
without sufficient scientific evidence in accordance with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1307. As mentioned above, Article 5.1 constitutes a "specific application" of the basic obligations
contained in Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement.2597 The Panel agrees with what has been said in
previous disputes to the effect that it may be presumed that a measure that is not based on a risk
assessment pursuant to Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement is not based on scientific principles or is
maintained without sufficient scientific evidence within the meaning of Article 2.2.2598 The Panel
considers that this presumption arises in the present case, as a result of its finding that, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Measure 4 is inconsistent with Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3.2599
Furthermore, the Panel observes that Panama has not presented any arguments to refute this
presumption.
7.1308. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 4 is not based on a risk assessment, in
breach of Articles 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 of the SPS Agreement, and given that Panama has failed to
refute the presumption of inconsistency with Article 2.2 of the Agreement, the Panel finds that
Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 because it is not based on scientific principles and is
maintained without sufficient scientific evidence.
7.6.1.3.3 Conclusion on Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1309. In the light of the above, the Panel finds that Measure 4 is inconsistent with the following
provisions of the SPS Agreement:
a. Article 5.1, because it is not based on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances,
of the risks to plant life or health, taking into account risk assessment techniques
developed by the relevant international organizations;
b. Article 5.2, because, in the absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account
the elements set out in this provision in the assessment of risks;

7.6.1.4.1.2 Panama
7.1319. Panama maintains that Measure 4 is exempt from the obligations established under

7.6.1.4.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.6.1.4.2.1 Relevant legal standard
7.1322. In order to determine whether Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.6, in accordance
with the legal standard set out above2624, the Panel will analyse whether the alternative measure
proposed by Costa Rica meets the three requirements in footnote 3 to Article 5.6, i.e. that (a) it is
reasonably available taking into account technical and economic feasibility; (b) it achieves Panama's
ALOP; and (c) it is significantly less restrictive to trade than Measure 4.
7.1323. With regard to Costa Rica's consequential claim under Article 2.2, the Panel recalls that,
based on the similarities between Articles 2.2 and 5.6, in previous disputes a finding of inconsistency
with Article 5.6 has been interpreted to result in a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with
Article 2.2.2625
7.1324. The Panel shares the assessments of the legal standard applicable under Articles 5.6 and
2.2 set out in section 7.3.1.3.2.1 on Measure 1 and will proceed with its analysis on that basis.
7.1325. The Panel will now address Costa Rica's claims under Articles 5.6 and 2.2 with respect to
Measure 4. The Panel will consider, first, whether Costa Rica has demonstrated that an alternative
to Measure 4 exists that meets the three cumulative requirements set out in footnote 3 to Article 5.6
of the SPS Agreement. Subsequently, the Panel will consider Costa Rica's consequential claim under
Article 2.2 of the Agreement.

Resolutions required the issue of a phytosanitary certificate, in addition to compliance with other

7.1328. The Panel observes that Panama's arguments that the alternative measure would not
achieve Panama's ALOP2630 centre around the risk of introduction of Foc TR4. Given that the ALOP
is also referred to as the "acceptable level of risk"2631, the risk that a Member is seeking to address
affects any assessment of an alternative measure and, in particular, whether that measure achieves
the Member's ALOP.
7.1329. In this respect, the Panel recalls that the risk that the Member imposing the measure is
seeking to address also forms part of determining the objective of the measure. The Panel also
recalls its finding in paragraph 7.1213 above that, as was communicated to Costa Rica in the notes
of October 2019, the reason why Panama adopted Measure 4 was the initiation of a review of the
resolutions containing the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and bananas, and the approval of plantain
packing plants. Accordingly, the Panel considered that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect against
the risks that Panama sought to address through this review and approval process. In the Panel's
view, the evidence in the record does not demonstrate that the objective of Measure 4 is to protect
against the risk of introduction of Foc TR4 into Panamanian territory. In view of these findings
concerning the objective of Measure 4, the Panel will focus its analysis under Article 5.6 on the
objective of phytosanitary protection against the risks that Panama sought to address by reviewing
the relevant resolutions and approving plantain packing plants. Accordingly, the Panel will not
address the arguments that the parties have made in relation to the risk of introduction of Foc TR4
into Panamanian territory.
7.1330. Although Panama's arguments, in particular those relating to the relevant ALOP, do not
address the scenario in which the Panel does not accept that the reason for Measure 4 was Foc TR4,
the Panel must be satisfied that Costa Rica has made a prima facie case that Measure 4 is
inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2632

and Resolution AUPSA-DINAN-019-2012 for more than seven years.2633
7.1332. The Panel recalls that, in previous disputes, it has been deemed that existing use of the
measure, even if in a different context, weighs in favour of a finding of feasibility.2634 The Panel also
recalls that Panama considers that the Panel should treat this approach by the panel in

Panama's ALOP in order to then compare it to the level of protection that the alternative measure

expressly set out in Law No. 47 of 9 July 1996, and is implemented specifically through the applicable
PIR.2638
7.1339. Costa Rica submits that Panama did not identify its ALOP with sufficient precision2639, and
requests the Panel bear in mind, when assessing or determining Panama's ALOP, that Panama's

entry, establishment and spread of pests of plants and plant products.2643

ALOP is, given that the notes through which Panama informed Costa Rica of that measure do not
mention any specific pest, such as Foc TR4.2644 The Panel recalls that the objective of Measure 4 is
to protect against the risks that Panama sought to address by revising the resolutions containing
the PIR for Costa Rican plantains and bananas and the approval of plantain packing plants. As a
result, in the light of the risk that Panama is seeking to address, Panama's ALOP may be defined as
preventing the entry, establishment and spread of pests of interest to Panama that may affect
plantains and bananas.

7.1345. As mentioned above, the Panel considers that the level of risk posed by imports may be

Furthermore, the Panel observes that Panama's ALOP for plantain and banana imports has not

Panama that affect plantain and banana. In this respect, the Panel also observes that, according to

7.1347. Accordingly, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that the alternative
measure it proposes would achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.1348. The Panel will now analyse the third requirement in footnote 3 to Article 5.6, which consists
of determining whether the alternative measure identified by Costa Rica is significantly less
restrictive to trade than Measure 4.
Whether the alternative measure is significantly less restrictive to trade
7.1349. Costa Rica submits that the alternative measure would enable "considerably wider access
to the market" than Measure 4.2653 Costa Rica adds that phytosanitary certificates are the standard
measure recognized by the IPPC and regulated by ISPM 12.2654 Panama has not responded to these
arguments made by Costa Rica.
7.1350. The Panel recalls that, in order for alternative measures to meet the requirements of
footnote 3 to Article 5.6, the difference in terms of restrictiveness must be significant.2655
7.1351. Measure 4 consists of temporarily inactivating the resolutions containing the PIR for
Costa Rican plantains and bananas.2656 As a result, Measure 4 makes it impossible to import
Costa Rican plantains and bananas into Panama, and implies the highest degree of restriction
possible.2657
7.1352. On the other hand, the alternative measure proposed by Costa Rica, which consists of
requiring a certificate issued by Costa Rica's NPPO, in addition to compliance with the other PIR,
allows imports. In the Panel's view, for exporters there would be a notable difference between the
inability to export, on the one hand, and the ability to export, albeit subject to the PIR established
in Resolutions AUPSA-DINAN-106-2009 and AUPSA-DINAN-019-2012, on the other. As a result, the
Panel considers that the alternative measure is significantly less restrictive to trade than an import

7.6.1.4.2.3 Conclusion on Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement
7.1354. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel concludes that Costa Rica has demonstrated that
the alternative measure to Measure 4 that it proposes meets the three requirements in footnote 3
to Article 5.6, given that it is reasonably available taking into account technical and economic
feasibility, achieves Panama's ALOP, and is significantly less restrictive to trade.
7.1355. Accordingly, the Panel finds that Costa Rica has demonstrated that Measure 4 is
inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement because it is more trade-restrictive than required
to achieve Panama's ALOP.
7.6.1.4.2.4 Whether Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1356. The Panel agrees with what has been said in previous disputes to the effect that a finding
that a measure is more trade-restrictive than required to achieve a Member's ALOP, in breach of
Article 5.6, leads to a rebuttable presumption of inconsistency with the obligation in Article 2.2 to
ensure that an SPS measure is applied only to the extent necessary to protect human, animal or
plant life or health.2660 The Panel considers that this rebuttable presumption arises in the present
case, owing to its finding that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2661
Panama has not presented any arguments to rebut this presumption of inconsistency with
Article 2.2.
7.1357. Accordingly, in the light of its finding that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the
SPS Agreement, and in the absence of any rebuttal by Panama of the presumption of inconsistency
with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement, the Panel finds that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 2.2,
because it is not applied only to the extent necessary to protect plant life or health.
7.6.1.4.2.5 Conclusion on Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement
7.1358. In the light of the foregoing, the Panel finds that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 2.2
because it is not applied only to the extent necessary to protect plant life or health.

7.1360. Costa Rica asserts that ISPM 2 provides that, when carrying out a national review of
phytosanitary regulations, requirements or operations, "[f]or existing trade, no new measures
should be applied until the revision or new PRA has been completed, unless this is warranted by new
or unexpected phytosanitary situations that may necessitate emergency measures".2664 According
to Costa Rica, pursuant to ISPM 20, "[e]mergency action may be required in a new or unexpected
phytosanitary situation, such as the detection of quarantine pests or potential quarantine pests";
and the prohibition of import "should be used when no alternatives for pest risk management exist"
and should be reserved for "case[s] of repeated non-compliance, or where a significant non-
compliance or interception warranting emergency action occurs".2665
7.1361. Costa Rica submits that Panama did not base Measure 4 on these standards, given that it
adopted a "prohibition on the import of plantains and bananas from Costa Rica".2666 Costa Rica
asserts that Measure 4 is not the measure foreseen by ISPM 2 and ISPM 20 in situations when a
country undertakes a review or revision of its phytosanitary requirements. According to Costa Rica,

based on that international standard.2667
7.1362. Furthermore, Costa Rica submits that, even if the Panel were to find that the reason for
Measure 4 is to prevent the introduction of Foc TR4, Measure 4 would still be a phytosanitary
measure that is not based on ISPM 2 and ISPM 20, contrary to Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement.2668
According to Costa Rica, as of October 2019 and with respect to Foc TR4, no "new or unexpected"
situation had arisen with regard to Costa Rica's phytosanitary status, i.e. as a territory from which
the pest is absent, that would warrant an emergency measure in accordance with ISPM 2 and
ISPM 20. In this sense, Costa Rica notes that Panama had not reported any "detection" of the pest,
"repeated non-compliance" or "significant non-compliance" in plantain and banana imports from
Costa Rica.2669 Costa Rica submits that, therefore, Measure 4 would not have "a very strong and
very close relationship" with the IPPC Standards, such that those standards could not form "the basis

7.1363. Panama submits that Costa Rica cannot establish a violation under Article 3.1 of the

assessment was not examined as part of the process that led to the adoption of the international

7.6.1.5.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.1364. The Panel recalls that Article 3.1 provides that "Members shall base their sanitary or
phytosanitary measures on international standards, guidelines or recommendations, where they
exist, except as otherwise provided for in this Agreement, and in particular in paragraph 3".
7.1365. The Panel has found that Measure 4 is not covered by Article 5.7 of the SPS Agreement.2673
In its analysis under that provision, the Panel concluded that nothing in the record indicated that
any new or unexpected phytosanitary situations referred to in the section of ISPM 2 that Costa Rica
also cites under Article 3.1 have arisen.2674 The Panel has also found that Measure 4 is inconsistent
with Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2675 These findings relate to both the
scientific basis as well as the trade-restrictiveness and necessity of Measure 4.
7.1366. Therefore, the Panel considers that, under the circumstances of this dispute, the
abovementioned findings are sufficient to enable the DSB to make recommendations or rulings that
achieve a satisfactory settlement of the matter and that are sufficiently precise to ensure prompt
compliance therewith.2676 Accordingly, the Panel does not deem it necessary to address Costa Rica's
claims under Article 3.1 with respect to Measure 4, and decides to exercise judicial economy in
respect of those claims.

7.6.2 Claims under the GATT 1994 and the Agreement on Agriculture
7.6.2.1 Main arguments of the parties
7.6.2.1.1 Costa Rica
7.1379. Costa Rica claims that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994, as this
measure: (a) falls within the broad residual category of "other measures" that may be challenged
under Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994; and (b) amounts to an import prohibition, since Panama's
decision to inactivate imports of plantains and bananas constitutes "a legal ban on the trade or
importation of a specified commodity".2690
7.1380. Costa Rica also claims that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the
Agreement on Agriculture. According to Costa Rica, the Agreement on Agriculture applies to
Measure 4, as plantains and bananas are agricultural products classified under Chapter 8 of the
Harmonized System. Costa Rica argues that the violation of Article XI:1 of the GATT 1994 implies a
violation of the obligation not to resort to quantitative import restrictions or similar border measures
on agricultural products, set forth in Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the Agreement on Agriculture.2691
7.6.2.1.2 Panama
7.1381. Panama submits that Costa Rica "has not established its claims" under Article XI:1 of the
GATT 1994 and Article 4.2 and footnote 1 of the Agreement on Agriculture.2692
7.6.2.2 Assessment by the Panel
7.1382. With respect to the claims under the GATT 1994, the Panel observes that previous panels
have recognized that the relationship between the SPS Agreement and the GATT 1994 could lead to
circular reasoning that does not contribute to securing a positive solution to the dispute. Those
panels have considered that any findings of inconsistency with GATT 1994 provisions would also
require an examination of whether the measure was justified under Article XX(b) of the GATT 1994,
which refers to measures necessary to protect human, animal or plant life or health.2693 The Panel
agrees with this reasoning and recalls that the SPS Agreement is an extension of Article XX(b) of
the GATT 1994.2694
7.1383. Furthermore, the Panel recalls that it has found that Measure 4 is inconsistent with
Articles 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 2.2, and 5.6 of the SPS Agreement.2695 These findings relate both to the
scientific basis and to the necessity and trade-restrictiveness of Measure 4.
7.1384. The Panel considers that, under the circumstances of this dispute, the findings above are
sufficient to enable the DSB to make recommendations or rulings that achieve a satisfactory
settlement of the matter and that are sufficiently precise to ensure prompt compliance therewith.2696
Accordingly, the Panel does not deem it necessary to address Costa Rica's claims under the
GATT 1994, and decides to exercise judicial economy in respect of those claims.2697
