8 CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATION
8.1. For the reasons set forth in this Report, the Panel concludes as follows:
a. Regarding its terms of reference:
i. Costa Rica's panel request is not inconsistent with the requirement under Article 6.2
of the DSU to provide "a brief summary of the legal basis of the complaint sufficient
to present the problem clearly";
ii. Costa Rica's claims under Annex B(5)(b) and Annex B(6)(a) to the SPS Agreement are
outside the Panel's terms of reference; and
iii. the procedures related to Measures 1, 3, and 4 challenged by Costa Rica are outside
the Panel's terms of reference.
b. Regarding the measure concerning the importation of strawberries (Measure 1):
i. the SPS Agreement is applicable to Measure 1 because the measure constitutes an
SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1)(b) to the SPS Agreement and directly
affects international trade within the meaning of Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement;
ii. Measure 1 does not constitute a provisional measure covered by Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement because Panama has not demonstrated that it adopted the measure
in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence or on the basis of available
pertinent information, in breach of two of the requirements under Article 5.7;
iii. Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement because it is not based
on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of the risks to human health,
taking into account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant international
organizations;
iv. Measure 1 is inconsistent with Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement because, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account the elements listed in
this provision in the assessment of risks;
para. 8.185; Japan – Apples, para. 8.328; EC – Approval and Marketing of Biotech Products, paras. 7.3422 and
6.3429; India – Agricultural Products, para. 7.803; US – Animals, paras. 7.731 and 7.732; and
Costa Rica – Avocados (Mexico), para. 7.2319.
2698 See Articles 3.4, 3.7, 11, and 21.1 of the DSU.
2699 The panel in Japan – Apples, in which measures were challenged under the SPS Agreement and the
Agreement on Agriculture, applied the principle of judicial economy in respect of the claims under the
Agreement on Agriculture, after having found inconsistencies with the provisions of the SPS Agreement. (See
Panel Report, Japan – Apples, para. 8.332). The panel in US – Poultry (China), in which measures were
challenged under the SPS Agreement, the GATT 1994, and the Agreement on Agriculture, applied the principle
of judicial economy in respect of the claims under the Agreement on Agriculture, after having found
inconsistencies with the provisions of the SPS Agreement and the GATT 1994. In particular, the panel noted
that it had resolved the aspects in the dispute related to the "restrictions". (See Panel Report, US – Poultry
(China), paras. 7.69 and 7.485-7.487).

in a situation of insufficient relevant scientific evidence or on the basis of available
pertinent information, in breach of two of the requirements under Article 5.7;
iii. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement because it is not based
on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of the risks to plant life or
health, taking into account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant
international organizations;
iv. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement because, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account the elements listed in
this provision in the assessment of risks;
v. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.3 of the SPS Agreement because, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account the economic factors
listed in this provision in assessing the risks;
vi. Measure 3 is inconsistent with the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
because it arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminates against pineapple from Costa Rica
compared to avocado from Colombia;
vii. Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Measure 3 arbitrarily or unjustifiably
discriminates against pineapple from Costa Rica compared to pineapple from the
United States and Colombia, in breach of the first sentence of Article 2.3 of the
SPS Agreement;
viii. Measure 3 is inconsistent with the second sentence of Article 2.3 of the SPS Agreement
because it is applied in a manner which constitutes a disguised restriction on
international trade;
ix. the Panel exercises judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under
Article 5.5 of the SPS Agreement;
x. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement because it is more
trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP;

WT/DS599/R
- 351 -
xi. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement because it is not
applied only to the extent necessary to protect plant life or health, is not based on
scientific principles, and is maintained without sufficient scientific evidence;
xii. Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Measure 3 is inconsistent with Article 7 and
Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement with respect to the publication obligations
established by these provisions;
xiii. Measure 3 is inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement because the
measure is inconsistent with other provisions of the SPS Agreement;
xiv. the Panel exercises judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under
Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement; and
xv. the Panel exercises judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under the
GATT 1994 and the Agreement on Agriculture.
e. Regarding the measure concerning the importation of plantains and bananas (Measure 4):
i. the SPS Agreement is applicable to Measure 4 because the measure constitutes an
SPS measure within the meaning of Annex A(1)(a) to the SPS Agreement and directly
affects international trade within the meaning of Article 1.1 of the SPS Agreement;
ii. Measure 4 does not constitute a provisional measure covered by Article 5.7 of the
SPS Agreement because Costa Rica has demonstrated, and Panama has failed to
rebut, that Panama did not adopt Measure 4 in a situation of insufficient relevant
scientific evidence;
iii. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.1 of the SPS Agreement because it is not based
on an assessment, as appropriate to the circumstances, of the risks to plant life or
health, taking into account risk assessment techniques developed by the relevant
international organizations;
iv. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.2 of the SPS Agreement because, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account the elements listed in
this provision in the assessment of risks;
v. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.3 of the SPS Agreement because, in the
absence of a risk assessment, Panama did not take into account the economic factors
listed in this provision in assessing the risks;
vi. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 5.6 of the SPS Agreement because it is more
trade-restrictive than required to achieve Panama's ALOP;
vii. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 2.2 of the SPS Agreement because it is not
applied only to the extent necessary to protect plant life or health, is not based on
scientific principles, and is maintained without sufficient scientific evidence;
viii. Costa Rica has failed to demonstrate that Measure 4 is inconsistent with Article 7 and
Annex B(1) of the SPS Agreement with respect to the publication obligations
established by these provisions;
ix. Measure 4 is inconsistent with Articles 1.1 and 2.1 of the SPS Agreement because the
measure is inconsistent with other provisions of the SPS Agreement;
x. the Panel exercises judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under
Article 3.1 of the SPS Agreement; and
xi. the Panel exercises judicial economy with respect to Costa Rica's claims under the
GATT 1994 and the Agreement on Agriculture.

WT/DS599/R
- 352 -
8.2. Under Article 3.8 of the DSU, in cases where there is an infringement of the obligations
assumed under a covered agreement, the action is considered prima facie to constitute a case of
nullification or impairment. The Panel concludes that, to the extent that the measures at issue are
inconsistent with the SPS Agreement, they have nullified or impaired benefits accruing to Costa Rica
under that agreement.
8.3. Pursuant to Article 19.1 of the DSU, the Panel recommends that Panama bring its measures
into conformity with its obligations under the SPS Agreement.

12 February 2021 A high-level binational meeting is held in Paso Canoas, where the following is agreed:
• The establishment of a Bilateral Technical Working Commission on sanitary and
phytosanitary measures between the authorities of Costa Rica and Panama.
• Panama would approve the plants of Biomar and Central Veterinaria before
19 February 2021 and conduct "an evaluation of the Costa Rican sanitary system for
products of trade interest, which w[ould] take place within 30 working days of the
request". If the evaluation were positive, Panama would automatically renew for six
months the plants exporting products of animal origin for human consumption.2803
15 February 2021 As part of the Paso Canoas commitments, AUPSA informs SENASA that it granted
establishment No. 158 of the company Central Veterinaria and establishment No. 762 of
the company Biomar a six-month extension of their sanitary approval.
In addition, AUPSA asks SENASA for a note in which it "give[s] [its] consent to initiate
the animal health eligibility process [for Costa Rica]".2804
16 February 2021 In response to the communication from AUPSA the previous day, SENASA requests "the
date on which Panama is to initiate the process, in order to prepare the information
required".2805
18 February 2021 The first session of the Bilateral Technical Working Commission is held, where it is
agreed, inter alia:
• that Panama would conduct the evaluation of Costa Rica's sanitary system within
30 working days of the Ministers' agreement;
• that Panama would inform Costa Rica about what information is required by its
authorities; and that
• Panama would send Costa Rica the information required in relation to the sanitary
approval of Panamanian plants by no later than 22 February 2021.2806
22 February 2021 Further to the Paso Canoas commitments, AUPSA:
• notifies SENASA of the formal initiation of Costa Rica's animal health evaluation
procedure;
• sends a new questionnaire; and
• indicates that, at "a later stage, Panama will carry out the relevant on-site
verifications, as provided for in our regulations".2807
